Jalen Navigation Lesson->Act1 16a Jalen Navigation Lesson]] * [[Optional 16b: Emergency Drill->Act1 16b Emergency Drill]] * [[Optional 16c: Maintenance Pairing Day->Act1 16c Maintenance Pairing Day]] Of Locality]] * [[Optional 17a: Assumption Lab->Act1 17a Assumption Lab]] * [[Optional 17b: Complaint Posting->Act1 17b Complaint Posting]] * [[Optional 17c: Reading Technical Manual->Act1 17c Reading Technical Manual]] * [[Optional 17d: Library Glossary Of Lies->Act1 17d Library Glossary Of Lies]] * [[Optional 17e: Nyx And Halcyon Ward Lesson->Act1 17e Nyx And Halcyon Ward Lesson]] * [[Chapter 18: Primordial Garden->Act1 18 Primordial Garden]] * [[Optional 18a: Cultural Context->Act1 18a Cultural Context]] * [[Optional 18b: Station Overwhelm Recovery->Act1 18b Station Overwhelm Recovery]] * Witness->Act1 19a Contract Negotiation Witness]] * [[Optional 19b: Teaching Consent To Client->Act1 19b Teaching Consent To Client]] * [[Optional 19c: Receipt Practice->Act1 19c Receipt Practice]] * [[Chapter 20: The Receipt->Act1 20 The Receipt]] * [[Optional 20a: Quiet Ship Night->Act1 20a Quiet Ship Night]] * [[Optional 20b: Witnessing Crew Conflict->Act1 20b Witnessing Crew Conflict]] * [[Optional 20c: Archive Ritual->Act1 20c Archive Ritual]] * [[Optional 20d: Aleena Pulls Elia From Wreckage->Act1 20d Aleena Pulls Elia From Wreckage]]Ending: Act 1 route complete.<h1>Chapter 00: Prologue Floors Not Thrones</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 00_prologue_floors_not_thrones.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **GUILD CONCORD - FORM 7-DELTA** &gt; *Vessel:* Lumen Thief (Independent) &gt; *Classification:* Boarding Action (Attempted) &gt; *Outcome:* Resolved &gt; *Casualties:* 0 (crew) | 4 (hostile, non-fatal) | 1 (hostile, fatal-self-inflicted during escape) &gt; *Note:* Command structure disputed. See attached waiver. &gt; *Addendum:* Coffee machine status unchanged. ████████ filed separately.</p> <hr> <p>[The hull screams before the alarms do.]</p> <p>[Elia is three corridors from the bridge when she feels it-that specific frequency of metal under thermal assault. Someone cutting through. The sound is high and wrong, the kind that lives in the back teeth.]</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t run. Running is a statement about priorities. Walking faster is just efficiency.]</p> <p>[The Lumen Thief&#x27;s corridors are narrow and stripped. Conduit bundles hang where paneling used to be. The air tastes like recycled everything and the faint chemical ghost of a coolant leak they fixed six months ago but never quite fixed. The deck plates tick beneath her boots-thermal expansion, the ship&#x27;s skeleton adjusting to stress it didn&#x27;t ask for. Somewhere behind her, the coffee machine continues its mechanical sulk-she&#x27;d been mid-argument with it when the cutting started, and neither of them had conceded.]</p> <p>(Thermal lance. Military-grade, probably. Two minutes to breach, maybe less.)</p> <p>[A pop-up flickers across her ocular feed, automatic and indifferent:]</p> <p>&gt; **HULL INTEGRITY ALERT** &gt; Unauthorized modification detected (starboard cargo, section 7). &gt; File waiver Y/N? &gt; *Note: Waivers filed during active breach events are subject to 12% processing surcharge.*</p> <p>[She blinks it away. The surcharge will be someone else&#x27;s problem.]</p> <p>Elia: [into comm, not breaking stride] Waffle. Report.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// ALERT: BOARDING ACTION | VESSEL: UNREGISTERED | WEAPONS: HOT | CONFIDENCE: 0.91 | PERSONNEL: 5 (4 MOBILE, 1 STATIONARY-SHIP-BOUND?)&gt;</p> <p>Waffle.bat: [through speaker, calm in the way that means not-calm] Unregistered vessel. Modified hauler. They&#x27;ve locked on. Cutting through starboard cargo. Five life signs-four are moving in. One&#x27;s still on their ship. Pilot, probably, or someone who knows better.</p> <p>Elia: Weapons?</p> <p>Waffle.bat: Standard pulse rifles, mostly. One plasma cutter doubling as close-range. And- [a pause, recalibrating] -one of them is doing something with the Lattice. Badly.</p> <p>Elia: [dry] Helpful.</p> <p>Waffle.bat: I try.</p> <p>[The cutting sound grows. She can feel the heat through the deck-not literally, but the ship can, and she&#x27;s been on it long enough to know its complaints. The ventilation pitch shifts, fans working harder to compensate for atmosphere they&#x27;re about to lose.]</p> <hr> <p>[Junction C-7. Rho is already there.]</p> <p>[He&#x27;s carrying the cannon-the one Jalen calls &quot;the conversation ender&quot; and Vesper calls &quot;a liability in seventeen jurisdictions.&quot; It&#x27;s too large for the corridor, which means he has to angle his shoulders and move slightly crab-wise. The power cell hums like something dreaming of collateral damage.]</p> <p>Rho: [into comm] Floor check. Who&#x27;s calling pattern?</p> <p>Elia: [into comm] Routing traffic. Starboard cargo breach, four mobile hostiles plus ship-bound. Rho, junction funnel. Elisira, comms intercept. Nyx, Lattice interference on standby.</p> <p>Vesper: [through comm, from somewhere that sounds like paperwork] Floor holds.</p> <p>Rho: Floor holds. [checking sight lines] Funnel position, confirmed.</p> <p>Elisira: [materializing from the nearest shadow] Floor holds. Their comms are spoofed. They think they&#x27;re talking to each other.</p> <p>Nyx: [calm, distant-probably still in their quarters, already working] Floor holds. I see the Lattice fluctuation. Amateur work.</p> <p>[The response comes fast, overlapping, muscle memory. No one asked permission. No one gave orders. The pattern just happened, the way patterns do when you&#x27;ve done them a hundred times.]</p> <p>Elia: [to Rho, quieter now] Don&#x27;t kill anyone we don&#x27;t have to.</p> <p>[A pause. Something in Rho&#x27;s shoulders changes-tighter. The weight of a count that never stops.]</p> <p>Rho: [quiet] I know.</p> <p>[They split. Rho toward his position. Elia toward the bay. The cutting whine is louder now, setting her teeth on edge. Through the metal, she can feel it-the ship&#x27;s skin being violated centimeter by centimeter.]</p> <p>[Elisira falls into step beside her, already talking:]</p> <p>Elisira: Their comms say the independent contractor vessel will be easy. That they&#x27;ve done this before. That the captain- [a pause, the word deliberate] -won&#x27;t be a problem.</p> <p>Elia: [jaw tightening] They said captain?</p> <p>Elisira: They did. Also: the pilot-the one staying ship-bound-keeps asking about &quot;the package.&quot; They&#x27;re here for something specific.</p> <p>Elia: Do they know what?</p> <p>Elisira: No. Just that it&#x27;s valuable. Just that someone&#x27;s paying. [beat] The pilot mentioned docking fees. His kids. He&#x27;s doing this because the alternative is worse.</p> <p>(Always. It&#x27;s always the same. The ones at the bottom eating the ones further down.)</p> <p>[The cargo bay door. Through it, the cutting stops-that particular silence before breakthrough, when the circle of molten metal is about to become a hole.]</p> <p>[Elia draws the blade. It&#x27;s not a sword, exactly. The edge exists in a way that makes geometry uncomfortable. Elisira has opinions about the blade that she expresses through careful silence.]</p> <p>Elia: [quiet] Bubbles. You ready?</p> <p>Bubbles: [through earpiece, voice like warm water over stones] Drones are positioned. They&#x27;ll have a very educational few seconds if they try to spread out.</p> <p>Elia: Keep it non-lethal if you can.</p> <p>Bubbles: I can. Whether they&#x27;ll let me is their decision.</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// CONSENT: PRESENT | COERCION: &quot;GUILD CONTRACT&quot; | ANALYSIS: HOSTILES BELIEVE THIS IS CHOICE. IT ISN&#x27;T. BUT OURS IS.&gt;</p> <p>[The bay door opens.]</p> <hr> <p>[The pirates come through the breach like they expect nothing.]</p> <p>[Four of them. The fifth-Pol, the pilot, the one with the kids-stayed on the ship. Smart. Mismatched armor on the rest-some of it military surplus, some of it junk dressed up with paint. They move in formation, but it&#x27;s rusty. The kind of coordination that comes from training they had once and practice they haven&#x27;t kept up.]</p> <p>[The lead one is large, bearded, carrying a pulse rifle with aftermarket modifications that probably void seventeen warranties. He has the face of someone who&#x27;s done this enough times to get bored. On his wrist, a debt ledger flickers-numbers scrolling in the bad light. Red. Deep red. The kind of debt that explains why someone boards ships for a living.]</p> <p>[The cargo bay is mostly empty. A few crates along the walls. Equipment that wasn&#x27;t worth selling. The lighting is bad-half the fixtures are dead, and the rest flicker with the rhythm of a ship that&#x27;s been repaired too many times by people who couldn&#x27;t afford to do it right.]</p> <p>[Elia steps through the door.]</p> <p>Elia: Gentlemen.</p> <p>[The lead pirate&#x27;s rifle comes up. The others follow. Four weapons, four sightlines. The geometry of threat establishing itself in the bad light.]</p> <p>Lead Pirate: Down on the ground. Hands visible. Now.</p> <p>[Elia doesn&#x27;t move. Behind her, she feels more than sees Elisira fade back-not retreating. Repositioning. Becoming harder to track.]</p> <p>Elia: I don&#x27;t think I will.</p> <p>Lead Pirate: I said-</p> <p>Elia: [cutting him off, same calm tone] I heard you. The acoustics are poor, but not that poor.</p> <p>[She takes a step forward. Just one. The pirates adjust their aim. Fingers tightening.]</p> <p>Elia: You&#x27;ve cut through my hull. That&#x27;s going to need repair. You&#x27;ve also made assumptions about command structure that I find- [considering the word] -philosophically distasteful. I&#x27;m going to file a Command Structure Dispute with the Guild. Form 7-Delta, Addendum C. It classifies your vessel as &quot;salvageable property under reciprocity statute&quot; and your crew as &quot;detained witnesses pending philosophical review.&quot;</p> <p>Lead Pirate: [face doing something complicated-confusion layered over threat] What the hell are you talking about?</p> <p>Elia: Paperwork. The kind that outlives everyone in this room. [another step, the blade still casual at her side] You came here expecting a captain. A throat to cut. Someone whose death makes the rest compliant.</p> <p>[The second pirate shifts. His rifle wavers.]</p> <p>Elia: We don&#x27;t have that. We have a ship full of people who work together. Floors, not thrones. No hierarchy for you to decapitate.</p> <p>Lead Pirate: [snarling now] Lady, I don&#x27;t care about your-</p> <p>[She moves.]</p> <hr> <p>[The blade doesn&#x27;t hurry. It arrives at the necessary points in the necessary order.]</p> <p>[Rifle barrel. Shoulder joint. The thin armor at the wrist.]</p> <p>[The lead pirate&#x27;s weapon comes apart in pieces, and his arm stops working in a way that&#x27;s painful but not permanent. He goes down screaming. Blood on the deck-not much, but enough to smell. The copper-bright edge of damage done.]</p> <p>[The second pirate fires. Misses. The cargo bay&#x27;s geometry works against him-crates providing cover she&#x27;s already using, bad lighting making tracking difficult. She&#x27;s behind a container before the pulse bolt finishes its trajectory.]</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// HOSTILE 2: PANICKING | HOSTILE 3: RETREATING | HOSTILE 4: LATTICE FLUCTUATION DETECTED | HOSTILE 5 (SHIP-BOUND): HEART RATE SPIKING-HE KNOWS | OBSERVATION: THEY CAME HERE EXPECTING PREY. MISCALCULATION.&gt;</p> <p>[Rho&#x27;s cannon speaks from the corridor junction.]</p> <p>[Not at the pirates-at the deck plating between them and the doors. The metal superheats. Warps. Becomes terrain that punishes anyone stupid enough to run.]</p> <p>[The third pirate-younger than the others, jittery, face tight with fear that predates this moment-tries to retreat toward the hull breach. Bubbles&#x27; drones intercept him. They&#x27;re small, fast, and deeply uninterested in his preferences. He goes down twitching, muscles locked by the kind of current that leaves bruises but not burns.]</p> <p>Bubbles: [through comm, mild] He&#x27;ll be fine in about four minutes. Possibly five. I was a little annoyed.</p> <p>[That leaves one in the bay.]</p> <p>[The fourth pirate is different. Smaller. Something in the way they move suggests training that isn&#x27;t combat-their hands have been making patterns since everything went wrong. A resonance worker. Someone trying to manipulate the Lattice. Trying to pull something, bend something, force gravity itself to give them an escape velocity that doesn&#x27;t exist.]</p> <p>[Nyx appears beside them like they&#x27;ve always been there.]</p> <p>Nyx: [quiet, academic, utterly certain] Don&#x27;t.</p> <p>[The fourth pirate freezes. Hands mid-gesture.]</p> <p>Fourth Pirate: I-what-</p> <p>Nyx: Whatever you&#x27;re attempting, the harmonics are wrong. The feedback will be significant.</p> <p>Fourth Pirate: [staring] How did you-</p> <p>Nyx: I understand the theory. I also understand that our ship&#x27;s Lattice architecture doesn&#x27;t work the way you think it does. [a pause, almost gentle] Please sit down. I&#x27;d rather not spend the next hour treating aetheric burns.</p> <p>[The fourth pirate sits.]</p> <p>[And on the pirate ship, still docked to their hull like a parasite-]</p> <hr> <p>[The fifth pirate makes a run for it.]</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// ALERT: HOSTILE 5 ATTEMPTING EMERGENCY SEPARATION | DOCKING CLAMPS: LOCKED (BUBBLES) | ESCAPE VECTOR: CALCULATING...&gt;</p> <p>[His name is Pol. He has two children on a station he&#x27;ll never see again because of a debt ledger that compounds faster than he can pay. He stayed on the ship because he&#x27;s the pilot, because someone has to be ready, because the others promised this would be quick.]</p> <p>[The others were wrong.]</p> <p>[Pol sees the feeds. Sees his captain on the deck bleeding. Sees the drones, the blade, the impossible coordination of a crew that fights like one animal with six heads. He tries the docking clamps. Locked. Tries to override. Can&#x27;t. The Lumen Thief&#x27;s AI citizens have him boxed in, and they&#x27;re not even trying hard.]</p> <p>Bubbles: [through his ship&#x27;s speakers, calm and kind and absolutely terrifying] Please don&#x27;t.</p> <p>[He doesn&#x27;t listen.]</p> <p>[The escape pod. Every ship has one-even a ship this broken, even a hauler modified for piracy on a shoestring. He keys the sequence. The pod is old, maintenance overdue, the kind of equipment that works until it doesn&#x27;t.]</p> <p>[It launches.]</p> <p>[The docking clamps haven&#x27;t released. The pod launches anyway-tears itself free at the seam where pirate hull meets clamp assembly. Metal screams. The stress fractures cascade: hull breach, seal failure, atmosphere gone in a second. The physics are simple and cruel: a pod can&#x27;t survive being ripped from a ship that won&#x27;t let go.]</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// ESCAPE POD: STRUCTURAL FAILURE | CAUSE: LAUNCH STRESS EXCEEDED CLAMP TOLERANCE | HULL INTEGRITY: 0% | HOSTILE 5 STATUS: ... | ...DECEASED | SELF-INFLICTED | WE WARNED HIM. HE DIDN&#x27;T LISTEN.&gt;</p> <p>[The pod tumbles into the black, spinning slowly, close enough that Bubbles&#x27; repair drones can reach it. Pol has perhaps three seconds to understand what he&#x27;s done before the cold takes him.]</p> <p>[On the Lumen Thief, the cargo bay goes quiet.]</p> <p>[Rho lowers the cannon. His face is still. The count in his head adds one more.]</p> <p>Rho: [into comm, flat] We have a casualty. Hostile. Pod breach. Self-inflicted during escape attempt.</p> <p>[No one says anything.]</p> <p>[The fans whine. The deck plates tick. The silence stretches.]</p> <hr> <p>[The cleanup is different now. Quieter.]</p> <p>[Rho supervises the restraints with patient attention. The remaining four pirates-Darros, the leader, still bleeding from Elia&#x27;s precision; the panicked shooter, zip-tied and staring; the jittery kid, still twitching from drone current; the resonance worker, sitting where Nyx left them-are silent in a way that has nothing to do with threat.]</p> <p>[They know about Pol. They saw the pod launch. They heard the silence that followed.]</p> <p>[The lead pirate-Darros-looks up at Elia with an expression that&#x27;s finished choosing between rage and fear. Fear won.]</p> <p>Darros: [through gritted teeth] You didn&#x27;t have to-</p> <p>Elia: [flat] We didn&#x27;t kill him. He killed himself trying to escape.</p> <p>Darros: The clamps-</p> <p>Elia: Were locked because you were boarding us. Because you chose to cut through our hull. Because- [she stops, breathes, continues quieter] -we gave him every chance to stay put. He didn&#x27;t take it.</p> <p>[Darros doesn&#x27;t have an answer for that.]</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// SOURCE: SHIP REGISTRY + COMM HEADERS | POL VERADA | AGE: 34 | DEPENDENTS: 2 | DEBT STATUS: TERMINAL | NOTE: HE DIDN&#x27;T HAVE TO DIE. BUT HE ALSO DIDN&#x27;T HAVE MANY OTHER OPTIONS. I HATE THIS.&gt;</p> <p>[Elia crouches. Bringing her face closer to his. The light is bad, but not so bad he can&#x27;t see her eyes.]</p> <p>Elia: You&#x27;ve hit other ships. Four, we think. Unreported. Either the crews didn&#x27;t survive or they couldn&#x27;t afford to file.</p> <p>[His jaw tightens. That&#x27;s answer enough.]</p> <p>Elia: We&#x27;re going to take your ship. Strip what&#x27;s useful. The things we find that belong to other people-we&#x27;ll return them if we can.</p> <p>[She stands.]</p> <p>Elia: And you&#x27;re going to tell me about the package.</p> <p>Darros: What?</p> <p>Elia: The package. The thing someone&#x27;s paying you to retrieve. The reason you were coming to this sector.</p> <p>[His expression shifts. Fear winning over everything now-but not fear of her. Fear of something behind the question.]</p> <p>Darros: I don&#x27;t know what it is. Just coordinates. A retrieval order. A mining colony, Outer Compact. Someone found something they shouldn&#x27;t have found, and someone else wants it collected.</p> <p>Elia: Who&#x27;s paying?</p> <p>Darros: [looking away] I don&#x27;t know. Money came through three layers of cutouts. Good money. Enough to make the questions feel optional.</p> <p>[Elia stands. Looks at Elisira, who has rematerialized near the hull breach with a portable console. Looks at Nyx, examining the resonance worker with clinical attention. Looks at the cargo bay-the damage, the blood, the evidence of violence that will need to be cleaned and reported and explained in forms that exist specifically to make explaining difficult.]</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t look at the viewport. Doesn&#x27;t look at the stars where Pol&#x27;s pod is tumbling, cold and quiet and done.]</p> <p>Elia: [into comm] Vesper. I assume you heard all that.</p> <p>Vesper: [voice coming from somewhere-not quite the comm, more like the air has decided to participate] I did. Cross-referencing the coordinates now. Also- [a pause] -I&#x27;m drafting the casualty report. Self-inflicted during escape. No liability attaches, but the forms are going to be extensive.</p> <p>Elia: [bitter] Of course they are.</p> <p>Vesper: Form 7-Delta. Addendum 3. Subsection &quot;Unintended Fatality During Lawful Defensive Action.&quot; They&#x27;ll want a timeline. They&#x27;ll want the drone logs. They&#x27;ll want to know why the clamps were locked.</p> <p>Elia: The clamps were locked because they were boarding us.</p> <p>Vesper: I know. The forms don&#x27;t care.</p> <p>[A pause. The fans whine overhead-that particular frequency of a ship that&#x27;s been patched too many times. The lights in the wounded cargo bay flicker, dim, stabilize-the ship&#x27;s systems rerouting power around the damage.]</p> <p>Vesper: Mining colony. Site K-9. Aurum Extraction operation. Corporate-held. Debtor-adjacent board.</p> <p>Elia: What are they mining?</p> <p>Vesper: Aurum ore. Lattice catalyst material. Standard extraction-officially.</p> <p>Elia: And unofficially?</p> <p>[A longer pause. Longer than Vesper usually pauses.]</p> <p>Vesper: Their energy reports don&#x27;t add up. Consumption patterns are wrong. Worker mortality rates are high. Higher than industry standard, which is already not good.</p> <p>(Someone&#x27;s pulling. Someone&#x27;s taking. The same gravity well, different orbit. Systems that extract until there&#x27;s nothing left.)</p> <p>Elia: How high?</p> <p>Vesper: Workers aging faster than they should. Dying younger. The company calls it &quot;occupational hazard.&quot; The Guild contract flags it as &quot;anomaly investigation.&quot;</p> <p>[A beat.]</p> <p>Vesper: But between us, Elia-it looks like extraction. Not ore. Something else. Someone&#x27;s pulling life from those workers the way Pol&#x27;s debt pulled him into this ship.</p> <p>[The cargo bay feels colder. Not physically-the life support hasn&#x27;t changed-but in the way a room changes when you learn something you can&#x27;t unlearn.]</p> <p>Elia: [into comm] Jalen. New course.</p> <p>Jalen: [slightly breathless-they&#x27;ve been at the helm the entire time, keeping the ship positioned for escape options that weren&#x27;t needed] Already calculating. Eighteen hours. Maybe sixteen if we push.</p> <p>Elia: Push.</p> <p>Jalen: [a grin in their voice] Thought you&#x27;d say that.</p> <p>[Darros is staring at her. Confusion again-but different now. The confusion of someone watching a script they didn&#x27;t write.]</p> <p>Darros: Who are you people?</p> <p>Elia: [considering him] We chase sparkles.</p> <p>Darros: That doesn&#x27;t-</p> <p>Elia: It doesn&#x27;t have to make sense to you. [to Rho, who is processing restraints with patient, deliberate attention] Med attention for the injuries. All of them.</p> <p>[She turns to leave. Pauses at the door. The lights flicker again-the ship complaining, adjusting, surviving the way it always has.]</p> <p>Elia: They&#x27;re witnesses now. That makes them valuable.</p> <p>[She walks out. Behind her, the cleanup continues-Rho&#x27;s hands steady even when his voice isn&#x27;t, Nyx helping the resonance worker to their feet, Bubbles&#x27; drones settling into patrol patterns that feel more like mourning than security.]</p> <p>[The cargo bay transitions: battlefield to crime scene to evidence log.]</p> <p>[The coffee machine, three corridors away, continues its sulk. But quieter now. Even it seems to know that something has changed.]</p> <hr> <p>[The observation deck is small and rarely used. A luxury the Lumen Thief wasn&#x27;t designed for-retrofitted by a previous owner with more money than sense.]</p> <p>[The viewport is scratched. The seating is minimal. The environmental controls are erratic-too cold most of the time, occasionally too warm for no reason anyone can diagnose.]</p> <p>[But it&#x27;s quiet. And it offers a view of stars that don&#x27;t ask anything of her.]</p> <p>[Vesper finds her there an hour later. The repairs are underway. The pirates are secured. The paperwork is multiplying with the organic determination of bureaucracy given enough raw material. Pol&#x27;s body has been recovered. They&#x27;ll return it to his station. His children will receive a form.]</p> <p>Vesper: [settling beside her-not touching, but present] You&#x27;re thinking about the mine.</p> <p>Elia: I&#x27;m thinking about Pol.</p> <p>Vesper: [quiet] That wasn&#x27;t your fault.</p> <p>Elia: It wasn&#x27;t not my fault either. He made a choice. We made the conditions where that choice killed him.</p> <p>Vesper: He was boarding us. He was part of a crew that has killed before.</p> <p>Elia: [flat] I know. And he had two kids and a debt ledger that left him no good options and he died trying to escape a situation he shouldn&#x27;t have been in. [beat] We didn&#x27;t kill him. But we&#x27;re part of why he&#x27;s dead.</p> <p>[Vesper doesn&#x27;t argue. She knows better.]</p> <p>Vesper: [after a moment] The mine is worse.</p> <p>Elia: I know.</p> <p>Vesper: Whatever they&#x27;re doing to those workers-it&#x27;s not fast like Pol. It&#x27;s slow. Years of it. Life leaking out in increments small enough to ignore.</p> <p>Elia: [turning from the viewport] That&#x27;s why we&#x27;re going.</p> <p>Vesper: [quiet] Floors, not thrones.</p> <p>Elia: Floors, not thrones.</p> <p>[Her hand finds Elia&#x27;s arm-brief, warm. The weight of solidarity more than affection. The orbit of two people who&#x27;ve learned to move together through conditions that should tear them apart.]</p> <p>Vesper: We can&#x27;t save everyone. We can save some. That&#x27;s not nothing.</p> <p>Elia: It&#x27;s not enough.</p> <p>Vesper: It&#x27;s never enough. That&#x27;s not an argument against trying.</p> <p>[Elia looks at her. Vesper&#x27;s face in the starlight is composed, certain. The expression of someone who made peace with insufficiency a long time ago.]</p> <p>(Seven years. They&#x27;ve known each other seven years. Stopped counting the impossible situations, the partial victories, the people saved and the people lost.)</p> <p>[She stands. Moves closer to the viewport. Her reflection ghosts over the stars-transparent, uncertain. Somewhere out there, Pol&#x27;s pod spins in the dark. Somewhere out there, a mine full of workers is dying slowly enough that the forms call it &quot;occupational hazard.&quot;]</p> <p>Elia: There&#x27;s something at that mine. Probably a person. Probably young. And when we find them, they&#x27;re going to look at us the way Darros did-expecting someone in charge. Expecting to be told what to do.</p> <p>Vesper: [standing, meeting her gaze] And we&#x27;re going to show them something different.</p> <p>Elia: [quiet] Every time we do this, we&#x27;re being tested. Not by the people we&#x27;re saving. By the philosophy itself.</p> <p>Vesper: [certain, but not harsh] That&#x27;s all we can do. Keep showing up. Keep being wrong and correcting and trying again.</p> <p>[The ship hums around them. Drives preparing for the push. Course set for a mining colony called the Kennel, where workers die young and someone found something valuable enough to kill for.]</p> <p>Elia: Sixteen hours.</p> <p>Vesper: Sixteen hours.</p> <p>Elia: I should sleep.</p> <p>Vesper: You should. You won&#x27;t. But you should.</p> <p>Elia: [almost a smile-almost] Record this conversation. All of it. Put it in the personal logs with the date stamp and mission parameters.</p> <p>Vesper: Why?</p> <p>Elia: Because when we find what we find-when we meet whoever we&#x27;re going to meet-I want a record. Of the doubt. Of the questions. Of the moment before we learned the answer. [moving toward the door] That&#x27;s the kind of documentation that actually matters. Not the forms. The uncertainty. The proof that we knew we could fail and went anyway.</p> <p>[She leaves before Vesper can respond.]</p> <p>[The observation deck settles into silence. Stars. Scratched viewport. The sound of a ship moving toward something it doesn&#x27;t yet understand.]</p> <p>[Vesper stays. Watching the dark. Thinking about Pol&#x27;s children, who will receive a form. Thinking about the workers in the mine, who receive forms every day. Thinking about the difference between systems that kill quickly and systems that kill slowly, and how neither kind ever apologizes.]</p> <hr> <p>[In the cargo bay below, the cleanup continues.]</p> <p>[Rho supervises the restraints with patient attention. The count matters-it always matters-and tonight one more has been added to it. Not by his hand. But close enough to feel.]</p> <p>[Nyx examines the resonance worker&#x27;s technique with academic concern. The harmonics were wrong, yes, but the underlying talent was there. Misused. Untrained. Wasted on piracy by a system that made piracy look like opportunity.]</p> <p>[Bubbles&#x27; drones patrol in quiet patterns, watching for trouble that&#x27;s already passed. Her attention is split between the prisoners and the hull breach-repair estimates running in parallel with something that might be grief, if AIs could grieve, which they can, because they&#x27;re people, because that&#x27;s the whole point.]</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// HULL INTEGRITY: COMPROMISED | REPAIR TIME: 4.2 HOURS | PRISONER STATUS: STABLE | POL VERADA: RECOVERED | PERSONAL NOTE: WE DID WHAT WE COULD. IT WASN&#x27;T ENOUGH. IT&#x27;S NEVER ENOUGH. WE DO IT ANYWAY.&gt;</p> <p>[And somewhere in the ship&#x27;s systems, Cinnamon.exe logs everything-the coordinates, the financial traces, the patterns that mean something if you know how to read them.]</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// MINING COLONY: K-9 &quot;THE KENNEL&quot; | WORKER MORTALITY: ELEVATED | ENERGY CONSUMPTION: ANOMALOUS | DEBTOR BOARD CONNECTIONS: PROBABLE | RECOMMENDATION: INVESTIGATE | SECONDARY RECOMMENDATION: PACK EXTRA MED SUPPLIES. PACK EXTRA GRIEF. THIS ONE LOOKS LONG.&gt;</p> <p>[The mine is real. The workers are dying. Someone found something. Someone else wants it collected.]</p> <p>*Sparkles*, the crew calls them. The points of light in the dark. The moments when wrong and right become visible enough to act on.</p> <p>They&#x27;re going to find one.</p> <p>They don&#x27;t know yet what it will cost.</p> <p>*But they know it will cost something. That&#x27;s how it works. That&#x27;s how it always works. You spend yourself on the things that matter, and the only question is whether you&#x27;re spending on floors or thrones.*</p> <p>*This crew builds floors.*</p> <p>*The universe keeps testing whether they mean it.*</p> <hr> <p>&gt; **ADDENDUM - FORM 7-DELTA** &gt; *Filed by:* V. Rook, Operations &gt; *Re:* Command Structure Dispute &gt; &gt; The Lumen Thief has no captain. This is not administrative error. This is philosophy. &gt; &gt; Credit for incident resolution should be attributed collectively. AI citizens Bubbles, Cinnamon.exe, and Waffle.bat are registered as full operational partners under Guild statute 17.3(b). Attempts to assign command authority to any individual will be formally disputed. &gt; &gt; We have documentation. &gt; &gt; *Attached:* Hull repair estimate. Prisoner transfer forms. Casualty report (hostile, self-inflicted-Form 7-Delta-3, &quot;Unintended Fatality During Lawful Defensive Action&quot;). Coffee machine maintenance ticket (unrelated, ongoing, classified: PERSONAL GRIEVANCE).</p> <hr> <p>*End Prologue*</p> </div> Where does the route go next? * [[Continue to Chapter 01: Cinder Hours->Act1 01 Cinder Hours]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Chapter 01: Cinder Hours</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 01_cinder_hours.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **AURUM EXTRACTION LTD. - DAILY ACCOUNTABILITY REPORT** &gt; *Site:* K-9 (&quot;The Kennel&quot;) &gt; *Shift:* Cinder (0400-1200) &gt; *Personnel Verified:* 847/849 &gt; *Discrepancy:* 2 (see mortality log, Form 9-ECHO) &gt; *Productivity Index:* 94.2% (within acceptable parameters) &gt; *Note:* Air filtration maintenance deferred (eighth consecutive quarter). Budget pending.</p> <hr> <p>[The siren comes first. Then the counting.]</p> <p>[Avyanna opens her eyes before the sound finishes. Her body knows the rhythm. Eight years of this rhythm. The siren is a knife through sleep, and her nerves flinch before it lands.]</p> <p>[The bunk above her creaks. Someone coughs three tiers down. The coffin-pods are stacked four high, and the air between them tastes like the inside of a recycler-metal, wet, old breath.]</p> <p>(Sixty for air. Thirty for water. Forty-five for food. One-twenty for the bunk.)</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t sit up yet. The count hasn&#x27;t reached her section.]</p> <p>[Supervisor Coil moves through the hab stack with a scanner. Her footsteps are heavy. Deliberate. She used to work Slurry 7, before the promotion. Everyone knows. No one mentions it.]</p> <p>Coil: [calling numbers, not names] Four-seven-two. Four-seven-three. Four-seven-four.</p> <p>[Each number gets a beep. Confirmation. The scanner checks vitals, location, debt balance. All at once.]</p> <p>[Coil pauses at four-seven-three. The bunk is empty. The sheets are cold. Everyone pretends not to notice. Coil marks something on her pad, and her jaw tightens-just slightly, just for a moment-before she moves on.]</p> <p>Coil: Four-seven-five. Four-seven-six.</p> <p>[Avyanna is four-seven-seven.]</p> <p>[She sits up. Swings her legs out. Her feet find the cold deck. The motion is automatic. Everything here is automatic.]</p> <p>Coil: Four-seven-seven.</p> <p>[The scanner beeps against Avyanna&#x27;s neck. The tag is embedded there-has been since she arrived. It&#x27;s warm against her skin. Always warm. Tracking, always.]</p> <p>[Coil&#x27;s eyes pass over her. Through her. Avyanna is good at being passed over. On Coil&#x27;s wrist, her own debt ledger flickers-numbers Avyanna isn&#x27;t supposed to see. Red, like everyone&#x27;s. Even the supervisors are bleeding.]</p> <p>Coil: [moving on] Four-seven-eight.</p> <hr> <p>[The wash line is long. The water is lukewarm. The ration is thirty ticks for three minutes, and everyone moves fast.]</p> <p>[Avyanna washes her face, her hands, the back of her neck where the tag itches. The water runs gray. It always runs gray. Aurum dust clings. Gets in the skin. Stays.]</p> <p>[The woman next to her-older, maybe forty, looks sixty-scrubs methodically. Her hands shake. The fine tremor of someone whose body is running out.]</p> <p>Older Woman: [not looking at Avyanna, just talking] Cold today.</p> <p>Avyanna: [flat] Always cold.</p> <p>Older Woman: Colder than yesterday. Vents again.</p> <p>[There&#x27;s always something wrong with the vents. The filtration unit on the wall has a maintenance tag so old the plastic has worn smooth where fingers have touched it. DEFERRED, it says. The word is barely legible. Someone scratched &quot;AGAIN&quot; underneath, years ago.]</p> <p>[Avyanna doesn&#x27;t say this. Saying things costs.]</p> <p>[The water cuts off. Her three minutes are done. She steps back. The next person steps up.]</p> <hr> <p>[The mess hall is loud and quiet at the same time. Loud with bodies-eight hundred workers cycling through in forty-minute windows. Quiet with voices. No one talks more than they have to.]</p> <p>[Avyanna collects her ration from the dispenser. The pouch is small. The paste inside is the color of old bandages and tastes like apology.]</p> <p>(Forty-five ticks. This is what my body costs. Forty-five ticks of work to keep working.)</p> <p>[She finds a place against the wall. Not a table-tables mean eyes. The wall is better. The wall lets her watch.]</p> <p>[And she watches.]</p> <p>[The workers move in patterns. Shift groups cluster together. Foremen sit closer to the recycler-better air, everyone knows. The very sick sit alone. No one wants to catch something that costs ticks to treat.]</p> <p>[A boy across the room is new. She can tell by how he holds his ration pouch-like it might be more than it is. He&#x27;ll learn. A week, maybe two. They all learn.]</p> <p>[Near the exit, a woman named Sela-not that names matter, but Avyanna remembers anyway-moves like her joints hurt. She&#x27;s going thin. The dust is in her lungs. She&#x27;ll be dead before the quarter ends. Everyone pretends not to notice.]</p> <p>[Two workers near the serving line are trading something. Filter patches, it looks like. Three patches for a painkiller tab. The math of survival, worked out in whispers.]</p> <p>[Avyanna notices everything. It&#x27;s the only thing she&#x27;s good at.]</p> <hr> <p>[Work assignment comes through the tag. A vibration against her neck, then the data feeds directly into her visual cortex-cheap augment, standard issue, designed for inventory.]</p> <p>&gt; **WORKER 477 - SHIFT ASSIGNMENT** &gt; *Location:* Processing Hall, Slurry Line 7 &gt; *Duration:* 8 hours (standard) &gt; *Tool Rental:* 90 ticks (deducted) &gt; *Quota:* 340 units &gt; *Note:* Productivity bonus available (+50 ticks) for 380+ units. Performance within acceptable parameters required for continued assignment.</p> <p>[She walks to Processing. The corridors are narrow. Workers call them by names the company doesn&#x27;t use: the Throat is the main elevator spine. Spit Lane runs behind the commissary, where the condensation drips. The Warm Wall is a pipe junction where heat leaks-workers press their hands there between shifts, stealing warmth the company hasn&#x27;t priced yet.]</p> <p>[The Throat is packed with Cinder shift workers descending. She takes the stairs instead. Cinder Steps, four levels down. Her knees ache by the third level. Her lungs feel heavy by the fourth. The dust is in her too. She doesn&#x27;t think about it.]</p> <p>[The Processing Hall opens up around her. The sound hits first-constant, industrial, the grinding of ore into slurry, the whine of conveyor belts, the hum of machines that never stop. Then the heat. Then the smell: metal, chemical, sweat.]</p> <p>[She finds her station. Slurry Line 7. Her job is simple: watch for blockages, clear them, report anomalies. Don&#x27;t get pulled into the machinery. Don&#x27;t breathe too deep. Don&#x27;t think about what the gold-colored dust is doing inside.]</p> <p>[Above the line, a sign reads: SAFETY IS PRODUCTIVITY. DO NOT WHISTLE NEAR MACHINERY. The second line is hand-painted, older than the first. A superstition turned policy, or a policy turned superstition. No one remembers which.]</p> <hr> <p>[Two hours in. The glitter is everywhere.]</p> <p>[Aurum dust sticks to sweat. Seeps into creases. Avyanna&#x27;s hands are gold-flecked. Her arms. The workers around her shimmer like they&#x27;re decorated for a celebration no one invited them to.]</p> <p>[The woman at the next station-Vera, or that&#x27;s what she was before-coughs into her sleeve. Wet. Heavy. The sound of lungs that are giving up.]</p> <p>(Gold-lung. Everyone gets it. The filters don&#x27;t work. Haven&#x27;t worked in years.)</p> <p>[Vera catches Avyanna looking. Holds her gaze for a moment. Then looks away. There&#x27;s nothing to say. They both know the numbers.]</p> <p>[A man further down the line starts singing. An old song, something from before, when people had the energy for music. His voice is rough. The words are about a river that leads somewhere better.]</p> <p>[No one tells him to stop. For a moment, the sound of the grinders fades. For a moment, there&#x27;s something that isn&#x27;t work.]</p> <p>[Then the song ends. The grinders are still there. They&#x27;re always still there.]</p> <hr> <p>[A commotion near Station 12. Avyanna looks up.]</p> <p>[A worker has stopped. Just stopped-standing at his post, hands at his sides, staring at the slurry line like he&#x27;s seeing something that isn&#x27;t there. His lips are moving. No sound.]</p> <p>[His partner is trying to move him. Gently at first. Then harder. The line doesn&#x27;t stop. The quota doesn&#x27;t stop. The machinery keeps grinding, and if he doesn&#x27;t move-]</p> <p>Partner: [hissing] Kev. Kev. You have to move. The line-</p> <p>[Kev doesn&#x27;t respond. His eyes are somewhere else.]</p> <p>[Supervisor Coil appears. She doesn&#x27;t speak. She grabs Kev&#x27;s arm and drags him backward, away from the machinery, and his partner takes over his station without a word. The line doesn&#x27;t stop. The quota adjusts-two people doing the work of three, until someone from overflow can be reassigned.]</p> <p>[Coil walks Kev toward the med bay corridor. His feet move, but the rest of him is gone. Avyanna watches them go. She&#x27;s seen this before. They call it &quot;checking out.&quot; When the numbers get too loud. When the body decides to leave before the debt does.]</p> <p>[Kev won&#x27;t come back to the line. Med bay will give him a rest recommendation-unpaid, interest accruing-and he&#x27;ll be reassigned to something slower. Lower Works, maybe. Where the air is worse but the pace is gentler.]</p> <p>[The Lower Works. She used to go there, before they banned her section from the deep shafts. Before-]</p> <p>[She stops the thought. Thinking about before costs.]</p> <hr> <p>[Lunch break. Fifteen minutes. The ration pouch is the same. The taste is the same. The wall is the same.]</p> <p>[But something is different today.]</p> <p>[Workers are gathered near the commissary. Voices louder than usual. Avyanna moves closer, staying at the edge, staying invisible.]</p> <p>Worker 1: Fifty. Can you believe it?</p> <p>Worker 2: Bram made fifty. The bastard actually made fifty.</p> <p>[Avyanna knows Bram. Old-the oldest worker she&#x27;s seen here. He works the Lower Works, the deep shafts where the good ore is and the bad air is worse. He knows things. Stories about the mine before it was the Kennel. Superstitions that might be true. He&#x27;s the one who told her about the wall-tapping-two taps before you enter a new cut, asking the rock for permission.]</p> <p>[The crowd parts slightly. Bram is in the center. His face is lined like cracked stone. His hands are gnarled. His eyes are still sharp.]</p> <p>Worker 3: [slapping Bram&#x27;s shoulder] Fifty-run, old man. You cheated.</p> <p>Bram: [voice like gravel shifting] Didn&#x27;t cheat. Just stubborn.</p> <p>[There&#x27;s laughter. It sounds wrong here. Laughter sounds wrong in the Processing Hall, like flowers at a murder. But they laugh anyway.]</p> <p>Worker 4: Speech! The old man has to give a speech.</p> <p>Bram: [waving them off] No speeches. Just- [he pauses, looks at the crowd, at the young faces and the ones already going thin] -just don&#x27;t burn too bright. That&#x27;s all I got. Kennel&#x27;s got enough light. Don&#x27;t give it more.</p> <p>[The laughter fades. Everyone knows what &quot;burning bright&quot; means. Dying young. Working until the body gives out. Being efficient with your own destruction.]</p> <p>Worker 2: [quieter now] Fifty&#x27;s a good run, Bram. Most of us don&#x27;t see forty-five.</p> <p>Bram: [flat] I know.</p> <p>[He looks at Avyanna then. Just a glance, brief and sharp. Like he&#x27;s seeing something. Like he knows something she doesn&#x27;t.]</p> <p>Bram: [quieter, almost to himself] Kennel&#x27;s got a new itch. Feel it in the deep cuts. Something scratching.</p> <p>[No one else seems to hear him. The moment breaks. Someone makes a joke, and the tension eases, and the workers drift back to their stations. Fifteen minutes is already over.]</p> <p>[Avyanna stays against the wall. Watching. Counting.]</p> <p>(Bram is fifty. The oldest person I&#x27;ve seen here.)</p> <p>(What does that make me? I&#x27;m sixteen. Twenty-nine years to match him. If I live that long. If the gold-lung doesn&#x27;t take me. If the balance doesn&#x27;t compound.)</p> <p>(If.)</p> <hr> <p>[Afternoon. The heat is worse. The grinders are louder. Someone down the line collapses.]</p> <p>[The medics come. Not quickly-nothing is quick here unless profit demands it-but they come. The worker is carried out. The line keeps moving. The quota doesn&#x27;t change.]</p> <p>[The worker next to Avyanna-a man with a cough he&#x27;s had for months-stares after the medics.]</p> <p>Worker: [voice low] Third one this week.</p> <p>[Avyanna doesn&#x27;t respond. There&#x27;s nothing to say.]</p> <p>Worker: They&#x27;re not going to fix the vents. They&#x27;re never going to fix anything.</p> <p>[She keeps her eyes on the slurry line. Her hands keep moving. The quota doesn&#x27;t care about conversation.]</p> <p>Worker: What&#x27;s the point? What&#x27;s the point of any of this?</p> <p>[He&#x27;s looking at her. Waiting for something. Hope, maybe. Solidarity. Something human.]</p> <p>[Avyanna looks at the slurry line. The gold-colored dust. The machines that never stop.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [flat] Next shift.</p> <p>Worker: [bitter] That&#x27;s not a point. That&#x27;s a sentence.</p> <p>(Yes. That&#x27;s exactly what it is.)</p> <hr> <p>[A blockage at Station 12. Avyanna moves to clear it.]</p> <p>[The process is simple: isolate the section, reach into the mechanism, remove the obstruction. Don&#x27;t get pulled in. Don&#x27;t lose fingers. Don&#x27;t think about the people who&#x27;ve lost more.]</p> <p>[She works fast. Efficient. The obstruction is a chunk of ore that didn&#x27;t grind properly. She pulls it free. The line resumes.]</p> <p>[Something catches her eye. A mark on the ore chunk. A symbol-angular, strange. Not mining equipment. Not corporate branding. Something older. Something that makes her think of the deep cuts, the places they don&#x27;t let workers go anymore.]</p> <p>[She should report it. Anomalies get reported. Anomalies get investigated. Anomalies get people noticed.]</p> <p>[She puts the ore chunk back on the line. It disappears into the grinder. The symbol is gone.]</p> <p>(Didn&#x27;t see anything. Nothing to report.)</p> <p>[Supervisor Coil walks past. Stops. Looks at Avyanna&#x27;s station, then at the quota display.]</p> <p>Coil: [flat] Four-seven-seven. You&#x27;re at 102%.</p> <p>Avyanna: [not meeting her eyes] Yes.</p> <p>Coil: Keep it there. Don&#x27;t get ideas.</p> <p>Avyanna: [automatic] No ideas.</p> <p>[Coil moves on. Her footsteps fade into the grinding.]</p> <p>(Don&#x27;t stand out. Don&#x27;t be noticed. Don&#x27;t be anything except a number that meets quota.)</p> <p>(I&#x27;m good at that.)</p> <hr> <p>[End of shift. The siren screams again. The machines don&#x27;t stop-second shift takes over-but Avyanna&#x27;s body is released.]</p> <p>[Her muscles ache. Her lungs feel heavy. Her hands are gold-stained, and no amount of washing will get it all out.]</p> <p>[She walks to the hab stack. Slow. Conservation. Her tag vibrates against her neck.]</p> <p>&gt; **WORKER 477 - SHIFT SUMMARY** &gt; *Quota:* 347/340 (102%) &gt; *Base Earnings:* 400 ticks &gt; *Tool Rental:* -90 ticks &gt; *Air Premium:* -60 ticks &gt; *Water Ration:* -30 ticks &gt; *Food Ration:* -45 ticks &gt; *Bunk Rental:* -120 ticks &gt; *Net:* +55 ticks &gt; *Balance Adjustment:* -72 ticks (interest accrual, current rate: 0.015% daily, compounding) &gt; *New Balance:* 487,341 ticks (debt)</p> <p>(Fifty-five earned. Seventy-two interest. Net: negative seventeen.)</p> <p>(I worked eight hours. Met quota. Didn&#x27;t break anything. Didn&#x27;t get sick. Didn&#x27;t die.)</p> <p>(Still further behind than when I woke up.)</p> <p>[She tries the math anyway. She always tries the math. It&#x27;s a habit she can&#x27;t break, like scratching at a scab.]</p> <p>(If the interest stayed at seventy-two per day-but it doesn&#x27;t. The rate compounds. The rate changes. The rate goes up when you get close to zero, she&#x27;s heard. The rate adjusts to keep you where you are.)</p> <p>(Bram. Fifty years. How? How does anyone-)</p> <p>(Maybe he doesn&#x27;t pay for air. Maybe he doesn&#x27;t eat. Maybe he made a deal with something, traded something, found a way the company doesn&#x27;t know about.)</p> <p>(Or maybe he&#x27;s just a glitch. A number that didn&#x27;t round correctly. A mistake the system hasn&#x27;t noticed yet.)</p> <p>(Don&#x27;t think about Bram. Don&#x27;t think about escape velocity. There is no escape velocity. The math doesn&#x27;t allow it.)</p> <hr> <p>[The hab stack is quieter in the evening. Second shift is working. Third shift is sleeping. The ones off-duty are conserving-breath, motion, hope. Everything has a cost.]</p> <p>[Avyanna sits on her bunk. The space is 2 meters by 0.8 meters by 0.8 meters. Coffin-sized. Her whole life fits in this box. A blanket. A water ration she&#x27;s saving. A smooth stone she found in the Lower Works two years ago, before they banned her section from going that deep.]</p> <p>[The stone is in her palm. Gray. Unremarkable. But-]</p> <p>[She stops. Looks at it. Feels it.]</p> <p>[It&#x27;s warm. Not hot-just warm. Warmer than it should be. Warmer than her hand, warmer than the bunk, warmer than anything in this cold metal box has any right to be.]</p> <p>(That&#x27;s wrong. Stones don&#x27;t warm themselves. Physics doesn&#x27;t work that way.)</p> <p>[She holds it tighter. The warmth spreads into her palm, into her fingers. Not unpleasant. Just... present. Like something inside is waiting.]</p> <p>(Put it away. Don&#x27;t think about it. Anomalies get reported. Anomalies get investigated. Anomalies get you noticed.)</p> <p>[She puts the stone back in her lockbox. Closes the lid. Tries not to think about how it felt. Tries not to notice that her hand still feels warm, even after she lets go.]</p> <hr> <p>[A voice from the bunk below.]</p> <p>Young Worker: Four-seven-seven?</p> <p>[Avyanna doesn&#x27;t answer at first. Numbers are all they have.]</p> <p>Young Worker: [persistent] I know you&#x27;re awake. I saw you at shift change.</p> <p>Avyanna: [flat] What.</p> <p>[The young worker climbs up to peer into Avyanna&#x27;s space. Young-maybe fourteen, eyes too big for her face. She&#x27;s wearing a charm on a string around her wrist, hidden under her sleeve. Contraband. Personal items get confiscated if they&#x27;re not in the lockbox. She&#x27;s risking ticks just by wearing it.]</p> <p>Young Worker: How do you do it?</p> <p>Avyanna: [not moving] Do what.</p> <p>Young Worker: Stay so... still. You never cry. You never yell. The rest of us, we- [she stops, swallows] -I can&#x27;t stop shaking sometimes. When the numbers come up and I&#x27;m further behind. When someone checks out or burns bright and no one says their name.</p> <p>[Avyanna looks at her. The charm on her wrist catches the dim light. A small metal shape. A star, maybe. Or a flower. Something from before.]</p> <p>Avyanna: What&#x27;s your name.</p> <p>Young Worker: [surprised] Mira. I&#x27;m- [she catches herself] -I&#x27;m four-eight-nine.</p> <p>Avyanna: What&#x27;s the charm.</p> <p>[Mira&#x27;s hand goes to her wrist. Protective. Afraid.]</p> <p>Mira: My sister made it. Before she... [she doesn&#x27;t finish] She was on Slurry 3. Last quarter.</p> <p>[Avyanna knows what &quot;last quarter&quot; means. Another number that stopped appearing in the count. Another bunk reassigned by end of shift.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] Keep it hidden.</p> <p>Mira: I know. I just- [she swallows again] -how do you stay so calm? How do you just... not feel it?</p> <p>(Do I? Do I feel anything anymore? When did I stop?)</p> <p>Avyanna: [turning away, looking at the ceiling of her pod] I don&#x27;t know. I stopped. At some point. It hurt less.</p> <p>Mira: Is that better?</p> <p>[Avyanna doesn&#x27;t answer. She&#x27;s not sure there is an answer. Not one that helps.]</p> <p>Mira: [after a long pause] My name is Mira.</p> <p>[She says it like it matters. Like names still mean something. Like being a person is still possible here.]</p> <p>[Avyanna closes her eyes.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] Go to sleep, Mira.</p> <p>[Mira climbs back down. The bunk creaks. Silence returns.]</p> <p>(Mira. Four-eight-nine. Sister on Slurry 3. Charm on her wrist.)</p> <p>(Don&#x27;t remember. Names cost. Caring costs.)</p> <p>(But she remembers anyway.)</p> <hr> <p>[Night cycle. The lights dim-not off, never off, they need to see you to count you-but dimmer. The hab stack settles into the sounds of sleep. Coughing. Breathing. The occasional muffled sob.]</p> <p>[Avyanna can&#x27;t sleep. The numbers run in her head. The math. Always the math.]</p> <p>(487,341 ticks. Interest compounds. Rate adjusts. The system is designed. The system is working.)</p> <p>(There&#x27;s no way out by working. The math doesn&#x27;t allow it. Bram is fifty, and he&#x27;s still here, and if he hasn&#x27;t escaped in thirty years-)</p> <p>(Thirty years. He&#x27;s been here thirty years. Longer than I&#x27;ve been alive.)</p> <p>(What was he, before? Did he have a name? Does he remember it?)</p> <p>(Will I be here in thirty years? Will I remember mine?)</p> <p>(What is my name?)</p> <p>[She stops. The question hits her like cold water.]</p> <p>(Avyanna. My name is Avyanna.)</p> <p>(When was the last time someone said it? When was the last time I thought it?)</p> <p>(Four-seven-seven. That&#x27;s what I am now. That&#x27;s what the system calls me. That&#x27;s what I answer to.)</p> <p>(But my name is Avyanna.)</p> <p>[She holds onto it. Just for a moment. A small rebellion that costs nothing. A word that belongs to her, before she was inventory.]</p> <hr> <p>[There&#x27;s a viewport at the end of the hab corridor. Small. Grimy. The plastic is scratched from years of workers pressing their faces against it, looking at something that isn&#x27;t here.]</p> <p>[Avyanna stands in front of it. Third hour of night cycle. She should be sleeping. Sleep is free. Sleep is the only thing that&#x27;s free.]</p> <p>[But sometimes she comes here. When the numbers get too loud. When the walls feel too close. When she needs to remember that there&#x27;s something else. Somewhere else.]</p> <p>[The stars are visible. Faint, through the grime, but there. Points of light in the black. Other places. Other lives.]</p> <p>[She traces a constellation with her finger. She doesn&#x27;t know its name. No one taught her. No one teaches anyone anything here unless it makes them more efficient.]</p> <p>(Out there. People live out there. Real people with real names. People who don&#x27;t count ticks. Who don&#x27;t get stamped for fitness. Who don&#x27;t watch their friends go thin and say nothing.)</p> <p>(Do they know about us? Do they care?)</p> <p>(Does anyone care?)</p> <p>[Her hand drifts to her neck. The tag is warm. Always warm. Tracking. Always tracking.]</p> <p>[The stars don&#x27;t answer. They never answer. They just burn, far away, indifferent to the girl pressing her palm against grimy plastic.]</p> <p>[Something shifts in her chest. Not pain-or not just pain. Something else. A weight. A pressure. Like something is waiting, just under the surface. Like the warmth of the stone, still lingering in her palm even though she put it away hours ago.]</p> <p>(What is that? What&#x27;s happening to me?)</p> <p>(Nothing. Nothing&#x27;s happening. Go to sleep. Sleep is free.)</p> <p>[She stays there until her legs ache. Until the cold of the floor seeps through her thin shoes. Until she&#x27;s too tired to stand.]</p> <p>[Then she walks back to her bunk. Lies down. Closes her eyes.]</p> <p>[The stone in her lockbox is warm. She can feel it, somehow, even through the metal. Waiting.]</p> <p>[Tomorrow is another shift. Another count. Another day of surviving until the next one.]</p> <p>[That&#x27;s all there is.]</p> <hr> <p>&gt; **AURUM EXTRACTION LTD. - MORTALITY LOG (FORM 9-ECHO)** &gt; *Site:* K-9 &gt; *Date:* [REDACTED] &gt; *Deceased:* Worker 431 (cause: respiratory failure, gold-lung complications), Worker 508 (cause: equipment proximity incident, Slurry 7) &gt; *Debt Status:* Transferred to next of kin (if applicable) per Contract Section 7.3(b) &gt; *Bunk Reassignment:* Complete &gt; *Productivity Impact:* Minimal. Shift coverage maintained within acceptable parameters. &gt; *Note:* Both workers performed within expectations until incident date. No anomalies reported.</p> <hr> <p>*End Chapter 1*</p> </div> Where does the route go next? * [[Continue to Chapter 02: The Debt Clock->Act1 02 The Debt Clock]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Chapter 02: The Debt Clock</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 02_the_debt_clock.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **AURUM EXTRACTION LTD. - WELLNESS ADVISORY** &gt; *Site:* K-9 (&quot;The Kennel&quot;) &gt; *Subject:* Quarterly Health Metrics Review &gt; *Status:* DEFERRED (resource allocation pending) &gt; *Note:* Workers experiencing respiratory distress should report to Medical Bay during off-shift hours. Wait times currently average 4.2 hours. Productivity exemptions not available. Rest recommendations unpaid. &gt; *Reminder:* Your health is your responsibility. Wellness is productivity.</p> <hr> <p>[The medical bay smells like disinfectant and failure.]</p> <p>[Avyanna stands near the entrance, not quite inside. She&#x27;s escorting-if you can call it that. The woman leaning against her shoulder is named Tella, or was. Worker 394. She collapsed on Slurry 5, and the line foreman sent Avyanna to walk her here because Avyanna was closest and the foreman didn&#x27;t want to file a transport request.]</p> <p>[The medical bay is small. Six cots. Four of them occupied. The lighting is the same sick yellow as everywhere else, but here it feels more honest-like the light knows what it&#x27;s illuminating.]</p> <p>Tella: [voice thin] You can let go. I can stand.</p> <p>[She can&#x27;t. Avyanna can feel the tremor running through her, the way her weight keeps shifting like her legs are negotiating with gravity.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [flat] Sit first.</p> <p>[There&#x27;s no free cot. Tella lowers herself against the wall instead, sliding down until she&#x27;s on the floor. Her breathing is wet. The gold-lung sound. Everyone knows it.]</p> <p>[A medical attendant approaches. Not a doctor-the Kennel doesn&#x27;t have doctors. This one is maybe twenty-five, with eyes that have already learned not to see too much.]</p> <p>Attendant: [checking a tablet] Worker 394. Respiratory distress. Third visit this quarter.</p> <p>Tella: [trying to sit straighter] I can work. I just need-</p> <p>Attendant: [not unkindly, but not kind either] You need rest. I&#x27;m recommending forty-eight hours off-shift. Unpaid. Interest will accrue at standard rates.</p> <p>[Tella&#x27;s face does something complicated. Relief and terror, fighting for space.]</p> <p>Tella: I can&#x27;t afford-</p> <p>Attendant: [already moving to the next patient] The recommendation is logged. If you return to work before it expires, the recommendation is voided and medical liability transfers to you. Sign the waiver or don&#x27;t. Your choice.</p> <p>[Avyanna watches Tella&#x27;s hands shake as she signs. Her choice. As if any of it is a choice.]</p> <hr> <p>[The next cot over. A woman Avyanna doesn&#x27;t know by number. She&#x27;s lying still, staring at the ceiling. Her skin has the gray-yellow tint of someone the dust has already claimed.]</p> <p>[She looks thirty. She&#x27;s probably younger.]</p> <p>[A different attendant is checking her vitals. The tablet beeps. The attendant&#x27;s expression doesn&#x27;t change.]</p> <p>Attendant 2: [to the woman] You&#x27;ll need to stay overnight. Observation.</p> <p>Woman: [voice barely there] For what?</p> <p>Attendant 2: [beat] Observation.</p> <p>[The woman laughs. It turns into a cough. The cough goes on too long.]</p> <p>[Avyanna looks away. At the corner of the medical bay, there&#x27;s a machine-old, covered in dust, clearly non-functional. A label on the side reads: PULMONARY REGENERATION UNIT. OFFLINE. PARTS PENDING (Est. 18 mo.)]</p> <p>[Eighteen months. The label is yellowed. Cracked at the edges. It&#x27;s been there longer than Avyanna has been in the mine.]</p> <p>(They have the equipment. They just don&#x27;t have the budget. Or the budget exists, but it&#x27;s allocated elsewhere. Or it&#x27;s allocated here, but someone took it. The reasons don&#x27;t matter. The result is the same.)</p> <p>[She looks at the woman on the cot. At Tella against the wall. At the other three patients-all with the same wet breathing, the same gray skin, the same eyes that have stopped expecting help.]</p> <p>(This isn&#x27;t neglect. Neglect is accidental. This is a decision. Someone decided we&#x27;re not worth the parts.)</p> <hr> <p>[The medical kiosk speaks.]</p> <p>[It&#x27;s mounted on the wall-a simple interface, a screen with a face that isn&#x27;t quite a face. Not an AI citizen, not a person-just a diagnostic terminal with a voice synthesizer and a script. The features are simplified, generic. Designed to be reassuring without being helpful.]</p> <p>Medical AI: Worker 477. You are not currently scheduled for a medical appointment. If you are experiencing symptoms, please register at the intake desk. Current wait time: 3.8 hours.</p> <p>[Avyanna doesn&#x27;t answer. She&#x27;s not here for herself.]</p> <p>Medical AI: Worker 477. Your latest health metrics indicate respiratory function at 71% baseline. This is within acceptable parameters. If function drops below 60%, please report for evaluation.</p> <p>(Seventy-one percent. When did it drop below eighty? When did I stop noticing?)</p> <p>Medical AI: Wellness recommendation: Practice mindfulness breathing exercises to optimize remaining lung capacity. Guided sessions available at 15 ticks per module. Would you like to schedule a preventive consultation? Note: Preventive consultations are charged at 40 ticks per fifteen-minute interval. Payment in advance required.</p> <p>(Mindfulness breathing. For lungs that are 71% dead. They want to sell me lessons in how to breathe.)</p> <p>Avyanna: [flat] Not really.</p> <p>Medical AI: Understood. Your wellness is your responsibility. Have a productive shift.</p> <p>[The kiosk&#x27;s face flickers. A glitch in the display. Nothing more.]</p> <p>[Then it&#x27;s blank again. Waiting. Ready to dispense the next useless recommendation.]</p> <p>(The kiosk has all the data. It knows the numbers. It just runs the script anyway.)</p> <p>(Because it&#x27;s not a person. It&#x27;s a menu with a face.)</p> <hr> <p>[On her way out, Avyanna passes the intake desk. There&#x27;s a line. Seven workers waiting. Eight. Nine.]</p> <p>[A woman near the front is arguing with the desk attendant. Her voice is sharp, desperate.]</p> <p>Worker: My husband needs treatment. He can&#x27;t breathe. He&#x27;s been waiting since yesterday-</p> <p>Desk Attendant: [patient in the way a knife is patient] His priority score is calculated based on productivity impact and recovery probability. Currently, there are three workers ahead of him with higher scores.</p> <p>Worker: He&#x27;s dying.</p> <p>Desk Attendant: [beat] His current status is &quot;critical-stable.&quot; If his status changes to &quot;critical-declining,&quot; his priority score will be recalculated. I recommend checking back in four hours.</p> <p>[The worker&#x27;s face crumbles. She doesn&#x27;t argue anymore. There&#x27;s nothing to argue with.]</p> <p>[Avyanna keeps walking. Her footsteps echo in the corridor. Behind her, the medical bay hums with quiet, efficient neglect.]</p> <hr> <p>[The debt terminal is in the commissary. A row of screens against the wall, where workers can check their &quot;balance&quot; during meal breaks. Most people avoid them. Looking at the numbers doesn&#x27;t change them. It just makes them harder to ignore.]</p> <p>[Avyanna stands in front of one. The screen is grimy, cracked in one corner. Someone has scratched &quot;TRAP&quot; into the metal frame, then scratched it out, then scratched it again.]</p> <p>[She presses her palm to the reader. The tag in her neck syncs. The numbers appear.]</p> <p>&gt; **WORKER 477 - DEBT STATUS** &gt; *Previous Balance:* 487,341 ticks &gt; *Overnight Adjustment:* +17 ticks (dormitory audit, standard) &gt; *Current Balance:* 487,358 ticks (debt) &gt; *Daily Accrual Rate:* 0.015% (compounding) &gt; *Yesterday&#x27;s Interest:* 72 ticks &gt; *Yesterday&#x27;s Net Earnings:* -17 ticks &gt; *Projected Clearance Date:* N/A (insufficient progress trajectory) &gt; *Note:* Balance has increased 2.4% over the past 30 days. Consider productivity enhancement opportunities.</p> <p>[N/A. Not applicable. The system has calculated that she will never pay it off. It&#x27;s not hiding it. It&#x27;s just noting it, like any other data point.]</p> <p>(Projected clearance date: never. They put it right there. They tell us.)</p> <p>[She stares at the numbers. Runs them in her head. The habit she can&#x27;t break.]</p> <p>(487,358 ticks. Plus seventeen overnight for &quot;dormitory audit.&quot; They charged me for sleeping. Interest at 0.015% daily means... 73 ticks today. Tomorrow, 73.1. The day after, 73.2. It compounds. It always compounds.)</p> <p>(If I worked a perfect shift every day-never sick, never slow, never injured-I&#x27;d earn maybe 55 ticks net. But the interest is 73. I fall behind by 18 ticks per day. Per day. That&#x27;s 540 per month. 6,480 per year.)</p> <p>(In ten years, I&#x27;ll owe 65,000 more than I do now. In twenty years, 130,000. And that&#x27;s if the rate doesn&#x27;t change. If they don&#x27;t add fees. If I don&#x27;t get sick.)</p> <p>(The math doesn&#x27;t allow escape. The math is the point.)</p> <p>[A worker next to her is checking his own balance. His face is blank. He&#x27;s seen the numbers before. They all have.]</p> <p>Worker: [not looking at her] How long for you?</p> <p>Avyanna: [not looking at him] Doesn&#x27;t matter.</p> <p>Worker: [flat] Eight years. That&#x27;s how long I&#x27;ve been here. Started with twelve thousand. Now I&#x27;m at four hundred thousand.</p> <p>(Eight years. He&#x27;s been here as long as I have. And his debt grew by almost forty times.)</p> <p>Worker: They adjust the rate, you know. When you get close to zero-when you&#x27;re almost out-they find something. An equipment fee. A safety violation. A health assessment charge. [beat] My buddy got within two thousand once. Two thousand. Then they hit him with a &quot;routine audit adjustment.&quot; Sixteen thousand. Just like that.</p> <p>[Avyanna doesn&#x27;t respond. There&#x27;s nothing to say.]</p> <p>Worker: [pushing away from the terminal] Good luck, four-seven-seven. Not that it matters.</p> <p>[He walks away. The screen blinks. Her numbers are still there. Waiting.]</p> <hr> <p>[She thinks about Bram.]</p> <p>[Fifty years. He&#x27;s been here fifty years. He started when the mine was new, when it was still called something other than the Kennel. He&#x27;s watched generations of workers come and go-mostly go.]</p> <p>(How is he still alive? The gold-lung should have taken him decades ago. The debt should have crushed him. The math should have-)</p> <p>[She stops. Reconsiders.]</p> <p>(Maybe the math is different for him. Maybe there&#x27;s something he knows. Something the rest of us don&#x27;t.)</p> <p>(Or maybe he&#x27;s just lucky. Maybe luck is the only thing the system can&#x27;t calculate.)</p> <p>(But luck runs out. Everyone&#x27;s luck runs out eventually.)</p> <p>[She presses her palm to the terminal again. The screen updates.]</p> <p>&gt; *Productivity Enhancement Opportunity Available* &gt; *Voluntary overtime: +4 hours* &gt; *Additional earnings: 200 ticks (before deductions)* &gt; *Air premium (overtime): 30 ticks* &gt; *Equipment rental (overtime): 45 ticks* &gt; *Net overtime earnings: 125 ticks* &gt; *Note: Overtime shifts may impact recovery and future productivity. Health consequences are the responsibility of the worker.*</p> <p>(One hundred twenty-five ticks. Four extra hours. I&#x27;d fall behind by only... negative 52 today instead of negative 73. That&#x27;s still negative. Still sinking. Just slower.)</p> <p>(They&#x27;re offering me the chance to work myself to death slightly less quickly.)</p> <p>[She declines the overtime. The screen chirps. Friendly. Helpful.]</p> <p>&gt; *Productivity Enhancement Opportunity Declined* &gt; *Note: Declining voluntary opportunities does not affect your standing. However, consistent non-participation may be noted in quarterly reviews.*</p> <p>(May be noted. In quarterly reviews. Which affect shift assignments. Which affect earnings. Which affect-)</p> <p>(Everything is connected. Everything is designed.)</p> <p>[She walks away from the terminal. The numbers stay behind. But she can feel them, following her like a shadow she can&#x27;t shake.]</p> <hr> <p>[The hab stack is quiet during second shift. Most of the bunks are empty-workers at their stations, earning ticks, falling behind.]</p> <p>[Avyanna should be resting. Conserving. But she can&#x27;t stop moving. The numbers are too loud in her head.]</p> <p>[She finds herself at Bunk 473. Empty. Cold. The bunk that was occupied yesterday, that is always occupied, that is now-]</p> <p>[She stops. Looks.]</p> <p>[The sheets have been stripped. The mattress is bare. The lockbox is empty, door hanging open. Standard procedure.]</p> <p>(Four-seven-three. The bunk Coil paused at this morning. The one with the cold sheets.)</p> <p>(Someone died. Someone didn&#x27;t wake up. And the system has already processed them out of existence.)</p> <p>[A worker passes behind her. Older. Thin. The kind of thin that comes from giving away food rations to someone who needed them more.]</p> <p>Della: [seeing Avyanna look] You knew him?</p> <p>Avyanna: [flat] Not really.</p> <p>Della: Bram. Fifty. Started coughing last month. [beat] Burned bright, that one.</p> <p>(Burned bright. Like it&#x27;s a compliment. Like making fifty and still dying in your bunk is an achievement.)</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] What does that mean?</p> <p>Della: [surprised] What?</p> <p>Avyanna: [still looking at the empty bunk] Burned bright. Everyone says it. What does it mean?</p> <p>[Della is silent for a moment. Looking at Avyanna like she&#x27;s said something strange. Something dangerous.]</p> <p>Della: [careful] It means he worked hard. Didn&#x27;t complain. Gave everything he had.</p> <p>Avyanna: [flat] Until it killed him.</p> <p>Della: [defensive now] It&#x27;s respect. It&#x27;s-</p> <p>Avyanna: Bram was killed.</p> <p>[The words come out before she can stop them. Three words. Too loud in the quiet hab stack.]</p> <p>[Della&#x27;s face goes pale. They look around-checking for supervisors, for cameras, for anyone who might be listening. Avyanna&#x27;s tag gives a small vibration against her neck. Acknowledgment. Logged.]</p> <p>(Stupid. Stupid. What did I just-)</p> <p>Della: [hissing] You can&#x27;t say things like that. You can&#x27;t-</p> <p>[Avyanna is already walking away. Her hands are cold. Her heart is too fast. The tag is warm against her neck, warmer than usual, and she doesn&#x27;t know if that means anything or nothing at all.]</p> <p>(I didn&#x27;t say anything. I didn&#x27;t say anything. I didn&#x27;t-)</p> <p>[She leaves the hab stack. Behind her, the empty bunk waits for its next occupant. They&#x27;ll arrive by end of shift. They always do.]</p> <hr> <p>[Processing Hall. Slurry Line 7. The grinders are louder today, or maybe she&#x27;s just more aware of them. Every sound feels sharp. Every smell feels deliberate.]</p> <p>[She works. Hands moving. Eyes watching. Mind somewhere else.]</p> <p>(Bram. Fifty. The oldest person I&#x27;ve seen here. And the system still stripped his sheets before his body was cold.)</p> <p>(In five years, I could be the one in the empty bunk. The one they strip the sheets for. The one they call &quot;burned bright&quot; and forget.)</p> <p>(Unless something changes. Unless I find a way out.)</p> <p>(But the math doesn&#x27;t allow-)</p> <p>[She stops the thought. Pushes it down. Thinking costs. Hoping costs more.]</p> <p>[The slurry line pulses. Ore becomes dust. Dust becomes profit. Profit goes somewhere else, to someone else, for reasons that have nothing to do with the people who die making it.]</p> <p>[A supervisor walks past. Not Coil-a different one. He glances at Avyanna&#x27;s quota display, nods, moves on. She&#x27;s invisible again. Good.]</p> <p>(Don&#x27;t be noticed. Don&#x27;t stand out. Don&#x27;t be anything except a number.)</p> <p>(But I noticed Bram. I remember his voice. I know he&#x27;s gone.)</p> <p>(That has to mean something. Doesn&#x27;t it?)</p> <p>[It doesn&#x27;t. It can&#x27;t. Meaning is a luxury she can&#x27;t afford.]</p> <p>[She keeps working. The grinders keep grinding. The quota keeps climbing. The math keeps running.]</p> <hr> <p>[Lunch break. Fifteen minutes. The ration pouch tastes like nothing.]</p> <p>[Avyanna sits against the wall-her wall, the one that lets her watch-and observes the commissary. The patterns of movement. The clusters of workers. The ones who talk and the ones who&#x27;ve stopped trying.]</p> <p>[Near the serving line, a foreman is explaining something to a group of younger workers. New arrivals, probably. Their eyes still have hope in them. It won&#x27;t last.]</p> <p>Foreman: [patient, like he&#x27;s said this a hundred times] The bonus structure is designed to reward productivity. If you exceed quota by fifteen percent, you earn an additional fifty ticks. Twenty percent, seventy-five ticks. The system is fair.</p> <p>[Fair. Avyanna almost laughs. Almost.]</p> <p>(The bonus is fifty ticks. The daily interest on average debt is seventy. Even if you hit twenty percent over quota every single day, you still fall behind.)</p> <p>(But they don&#x27;t tell the new ones that. They let them figure it out. Let them run the math themselves, if they can. Most can&#x27;t. Most just work harder and sink faster, thinking they&#x27;re climbing.)</p> <p>[One of the new workers raises a hand. Young. Maybe fifteen. Eyes still bright.]</p> <p>New Worker: What if you hit quota every day for a year? Could you pay off your induction debt?</p> <p>[The foreman&#x27;s smile doesn&#x27;t waver. But something shifts behind his eyes.]</p> <p>Foreman: The system is designed for long-term workers who demonstrate sustained commitment. Early clearance is... uncommon. But not impossible.</p> <p>(Not impossible. Just mathematically prevented.)</p> <p>[The new worker nods, satisfied. They don&#x27;t see it yet. They will.]</p> <p>[Avyanna finishes her ration. The paste sits heavy in her stomach. Fuel for the machine. Nothing more.]</p> <hr> <p>[Afternoon shift. The heat rises. The air thickens. The grinders never stop.]</p> <p>[A worker two stations down-young, thin, coughing-stumbles. Catches himself on the safety rail. Keeps working.]</p> <p>[Another worker, three stations up-older, steadier, but slowing-miscounts a measure. Catches the error. Corrects it. The quota display flickers.]</p> <p>[The line foreman watches from his elevated platform. His tablet tracks productivity in real-time. Every worker, every minute, every tick.]</p> <p>[Avyanna watches the foreman. He&#x27;s been here longer than most. His skin has the gold tint of deep exposure. His lungs probably sound like Vera&#x27;s-wet, heavy, dying by degrees.]</p> <p>(He&#x27;s not management. He&#x27;s one of us, with a slightly better title and slightly worse debt. They promote from within so we have someone to blame who isn&#x27;t them.)</p> <p>[The foreman catches her looking. Holds her gaze for a moment. Then looks away.]</p> <p>(He knows. He has to know. He sees the numbers. He runs the same math.)</p> <p>(But what can he do? Report it? To whom? The system that designed it?)</p> <p>[She looks back at her station. The ore keeps coming. The dust keeps settling. The clock keeps running.]</p> <hr> <p>[A noise. Different from the grinders.]</p> <p>[Avyanna looks up. Something is happening near the main corridor-a commotion, voices raised, the sound of bodies moving too fast in a space designed for slow compliance.]</p> <p>[Workers are stepping back from their stations. Supervisors are converging. The grinders keep grinding-they always keep grinding-but the humans have stopped.]</p> <p>[Someone is on the floor. A worker. Young. Too young. She&#x27;s convulsing, gold-flecked foam at her mouth, her body arching in a way that isn&#x27;t natural.]</p> <p>[Medical response team arrives. Two attendants with a stretcher. They work fast-not out of urgency, but out of efficiency. The less time the line is disrupted, the better the numbers.]</p> <p>Attendant 1: [clinical] Toxic reaction. Probably filter failure.</p> <p>Attendant 2: [checking vitals] She&#x27;s crashing. We need to-</p> <p>Attendant 1: [glancing at the line foreman] Can we stabilize on-site?</p> <p>[The line foreman looks at his tablet. At the numbers. At the girl on the floor.]</p> <p>Line Foreman: [beat] Get her to medical. I&#x27;ll log the incident as &quot;equipment malfunction.&quot; Productivity impact: minimal.</p> <p>[They carry her away. The convulsing has stopped. Her eyes are open, but there&#x27;s nothing behind them anymore.]</p> <p>[Avyanna watches until they&#x27;re gone. The workers around her are already returning to their stations. The grinders keep grinding. The line keeps moving.]</p> <p>(Equipment malfunction. Not filter failure. Not decades of deferred maintenance. Not a girl who breathed in too much of the dust we all breathe.)</p> <p>(Equipment malfunction. Because that&#x27;s a category that doesn&#x27;t require investigation. Doesn&#x27;t require change. Doesn&#x27;t require someone to admit that the equipment is us.)</p> <p>[Her hands move. The quota climbs. The shift continues.]</p> <p>(Don&#x27;t think about it. Thinking costs.)</p> <p>(But she thinks about it anyway.)</p> <hr> <p>[End of shift. The siren screams. Avyanna&#x27;s body releases-not relaxes, never relaxes, just... releases. The difference between working and waiting.]</p> <p>[She walks to the hab stack. Her muscles ache. Her lungs feel heavy. The gold dust is in her creases, her pores, her eyelashes. It never comes out completely.]</p> <p>[The wash line is long. The water is lukewarm. She scrubs mechanically, watching the gray-gold water swirl down the drain. Her body, dissolving by degrees.]</p> <p>[Her tag vibrates. The daily summary.]</p> <p>&gt; **WORKER 477 - SHIFT SUMMARY** &gt; *Quota:* 351/340 (103%) &gt; *Base Earnings:* 420 ticks &gt; *Tool Rental:* -90 ticks &gt; *Air Premium:* -60 ticks &gt; *Water Ration:* -30 ticks &gt; *Food Ration:* -45 ticks &gt; *Bunk Rental:* -120 ticks &gt; *Net:* +75 ticks &gt; *Balance Adjustment:* -73 ticks (interest accrual) &gt; *New Balance:* 487,360 ticks (debt) &gt; *Daily Net Change:* +2 ticks</p> <p>(Plus two. I made quota plus thirteen percent. Worked harder than yesterday. And I moved the needle by two ticks.)</p> <p>(At this rate-two ticks per day-I&#x27;ll be free in... 667 years.)</p> <p>[She laughs. It comes out wrong-harsh, broken, the sound of something tearing. The woman next to her glances over, then away. No one asks. Everyone understands.]</p> <hr> <p>[Night cycle. The viewport.]</p> <p>[Avyanna stands where she stood before, palms pressed against grimy plastic, eyes fixed on stars she&#x27;ll never reach.]</p> <p>[The mine hums around her. Second shift is working. Third shift is sleeping. The machines never stop. The debt never stops. The dying never stops.]</p> <p>(Do they know? Out there, in the black, do they know about places like this? About people like us?)</p> <p>(They must. Someone is buying what we dig. Someone is profiting from what we die for. They know.)</p> <p>(They just don&#x27;t care.)</p> <p>[She traces a constellation with her finger. Three stars in a rough line. She doesn&#x27;t know its name. She doesn&#x27;t know any of their names. No one taught her. No one teaches anything here that doesn&#x27;t make you more productive.]</p> <p>(Out there. Ships move out there. People with choices. People who can leave when they want, go where they want, be who they want.)</p> <p>(What does that feel like? To not have a number? To not have a balance that only goes up?)</p> <p>[Her hand drops. The stars keep burning, indifferent and eternal.]</p> <p>(Bram burned bright. Fifty years, and the system called it acceptable. Called it normal. Called it the cost of doing business.)</p> <p>(In seven years, will they say that about me? &quot;Four-seven-seven burned bright. Shame. Good worker.&quot;)</p> <p>(Is that all I am? All I&#x27;ll ever be? A number that worked until it didn&#x27;t?)</p> <p>[Something shifts in her chest. The same weight from before. The same pressure. Like something is waiting, just under the surface.]</p> <p>[Her hand goes to the lockbox key around her neck. The stone is in there. The warm stone. She hasn&#x27;t touched it since that first night, but she can feel it anyway. Waiting.]</p> <p>(What are you? What did you do to me?)</p> <p>(Are you why I can&#x27;t stop thinking? Why I notice things? Why the numbers won&#x27;t stay quiet in my head?)</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t know. She doesn&#x27;t have answers.
