She only has questions, and questions cost ticks, and everything costs ticks, and she&#x27;s so tired of counting.]</p> <hr> <p>[The corridor is empty. Night cycle deep. The lights are dimmer, the air is colder, and the usual sounds of human habitation have faded to the soft rhythm of sleeping bodies.]</p> <p>[Avyanna walks. Not toward the hab stack-past it. Toward the observation deck, the one place on the lower levels with a window big enough to see the planet below.]</p> <p>[The observation deck is a joke. A leftover from when the mine was built, when someone thought workers might want to look at something other than rock and machinery. The viewport is huge-takes up most of one wall-but it&#x27;s covered in grime and scratches. The seating has been removed to make space for storage crates.]</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t care about the seating. She cares about the view.]</p> <p>[The planet hangs below. Brown and gray and orange, wrapped in clouds of industrial haze. Not a living world-a resource world. Extracted, processed, shipped elsewhere. Like her.]</p> <p>(Is that what I am? A resource? Something to be extracted until there&#x27;s nothing left?)</p> <p>[The thought should hurt more than it does. Maybe she&#x27;s too tired for pain. Maybe she&#x27;s used it up already.]</p> <p>[She sits on a crate. Pulls her knees to her chest. Watches the planet turn, slowly, below the viewport.]</p> <p>(Bram is dead. The girl from the line is probably dead. Tella is dying. Everyone is dying.)</p> <p>[She thinks about the math again. She can&#x27;t help it-it runs in her head like a loop, like the grinders, like the debt clock that never stops.]</p> <p>(487,360 ticks. Interest compounding. Rate adjusting. Every day, I fall a little further behind. Every day, the number gets bigger, the hope gets smaller, the exit gets further away.)</p> <p>(There is no exit. The math doesn&#x27;t allow it.)</p> <p>(But what if the math is wrong?)</p> <p>[She stops. The thought is strange. Dangerous. Useless.]</p> <p>(The math isn&#x27;t wrong. The math is the one thing that&#x27;s honest here. The system shows you exactly how trapped you are.)</p> <p>(Unless there&#x27;s something the math doesn&#x27;t account for. Something outside the system.)</p> <p>[Her hand goes to the lockbox key again. The stone. The warmth that shouldn&#x27;t be there.]</p> <p>(Anomalies get reported. Anomalies get investigated. Anomalies get you noticed.)</p> <p>(But the stone is an anomaly. And I haven&#x27;t reported it. And nothing has happened. Yet.)</p> <p>[She looks at the planet below. At the stars beyond. At the vast, indifferent universe that doesn&#x27;t care whether she lives or dies or burns bright or fades to nothing.]</p> <p>(Something is different. Something has changed.)</p> <p>(I don&#x27;t know what. I don&#x27;t know why. But I can feel it. Like pressure behind my eyes. Like words I can&#x27;t quite hear.)</p> <p>(Maybe I&#x27;m going mad. Maybe the dust has gotten to my brain, like it&#x27;s gotten to my lungs.)</p> <p>(Or maybe-)</p> <p>[She stops the thought. Stops all the thoughts. Sits in silence, watching the planet turn, waiting for something she can&#x27;t name.]</p> <hr> <p>[When she finally sleeps, she dreams of numbers.]</p> <p>[They spiral around her-ticks and balances, interest rates and quotas, an endless calculation that never resolves. She&#x27;s drowning in them, sinking, the weight of mathematics pulling her down into something cold and dark and absolute.]</p> <p>[And somewhere, far away, something watches.]</p> <p>[Not the numbers. Not the system. Something older. Something patient. Something that doesn&#x27;t calculate, doesn&#x27;t measure, doesn&#x27;t count.]</p> <p>[It just waits.]</p> <p>[And in the dream, Avyanna reaches toward it-toward the only thing in the dark that isn&#x27;t made of math.]</p> <hr> <p>[She wakes to the siren. Another day. Another count. Another shift.]</p> <p>[The numbers are waiting.]</p> <p>[They&#x27;re always waiting.]</p> <hr> <p>&gt; **AURUM EXTRACTION LTD. - INCIDENT LOG (SUMMARY)** &gt; *Site:* K-9 &gt; *Date:* [REDACTED] &gt; *Incident Type:* Equipment Malfunction (Filtration) &gt; *Personnel Affected:* 1 (Worker 612 - status: TRANSFERRED TO LONG-TERM CARE) &gt; *Root Cause:* Pending investigation &gt; *Corrective Action:* Pending budget allocation &gt; *Productivity Impact:* 0.3% (within acceptable parameters) &gt; *Note:* Incident does not meet threshold for external reporting. Internal review scheduled for Q4. Maintenance deferral policy remains in effect per Directive 7.8(c).</p> <hr> <p>*End Chapter 2*</p> </div> Where does the route go next? * [[Continue to Chapter 03: The Thing In The Rock->Act1 03 The Thing In The Rock]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Chapter 03: The Thing In The Rock</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 03_the_thing_in_the_rock.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **AURUM EXTRACTION LTD. - RESTRICTED SECTION MAINTENANCE SCHEDULE** &gt; *Site:* K-9 (&quot;The Kennel&quot;) &gt; *Section:* Lower Works, Subsection 7-Gamma (DECOMMISSIONED) &gt; *Status:* Quarterly cleaning cycle (minimal crew) &gt; *Authorization Level:* Standard labor (supervised) &gt; *Note:* Section sealed following geological instability event (see Incident Report 7-G-441, CLASSIFIED). Access for maintenance only. No extraction activity. Report anomalies to Shift Supervisor immediately. &gt; *Reminder:* Curiosity is not a productivity metric.</p> <hr> <p>[The assignment comes through her tag at second-shift break. A vibration against her neck, then the data:]</p> <p>&gt; **WORKER 477 - SPECIAL ASSIGNMENT** &gt; *Location:* Lower Works, Subsection 7-Gamma &gt; *Duration:* 4 hours (off-quota) &gt; *Task:* Debris clearance, surface cleaning &gt; *Compensation:* 80 ticks (flat rate) &gt; *Note:* Report to Section Access Point 7-G at 1400. Supervisor will provide equipment.</p> <p>[Off-quota. That means it doesn&#x27;t count toward her daily numbers. Eighty ticks for four hours-less than she&#x27;d make on the line, but not by much.]</p> <p>(Lower Works. They&#x27;re sending me to the Lower Works.)</p> <p>[She hasn&#x27;t been down there since they closed Section 7. Two years ago. Before-]</p> <p>[She stops the thought. Thinking about before costs.]</p> <hr> <p>[The access point is a reinforced door at the bottom of the Cinder Steps. The kind of door that says &quot;authorized personnel only&quot; in the way it&#x27;s built: heavy, scarred, clearly designed to keep things in as much as out.]</p> <p>[Supervisor Coil is waiting. She looks tired. More tired than usual.]</p> <p>Coil: Four-seven-seven. You&#x27;re on cleaning detail.</p> <p>Avyanna: [flat] Yes.</p> <p>Coil: [handing her a kit-brushes, solvents, a respiratory mask that&#x27;s seen better decades] Section&#x27;s been sealed two years. Dust, debris, some water damage from the condensation. You&#x27;ll start in the equipment bays, work your way back to the old extraction face.</p> <p>Avyanna: [not quite a question] Alone?</p> <p>Coil: [beat] You&#x27;re steady. You don&#x27;t panic. That&#x27;s why you.</p> <p>(That&#x27;s why me. Because I&#x27;m quiet. Because I make quota and don&#x27;t ask questions. Because I&#x27;m the safest body to throw into a sealed section.)</p> <p>Coil: [she keys the door, which groans as it unseals] Stay on the marked paths. Don&#x27;t touch anything that looks unstable. Don&#x27;t go past the barrier tape.</p> <p>Avyanna: [automatic] Yes, Supervisor.</p> <p>[Coil looks at her. Something in the supervisor&#x27;s eyes-not concern. Fear. The kind of fear that doesn&#x27;t know its own name.]</p> <p>Coil: The Lower Works are... different. Old equipment down there. Things that were here before the current operation. [she pauses, seems to stop herself from saying more] Just-clean what you can reach. Don&#x27;t wander. And if you hear anything... don&#x27;t answer.</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t wait for Avyanna to respond. The door is open. The darkness beyond it is patient.]</p> <hr> <p>[The Lower Works smell wrong.]</p> <p>[Stale. Mineral. The particular staleness of air that hasn&#x27;t moved in years. The corridor is narrow, lit by emergency strips that flicker and dim at intervals. The walls are old alloy, pitted with corrosion. Everything down here is old. Older than the mine she knows.]</p> <p>[Avyanna walks carefully. Her footsteps echo. The sound bounces off surfaces she can&#x27;t see, comes back distorted, strange.]</p> <p>(This was here before. Before the Kennel. Before the company. This was something else.)</p> <p>[The equipment bays are exactly what Coil described: abandoned spaces filled with machinery that hasn&#x27;t run in years. Dust covers everything-thick, gray-gold, the same color as the aurum dust but somehow different. Older.]</p> <p>[She starts cleaning. Methodical. Careful. The way she does everything.]</p> <p>[The cleaning kit has a checklist clipped inside. Standard corporate procedure. She reads it as she works:]</p> <p>&gt; *1. Confirm barrier tape integrity (photograph if damaged).* &gt; *2. Clear debris from marked walkways only.* &gt; *3. Report unauthorized symbols to Shift Supervisor.* &gt; *4. Do not engage with auditory phenomena.* &gt; *5. If equipment activates spontaneously, evacuate immediately.*</p> <p>(Do not engage with auditory phenomena. They put that in the checklist. Like it&#x27;s normal.)</p> <p>[The work is simple. Sweep debris. Wipe surfaces. Bag what can be removed, mark what can&#x27;t. The motions are automatic. Her mind wanders.]</p> <p>(Why send me? Why now? Two years this place has been sealed, and suddenly they need it cleaned.)</p> <p>(Maybe they&#x27;re reopening it. Maybe they need the space. Maybe-)</p> <p>[She stops. Listens.]</p> <p>[Something. At the edge of hearing. Not quite a sound-more like the ghost of a sound, the impression that something should be audible but isn&#x27;t.]</p> <p>(The vents. Just the vents. Air moving through old ducts.)</p> <p>[She keeps working. The feeling doesn&#x27;t go away.]</p> <hr> <p>[Two hours in. She&#x27;s worked her way past the equipment bays, into the older sections. The corridors here are narrower. The emergency lighting is spottier. The air tastes different-metallic, with an undertone of something she can&#x27;t name.]</p> <p>[The barrier tape is ahead. Yellow and black, faded with age. RESTRICTED SECTION - NO ENTRY. Someone has scratched words beneath the official warning, in letters that took effort:]</p> <p>*TAP TWICE. DON&#x27;T WHISTLE. DON&#x27;T LOOK BACK.*</p> <p>[Older graffiti, almost worn away: *DON&#x27;T ANSWER IF IT KNOCKS.*]</p> <p>[Beyond the tape, the corridor continues into darkness. The extraction face that Coil mentioned. The place they stopped mining two years ago.]</p> <p>(Don&#x27;t go past the barrier tape. Those were the orders.)</p> <p>(But the orders also said clean what I can reach. And there&#x27;s debris on both sides of the line.)</p> <p>[She looks around. No cameras she can see. No one watching. The silence is complete except for that not-quite-sound at the edge of her hearing, growing louder the longer she listens.]</p> <p>(It&#x27;s just dust. Just debris. I&#x27;ll sweep it up and go back.)</p> <p>[She ducks under the tape.]</p> <hr> <p>[The extraction face is wrong.]</p> <p>[She can see it as soon as the emergency lights catch the far wall. This isn&#x27;t a normal mining surface-the kind she knows from the upper levels, where the aurum ore appears in veins and seams. This is something else.]</p> <p>[The rock here is different. Darker. Smoother. It looks almost polished, like something has worn it down over time. And embedded in it, partially exposed-]</p> <p>[She stops.]</p> <p>[It&#x27;s not ore. It&#x27;s not any material she recognizes. It&#x27;s-]</p> <p>(What is that?)</p> <p>[A shard. That&#x27;s the only word that fits. A fragment of something, jutting from the rock like a broken bone. It&#x27;s maybe the length of her forearm, irregular, angular in a way that hurts to look at. The color is wrong-not quite black, not quite purple, shifting between the two depending on how the light catches it.]</p> <p>[And it glows. Faintly. A light that doesn&#x27;t seem to come from the surface but from somewhere inside, somewhere deeper than the material should allow.]</p> <p>(That shouldn&#x27;t be here. This isn&#x27;t an extraction face. They sealed this section because of geological instability, not because-)</p> <p>(Not because of this.)</p> <p>[She should turn around. She should go back to the barrier tape, finish her cleaning, report nothing. Anomalies get reported. Anomalies get investigated. Anomalies get you noticed.]</p> <p>[Her feet carry her forward anyway.]</p> <hr> <p>[The closer she gets, the louder the not-sound becomes.]</p> <p>[It&#x27;s not a humming, exactly. More like pressure-the sense of something vibrating at a frequency her ears can&#x27;t catch but her body can feel. It moves through her chest, her bones, the soft tissue behind her eyes.]</p> <p>[The shard is-]</p> <p>[The thought arrives fully formed, in words that aren&#x27;t hers: *beautiful*.]</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t think that. She doesn&#x27;t know that word in this context. But something does. Something that&#x27;s already noticing her back.]</p> <p>(Don&#x27;t touch it. Don&#x27;t touch it. Whatever you do-)</p> <p>[Her hand reaches out.]</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t decide to do it. Her arm moves like it&#x27;s following instructions she didn&#x27;t give. Like someone else is reaching through her. Her fingers extend. The tips of them brush the surface-]</p> <hr> <p>[Pain.]</p> <p>[Not the clean pain of injury. Not the dull pain of work-worn muscles. Something else. Pain that has a shape. Pain that has a direction.]</p> <p>[Her vision fractures. Splits into pieces that don&#x27;t fit together. She sees-]</p> <p>-geometric patterns, angles that hurt her eyes, a spiral that pulls inward-</p> <p>-pressure, weight, something too large trying to fit into a space too small-</p> <p>-and then words that aren&#x27;t hers, thoughts that don&#x27;t belong to her brain:</p> <p>***ledger-open***</p> <p>***balance-due***</p> <p>***asset-tagged***</p> <p>[Her nose is bleeding. Hot copper on her lips. Her knees hit the ground-when did she fall?-and her hand is still touching the shard, she can&#x27;t pull it away, something is holding her there-]</p> <p>(Let go let go let go-)</p> <p>-shapes behind her eyes, diagrams she doesn&#x27;t understand, something being written into her-</p> <p>***interest-begins***</p> <p>***contract-sealed***</p> <p>-a presence, vast and patient, doing math she can&#x27;t follow, counting something about her-</p> <p>(WHAT ARE YOU)</p> <p>[The question isn&#x27;t hers. It comes from inside. From the shard. From the thing that&#x27;s already inside her, asking itself what it&#x27;s found.]</p> <p>[And then-]</p> <p>[Something breaks. The connection snaps. She yanks her hand back with a cry that echoes through the empty corridor, and the shard is cold, dead, just a rock again. Just a fragment of something strange embedded in a wall that shouldn&#x27;t exist.]</p> <p>[Avyanna is on her knees, gasping, bleeding, shaking so hard her teeth chatter.]</p> <p>(What was that. What did I just-)</p> <p>(What did I let in?)</p> <hr> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t know how long she stays there. Minutes. Hours. Time moves wrong in the Lower Works.]</p> <p>[Eventually, the shaking stops. Eventually, she can think again. Fragments of thoughts, not full sentences, but enough to function.]</p> <p>(Get up. Clean up. Hide this.)</p> <p>[The nosebleed has stopped. The blood on her face has dried to a crust. She wipes it away with her sleeve-the motion automatic, a habit from years of minor injuries that couldn&#x27;t be reported.]</p> <p>[The shard is still there. Still embedded in the rock. Still faintly glowing, though dimmer now. Colder.]</p> <p>(It&#x27;s not hot. The stone in my lockbox is warm. This is cold. What does that mean?)</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t know. She doesn&#x27;t want to know. She wants to go back to the upper levels, finish her shift, forget this ever happened.]</p> <p>(But I can&#x27;t forget. It&#x27;s in my head now. The patterns. The shapes. The voice that wasn&#x27;t a voice.)</p> <p>(Something is inside me.)</p> <hr> <p>[She makes it back to the barrier tape. Ducks under it. Returns to the equipment bays, where her cleaning kit waits, untouched for however long she was gone.]</p> <p>[Her hands move automatically. Sweep. Wipe. Bag. The motions are mechanical. Her mind is somewhere else.]</p> <p>(The thing in the rock. The thing that saw me. The thing that-)</p> <p>[She stops. Breathes. Forces herself to focus.]</p> <p>(I need to think like a number. I need to act like nothing happened. If they find out-)</p> <p>(Everything gets confiscated here. Everything. Personal items, contraband, anything that might have value outside the debt ledger. If they find out I touched something-something like that-)</p> <p>(I&#x27;ll disappear. The way Bram disappeared. The way everyone disappears.)</p> <p>[She finishes the cleaning. Packs up the kit. Walks back through the Lower Works, past the old equipment, through the reinforced door, up the Cinder Steps to the world she knows.]</p> <p>[Supervisor Coil is gone. A different guard waves her through the checkpoint. The scanner beeps against her tag-]</p> <p>[And stops. The guard frowns. Checks his tablet.]</p> <p>Guard: [flat] Hold.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s blood goes cold. Her hands stay at her sides. She doesn&#x27;t move. Doesn&#x27;t breathe.]</p> <p>(They know. The scanner saw something. They&#x27;re going to-)</p> <p>Guard: [tapping the tablet, annoyed] Calibration drift. Third time today. [he waves her through] Go.</p> <p>[The tag vibrates against her neck. A message she&#x27;s not supposed to see-a flicker in her visual cortex, there and gone:]</p> <p>&gt; *NEUROLOAD SPIKE / FLAGGED FOR REVIEW / AUTO-CLEARED: ENVIRONMENTAL INTERFERENCE (7-GAMMA)*</p> <p>[Auto-cleared. The system saw something. The system decided it was nothing.]</p> <p>(It&#x27;s not nothing. But the system doesn&#x27;t know that. Not yet.)</p> <p>[Her head pounds. Something behind her eyes pulses in response-almost like gratitude. Almost like it&#x27;s pleased the system didn&#x27;t notice.]</p> <p>(What are you? What did you do?)</p> <p>[No answer. Just pressure. Just waiting.]</p> <hr> <p>[The rest of her shift is a blur. She returns to Slurry Line 7, takes her station, works her quota. Her hands move. Her eyes track the slurry. Her mind is somewhere else.]</p> <p>[The patterns from the shard keep flickering at the edges of her vision. Not hallucinations-not quite. More like afterimages. Something marked into her.]</p> <p>[She makes quota. She always makes quota. The numbers at the end of her shift are normal. Earnings, deductions, interest, debt. The math continues.]</p> <p>(But there&#x27;s something else now. Something the math doesn&#x27;t account for.)</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t know if that&#x27;s good or bad. She&#x27;s not sure there&#x27;s a difference anymore.]</p> <hr> <p>[Night cycle. The hab stack. Her bunk.]</p> <p>[Avyanna lies in the dark, eyes open, staring at the ceiling of her coffin-pod. Her head is pounding. The pressure hasn&#x27;t faded-if anything, it&#x27;s grown stronger since she left the Lower Works.]</p> <p>[In her lockbox, the smooth stone she found two years ago is warm. Warmer than ever. She can feel it through the metal, through the distance, like a second heartbeat.]</p> <p>(Two years ago. I found the stone in the Lower Works. In the same section they just sent me back to.)</p> <p>(Is that a coincidence? Is any of this a coincidence?)</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t believe in coincidences. Coincidences are a luxury, like hope. Like names. Like meaning.]</p> <p>[But something is happening. Something that started two years ago, when she picked up a warm stone that shouldn&#x27;t be warm. Something that continued today, when she touched a shard that shouldn&#x27;t exist.]</p> <p>(What did it put in me? What did it take out?)</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t have answers. She only has the pressure behind her eyes, and the patterns flickering at the edge of her vision, and the feeling-bone-deep, impossible to ignore-that something is different now. Something has changed.]</p> <p>[She closes her eyes. Sleep comes slowly, reluctantly, like a guest who knows it&#x27;s not wanted.]</p> <hr> <p>[The dreams are not dreams.]</p> <p>[She knows this even as they&#x27;re happening. Dreams are soft. Dreams are fragments, nonsense, the brain processing noise. This is something else.]</p> <p>[She&#x27;s standing in a space that has no walls, no floor, no ceiling. Just darkness-but not empty darkness. Full darkness. The darkness of something so large it doesn&#x27;t fit in three dimensions.]</p> <p>[And in the darkness: shapes.]</p> <p>[They spiral around her. Geometric patterns, the same ones from the shard, but bigger now. Complete. A language she can&#x27;t read, a diagram she can&#x27;t interpret, a blueprint for something that hasn&#x27;t been built yet.]</p> <p>[She should be afraid. She is afraid. But she&#x27;s also-]</p> <p>(Curious. I&#x27;m curious. What is this? What does it mean?)</p> <p>[The shapes pulse. Respond to her attention. And then-]</p> <p>[Not a voice. Something older than voice. Impressions. Concepts. Like someone reading her file:]</p> <p>***asset-acquired***</p> <p>***principal-invested***</p> <p>***returns-pending***</p> <p>[She wants to ask: What are you? What do you want? Why me?]</p> <p>[But she can&#x27;t speak. Can&#x27;t move. Can only watch as the patterns spiral closer, as the presence behind them grows larger, as something vast and patient takes inventory of what it&#x27;s found-]</p> <hr> <p>[She wakes gasping. The siren hasn&#x27;t sounded yet-it&#x27;s still night cycle, still dark, still quiet.]</p> <p>[Her head is pounding. Her nose is bleeding again. She wipes it on her sleeve, watches the dark stain spread on the fabric.]</p> <p>(Not a dream. That wasn&#x27;t a dream.)</p> <p>(Something is in my head. Something from the shard. Something that&#x27;s been waiting-)</p> <p>[Waiting for what? For her? Why?]</p> <p>(Maybe I&#x27;m going mad. Maybe the dust has finally gotten to my brain. Maybe this is what happens before you check out-you start seeing things, hearing things, dreaming things that aren&#x27;t dreams.)</p> <p>(Maybe that&#x27;s easier. Mad is easier. Mad has a name.)</p> <p>[But she doesn&#x27;t feel mad. She feels different. Changed. Like a door has opened somewhere inside her, and something has stepped through.]</p> <p>[Her lockbox is warm. The stone inside is almost hot now-she can feel it radiating through the metal, through the air. Calling to her.]</p> <p>(The stone is from the same place. The stone has been warming up for two years. The stone is connected to the shard.)</p> <p>(And now the shard is connected to me.)</p> <hr> <p>[The siren screams. Another day. Another count. Another shift.]</p> <p>[Avyanna sits up. Swings her legs out. Her feet find the cold deck. The motion is automatic. Everything here is automatic.]</p> <p>[But something is different now.]</p> <p>[Behind her eyes, the patterns wait. Dormant but present. The ghost of a presence, watching from inside her own skull. Not speaking-not yet-but there. Aware. Patient.]</p> <p>(What are you?)</p> <p>[No answer. Just the faint impression of attention. Of something old learning her shape.]</p> <p>(I should report this. I should tell someone. I should-)</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t. She won&#x27;t. Old habits. Don&#x27;t show weakness. Don&#x27;t draw attention. Don&#x27;t be anything except a number that makes quota.]</p> <p>[She stands. Walks to the count. Lets Coil&#x27;s scanner beep against her neck.]</p> <p>Coil: [calling numbers] Four-seven-five. Four-seven-six. Four-seven-seven.</p> <p>[Avyanna is four-seven-seven. She is nothing more than that. She is invisible. She is safe.]</p> <p>(Except I&#x27;m not. Not anymore. Something marked me.)</p> <p>(And I don&#x27;t know what comes next.)</p> <hr> <p>[The day passes. She works. She makes quota. She says nothing.]</p> <p>[But she notices things now. Details she would have missed before. The way the light bends strangely near certain equipment. The way some of the older miners avoid specific sections of the Processing Hall. The way Bram&#x27;s station is empty.]</p> <p>(Bram made fifty.)</p> <p>(Bram burned bright.)</p> <p>(Kennel&#x27;s got a new itch, he said. Feel it in the deep cuts. Something scratching.)</p> <p>[She keeps moving. Her heart is pounding. The pressure behind her eyes pulses in response, like a second heartbeat.]</p> <p>(Was that what he meant?)</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t ask. Asking would mean acknowledging. Acknowledging would mean explaining. And she can&#x27;t explain what she doesn&#x27;t understand.]</p> <p>[She works. She makes quota. She survives.]</p> <p>[That night, the dreams come again.]</p> <hr> <p>[They&#x27;re stronger now. Clearer. The patterns are sharper, the presence more distinct. She can almost see it-a shape behind the shapes, a form trying to become visible.]</p> <p>[The impressions are louder too. Still not words, not exactly. But clearer. Like someone learning to use her vocabulary:]</p> <p>***debtor-identified***</p> <p>***terms-accepted***</p> <p>***we-hold-the-note***</p> <p>[We. Not &quot;I.&quot; We.]</p> <p>(Who are you? What do you want?)</p> <p>[The patterns pulse. Reorganize. For a moment-just a moment-they form something almost recognizable. A receipt. A ledger. A-]</p> <p>[She wakes before she can understand it.]</p> <p>[Her nose is bleeding again. Her head is pounding. The stone in her lockbox is hot enough to feel through the air.]</p> <p>(This is going to kill me. Whatever this is. It&#x27;s too big. Too old. It doesn&#x27;t fit in a human brain, and it&#x27;s trying to fit anyway.)</p> <p>(I should tell someone. I should get help. I should-)</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t. She can&#x27;t. Because who would she tell? What would she say? &quot;Something from the deep cuts is living in my head&quot;? They&#x27;d ship her to the disposal section before she finished the sentence.]</p> <p>(Survive. That&#x27;s all I know how to do. Survive, and keep my head down, and wait for whatever comes next.)</p> <p>[She lies in the dark, bleeding, shaking. The presence behind her eyes pulses in time with her heartbeat. Learning her rhythm.]</p> <p>[The siren will scream soon. Another day. Another count. Another shift.]</p> <p>[And something new. Something that marked her. Something that&#x27;s learning her number.]</p> <hr> <p>&gt; **AURUM EXTRACTION LTD. - ANOMALY REPORT (INTERNAL)** &gt; *Site:* K-9 &gt; *Section:* Lower Works, Subsection 7-Gamma &gt; *Date:* [REDACTED] &gt; *Reporting Officer:* [REDACTED] &gt; *Observation:* Routine maintenance cleaning completed. No significant debris. No structural concerns. Barrier integrity maintained. &gt; *Anomalies Detected:* None. &gt; *Recommendation:* Continue quarterly maintenance schedule. No further action required. &gt; *Note:* Worker assigned to cleaning detail (477) completed task within acceptable parameters. No deviations observed. Performance adequate.</p> <hr> <p>*End Chapter 3*</p> </div> Where does the route go next? * [[Continue to Chapter 04: Contract Glitter Vein->Act1 04 Contract Glitter Vein]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Chapter 04: Contract Glitter Vein</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 04_contract_glitter_vein.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **AURUM EXTRACTION LTD. - EXTERNAL VISITOR ADVISORY** &gt; *Site:* K-9 (&quot;The Kennel&quot;) &gt; *Date:* [CURRENT] &gt; *Visitor Class:* Guild Contractor (Vessel: Lumen Thief, Registry: Independent) &gt; *Contract Type:* Equipment Diagnostics / Lattice Coherence Assessment (7-Gamma Sector) &gt; *Duration:* Estimated 72 hours &gt; *Worker Instructions:* Maintain productivity during visitor presence. Do not approach contractor personnel. Do not discuss operational concerns. Direct all inquiries to Shift Supervisor. &gt; *Reminder:* Professional appearance reflects company values. Violations will be noted.</p> <hr> <p>[The rumor moves through the Processing Hall faster than the slurry line.]</p> <p>[Avyanna hears it first from the woman at Station 8-a whisper passed during the three-second gap when the grinders cycle down for recalibration.]</p> <p>Woman: [barely audible] Guild ship. Docking Bay 3. They&#x27;re already inside.</p> <p>[The words ripple outward. By the time the grinders spin back up, half the line knows. By lunch, everyone will.]</p> <p>(Guild ship. Here. Why?)</p> <p>[She keeps working. Hands moving. Eyes on the line. Mind elsewhere.]</p> <p>[The presence behind her eyes stirs-a faint pulse, like a question mark she can&#x27;t read. It&#x27;s been doing that since the deep cuts. Responding to things she doesn&#x27;t understand.]</p> <hr> <p>[Lunch break. The commissary is different today. Louder. Workers clustered in groups, talking in voices that are almost too quiet to hear. The air has that particular electricity that comes before something changes.]</p> <p>[Avyanna finds her wall. Watches.]</p> <p>Worker 1: [to a group near the serving line] Equipment diagnostics, they&#x27;re saying. Lattice something.</p> <p>Worker 2: [skeptical] Since when does the company pay Guild rates for diagnostics?</p> <p>Worker 3: Guild means credits. Credits mean they&#x27;ll squeeze us harder to pay for it.</p> <p>[Avyanna doesn&#x27;t disagree. Nothing good comes from outside. Outside means attention. Attention means change. Change means the math gets worse.]</p> <p>(But something is different. The administration is nervous. I can see it in the way the foremen move-too fast, too careful. Like they&#x27;re being watched.)</p> <p>[Near the commissary exit, two supervisors are talking in low voices. One of them keeps glancing at the corridor that leads to the administrative section. The other has a tablet, scrolling through something, face tight.]</p> <p>[Something is wrong. Something beyond the usual wrongness.]</p> <hr> <p>[She&#x27;s assigned to corridor cleanup for the afternoon. Not her usual work-quota-exempt, low-priority, the kind of task they give workers when they need bodies visible but not productive.]</p> <p>(They want us out of the way. Whatever the Guild contractors are here to see, they don&#x27;t want workers nearby.)</p> <p>[But corridor cleanup means she can move. Means she can see things the Processing Hall doesn&#x27;t show.]</p> <p>[She works her way through the lower administrative level-sweeping, wiping, invisible. The motions are automatic. Her eyes are elsewhere.]</p> <p>[The Docking Bay corridor. She can see the entrance from here, if she positions herself right. If she pretends to be cleaning the same stretch of floor for ten minutes longer than necessary.]</p> <p>[And then-]</p> <p>[She sees them.]</p> <hr> <p>[They move wrong.]</p> <p>[That&#x27;s the first thing she notices. The way they walk through the corridor, past the checkpoint, into the facility. They move like they own the space they&#x27;re standing in. Like they&#x27;ve never been property. Like the concept of &quot;authorized access&quot; applies to other people.]</p> <p>[There are five of them. Six, if you count the small drone that hovers near the one in front-a bright spherical thing with scorch marks on one panel, bobbing and weaving like it&#x27;s happy to be here.]</p> <p>[The one in front: a woman, medium height, dark hair, wearing clothes that look expensive but worn-stained at the cuffs, patched at one elbow. She walks like violence is an option she&#x27;s deliberately not choosing. Her hand rests near something at her hip-not quite a weapon, not quite not. There&#x27;s engine grease under her fingernails.]</p> <p>[Behind her: another woman, taller, with the careful posture of someone who&#x27;s cataloging everything she sees. Her jacket has a tear at the shoulder, mended with thread that doesn&#x27;t quite match. Her eyes move constantly-walls, workers, camera positions, exit routes. Taking inventory.]</p> <p>[A third: lean, pale, moving with a grace that doesn&#x27;t match the industrial corridors. Something about them makes Avyanna&#x27;s skin prickle. The presence behind her eyes stirs, recognizing something she can&#x27;t name.]</p> <p>[A fourth: older, broad-shouldered, carrying equipment cases with patient competence. His boots are scuffed, his hands callused. The kind of person who&#x27;s seen everything and stopped being surprised by any of it. She doesn&#x27;t catch his name yet.]</p> <p>[And walking slightly behind them all: another woman with a tablet, talking to the mine&#x27;s governor. Her voice is smooth, professional. The governor is nodding too much, smiling too wide. He looks afraid. This one doesn&#x27;t get named either-just a function. The smooth one.]</p> <p>[Avyanna watches from her corridor. Her mop moves in slow circles. No one looks at her. She&#x27;s invisible. A number cleaning a floor.]</p> <p>(They&#x27;re not like anyone I&#x27;ve seen here. They&#x27;re not like anyone I&#x27;ve seen anywhere.)</p> <p>(They look... free.)</p> <hr> <p>[The group stops near the main junction. The governor is gesturing toward the Processing Hall-probably offering a tour, a demonstration, all the things administrators offer when they want visitors to see the right parts and miss the wrong ones.]</p> <p>[The woman in front-the one who walks like violence-shakes her head.]</p> <p>Woman: [voice carrying, flat] I&#x27;m not interested in the tour. I&#x27;m interested in the lattice failures in your 7-Gamma sector. The ones you waited three months to report.</p> <p>Governor: [too smooth] Of course, of course. But perhaps first, a meal? We&#x27;ve prepared something in the executive dining-</p> <p>Woman: [cutting him off] I don&#x27;t do dinners. And I&#x27;m not a captain.</p> <p>Governor: [confused] I-the registry listed-</p> <p>Woman: [already walking] The registry is wrong about a lot of things. Show me the equipment, or I&#x27;ll find it myself.</p> <p>[The governor&#x27;s face does something complicated. Fear and anger and the particular frustration of someone who&#x27;s used to being in charge and suddenly isn&#x27;t.]</p> <p>(She just told him no. She just... refused.)</p> <p>(I didn&#x27;t know that was possible.)</p> <hr> <p>[The woman with the tablet-the smooth one, the one who was talking to the governor-steps forward. Her voice is calm, reasonable, the sound of someone who&#x27;s very good at managing people who are very bad at being managed.]</p> <p>Tablet Woman: Governor Hask, we appreciate the hospitality. But Captain- [she pauses, glances at the other woman, seems to correct herself] -but Elia is focused on the contract terms. The 7-Gamma lattice assessment first. Cultural exchange after.</p> <p>[Elia. That&#x27;s her name. The one who walks like violence.]</p> <p>Governor: [recovering] Of course. The equipment is in the Lower Works access corridor. I&#x27;ll have my assistant-</p> <p>Elia: We&#x27;ll find it. [to the cataloging woman] Get eyes on worker movement patterns. I want to know what&#x27;s normal here.</p> <p>[The cataloging woman is already moving. On it, without needing to say it.]</p> <p>[Elia turns to the pale one-the one who makes Avyanna&#x27;s skin prickle.]</p> <p>Elia: Nyx. Feel anything?</p> <p>[The pale one tilts their head. Closes their eyes for a moment. When they open again, they look directly at the corridor where Avyanna is standing.]</p> <p>Nyx: [quiet] Something&#x27;s listening. Has been for a while. Deeper than this level.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s blood goes cold. The mop freezes in her hands.]</p> <p>(They know. They can tell. The thing in my head-)</p> <p>[But Nyx&#x27;s gaze moves past her. Continues down the corridor. One pale hand rises, pointing toward the Lower Works access-toward 7-Gamma, toward the deep cuts where she found the stone.]</p> <p>Nyx: [quiet, certain] There. Old. Patient. Not hostile. Yet.</p> <p>Elia: [flat] Great. Love a situation with a &quot;yet.&quot; [to the broad one] Set up monitoring in whatever space they give us. I want Bubbles with eyes on everything.</p> <p>[The broad one nods. Already walking.]</p> <p>[The small drone bobs toward the governor, who flinches.]</p> <p>Bubbles: [cheerful, through a speaker] Hello! I&#x27;m an AI citizen. I&#x27;ll need access to your facility&#x27;s sensor network for diagnostic purposes. [beat, tone shifting slightly] Your network security is thirteen patches behind current standard. Also your coffee machine is offline. Both of these are noted.</p> <p>[The governor&#x27;s smile freezes. The drone&#x27;s cheer sounds less like friendliness now. More like a warning dressed in manners.]</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna watches them disperse. The smooth one stays with the governor, asking questions in a voice that sounds friendly but isn&#x27;t. The cataloging one walks through the facility with eyes that miss nothing. The broad one heads toward the administrative section with his equipment cases.</p> <p>[And Elia stands in the middle of the junction, hands at her sides, looking at everything like she&#x27;s already found what&#x27;s wrong and is deciding what to do about it.]</p> <p>[The presence behind Avyanna&#x27;s eyes pulses. Curious. Alert.]</p> <p>(They&#x27;re here for the equipment. That&#x27;s what the contract says.)</p> <p>(But she&#x27;s watching the foreman, not the machinery. Watching how he moves us. Counting something.)</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna finishes her corridor. Moves to the next section. Keeps working, keeps invisible, keeps watching.]</p> <p>[The facility changes around the visitors. Workers move faster, more carefully. Supervisors appear in corridors where they&#x27;re usually absent. The lighting seems brighter-or maybe that&#x27;s just her imagination, the sense that everything is being examined.]</p> <p>[She passes a group of workers being herded away from a junction where the cataloging woman is standing. She&#x27;s making notes on a tablet, her eyes tracking movement patterns, shift changes, the small inefficiencies that reveal how a system actually works.]</p> <p>Worker: [muttering, as they&#x27;re redirected] What&#x27;s she looking at?</p> <p>Another Worker: [quieter] Us. She&#x27;s looking at us.</p> <p>[Avyanna keeps her head down. Keeps moving. Her mop traces patterns on the floor-circles within circles, the same motions she&#x27;s made a thousand times.]</p> <p>[But she&#x27;s not thinking about the floor. She&#x27;s thinking about the way the cataloging woman looked at that group of workers. Looked at their faces, not their tags. Wrote something down when the supervisor grabbed Terrin&#x27;s arm too hard.]</p> <hr> <p>[Late afternoon. The light in the corridors shifts-the artificial cycle that mimics a sun this station has never seen. Avyanna is finishing her assignment, working her way back toward the Processing Hall for end-of-shift count.]</p> <p>[And she turns a corner, and Elia is there.]</p> <p>[Just standing. Looking at a wall display-one of the old motivational posters the company installed when the mine was new. PRODUCTIVITY IS FREEDOM. The words are faded. Someone has scratched &quot;LIES&quot; underneath, then scratched it out, then scratched it again.]</p> <p>[Elia is reading the scratches like they&#x27;re more important than the words.]</p> <p>[Avyanna freezes. Her mop stops moving. She&#x27;s too close-close enough to be noticed, close enough to be spoken to. She should back away, find another route, be invisible-]</p> <p>[Elia turns.]</p> <p>[And looks at her.]</p> <hr> <p>[It&#x27;s not a long look. Maybe two seconds. Maybe three. But something happens in those seconds that Avyanna doesn&#x27;t have words for.]</p> <p>[Elia&#x27;s eyes don&#x27;t slide past her. Don&#x27;t look through her. Don&#x27;t treat her as furniture, as inventory, as a number that happens to be holding a mop.]</p> <p>[Elia sees her.]</p> <p>(She looked at me like I was a person. Not inventory. Not four-seven-seven. A person.)</p> <p>(When was the last time anyone looked at me like that?)</p> <p>[The presence behind Avyanna&#x27;s eyes surges-a pulse of something that might be recognition, might be warning, might be both.]</p> <p>[Elia&#x27;s expression doesn&#x27;t change. But something shifts in her posture. A question forming. A decision being made.]</p> <p>Elia: [quiet, almost conversational] You&#x27;ve been watching us all day.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat closes. She should deny it. Should apologize. Should be invisible, the way she&#x27;s supposed to be-]</p> <p>Avyanna: [before she can stop herself] You move wrong.</p> <p>[The words come out flat. Automatic. The same voice she uses for everything here-survival voice, number voice, the voice that doesn&#x27;t get noticed.]</p> <p>[But Elia&#x27;s eyebrow rises. A flicker of something that might be amusement.]</p> <p>Elia: Wrong how?</p> <p>Avyanna: [still flat, still automatic] Like no one owns you.</p> <p>[Silence. The corridor hums around them-ventilation, distant machinery, the sound of a facility that never stops working.]</p> <p>[Elia looks at her for another long moment. The amusement is gone. Something else has replaced it-something harder, sharper. Something that looks like anger, but not at her.]</p> <p>Elia: [quiet] No. I haven&#x27;t been property. [beat] But I&#x27;ve met people who were. And I remember every one of their faces.</p> <p>[She turns. Walks away. The small drone-Bubbles-bobs after her, pausing for just a moment near Avyanna before following.]</p> <p>[Avyanna stands in the corridor, mop in hand, heart pounding, the presence behind her eyes pulsing with something she can&#x27;t name.]</p> <p>(She&#x27;ll remember my face.)</p> <p>(I don&#x27;t know if that&#x27;s good or bad.)</p> <p>(I don&#x27;t know anything anymore.)</p> <hr> <p>[End of shift. The siren screams. The count happens. Avyanna is four-seven-seven, nothing more, nothing less.]</p> <p>[But something has changed.]</p> <p>[She felt it when Elia looked at her. Felt it in the way the presence behind her eyes responded-curious, alert, almost excited. As if it recognized something in the visitor that Avyanna couldn&#x27;t see.]</p> <p>[The crew is still in the facility. She can feel their presence like a disturbance in the pattern-the way workers move around them, the way supervisors hover at the edges of their attention, the way the whole facility seems to be holding its breath.]</p> <p>(They&#x27;re here for the equipment. That&#x27;s what the contract says.)</p> <p>(But the smooth one asked a supervisor how many workers came back from Incident 7-Gamma. Asked it casual, like it was nothing. The supervisor&#x27;s face went white.)</p> <p>(And I saw the cataloging woman&#x27;s tablet. Just a glimpse. The file was labeled LABOR CONDITIONS.)</p> <p>(What if they find me?)</p> <hr> <p>[Night cycle. Her bunk. The stone in her lockbox is warm-warmer than usual, as if it&#x27;s responding to the day&#x27;s events.]</p> <p>[The presence behind her eyes is restless. Patterns flickering at the edge of her vision. The sense of something paying attention, of calculations she can&#x27;t follow, of interest she didn&#x27;t ask for.]</p> <p>(What are you? What do you want?)</p> <p>[No answer. Just the usual impressions. The usual sense of being counted, cataloged, prepared for something she doesn&#x27;t understand.]</p> <p>[But there&#x27;s something else now. Something new.]</p> <p>[When she closes her eyes, she sees Elia&#x27;s face. The way she looked at the scratched-out poster. The way she looked at Avyanna. The anger in her voice when she said *I&#x27;ve met people who were property.*]</p> <p>(She&#x27;s here to investigate equipment. That&#x27;s what the contract says.)</p> <p>(But she looked at that poster like it told her something. Looked at me like I was evidence.)</p> <p>(And if she finds it-)</p> <p>[Avyanna doesn&#x27;t know what happens next. Doesn&#x27;t know if the crew can help, or if helping is even possible. Doesn&#x27;t know if she wants to be found, or if being found is the worst thing that could happen.]</p> <p>[But for the first time in years, she feels something that isn&#x27;t survival. Something that isn&#x27;t the math of debt and interest and quota.]</p> <p>[She feels seen.]</p> <p>[And she doesn&#x27;t know what to do with that.]</p> <hr> <p>[Sleep comes eventually. The dreams are different tonight.]</p> <p>[Not the spiraling patterns. Not the ledger-voice of the presence. Something else.]</p> <p>[She dreams of a ship. A corridor that isn&#x27;t this corridor. People moving through it like they belong there-talking, laughing, existing in ways that have nothing to do with productivity or debt.]</p> <p>[She dreams of freedom. Of what it might look like. Of what it might cost.]</p> <p>[And somewhere in the dream, something ancient and patient watches. Takes notes. Adjusts its calculations.]</p> <p>***asset-value: increasing***</p> <p>***extraction-timeline: accelerating***</p> <p>***contact-probability: high***</p> <p>[She wakes before the siren. The presence is quiet. Waiting.]</p> <p>[Outside her bunk, the facility hums with the sound of visitors who move wrong.]</p> <p>[Something is going to happen. She can feel it.]</p> <p>[She just doesn&#x27;t know if she&#x27;ll survive it.]</p> <hr> <p>&gt; **AURUM EXTRACTION LTD. - INTERNAL MEMO (CONFIDENTIAL)** &gt; *To:* Site Director Hask &gt; *From:* Security Chief Morrow &gt; *Subject:* Guild Contractor Observations &gt; *Classification:* EYES ONLY &gt; &gt; Director, &gt; &gt; Initial assessment of the Lumen Thief crew suggests atypical behavior for standard contract workers. The lead operative (&quot;Elia&quot;) refused formal protocols and has been observed examining worker movement patterns rather than equipment systems. The secondary operative (&quot;Elisira&quot;) has been conducting unauthorized interviews with line supervisors under the guise of &quot;operational context.&quot; &gt; &gt; Recommend enhanced monitoring. Their questions are not consistent with equipment diagnostics. &gt; &gt; Additionally: one of their AI citizens (&quot;Bubbles&quot;) has been attempting to access restricted network segments. Access has been denied per standard protocol. The AI expressed &quot;disappointment&quot; and filed a formal complaint with the Guild. &gt; &gt; Will advise on further developments. &gt; &gt; *-Morrow* &gt; &gt; *Note: Worker 477 was observed in proximity to the lead operative for approximately 90 seconds. Reviewing footage. Audio degraded in that corridor segment (see maintenance ticket #4471). No apparent interaction beyond visual contact. Flagging for follow-up.*</p> <hr> <p>*End Chapter 4*</p> </div> Where does the route go next? * [[Continue to Chapter 05: Flicker Day->Act1 05 Flicker Day]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Chapter 05: Flicker Day</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 05_flicker_day.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **AURUM EXTRACTION LTD. - SHIFT BULLETIN** &gt; *Site:* K-9 (&quot;The Kennel&quot;) &gt; *Date:* [CURRENT] &gt; *Shift:* Alpha &gt; *Weather Designation:* AMBER (intermittent equipment variance expected) &gt; *Worker Advisory:* Report all tool malfunctions to nearest supervisor. Do not attempt field repairs. Do not discuss equipment issues with visiting contractors. &gt; *Reminder:* Quota adjustments during AMBER weather conditions are at management discretion.</p> <hr> <p>[The grinders sound wrong today.]</p> <p>[Avyanna notices it before the siren, before the bulletin, before anyone says the word *flicker*. A catch in the rhythm. A hesitation where there shouldn&#x27;t be one. The machines are breathing wrong.]</p> <p>[The presence behind her eyes stirs. Curious. Alert.]</p> <p>(Something&#x27;s happening.)</p> <p>[She keeps her hands moving. Keeps her head down. But she&#x27;s watching now-watching the way the processing line stutters every few seconds, watching the way the foremen cluster near the control panel, watching the way no one wants to say what everyone can feel.]</p> <hr> <p>[By mid-morning, the word is everywhere. Whispered in the gap between grinder cycles. Passed along the slurry line. Muttered at the water stations.]</p> <p>Worker 1: [barely audible] Flicker day. Third one this rotation.</p> <p>Worker 2: They&#x27;re getting worse.</p> <p>[Flicker day. When tools fail without breaking. When machines hesitate without jamming. When reality stutters and no one&#x27;s allowed to notice.]</p> <p>(The foremen call it &quot;weather.&quot; Like it&#x27;s something that happens. Like it&#x27;s no one&#x27;s fault.)</p> <p>(But weather doesn&#x27;t make equipment forget how to work.)</p> <hr> <p>[The failures cascade through the morning shift. Small at first-a calibration tool that won&#x27;t zero, a welding torch that sparks when it should flame, a cart that refuses to move in one direction but rolls fine in the other.]</p> <p>[And one that isn&#x27;t mechanical at all: Grinder 7 skips. Not the machine-the *sound*. It grinds, stops, and then the noise arrives a half-second late, like audio dragged through water. Everyone on the line freezes. Avyanna tastes copper. A worker near the railing taps twice, reflexive, not thinking about it. The moment passes. They go back to work.]</p> <p>[Then bigger.]</p> <p>[Grinder Bank Three evacuates when three units seize simultaneously. The sound is horrible-metal screaming, then silence, then nothing. Just workers standing in the corridor, waiting to be told what comes next.]</p> <p>[A foreman appears. His face is tight. His tablet is out.]</p> <p>Foreman: [loud enough for everyone] Quota remains unchanged under AMBER unless management approves an adjustment. Management has not approved an adjustment. Get back to your stations.</p> <p>[No one argues. Arguing costs ticks.]</p> <p>[Avyanna is moved to assist. Her hands are steady. She helps carry a woman whose arm was caught when a safety gate didn&#x27;t close fast enough. The woman is silent, her face gray, her blood too bright against the processing floor.]</p> <p>(Her tag says 331. She was on the line when I started. Five years ago.)</p> <p>(Medics will log it as recoverable. Recoverable means back to work or debt transfer. If she can&#x27;t work, her balance goes to next of kin. If there&#x27;s no kin-)</p> <p>(That&#x27;s what people say happens. &quot;Biological reclamation.&quot; No one&#x27;s ever come back to confirm it.)</p> <p>[The medics arrive. They&#x27;re efficient. The woman is taken. The blood is cleaned. The evacuation ends.]</p> <p>[Back to work. Back to the line. The grinders start again, their rhythm still wrong, still hesitant.]</p> <hr> <p>[And then she sees them.]</p> <p>[The crew. Moving through the facility like they belong here. Like the chaos is something they expected.]</p> <p>[Nyx is at the center of it-the pale one, the one who pointed toward 7-Gamma. They&#x27;re standing near the seized grinders now, eyes closed, hands moving in small patterns that shouldn&#x27;t mean anything.]</p> <p>[But the grinders respond. The hesitation in the rhythm... smooths. Not fixed, exactly. Calmed. Like a wound that stops bleeding but hasn&#x27;t healed.]</p> <p>(What are they doing?)</p> <p>[The presence behind Avyanna&#x27;s eyes pulses. Recognition. Interest.]</p> <p>[Near Nyx, the cataloging woman is taking notes. Not on the equipment-on the workers. On how they move. On who&#x27;s injured. On who&#x27;s pretending not to be.]</p> <p>[And near the administrative junction, the smooth one is talking to a supervisor. Her voice is friendly. Her tablet is out. The supervisor&#x27;s hands are shaking.]</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna is assigned to flood salvage-the dormitory wing, where the burst main destroyed sleeping quarters. The company wants workers visible but not productive, away from the areas where the visitors might ask uncomfortable questions.]</p> <p>[She hauls sodden mattresses to the disposal chute. Personal items float in the ankle-deep water: a child&#x27;s drawing, a letter that&#x27;s now pulp, a prayer card to a saint she doesn&#x27;t recognize. She saves nothing. Saving is theft.]</p> <p>[Her eyes track everything.]</p> <p>[Near the equipment bay, she sees Elia talking to the broad one-the equipment specialist with the callused hands. Their voices are too low to hear, but their body language is clear: something is wrong, and they&#x27;re not surprised by it.]</p> <p>[Elia&#x27;s gaze sweeps the corridor. Passes over Avyanna. Pauses.]</p> <p>(She remembers me.)</p> <p>(From yesterday. From the poster. From when I said-)</p> <p>[The moment stretches. Then Elia nods, once, barely perceptible, and turns back to her conversation.]</p> <p>(What does that mean? What does any of this mean?)</p> <hr> <p>[The failures continue through midday. A section of lighting goes dark for six minutes. A water main bursts in the dormitory wing-no injuries, but sleeping quarters flooded, personal items destroyed. A lift refuses to descend past Level 4.]</p> <p>[The crew moves through all of it. Stabilizing. Observing. Taking notes on things the foremen don&#x27;t want recorded.]</p> <p>[Avyanna watches from her corridors. Her mop moves. Her eyes stay elsewhere.]</p> <p>[Near an access junction, she catches a fragment-Elisira talking to Vesper, voices low but not quite low enough:]</p> <p>Elisira: The lattice is slipping in pulses. Someone drilled into a node.</p> <p>Vesper: A node?</p> <p>Elisira: A thing the company calls &quot;weather&quot; so no one asks why it&#x27;s getting worse.</p> <p>[They move on. Avyanna keeps mopping. But the words stay with her.]</p> <p>(They&#x27;re not repairing the equipment. They&#x27;re *negotiating* with it. Like it&#x27;s telling them something.)</p> <p>[The presence behind her eyes stirs. Agreement. Confirmation.]</p> <p>(You know what they&#x27;re doing. Don&#x27;t you.)</p> <p>[No answer. Just warmth. Just patterns at the edge of her vision-spirals that mean nothing, grids that hold no data. The thing in her head is paying attention, but it&#x27;s not explaining.]</p> <hr> <p>[Near the end of her shift, she passes the equipment bay. Nyx is inside, alone, standing in the center of the room with their eyes closed.]</p> <p>[And Avyanna feels something.]</p> <p>[Not pain. Not the sharp intrusion from when she touched the shard. Something softer. A pressure behind her eyes, like a question being asked in a language she doesn&#x27;t speak.]</p> <p>[Nyx&#x27;s eyes open. They look directly at her.]</p> <p>Nyx: [quiet, curious] You&#x27;re sensitive.</p> <p>[Avyanna freezes. Her mop stops moving. The bulletin said *do not discuss operational concerns with visiting contractors.* If a supervisor sees her talking to them-that&#x27;s a fine. A reassignment. A disappearance.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [flat, automatic] I&#x27;m just tired.</p> <p>Nyx: [a flicker of something-recognition, maybe] That&#x27;s what sensitive people say when they&#x27;re scared.</p> <p>[They step closer. Not threatening. Curious.]</p> <p>Nyx: [still quiet] The shard left marks. I can see them. [beat] They&#x27;re old for something so recent.</p> <p>(They know. They can tell. The thing in my head-)</p> <p>[Nyx tilts their head. Studies her like she&#x27;s a puzzle that needs solving.]</p> <p>Nyx: It&#x27;s not hostile. Whatever found you. Not yet.</p> <p>[That word again. *Yet.*]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat is dry. Her hands are shaking. She should run. Should report to the supervisor. Should-]</p> <p>Nyx: [softer now] You don&#x27;t have to answer. You don&#x27;t have to trust me. But if you need help-if the marks start hurting-find us. The Lumen Thief. Docking Bay 3. [beat] Don&#x27;t come direct. Move like you&#x27;re heading to the wash line.</p> <p>[They turn back to the equipment. Close their eyes. Return to whatever they were doing before.]</p> <p>[Avyanna stands in the doorway, her mop gripped tight, her heart pounding, the presence behind her eyes pulsing with a feeling she hasn&#x27;t budgeted for.]</p> <p>(They know what I am.)</p> <p>(They offered help.)</p> <p>(They know how to hide the approach. Like they&#x27;ve done this before. Like they&#x27;ve helped people like me before.)</p> <p>(But not going means bleeding in the dark until I check out.)</p> <hr> <p>[She finishes her shift. Returns to her bunk. Sits in the dark while the night cycle hums around her.]</p> <p>[The stone in her lockbox is warm. The presence behind her eyes is restless. Too many signals, all at once, all meaning something she can&#x27;t read.]</p> <p>(Nyx saw the marks. Said they were old for something so recent.)</p> <p>(What does that mean?)</p> <p>[She closes her eyes. The patterns are there-spirals and grids and things that might be numbers. The ledger-voice of the presence, taking inventory of everything that happened today.]</p> <p>***contact-noted***</p> <p>***assessment-valid***</p> <p>***trust-parameters: undefined***</p> <p>(The crew can feel what&#x27;s wrong. They can fix the equipment. They can see the shard in me.)</p> <p>(And they offered help.)</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t know what to do with that. Doesn&#x27;t know if help is possible, or if accepting it would make everything worse. The math of survival doesn&#x27;t include &quot;help.&quot; The math of survival is alone, always alone, trust no one, need nothing.]</p> <p>(But the math hasn&#x27;t been working. The math says I die here. Old and empty, still counting ticks.)</p> <p>(Maybe the math is wrong.)</p> <hr> <p>[Sleep comes eventually. The dreams are strange-not the usual spirals, not the ledger-voice counting her down. Something else.]</p> <p>[She dreams of the equipment bay. Of Nyx&#x27;s hands moving in patterns. Of the grinders responding, calming, accepting repair.]</p> <p>[She dreams of the shard in her lockbox, glowing in the dark. And in the dream, something stirs inside it-something that&#x27;s been waiting, watching, calculating.]</p> <p>[Something that recognized the crew before she did.]</p> <p>***compatibility-confirmed***</p> <p>***extraction-timeline: optimal***</p> <p>***variance-window: open***</p> <p>***catalyst: approaching***</p> <p>[She wakes to the siren. Another shift. Another day.]</p> <p>[But something has changed. She can feel it in the way the presence pulses behind her eyes. In the way the stone feels against her palm when she checks the lockbox. In the way the air itself seems to hum with anticipation.]</p> <p>(The crew is here. They know something is wrong. They know something is in me.)</p> <p>(And they offered help.)</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t know what happens next.]</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t know if she&#x27;ll survive it.]</p> <p>[But for the first time since the shard, she&#x27;s not alone in knowing.]</p> <hr> <p>## Elisira&#x27;s Echo</p> <p>[*Intercut: Later that night. The Lumen Thief.*]</p> <hr> <p>[Elisira sits in her quarters, reviewing footage. Security feeds scroll past in organized grids. Workers. Thousands of them. Numbers on tags instead of names.]</p> <p>[She pauses on a frame. Worker 477. The one who told Elia she moved wrong.]</p> <p>[There&#x27;s something in the girl&#x27;s posture. The way she holds herself small, invisible, ready to disappear if anyone looks too close.]</p> <p>(That was me. Before Elia&#x27;s family pulled me out of the wreckage.)</p> <p>[Memory surfaces: slavers, a cargo container, three days without food. And then a twelve-year-old girl sitting at the edge of her hiding place, already carrying herself like something dangerous.]</p> <p>[Elia&#x27;s words, unchanged after eighteen years: &quot;We don&#x27;t take prisoners and we don&#x27;t make promises. But we don&#x27;t leave people behind either.&quot;]</p> <p>(That was when I decided. Wherever she goes, I go.)</p> <hr> <p>[Now she&#x27;s watching again. Different facility. Same extraction. Same parasitism dressed as employment.]</p> <p>[Her tablet shows evidence growing by the hour. Injury records that don&#x27;t match. Debt math designed to be inescapable. Quota systems that accelerate faster than humans can sustain.]</p> <p>[Bubbles pings her.]</p> <p>Bubbles: Found something in their internal policy index. AMBER weather designation-it&#x27;s not meteorological.</p> <p>Elisira: What is it?</p> <p>Bubbles: Classified lattice instability warning. They&#x27;ve been logging &quot;AMBER days&quot; for two years. Frequency increasing. 12% month over month. Compounding. Like debt.</p> <p>[Elisira stares at the data. The company built two systems with the same math: one to trap workers, one to ignore warnings. Both accelerating toward the same cliff.]</p> <p>Bubbles: Also: Worker 477. The signature Nyx flagged is old. Older than her shard contact.</p> <p>Elisira: Meaning?</p> <p>Bubbles: Someone marked her before. Or something did.</p> <hr> <p>[A knock. Elia enters.]</p> <p>Elia: Findings?</p> <p>Elisira: The math is rigged. Debt never decreases. Workers die on schedule. [beat] And their &quot;weather&quot; is actually a lattice warning they&#x27;ve been ignoring for two years.</p> <p>Elia: [quiet] That tracks with what Nyx felt. Something down there. Old. Patient.</p> <p>Elisira: The girl. 477. Bubbles says she was marked before the shard.</p> <p>Elia: [long pause] Not yet dangerous. But something in her recognized something in us.</p> <p>[Elisira turns back to her tablet. Worker 477 frozen on screen. A girl pretending to be invisible.]</p> <p>Elisira: We won&#x27;t leave them behind.</p> <p>Elia: [standing] No. We won&#x27;t.</p> <p>[She leaves. Elisira returns to her evidence. Tomorrow, they&#x27;ll dig deeper.]</p> <p>[And somewhere in the facility, Worker 477 is sleeping, dreaming of patterns she doesn&#x27;t understand, carrying something that&#x27;s been waiting for a very long time.]</p> <hr> <p>&gt; **AURUM EXTRACTION LTD. - AMBER PROTOCOL ADDENDUM** &gt; *Effective immediately* &gt; AMBER conditions may require workforce stabilization measures. Curfew enforcement authorized during night cycle. Unauthorized movement between dormitory and work areas will be penalized. &gt; *Reminder:* Compliance is a choice. Choose wisely.</p> <hr> <p>*End Chapter 5*</p> </div> Where does the route go next? * [[Continue to Chapter 06: Minebreak->Act1 06 Minebreak]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Chapter 06: Minebreak</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 06_minebreak.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **AURUM EXTRACTION LTD. - INCIDENT REPORT (PRELIMINARY)** &gt; *Site:* K-9 (&quot;The Kennel&quot;) &gt; *Date:* [CURRENT] &gt; *Incident Classification:* Structural Failure / Equipment Malfunction &gt; *Affected Sector:* 7-Gamma (Lower Works) &gt; *Worker Status:* Assessment pending &gt; *Corporate Liability:* Under review &gt; *Note:* All documentation subject to legal hold. Do not distribute.</p> <hr> <p>[The morning starts like every other morning.]</p> <p>[Siren. Count. Assignment. The rhythm of the Kennel, mechanical and inevitable. Avyanna moves through it the way slurry moves through pipes-present but not participating, functional but not alive.]</p> <p>[The curfew held last night. She stayed in her bunk. Didn&#x27;t go to Docking Bay 3. Didn&#x27;t move like she was heading to the wash line. Just lay in the dark and felt the presence behind her eyes pulse with something that pressed against her skull like a headache that wasn&#x27;t hers.]</p> <p>(Today. Maybe today.)</p> <p>(Or maybe never. Maybe the math is right after all.)</p> <p>[Her assignment: Corridor 7-G. Near the Lower Works access. Near the deep cuts.]</p> <p>[Near where she found the shard.]</p> <hr> <p>[The work is routine. Floors don&#x27;t clean themselves. Dust accumulates. Grime builds. The Kennel sweats metal particulate and recycled air and the particular residue of bodies pushed past quota.]</p> <p>[She moves through the corridor, mop tracing circles. Other workers pass-heads down, tags visible, faces slack. A woman swallows dust and doesn&#x27;t cough. Coughing costs time. Time costs ticks.]</p> <p>[The presence behind her eyes is quiet today. Watchful. Like holding breath before a blow.]</p> <p>(Something&#x27;s different.)</p> <p>[She can feel it in the floor. A vibration that doesn&#x27;t match the grinder rhythm. A pulse that comes from deeper than the machines should reach.]</p> <p>[Near the 7-Gamma access point, a foreman is arguing with two of the crew-the broad one with the callused hands, and Elisira. Their voices are tense. The foreman&#x27;s tablet is out, showing something that makes his face go the color of slag.]</p> <p>Foreman: You can&#x27;t go down there. It&#x27;s a restricted zone.</p> <p>Elisira: [flat] Your restricted zone is venting coherence. We can feel it from the upper levels.</p> <p>Broad One: [patient, the kind of patient that comes before violence] Either we stabilize it now, or you evacuate the sector. Your choice.</p> <p>Foreman: [desperate] I don&#x27;t have the authority to-</p> <p>[The floor shudders.]</p> <p>[Everyone freezes.]</p> <p>[And then the world breaks.]</p> <hr> <p>[The explosion comes from below. Not fire-something worse. A sound like metal screaming through a frequency that shouldn&#x27;t exist. Like the grinders seizing, but everywhere, all at once.]</p> <p>[The floor buckles. Walls crack. The lights die, then flicker back in emergency red.]</p> <p>[Avyanna is thrown. Her mop goes flying. She hits the corridor wall, slides down, tastes blood. Dust fills everything-choking, blinding, the taste of pulverized rock and something chemical underneath.]</p> <p>[Screaming. Somewhere. Everywhere. The particular pitch of people who&#x27;ve just realized their tags won&#x27;t save them.]</p> <p>[She can&#x27;t see. Can barely breathe. The red emergency lights pulse like a heartbeat that&#x27;s forgotten how to keep time.]</p> <p>(Get up. Get up. Get up.)</p> <p>[She crawls. Debris under her hands-chunks of ceiling, twisted ductwork, something soft that might be a person. She doesn&#x27;t stop to check. Can&#x27;t afford to check. The corridor is collapsing in sections, supports groaning, the structure deciding whether to hold or fold.]</p> <p>[The presence behind her eyes surges. Hot. Wrong. Her teeth hum. Copper floods her mouth even though she&#x27;s not bleeding there.]</p> <p>[Geometric shapes flicker at the edge of her vision-grids that aren&#x27;t real, patterns that press against the inside of her skull.]</p> <p>(What are you doing? What&#x27;s happening?)</p> <p>[No answer. Just heat. Just pressure. Just the sense of something vast stirring in its sleep, reaching for the edges of whatever&#x27;s breaking.]</p> <hr> <p>[She reaches a junction. Other workers are here-clustered, coughing, bleeding. Some are trying to move debris. Others are frozen, shock turning them into furniture.]</p> <p>[The corridor behind her collapses completely. A sound like a grinder eating itself, and then dust, and then silence except for the groaning of metal that hasn&#x27;t given up yet.]</p> <p>Worker: [voice cracking] We&#x27;re trapped. The main corridor&#x27;s gone.</p> <p>Another Worker: [bleeding from the head, the blood mixing with dust into paste] Security sealed us in. Said they&#x27;re &quot;containing the damage.&quot;</p> <p>(Containing. That&#x27;s what they call it when they decide the math doesn&#x27;t work.)</p> <p>[The presence behind her eyes pulses. Heat spreading behind her temples.]</p> <p>[Avyanna looks around. Twelve workers. Maybe thirteen-hard to count in the red light, with the dust and the blood and the fear. Some she recognizes by tag number. Most she doesn&#x27;t. Just bodies. Just assets. Just people who are about to become write-offs because someone drilled into something they shouldn&#x27;t have touched.]</p> <p>Worker 331: [the woman from two days ago, her arm in a makeshift sling from the safety gate] There&#x27;s a maintenance shaft. I know where-</p> <p>[The ceiling groans. A support beam cracks. Dust rains down thick enough to choke on.]</p> <p>[And then-]</p> <p>[Light. Not the red emergency pulse. Something brighter. Steadier. Coming from the collapsed corridor behind them.]</p> <hr> <p>[The crew emerges from the dust.]</p> <p>[Nyx is first. Their hands are raised, fingers splayed, and something is *wrong* with the air around them-it ripples, shimmers, holds. The collapsing structure above isn&#x27;t falling. It&#x27;s suspended. Waiting.]</p> <p>[But there&#x27;s a cost. Nyx&#x27;s face is pale, sheened with sweat. Their hands tremble. A thin line of blood traces from their nose to their upper lip, and they don&#x27;t wipe it away. Can&#x27;t spare the focus.]</p> <p>[Behind them: Elia. A blade in one hand, but she&#x27;s not fighting-she&#x27;s cutting. Precise. Surgical. Debris that blocks the path falls away in sections that shouldn&#x27;t be possible.]</p> <p>[The broad one-Rho, she remembers-his weapon is up, covering the rear. Not aimed at the collapse. Aimed at the corridor behind them, where security should be. Where security isn&#x27;t coming.]</p> <p>[And Elisira, her tablet out, but she&#x27;s not recording-she&#x27;s *doing* something to the emergency panels, the cameras, the surveillance network. Making the company blind to its own crime.]</p> <p>[Moving through the group: a man she hasn&#x27;t seen before. Tall. Calm. Reading the structure like it&#x27;s a language.]</p> <p>Jalen: Left junction holds for another ninety seconds. Right is gone. The maintenance shaft is-[they pause, head tilted, listening to something no one else can hear]-compromised but passable. Move now.</p> <p>Elia: Then we move now. [to the workers] Everyone who can walk, follow him. Everyone who can&#x27;t, we carry.</p> <p>[The workers stare. This isn&#x27;t rescue protocol. Rescue is negotiated. Rescue is documented. Rescue is something that happens to workers whose output-to-debt ratio justifies the recovery expense.]</p> <p>Elia: [sharper] I said move.</p> <p>[They move.]</p> <hr> <p>[The maintenance shaft is dark, narrow, the air thick with the smell of something burning-insulation, maybe, or wiring, or whatever keeps the deep cuts sealed from the upper levels.]</p> <p>[Workers crawl single-file. Avyanna is near the middle, her palms raw on the metal grating, her lungs screaming for air that doesn&#x27;t taste like the inside of a furnace.]</p> <p>[The presence behind her eyes is fully awake now. Not patterns-pressure. A weight behind her forehead like someone&#x27;s pressing their thumbs into the back of her skull.]</p> <p>[Heat builds in her chest. In her hands. In the spaces between her ribs where the shard residue has settled into her marrow.]</p> <p>(What do you need? What are you trying to-)</p> <p>[And then something *happens*.]</p> <p>[Not words. Not thoughts. A surge of... she doesn&#x27;t have a word for it. The shaft stops groaning. The metal under her hands goes from trembling to still. Around her, the structure steadies-not fixed, but *held*. Locked in place by something that isn&#x27;t physics.]</p> <p>[Her nose starts bleeding. She tastes copper, thick and warm, running over her lip and dripping onto the grating.]</p> <p>[Ahead, Nyx&#x27;s head snaps around. Their eyes find hers in the darkness, wide with recognition. Wide with something else-alarm, maybe, or calculation.]</p> <p>Nyx: [voice tight, strained] Someone&#x27;s resonating. Someone in here is anchoring the structure. [to Elia, urgent] We need to move faster. The anchor won&#x27;t hold-they don&#x27;t know what they&#x27;re doing.</p> <p>Elia: [from somewhere ahead] Then we move faster.</p> <p>[The crawl becomes desperate. Workers hauling workers. The crew at the edges, Nyx&#x27;s hands still raised, still bleeding, reality bending around their will but cracking at the seams.]</p> <p>[And Avyanna in the middle, burning from the inside, the presence behind her eyes pouring itself into the walls around her through channels she didn&#x27;t know she had.]</p> <hr> <p>[They emerge into a service corridor. Intact. Lit with actual lights, not emergency red. Air that doesn&#x27;t taste like death.]</p> <p>[Workers collapse against walls. Some are crying. Some are silent. Some are staring at the darkness behind them like they expect it to follow.]</p> <p>[Avyanna can&#x27;t stand. Her legs have stopped taking orders. She slides down the wall, hands shaking, vision swimming, blood still dripping from her nose onto her work coveralls.]</p> <p>[The presence behind her eyes is quiet again. Pressure released. Still, like a machine cycling down.]</p> <p>[Footsteps. Someone crouching in front of her.]</p> <p>[Elia.]</p> <p>[Her face is covered in dust and someone else&#x27;s blood. Her eyes are dark, steady, looking at Avyanna like she&#x27;s reading a manifest.]</p> <p>Elia: You. [beat, flat, like a diagnosis] I see you.</p> <p>[Avyanna can&#x27;t speak. Her throat is dust and copper. But something in those three words lands like a brand-the first time in five years someone has looked at her and acknowledged that she exists.]</p> <p>Elia: You did something back there. The structure stabilized around you. Nyx felt it.</p> <p>[No response. What can she say? She doesn&#x27;t understand it herself. The presence behind her eyes offers nothing-just stillness, just waiting.]</p> <p>Elia: [quieter, not gentle but not hard either] You&#x27;re the one with the marks. The one Nyx talked to.</p> <p>(They know. They all know now.)</p> <p>Elia: [studying her] You don&#x27;t know what you are. What&#x27;s in you.</p> <p>[Avyanna manages to shake her head. The motion makes her vision swim worse.]</p> <p>[Elia stands. Looks at Nyx, who&#x27;s leaning against the wall, wiping blood from their face with a trembling hand.]</p> <p>Elia: Assessment?</p> <p>Nyx: [hoarse] Old. The signature&#x27;s old. But the activation is new. Something woke up in there when the lattice ruptured. [beat] She&#x27;s not hostile. The thing in her isn&#x27;t hostile. Not to us.</p> <p>Elia: [a pause] Not to us meaning what?</p> <p>Nyx: [meeting her eyes] Meaning it recognized us. Meaning it *helped*.</p> <p>[Silence. The corridor hums with emergency systems and the distant sound of structure still settling. Workers huddle in groups. The crew exchanges looks that hold entire conversations.]</p> <p>[Vesper appears from somewhere-Avyanna didn&#x27;t see her during the extraction, but here she is, tablet out, recording everything. Her face is calm. Professional. The face of someone who&#x27;s already building a case.]</p> <p>Vesper: Chain of custody starts now. Everything they deny, we prove. [to Elia] Corporate security is forty seconds out. They&#x27;re bringing documentation teams.</p> <p>[Rho shifts without being told. One hand places something small on the corridor wall-a charge, maybe, or a sensor. The hallway&#x27;s geometry changes: what was a straight line becomes a funnel. Anyone coming fast will slow down or regret it.]</p> <p>Rho: [flat] Forty seconds is plenty.</p> <p>Elia: [to Avyanna] Can you walk?</p> <p>[Avyanna doesn&#x27;t know. Her legs feel like they belong to someone else. But she tries-pushes herself up the wall, one hand braced against the metal, the presence behind her eyes offering nothing but silence.]</p> <p>[She manages to stand. Barely.]</p> <p>Elia: [not a question] You&#x27;re coming with us.</p> <p>[It&#x27;s not a request. But it&#x27;s not an order either. It&#x27;s something in between-a statement of fact about what happens next, delivered by someone who&#x27;s already decided the math.]</p> <p>[Avyanna should argue. Should ask questions. Should do anything except stand here bleeding and shaking and letting strangers decide her trajectory.]</p> <p>[But the presence behind her eyes pulses once-pressure shifting, something unlocking-and she finds herself nodding.]</p> <p>[Corporate security rounds the corner. Six of them. Armed. The documentation team behind them, already filming.]</p> <p>[Vesper steps forward, tablet raised, smile sharp as broken glass.]</p> <p>Vesper: Perfect timing. [beat] Do you want the footage now, or in arbitration?</p> <p>[The security lead&#x27;s face does something complicated. The question is a trap, and he knows it.]</p> <p>[Elia&#x27;s hand settles on Avyanna&#x27;s shoulder. Light. Grounding. The first time anyone&#x27;s touched her without malice in five years.]</p> <p>Elia: [quiet, just for her] Whatever happens next, you&#x27;re not going back in the hole. That part&#x27;s done.</p> <p>[Avyanna doesn&#x27;t know if she believes it. Doesn&#x27;t know if belief is something she still has access to.]</p> <p>[But she lets herself be led away from the collapsed sector, away from the workers being triaged, away from the life that was killing her one tick at a time.]</p> <p>[The presence behind her eyes settles. Pressure stabilizing. The hum of something that&#x27;s finished doing whatever it was doing.]</p> <hr> <p>*End Chapter 6*</p> </div> Where does the route go next? * [[Continue to Chapter 07: Exit Writ->Act1 07 Exit Writ]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Chapter 07: Exit Writ</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 07_exit_writ.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **AURUM EXTRACTION LTD. - INTERNAL MEMORANDUM (CONFIDENTIAL)** &gt; *Re:* 7-Gamma Structural Event &gt; *Classification:* RESTRICTED - Executive Eyes Only &gt; *Summary:* Equipment malfunction resulted in localized structural instability. Cause under investigation. Worker casualties minimal. External contractor presence complicated containment. Writbound liaison dispatched. &gt; *Action Required:* Narrative alignment across all documentation. See attached talking points. &gt; *Reminder:* This incident did not occur as described in preliminary reports. It occurred as described in final reports.</p> <hr> <p>[The corridor is different now.]</p> <p>[Same metal. Same emergency lighting, though dimmer-the crisis protocols winding down, the facility deciding to pretend everything is normal. But the air tastes wrong. Tastes like lies being assembled.]</p> <p>[Avyanna stands with the crew, her legs still unsteady, her nose finally done bleeding. Corporate security has them in a holding pattern-not detained, exactly, but not free to move either. The documentation team is still filming. Recording everything for later editing.]</p> <p>[Around them, the cover-up is already beginning.]</p> <p>Supervisor: [to a cluster of rescued workers] You&#x27;ll receive compensation for the disruption. Sign here. This confirms you experienced an equipment malfunction and are satisfied with the company&#x27;s response.</p> <p>Worker: [hollow] And if we don&#x27;t sign?</p> <p>Supervisor: [patient, the patience of someone who&#x27;s done this before] Then the investigation takes longer. Your dormitory assignment gets reviewed. Your quota gets recalculated. [beat] It&#x27;s easier if you sign.</p> <p>[The workers sign. One by one. Heads down. Pens moving. The truth becoming whatever the company decides it is.]</p> <p>[Avyanna watches. The presence behind her eyes is quiet-exhausted, maybe, from whatever it did in the collapse. But she can feel it paying attention. Recording. Filing.]</p> <p>(This is how it works. This is how it&#x27;s always worked.)</p> <p>(They&#x27;re going to make the explosion never happen. They&#x27;re going to make those workers never exist.)</p> <p>(They&#x27;re going to make me never exist.)</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper is already working.]</p> <p>[Her tablet is out, but she&#x27;s not recording anymore-she&#x27;s transmitting.
