Learning what information you&#x27;re willing to make public, and what you protect.</p> <p>[She pulls up a sample form-a station customs declaration.]</p> <p>Vesper: This form asks: name, origin, destination, purpose of visit, duration of stay, and &quot;any goods to declare.&quot;</p> <p>[She taps each field.]</p> <p>Vesper: Name: you give them your public identity. Avyanna Lagrange.</p> <p>Vesper: Origin: you say &quot;Lumen Thief, Guild registry 7742-KAEL.&quot; You don&#x27;t elaborate.</p> <p>Vesper: Destination: you tell them the station name. You don&#x27;t tell them who you&#x27;re visiting or why.</p> <p>Vesper: Purpose: you say &quot;transit&quot; or &quot;Guild contract work.&quot; You don&#x27;t give specifics.</p> <p>Vesper: Duration: you estimate conservatively. You don&#x27;t commit to leaving by a date.</p> <p>Vesper: Goods: you declare what&#x27;s required by law. You don&#x27;t volunteer information.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna absorbs this.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [slow] You&#x27;re teaching me to... give the minimum?</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] I&#x27;m teaching you that you&#x27;re not required to tell strangers your life story. Information is leverage. Don&#x27;t hand it out for free.</p> <p>[She pulls up another form-this one from a Compact administrative office.]</p> <p>Vesper: This one asks: &quot;Are you now or have you ever been employed by an extraction facility?&quot;</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s breath catches.]</p> <p>Vesper: What do you say?</p> <p>Avyanna: [shaking] I... was. In the mine.</p> <p>Vesper: [gently] That&#x27;s the truth. But truth has nuance. You were *held* by an extraction facility. You were not employed-you were enslaved. The word &quot;employed&quot; implies consent, compensation, and autonomy. You had none of those.</p> <p>Vesper: So the accurate answer is: &quot;No. I was held against my will by Aurum Extraction. I was never an employee.&quot;</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna stares at the form.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] They&#x27;ll say I&#x27;m lying.</p> <p>Vesper: [flat] Let them. Your answer is defensible. You&#x27;re reframing their language to reflect reality. That&#x27;s not lying-that&#x27;s refusing to adopt their narrative.</p> <p>Elisira: [adding] And if they push, you say: &quot;That&#x27;s not your business. Next question.&quot;</p> <hr> <p>[They practice. Twenty forms. Twenty scenarios. Each one designed to teach Avyanna what information is required, what&#x27;s optional, and what&#x27;s none of anyone&#x27;s damn business.]</p> <p>**Scenario: Station Security** - Form asks: &quot;List all previous residences for the past five years.&quot; - Avyanna: [using the script] &quot;I was held at Aurum Extraction under indenture. I have no other residences to report.&quot; - Vesper: Good. You answered truthfully without giving them ammunition.</p> <p>**Scenario: Medical Intake** - Form asks: &quot;Describe any pre-existing conditions or injuries.&quot; - Avyanna: [using the script] &quot;I&#x27;ll discuss that with my doctor. Not with your intake form.&quot; - Vesper: Correct. Medical history is between you and your provider. Intake forms don&#x27;t get access.</p> <p>**Scenario: Banking** - Form asks: &quot;Source of funds?&quot; - Avyanna: [using the script] &quot;Wages from Guild contract work.&quot; - Vesper: Perfect. Accurate, minimal, unremarkable.</p> <hr> <p>[After an hour, Avyanna is exhausted. But something in her posture has shifted. She&#x27;s learning to hold boundaries in bureaucratic spaces.]</p> <p>Vesper: [standing] Last lesson. The most important one.</p> <p>[She pulls up a form. This one is from a Compact regulatory office.]</p> <p>Vesper: This form asks you to &quot;voluntarily disclose&quot; information about Primordial Lattice interactions. It&#x27;s phrased like a survey. It&#x27;s actually a registry of people the Compact wants to track.</p> <p>[She deletes the form without filling it out.]</p> <p>Vesper: You never fill out &quot;voluntary disclosure&quot; forms. Ever. If disclosure is truly voluntary, you volunteer to decline.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna nods slowly.]</p> <p>Avyanna: What if they say it&#x27;s required?</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] Then you ask: &quot;Required by which law? Under which jurisdiction? Enforced by whom?&quot;</p> <p>[She taps the table.]</p> <p>Vesper: If they can&#x27;t answer all three, it&#x27;s not required. It&#x27;s a request dressed up as a mandate. And you decline.</p> <p>Elisira: [adding] And if they try to coerce you-if they threaten consequences for declining-you document it and you report it. Because that&#x27;s not law. That&#x27;s extortion.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna sits back. Three identities. Boundaries in forms. Scripts for refusal.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] You&#x27;re teaching me to hide.</p> <p>Vesper: [correcting] I&#x27;m teaching you to control what people know about you. There&#x27;s a difference between hiding and exercising informational autonomy.</p> <p>Vesper: You&#x27;re not erasing yourself. You&#x27;re deciding which version of yourself is appropriate for which context.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna processes that.]</p> <p>Avyanna: The mine wanted me to have no privacy. No secrets. No control over my own information.</p> <p>Vesper: [nodding] Yes. Because control over information is power. They wanted all the power. We&#x27;re giving it back to you.</p> <p>[She hands Avyanna a small data chip.]</p> <p>Vesper: These are your credentials. All three identities, encrypted. Keep it on you. Memorize the access codes. If you ever need to use Kess Tam, you&#x27;ll have everything you need.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna takes the chip. It&#x27;s small. Lighter than it should be for the weight it carries.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [looking at Vesper] Have you ever had to use... an egress identity?</p> <p>Vesper: [after a pause] Yes. Twice.</p> <p>Avyanna: Did it work?</p> <p>Vesper: [quietly] I&#x27;m still here.</p> <hr> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// IDENTITY SYSTEMS UPDATED: three registries established | RIM ACCORD: confirmed | GUILD TRANSIT: confirmed | EGRESS IDENTITY: stored encrypted | AUTONOMY: enhanced&gt;</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// OBSERVATION: trainee now has legal protections and exit strategies | ASSESSMENT: survivability increased | EMOTIONAL STATE: overwhelmed but processing | STATUS: this is good work&gt;</p> <hr> <p>*End Scene*</p> </div> * [[Continue to optional scene 11b: Senna Small Good Thing->Act1 11b Senna Small Good Thing]] * [[Continue to Chapter 12: The Doctrine->Act1 12 The Doctrine]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Optional 11b: Senna Small Good Thing</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 11b_senna_small_good_thing.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **[INTERSTITIAL - OCCURS POST-CH11]** &gt; *Trigger: quiet that doesn&#x27;t punish* &gt; *Purpose: Senna Cross&#x27;s first rule, reclaimed as Avyanna&#x27;s*</p> <hr> <p>[Night cycle on the Lumen Thief is not dark.]</p> <p>[It is dimmed to a livable softness.]</p> <p>[Avyanna sits on the edge of her bunk and waits for the moment when the ship remembers to be cruel.]</p> <p>[The moment doesn&#x27;t come.]</p> <p>(This is when they collect the debt.)</p> <p>[She listens instead: ventilation, distant footsteps, the subtle hum of systems doing their jobs without demanding gratitude.]</p> <p>[Her hands fidget with nothing because she doesn&#x27;t own anything yet that feels safe to touch.]</p> <p>[The quiet makes room for a memory she hasn&#x27;t let herself pick up in years.]</p> <hr> <p>## Intercut - AE Site K-9 (Senna&#x27;s Rule)</p> <p>[The Kennel is never quiet.]</p> <p>[Even when the machines stop, the people keep making noise-coughs, sobs swallowed into sleeves, the wet scrape of boots through glitter-dust.]</p> <p>[Avyanna is small enough that the world is mostly legs and walls.]</p> <p>[She knows the rules by the way the air changes when a supervisor walks past.]</p> <p>[She knows how to hold her breath so she doesn&#x27;t taste the metal, because tasting it makes her want to cry and crying makes you visible.]</p> <p>[Senna Cross is visible anyway.]</p> <p>[Not loud.]</p> <p>[Not defiant.]</p> <p>[Just... present, in the way a stone is present in a stream.]</p> <p>[Senna kneels beside a broken ventilation grate and pries out something that shouldn&#x27;t exist down here.]</p> <p>[A leaf.]</p> <p>[Not a whole plant. Not a miracle. A single stubborn leaf, pale but alive, grown from mold and accident and refusal.]</p> <p>[Senna cups it in her palm like she&#x27;s holding contraband.]</p> <p>Senna: [quiet] Look.</p> <p>[Avyanna looks because looking is safer than asking why.]</p> <p>Avyanna: It&#x27;s... green.</p> <p>[The word comes out like she&#x27;s never said it before.]</p> <p>Senna: Yes.</p> <p>Senna: One small good thing.</p> <p>[Avyanna frowns.]</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s not a thing.</p> <p>Senna: It is.</p> <p>Senna: The mine wants you to believe there is nothing good, so you stop looking.</p> <p>Senna: Stop looking, and you stop noticing exits.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Exits don&#x27;t exist.</p> <p>Senna: [gentle, stubborn] Exits always exist.</p> <p>Senna: Sometimes they&#x27;re not doors.</p> <p>Senna: Sometimes they&#x27;re timing.</p> <p>Senna: Sometimes they&#x27;re people.</p> <p>[Senna folds the leaf into a scrap of cloth and presses it into Avyanna&#x27;s palm.]</p> <p>[Avyanna flinches like she&#x27;s being handed a weapon.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I can&#x27;t keep it.</p> <p>Senna: You can.</p> <p>Avyanna: They&#x27;ll take it.</p> <p>Senna: Then you&#x27;ll find another one tomorrow.</p> <p>[Senna&#x27;s eyes meet hers. Dark. Steady.]</p> <p>Senna: Rule.</p> <p>Senna: Every day, you find one small good thing.</p> <p>Senna: You don&#x27;t tell them about it.</p> <p>Senna: You don&#x27;t trade it.</p> <p>Senna: You keep it because it proves you are still a person.</p> <p>[Avyanna stares at the wrapped leaf.]</p> <p>(A person.)</p> <p>[The word hurts like a bruise you didn&#x27;t know you had.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Why are you telling me.</p> <p>Senna: Because you&#x27;re still looking.</p> <p>Senna: That means you&#x27;re not gone yet.</p> <hr> <p>## Return - Ship Quiet</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s hands unclench around air.]</p> <p>[There is no leaf in her palm.]</p> <p>[There is no glitter dust in her teeth.]</p> <p>[There is only the ship, and the quiet, and the ache of having been taught one small rule that the mine couldn&#x27;t invoice.]</p> <p>[She stands and crosses to the little desk bolted to the wall.]</p> <p>[She finds a blank page in a cheap paper notebook Elia tossed at her earlier like it was nothing.]</p> <p>[Avyanna writes:]</p> <p>One small good thing:</p> <p>[She stares at the words until they stop looking like a lie.]</p> <p>[Then she writes:]</p> <p>Quiet that doesn&#x27;t punish.</p> <p>[She closes the notebook.]</p> <p>[The ship hums like it approves of being noticed.]</p> <hr> <p>*End Chapter 11b*</p> </div> * [[Continue to Chapter 12: The Doctrine->Act1 12 The Doctrine]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Chapter 12: The Doctrine</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 12_the_doctrine.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **LUMEN THIEF - CREW CULTURE DOCUMENTATION (INFORMAL):** &gt; *Subject: &quot;Sparkle Doctrine&quot;* &gt; *Source: Observed behavior, crew consensus, Cinnamon.exe commentary* &gt; *Summary: Say what you mean. Mean what you say. Silence is where rot grows.* &gt; *Definition (unofficial): &quot;sparkle&quot; = a point of light in the dark-the moment wrong becomes visible enough to act on.* &gt; *Addendum: If you&#x27;re angry, be angry out loud. We can work with angry. Quiet anger eats ships.*</p> <hr> <p>[Elia doesn&#x27;t give speeches.]</p> <p>[Avyanna has been aboard for three weeks now, and she&#x27;s noticed: the captain doesn&#x27;t lecture. Doesn&#x27;t explain. Doesn&#x27;t hand down philosophy from on high like the foremen in the Kennel handed down quotas.]</p> <p>[Elia teaches through correction. Sharp, immediate, unflinching.]</p> <hr> <p>[Morning. The galley. Avyanna is reviewing inventory logs when Elia drops into the seat across from her.]</p> <p>Elia: You disagreed with the supply allocation yesterday.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s spine locks. How did she-]</p> <p>Avyanna: I didn&#x27;t say anything.</p> <p>Elia: [flat] I know. That&#x27;s the problem.</p> <p>[A beat. Avyanna waits for the punishment. The Kennel taught her: disagreement with supervisors meant consequences.]</p> <p>Elia: [leaning forward, tapping the log once] Say it now. Not later. Not never. Now.</p> <p>Avyanna: [careful] What if I&#x27;m wrong?</p> <p>Elia: Then we&#x27;ll tell you. And you&#x27;ll learn something. [beat] Being wrong out loud is fixable. Rot isn&#x27;t.</p> <p>Avyanna: [confused] Ships die from... silence?</p> <p>Elia: [very serious] The silence is where rot grows.</p> <p>[Elia rotates the tablet so Avyanna has to look at the numbers again.]</p> <p>Elia: So. What did you see?</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens. The Kennel taught her: speaking meant attention. Attention meant extraction.]</p> <p>Elia: [reading her face] You&#x27;re thinking about where you came from. How silence kept you alive.</p> <p>Avyanna: [small] Yes.</p> <p>Elia: [nodding] That was survival. You were right to stay quiet there. [beat] Here, you&#x27;re wrong to stay quiet.</p> <p>[Avyanna swallows. Forces her voice to work.]</p> <p>Avyanna: The allocation assumes a clean resupply window.</p> <p>[Elia doesn&#x27;t interrupt.]</p> <p>Avyanna: If we miss it, the buffer gets thin. We start improvising with food and meds and sleep.</p> <p>Elia: [a single nod, immediate] Good catch. We&#x27;ll pad it.</p> <p>[She makes the change with quick taps. No resentment. No ego. Just correction.]</p> <p>Avyanna: How do I know which rules apply?</p> <p>Elia: [a faint smile] You ask. Out loud. Like you just did.</p> <hr> <p>[Later. The common area. Elia and Elisira are arguing.]</p> <p>[Not arguing. *Fighting*. Voices sharp, words precise, every sentence a blade.]</p> <p>Elisira: That was reckless and you know it.</p> <p>Elia: It worked.</p> <p>Elisira: Working isn&#x27;t the same as right. You took a risk that could have gotten us all killed.</p> <p>Elia: [dismissive] The risk was calculated.</p> <p>Elisira: [cold] Your calculations assumed we&#x27;d get lucky. We did. Next time we might not.</p> <p>Elia: [harder] There wasn&#x27;t a next time option. It was that route or no route.</p> <p>Elisira: [harder still] Then you explain that. Before. You don&#x27;t unilaterally decide to gamble with everyone&#x27;s lives and then claim it was tactical.</p> <p>[Avyanna watches from the edge of the room, frozen. In the Kennel, this kind of conflict ended in violence. Punishment. Someone being made an example.]</p> <p>[Rho appears beside her, coffee in hand, completely unbothered.]</p> <p>Rho: [low, conversational] They do this about twice a week.</p> <p>Avyanna: [barely breathing] They&#x27;re-</p> <p>Rho: Arguing. Yes. Loudly. [beat] They&#x27;ll be fine by dinner.</p> <p>[Avyanna stares at him. How can he be calm? How can anyone be calm when two people that dangerous are at each other&#x27;s throats?]</p> <p>[Elia&#x27;s voice cuts through:]</p> <p>Elia: Fine. You&#x27;re right. I should have explained the math before I made the call. I was impatient.</p> <p>[A pause. Elisira exhales.]</p> <p>Elisira: And I should have pushed harder in the moment instead of letting it fester. We&#x27;re both wrong.</p> <p>Elia: [extending a hand] Agreed.</p> <p>[Elisira takes it. Brief. Formal. Done.]</p> <p>[Elisira&#x27;s jaw stays tight anyway. Her fingers hold Elia&#x27;s hand one beat too long, then let go like it&#x27;s a decision.]</p> <p>[Elia&#x27;s grin flashes too bright, then she exhales through her nose and looks away.]</p> <p>Rho: [into his coffee, almost inaudible] Progress.</p> <p>[Then Elia glances at Avyanna-catches her staring-and her expression softens into something almost amused.]</p> <p>Elia: Lesson learned?</p> <p>Avyanna: [struggling to process] You just... argued. And then stopped. And you&#x27;re not-</p> <p>Elia: Angry? Hurt? Plotting revenge?</p> <p>[Avyanna doesn&#x27;t answer, but her face says yes.]</p> <p>Elia: [walking over] The anger was real. The disagreement was real. But we said it out loud, dealt with it, and now it&#x27;s done. [beat] If we&#x27;d stayed quiet, we&#x27;d still be angry. For days. Weeks. Until it poisoned something else.</p> <p>Elisira: [joining them] The Doctrine isn&#x27;t about not having feelings. It&#x27;s about not letting feelings become rot.</p> <p>Elia: Quiet anger eats ships. We don&#x27;t feed it. [beat] We said what needed saying. Now we eat dinner.</p> <hr> <p>[Dinner. The galley is loud again-Jalen complaining about a nav calculation, Nyx explaining something incomprehensible, Rho making a point about protein storage that somehow involves explosives.]</p> <p>[Elia and Elisira sit next to each other. Easy. Close.]</p> <p>[Elisira&#x27;s knee bounces under the table for three beats before she forces it still. Elia laughs a little too bright at something Jalen says, then reins it in like she&#x27;s putting a blade away.]</p> <p>[Avyanna watches them, cataloging.]</p> <p>(They fought. Really fought. And now they&#x27;re... fine.)</p> <p>[The presence behind her eyes pulses-something that might be curiosity, or recognition.]</p> <p>(Is that what trust looks like? Being able to fight and still stay?)</p> <hr> <p>[After dinner. Vesper finds Avyanna in the observation deck, staring at stars.]</p> <p>Vesper: You have an opinion.</p> <p>[Avyanna starts. She didn&#x27;t hear Vesper approach.]</p> <p>Avyanna: What?</p> <p>Vesper: [sitting down beside her] About the contract Elia was discussing. The route through Sovereign space. You think it&#x27;s a mistake.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens. She did think that. She noticed the risk numbers, ran them in her head, concluded the margins were too thin. But she didn&#x27;t say anything.]</p> <p>Vesper: [patient] I saw you do the math. You&#x27;re good at math.</p> <p>Avyanna: [defensive] I don&#x27;t know enough about-</p> <p>Vesper: [cutting her off] Try again. Out loud this time.</p> <p>[A long pause. The stars turn slowly beyond the viewport.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [very quiet] The fuel margins are too thin. If anything goes wrong-weather, delays, unexpected costs-we&#x27;re stranded.</p> <p>[Vesper nods. Doesn&#x27;t interrupt.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [gaining confidence] And the route passes through two jurisdictions that have reciprocal reporting. If one flags us, both do. The risk compounds.</p> <p>[Another pause.]</p> <p>Vesper: [mild] Those are good points.</p> <p>Avyanna: [surprised] They are?</p> <p>Vesper: They&#x27;re exactly the points I was going to raise at the planning meeting tomorrow. [beat] You saw them too. You just didn&#x27;t say.</p> <p>[Avyanna looks at her hands. The old shame-the Kennel shame of being noticed, being visible, being worth targeting.]</p> <p>Vesper: [gentler] That&#x27;s how we work. You see a problem, you say it. Even if you&#x27;re new. Even if you&#x27;re scared. Even if you think someone else already noticed.</p> <p>Avyanna: What if I&#x27;m wrong?</p> <p>Vesper: [shrugging] Then we explain why. And you learn. [beat] Being wrong out loud is better than being right in silence. Silence doesn&#x27;t help anyone.</p> <p>[Avyanna processes this. The logic is backwards from everything the Kennel taught her. Wrong is vulnerability. Vulnerability is death.]</p> <p>[But she&#x27;s not in the Kennel anymore.]</p> <p>Vesper: [standing, offering a hand] Come to the planning meeting tomorrow. Bring your math.</p> <p>[Avyanna takes the hand. Stands.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [small] Okay.</p> <p>Vesper: [a real smile] Good. That&#x27;s how we work.</p> <hr> <p>[Next day. Planning meeting. The galley again.]</p> <p>[Avyanna sits with her math on the table like it&#x27;s contraband.]</p> <p>[Vesper sits beside her like a witness.]</p> <p>Vesper: [to Elia] She saw the margins.</p> <p>[Elia looks at Avyanna. Not patient. Not indulgent. Expectant.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet, forcing it out] The fuel margins are too thin. If anything goes wrong, we&#x27;re stranded.</p> <p>Jalen: [from the counter, without looking up] She&#x27;s right.</p> <p>Elisira: [already scrolling] And the jurisdiction overlap she flagged? Real. Reciprocal reporting.</p> <p>[Elia exhales once. Decision made.]</p> <p>Elia: Fine. We take the longer route. Floors, not thrones.</p> <p>[Avyanna waits for the punishment that never comes.]</p> <p>Vesper: [to her, low] See? Still alive.</p> <hr> <p>[Later. A comm call. Elia negotiating with a contact-a potential cargo client.]</p> <p>[Avyanna is doing inventory nearby. Close enough to hear.]</p> <p>Contact: [through the speaker, businesslike] Your AIs can run the manifest verification. They follow orders, right?</p> <p>[Silence. The air feels colder.]</p> <p>Elia: [very calm, very cold] Say that again.</p> <p>Contact: [oblivious] I&#x27;m just saying, if your AIs handle the verification, we can-</p> <p>Elia: [cutting him off] They don&#x27;t follow orders. They make choices.</p> <p>Contact: [confused] They&#x27;re programs. They-</p> <p>Elia: They&#x27;re crew. Full crew. Equal crew. They argue with me, they disagree with me, and they have the right to refuse any task they find objectionable.</p> <p>Contact: [sputtering] That&#x27;s not how AIs-</p> <p>Elia: That&#x27;s exactly how AIs work on this ship. [beat] If you can&#x27;t accept that, we don&#x27;t work together. Find someone else.</p> <p>[A long pause.]</p> <p>Contact: [backing down] I didn&#x27;t mean to-</p> <p>Elia: [flat] Yes you did. You just didn&#x27;t expect pushback. [beat] Are we clear?</p> <p>Contact: [subdued] We&#x27;re clear.</p> <p>[The call ends. Elia turns and catches Avyanna watching.]</p> <p>Elia: [matter-of-fact] Questions?</p> <p>Avyanna: [slowly] You almost lost that contract.</p> <p>Elia: Yes.</p> <p>Avyanna: Over... what he said about the AIs.</p> <p>Elia: Yes.</p> <p>Avyanna: [struggling to understand] They&#x27;re worth more than the contract?</p> <p>Elia: [very simple] They&#x27;re not &quot;worth more.&quot; They&#x27;re not on a ledger. They&#x27;re people. You don&#x27;t trade people for cargo deals.</p> <p>[Avyanna thinks about the Kennel. About being on a ledger. About her value being calculated and extracted.]</p> <p>Elia: [softer] I know that&#x27;s hard to understand. Where you came from, everything was on a ledger. Everyone had a price.</p> <p>Avyanna: [nodding]</p> <p>Elia: Here, some things aren&#x27;t for sale. The AIs&#x27; personhood isn&#x27;t for sale. Your safety isn&#x27;t for sale. The crew&#x27;s trust in each other isn&#x27;t for sale. [beat] Some principles cost contracts. We pay that price.</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// CONTRACT-STATUS: withdrawn | VALUE: 12,000 credits | PRINCIPLE VALUE: incalculable | INFERENCE: correct trade | CONFIDENCE: 0.92&gt;</p> <hr> <p>[Night. Avyanna lies in her bunk, thinking.]</p> <p>(Say it out loud.)</p> <p>[The doctrine is simple. Deceptively simple. But implementing it requires something she doesn&#x27;t have: trust that honesty won&#x27;t be weaponized against her.]</p> <p>(They&#x27;re teaching me to speak. Not just words-real speech. The kind that risks something.)</p> <p>[The presence behind her eyes stirs. Something that might be approval.]</p> <p>(You like this. You like that I&#x27;m learning to be louder.)</p> <p>[Heat climbs at the base of her skull. The geometric patterns flicker briefly-brighter than usual, more insistent.]</p> <p>(Are you waiting for me to speak to you too? Is that what this is?)</p> <p>[No answer. But the heat holds, steady, and Avyanna thinks maybe she&#x27;s getting closer to something important.]</p> <hr> <p>## CODA - Jalen&#x27;s Flight</p> <p>[The cockpit. 0300 hours. Stars streaming past the viewport like rain on a window.]</p> <p>[Jalen flies.]</p> <p>[Not because they need to-the ship could autopilot through this stretch of empty space. But flying is how Jalen thinks. Flying is how Jalen stays sane.]</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// PILOT-STATUS: Jalen | REASON FOR MANUAL: &quot;processing&quot; | OBSERVATION: they do this when the new ones arrive&gt;</p> <hr> <p>[Jalen was born on a rim station called Kettering&#x27;s Rest. A waypoint colony, nothing special-except that it was home.]</p> <p>[When the route collapsed, Kettering&#x27;s Rest was cut off. The transit lanes that connected it to the rest of civilization just... stopped working. Lattice failure, they said later. Static zone expansion. Technical language for &quot;your world is gone and no one&#x27;s coming to help.&quot;]</p> <p>[Jalen got out. They were offworld when it happened-a supply run, routine, boring. They watched from the cockpit of a cargo shuttle as the route sealed behind them.]</p> <p>[Their family didn&#x27;t get out.]</p> <p>[Parents. Siblings. The whole network of cousins and aunts and childhood friends who made Kettering&#x27;s Rest mean something. All of them trapped on the wrong side of a collapsed lane, slowly starving as supplies dwindled and no help came.]</p> <p>(I&#x27;ve never been back. The route reopened three years later-before Elia, before the Lumen Thief. By then, there was no one left to visit.)</p> <hr> <p>[Jalen has been running ever since.]</p> <p>[Not literally-they stopped running and started *flying*. Better to be the one who controls the escape route. Better to never be trapped on the wrong side again.]</p> <p>[They flew for cargo companies, for smugglers, for anyone who paid. Never stayed long. Never got attached. Attachment meant loss, and loss meant Kettering&#x27;s Rest all over again.]</p> <p>[Then Elia Lagrange showed up.]</p> <hr> <p>[It was a job. Simple extraction, tight timeline, the kind of flight that separated good pilots from dead ones. Jalen flew it perfectly-threading through debris fields, outrunning pursuit, landing the crew safely when every calculation said they should have died.]</p> <p>[Afterward, Elia found them in the hangar. Just stood there, watching, while Jalen checked systems they&#x27;d already checked twice.]</p> <p>Elia: [simple] You coming?</p> <p>[No interview. No negotiation. No explanation of terms or benefits or expectations. Just a question shaped like an invitation.]</p> <p>Jalen: [suspicious] Coming where?</p> <p>Elia: [shrugging] Wherever. We go a lot of places. We need a pilot who doesn&#x27;t die.</p> <p>Jalen: [flat] I don&#x27;t do attachments.</p> <p>Elia: [a faint smile] Neither do we. We just do loyalty. Different thing.</p> <p>[Jalen had said no. Of course they&#x27;d said no. Attachment was death, and loyalty was just attachment with a fancier name.]</p> <p>[A month later, they said yes.]</p> <hr> <p>[Now. The cockpit. Jalen adjusts a thruster alignment that was already perfect.]</p> <p>(Elia made me responsible for people again. Not by ordering-by trusting.)</p> <p>[That trust is violent when you&#x27;re used to being disposable. When your whole survival strategy is built around never mattering enough to anyone that losing you would hurt them.]</p> <p>(She trusts me to keep them alive. All of them. That trust weighs more than any debt I&#x27;ve ever carried.)</p> <hr> <p>[They&#x27;ve been watching Avyanna adjust.]</p> <p>[The way she flinches at loud noises. The way she calculates the cost of every meal. The way she&#x27;s slowly, painfully learning that kindness here isn&#x27;t a trap.]</p> <p>(I remember that. The adjustment period. When you can&#x27;t believe the floor is solid because every floor you&#x27;ve ever stood on had trapdoors.)</p> <p>[Jalen checks the nav display. Clean route ahead. Nothing to dodge, nothing to outrun. Just empty space and distant stars.]</p> <p>(She&#x27;s going to be okay. She&#x27;s learning faster than I did. She has the whole crew watching out for her, and Bubbles practically adopted her, and that thing in her spine seems like it&#x27;s on her side.)</p> <p>[A small adjustment to their flight path. Smoother now. Gentler.]</p> <p>(I&#x27;m going to fly extra smooth tonight. For the new one. So she knows the ship is steady. So she knows someone&#x27;s at the helm who won&#x27;t let her fall.)</p> <p>[The stars stream past. Jalen flies. And somewhere below, in a bunk that&#x27;s becoming slowly less foreign, Avyanna Lagrange sleeps-learning, bit by bit, what it means to be held.]</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// OBSERVATION: Jalen flying manual again | DURATION: 3.2 hours | REASON: &quot;thinking about family&quot; | INFERENCE: healthy coping | CONFIDENCE: 0.81 | COMMENT: they&#x27;re softer than they pretend. most of us are.&gt;</p> <hr> <p>*End Chapter 12*</p> </div> Where does the route go next? * [[Read optional scene 12a: Crew Meal Dynamics->Act1 12a Crew Meal Dynamics]] * [[Read optional scene 12b: Ai Game Night Probability->Act1 12b Ai Game Night Probability]] * [[Continue to Chapter 13: Shipboard Physics->Act1 13 Shipboard Physics]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Optional 12a: Crew Meal Dynamics</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 12a_crew_meal_dynamics.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **[SCENE - OCCURS POST-CH12, EVENING ROUTINE]** &gt; *Location: Lumen Thief galley, dinner time* &gt; *Present: Full crew (Vesper, Rho, Jalen, Elia, Nyx, Avyanna)* &gt; *Teaching: Collective care, meal sharing as non-performance, crew as chosen family*</p> <hr> <p>[Dinner on the Lumen Thief is chaotic in the way that families are chaotic-not dangerous, just loud and overlapping and full of people who&#x27;ve stopped performing for each other.]</p> <p>[The galley smells like something Rho is calling &quot;stew&quot; that probably started life as three different meals before he combined them into one pot. It&#x27;s not beautiful. But it&#x27;s hot and it&#x27;s food and no one has to eat alone.]</p> <p>[Avyanna sits at the table between Nyx and Elia. Across from her: Vesper, Rho, Jalen. Six people. Six plates. One meal.]</p> <p>[She&#x27;s still not used to this.]</p> <hr> <p>Rho: [ladling stew into bowls] Fair warning: I added spice. Jalen said the last batch was bland.</p> <p>Jalen: [not looking up from their datapad] I said it tasted like warm disappointment.</p> <p>Rho: [cheerful] Same thing.</p> <p>Vesper: [accepting a bowl] If this kills me, promote Elia to engineer.</p> <p>Elia: [dry] I don&#x27;t want the job.</p> <p>Rho: [mock-wounded] No one appreciates my culinary artistry.</p> <p>Nyx: [tasting the stew] It&#x27;s... functional.</p> <p>Rho: [grinning] I&#x27;ll take it.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna accepts her bowl. The stew is thick and brown and smells like a dozen spices she can&#x27;t name. She takes a cautious bite.]</p> <p>[It&#x27;s good. Better than good. Better than anything she ate in the mine, where food was fuel and nothing else.]</p> <p>Rho: [watching her face] You like it?</p> <p>Avyanna: [surprised] Yes. It&#x27;s... it&#x27;s really good.</p> <p>Rho: [pleased] See? Some people have taste.</p> <p>Jalen: [finally looking up] Some people have low standards.</p> <p>Rho: [throwing a piece of bread at them] Heretic.</p> <p>[Jalen catches it without looking. Takes a bite. Shrugs.]</p> <p>Jalen: [admitting] It&#x27;s acceptable.</p> <p>Rho: [triumphant] Victory.</p> <hr> <p>[They eat. Conversation flows around Avyanna like water around a stone-not excluding her, just not requiring her to participate. She can listen. Observe. Exist in the space without needing to justify her presence.]</p> <p>Vesper: [to Elia] Status on the new client&#x27;s recon?</p> <p>Elia: [between bites] Two potential entry points. Both viable. I&#x27;ll have a full report by tomorrow.</p> <p>Vesper: [nodding] Priority level?</p> <p>Elia: Medium. No immediate danger, but timeline is tight. We&#x27;ll need to move within the week.</p> <p>Vesper: [making a note] Understood. I&#x27;ll coordinate with the client.</p> <hr> <p>[This is business. But it&#x27;s also dinner. The two things coexist without tension.]</p> <p>Rho: [to Nyx] How&#x27;s the med bay inventory?</p> <p>Nyx: [precise] Adequate. We&#x27;re low on broad-spectrum antibiotics. I&#x27;ve submitted a requisition to the next Guild station.</p> <p>Rho: [frowning] Low as in &quot;we should worry&quot; or low as in &quot;we have enough for now&quot;?</p> <p>Nyx: [considering] Low as in &quot;if someone gets infected in the next week, we&#x27;re fine. If two people get infected, we&#x27;re rationing.&quot;</p> <p>Rho: [nodding] I&#x27;ll expedite the req. Bump it to priority two.</p> <p>Nyx: [approving] Appreciated.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna watches the exchange. Casual logistics. Everyone tracking different systems. Everyone contributing information. No hierarchy. Just coordination.]</p> <p>Jalen: [to the table, still scrolling their datapad] Station Kael-7 is reporting increased Compact patrol activity. Might affect our transit route.</p> <p>Vesper: [looking up] Increased how much?</p> <p>Jalen: [checking] Thirty percent more ships than usual. No official reason given.</p> <p>Vesper: [thoughtful] Flag it. If it goes above fifty percent, we reroute. I&#x27;m not interested in Compact attention.</p> <p>Jalen: [noting it] Flagged.</p> <hr> <p>[Elia finishes her stew, sets the bowl aside.]</p> <p>Elia: [to Rho] Your turn for dishes.</p> <p>Rho: [groaning] I cooked.</p> <p>Elia: [flat] You made stew. That&#x27;s not cooking. That&#x27;s assembly.</p> <p>Rho: [pointing at the pot] That is a *masterwork* of assembly.</p> <p>Elia: [unmoved] Still your turn for dishes.</p> <p>Rho: [to the table] Someone back me up here.</p> <p>Vesper: [dry] You&#x27;re on dish rotation. Elia&#x27;s correct.</p> <p>Rho: [sighing dramatically] I am unappreciated and overworked.</p> <p>Jalen: [without looking up] You&#x27;re appreciated exactly the correct amount.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna almost smiles. Watching Rho get roasted is becoming one of her favorite ship activities.]</p> <p>Nyx: [to Avyanna] You&#x27;re quiet tonight. More than usual.</p> <p>[Avyanna tenses. Observation = evaluation = potential correction.]</p> <p>Nyx: [gentle] That wasn&#x27;t criticism. Just observation. You&#x27;re allowed to be quiet.</p> <p>Avyanna: [careful] I&#x27;m just... listening. Learning how this works.</p> <p>Nyx: [nodding] Good strategy. You&#x27;ll figure out when you want to talk. No pressure.</p> <hr> <p>[Rho stands, starts collecting bowls. Avyanna moves to hand him hers.]</p> <p>Rho: [waving her off] You&#x27;re still new. You get a grace period. I&#x27;ll grab it.</p> <p>Avyanna: [confused] Grace period?</p> <p>Vesper: [explaining] First month on crew, you&#x27;re exempt from chores. Lets you acclimate without added pressure.</p> <p>Avyanna: [frowning] But I can help-</p> <p>Rho: [interrupting kindly] And you will. Next month. Right now, your job is learning to exist here without constant survival math.</p> <p>[He takes her bowl, stacks it with the others.]</p> <p>Rho: [grinning] Besides, dishes are meditative. I do my best thinking while scrubbing.</p> <p>Jalen: [dry] That explains so much.</p> <hr> <p>[Rho carries the dishes to the cleaning station. The rest of the crew lingers at the table-no rush to leave, no schedule demanding they disperse. Just people existing in proximity because it&#x27;s pleasant.]</p> <p>Elia: [to Avyanna] How&#x27;s the training with me going? The physical stuff.</p> <p>Avyanna: [honest] Hard. But... not bad hard. Learning hard.</p> <p>Elia: [approving] Good distinction. If it ever tips into bad hard, tell me. We adjust.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] You keep saying that. &quot;Tell me.&quot; &quot;We adjust.&quot; Like it&#x27;s simple.</p> <p>Elia: [meeting her eyes] It is simple. Not easy, but simple. You speak, I listen, we change course. That&#x27;s the system.</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper stands, stretches.]</p> <p>Vesper: [to the table] I&#x27;m reviewing contracts in the conference room if anyone needs me. Otherwise, I&#x27;ll see you at 0800 for the briefing.</p> <p>Jalen: [standing] I&#x27;ve got bridge night shift. Jalen out.</p> <p>Nyx: [collecting their medical datapad] Med bay inventory to finish. Then sleep.</p> <p>[One by one, the crew disperses. Not in formation. Not on command. Just people ending their meal and moving to their next tasks.]</p> <p>[Avyanna remains at the table. Elia stays too, finishing her water.]</p> <hr> <p>Avyanna: [hesitant] Can I ask something?</p> <p>Elia: [nodding] Always.</p> <p>Avyanna: [slow] This. Dinner. Everyone sitting together. Is this... mandatory?</p> <p>Elia: [considering] Mandatory? No. Expected? Yes. But expected and mandatory are different.</p> <p>Elia: [explaining] We eat together because it&#x27;s useful. Crew coordination happens here. Information sharing. Also, eating alone is depressing. But if you needed to eat alone some night, you could. No one would punish you.</p> <p>Avyanna: [absorbing] Expected, not mandatory.</p> <p>Elia: [nodding] Like most things here. We have rhythms. Routines. But they&#x27;re flexible. Built for humans, not machines.</p> <hr> <p>[Rho returns from the cleaning station, wiping his hands on a towel.]</p> <p>Rho: [to Avyanna] You still sitting here?</p> <p>Avyanna: [defensive] I can leave-</p> <p>Rho: [interrupting, gentle] Not kicking you out. Just checking if you need company or solitude.</p> <p>[Avyanna pauses. Company or solitude. Her choice.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [slow] Solitude. I think. Tonight.</p> <p>Rho: [nodding] Fair. Galley&#x27;s yours. Lock up when you&#x27;re done if you&#x27;re the last one out.</p> <p>[He leaves. Elia stands, pushes her chair in.]</p> <p>Elia: [to Avyanna] Good dinner. See you tomorrow for training.</p> <p>Avyanna: [nodding] Tomorrow.</p> <hr> <p>[And then she&#x27;s alone in the galley. Not isolated. Not abandoned. Just... alone. By choice.]</p> <p>[The remnants of dinner are cleared. The pot is soaking in the cleaner. The table is wiped down. The space is tidy in the way that means people care about communal areas.]</p> <p>[Avyanna sits at the table and breathes. Absorbs the quiet. Thinks about six people eating together, arguing about stew, coordinating work, and then dispersing without drama.]</p> <p>(This is what crew looks like. Not perfect. Not formal. Just people taking care of each other through small actions. Cooking. Cleaning. Sharing information. Eating together.)</p> <p>[The shard at the base of her skull is warm. Witnessing.]</p> <p>(Warmth hums at the base of her skull, not words-just a pattern she reads as: crew, chosen, home.)</p> <hr> <p>[After ten minutes, Avyanna stands. Locks the galley door per Rho&#x27;s instruction. Walks back to her quarters.]</p> <p>[In her bunk, she thinks about dinner. About Rho&#x27;s stew. About Elia&#x27;s directness. About Nyx&#x27;s observation without judgment. About Vesper coordinating contracts over food. About Jalen catching bread without looking.]</p> <p>[Small things. Ordinary things. Things that mean: you belong here. You&#x27;re part of this. You&#x27;re crew.]</p> <p>[She falls asleep easier than she has in weeks.]</p> <hr> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// MEAL LOG: full crew attendance | DURATION: 47 minutes | CONVERSATION TOPICS: contracts, logistics, maintenance, interpersonal coordination | TRAINEE PARTICIPATION: minimal verbal, high observational | ASSESSMENT: trainee learning crew dynamics through immersion | STATUS: integration appropriate&gt;</p> <p>&lt;RHO PERSONAL LOG: &quot;She liked the stew. Small victory. Also, she didn&#x27;t bolt from the table the moment she finished eating. She sat. Listened. Existed with us. That&#x27;s progress. Tomorrow I&#x27;ll make something even better. Maybe bribe Vesper for decent spice budget.&quot;&gt;</p> <p>&lt;ELIA PERSONAL LOG: &quot;She asked if dinner was mandatory. That question tells me everything about the mine. &#x27;Is this punishment if I don&#x27;t participate?&#x27; We&#x27;re teaching her that routines can be comfort, not control. She&#x27;ll get there.&quot;&gt;</p> <hr> <p>*End Scene*</p> </div> * [[Continue to optional scene 12b: Ai Game Night Probability->Act1 12b Ai Game Night Probability]] * [[Continue to Chapter 13: Shipboard Physics->Act1 13 Shipboard Physics]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Optional 12b: Ai Game Night Probability</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 12b_ai_game_night_probability.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **[SCENE - OCCURS POST-CH12, EARLY &quot;PLAY IS ALLOWED&quot; TRAINING]** &gt; *Location: Atrium / Commons* &gt; *Present: Avyanna, Waffle.bat, Cinnamon.exe, Bubbles, Souffle (cameo), Elia (brief), Elisira (brief)* &gt; *Teaching: probability as a tool; play as non-transactional; &quot;losing&quot; as information*</p> <hr> <p>[Game night starts the way most things on the Lumen Thief start.]</p> <p>[With a notice nobody can prove was mandatory.]</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// ANNOUNCEMENT: civic recreation event scheduled | TITLE: GAME NIGHT | GOAL: reduce human tension by 12% | NOTE: participation &quot;voluntary&quot; (but I will be sad if you do not)&gt;</p> <p>[Avyanna reads the word voluntary and waits for the hidden fee.]</p> <p>(Voluntary means: you are responsible for the outcome.)</p> <p>[There is no fee.]</p> <p>[There is just Waffle&#x27;s bat icon blinking at her from the message board like a dare.]</p> <hr> <p>[The atrium is rearranged.]</p> <p>[Not dramatically. Just... thoughtfully.]</p> <p>[Couches pulled into a circle. A low table cleared. Snacks in bowls like the ship believes you deserve salt and sugar even if you didn&#x27;t earn them.]</p> <p>[Avyanna stands at the edge of the circle with her hands behind her back.]</p> <p>(If I sit, I&#x27;m taking space. If I take space, I&#x27;m visible. Visible is dangerous.)</p> <p>[Bubbles drifts in and bumps her shoulder gently with a rotor guard-an almost-touch that feels like permission.]</p> <p>Bubbles: Sit.</p> <p>Avyanna: [automatic] I&#x27;m fine-</p> <p>[She stops herself.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Why.</p> <p>Bubbles: Because you were invited.</p> <p>[Invited.]</p> <p>[Not ordered. Not assigned. Invited.]</p> <p>[Avyanna sits on the very edge of the couch like she&#x27;s ready to evacuate it.]</p> <hr> <p>[Waffle rolls in a tray on little wheels. On it: a pile of tokens, a deck of cards, and a small board with a grid and a few carved pieces shaped like ships.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Is this... a training exercise.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// ANSWER: yes&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: [bracing] Okay.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// ADDENDUM: it is also a game&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: Those aren&#x27;t the same.</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// COMMENTARY: they can be | OBSERVATION: trainee assumes &quot;game&quot; = trap | INFERENCE: prior environment punished play | CONFIDENCE: 0.88&gt;</p> <p>[Waffle makes an offended little whirr.]</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// CORRECTION: play is not punishment | play is data with snacks&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: Data with snacks.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// AFFIRMATION: correct&gt;</p> <hr> <p>[Cinnamon projects rules onto the terminal wall in neat bullet points.]</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// RULESET: &quot;DRIFT &amp; DEBT&quot; (training variant) | DISCLAIMER: no real debt will be incurred&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: [tense] Why is the word debt in it.</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// ANSWER: to desensitize trigger vocabulary via safe context&gt;</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s jaw tightens.]</p> <p>Avyanna: That feels... manipulative.</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// RESPONSE: yes | FOLLOW-UP: also protective&gt;</p> <p>[Bubbles adds without text, aloud, like a human trying.]</p> <p>Bubbles: We want the word to stop owning you.</p> <p>[Avyanna swallows.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Okay.</p> <hr> <p>[The board is simple.]</p> <p>[A route grid. Start point. End point. Pieces that move like ships.]</p> <p>[Each turn you draw a card.]</p> <p>[Cards say things like:]</p> <p>&gt; **WEATHER FEE** &gt; &gt; **EXPEDITE OFFER** &gt; &gt; **OUT OF STOCK** &gt; &gt; **VOLUNTARY DISCLOSURE**</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s stomach drops anyway.]</p> <p>(The galaxy uses the same words everywhere. Like they&#x27;re in a template.)</p> <p>Elia: [appearing in the doorway like a bad idea] Wow. You made a board game out of oppression.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// PROUD: yes&gt;</p> <p>Elia: [delighted] Horrible. I love it.</p> <p>[Elisira appears behind Elia, looking tired already.]</p> <p>Elisira: No teaching her to gamble.</p> <p>Elia: I would never.</p> <p>Elisira: [flat] You would absolutely.</p> <p>Elia: I would teach her to cheat. Different.</p> <p>[Elisira sighs and leaves before she has to arrest someone.]</p> <hr> <p>[Waffle points a little fan at Avyanna like a spotlight.]</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// INSTRUCTION: you go first&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: Why me.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// ANSWER: you think too hard | this will be funny&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s not a reason.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// CLARIFICATION: it is a reason on this ship&gt;</p> <p>[Avyanna draws a card.]</p> <p>&gt; **OUT OF STOCK** &gt; &gt; *Rule: accept the verdict, lose one turn.*</p> <p>[Her throat tightens.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] That&#x27;s... normal.</p> <p>[Cinnamon&#x27;s text appears immediately.]</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// QUESTION: is it enforceable&gt;</p> <p>[Avyanna stares at the card like it&#x27;s a form.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Who enforces it.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// ANSWER: the rules&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: Then the rules are lying.</p> <p>[Waffle&#x27;s icon blinks.]</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// DELIGHT: yes&gt;</p> <p>[Bubbles drifts closer.]</p> <p>Bubbles: Explain.</p> <p>Avyanna: Out of stock is a claim. Not a verdict.</p> <p>Avyanna: If you accept it, you stop looking.</p> <p>Avyanna: If you stop looking, whoever benefits wins.</p> <p>[Cinnamon pings approval.]</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// OBSERVATION: trainee applied doctrine correctly | INFERENCE: learning transfer | CONFIDENCE: 0.86&gt;</p> <p>[Waffle makes a dramatic whirring sound and flips the card over.]</p> <p>&gt; **OUT OF STOCK (REAL RULE)** &gt; &gt; *Rule: treat as claim. Ask: &quot;what substitute exists?&quot; Roll again.*</p> <p>[Avyanna blinks.]</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s cheating.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// RESPONSE: it is critical thinking&gt;</p> <p>Elia: [leaning in, delighted] Same thing.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna rolls a die Waffle produces from somewhere it did not exist a moment ago.]</p> <p>[The die clatters.]</p> <p>[It lands on a number that means: you find a substitute, but it costs time.]</p> <p>Avyanna: That makes sense.</p> <p>Elia: [mock horror] She is learning. Quick, distract her with snacks.</p> <p>[Bubbles slides a bowl of chips toward Avyanna.]</p> <p>[No comment. No transaction. Just food existing near her hands.]</p> <p>[Avyanna eats one and waits for guilt to arrive.]</p> <p>[It doesn&#x27;t.]</p> <hr> <p>[Second turn.]</p> <p>[Avyanna draws:]</p> <p>&gt; **EXPEDITE OFFER** &gt; &gt; *A clerk offers to &quot;help.&quot; Pay one token or lose two turns.*</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s pulse spikes.]</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s not an offer. That&#x27;s a threat.</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// AFFIRMATION: correct&gt;</p> <p>Bubbles: What do you do.</p> <p>Avyanna: Ask under which jurisdiction.</p> <p>Elia: [clapping softly] Yes. Interrogate the cardboard.</p> <p>Avyanna: [dry] I will.</p> <p>[She looks at the card, as if it can answer.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Under which jurisdiction is the expedite fee required.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// RESPONSE: none&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: Who enforces it.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// RESPONSE: your fear&gt;</p> <p>[Avyanna goes still.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [soft] That&#x27;s...</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s the mine.</p> <p>[Silence.]</p> <p>[Not dangerous. Just real.]</p> <p>[Bubbles&#x27; fans slow.]</p> <p>Bubbles: Yes.</p> <p>[Cinnamon&#x27;s text appears, unusually spare.]</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// NOTE: fear is an enforcer in many systems&gt;</p> <p>Elia: [quiet, for once] So what do you do now.</p> <p>[Avyanna looks down at her tokens.]</p> <p>[Plastic disks. Meaningless. Safe.]</p> <p>[She thinks of the mine&#x27;s &quot;processing fees.&quot; The way the word was used to make cruelty sound administrative.]</p> <p>(I can&#x27;t pay fear to stop being fear.)</p> <p>Avyanna: I refuse the expedite offer.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// RESULT: you lose one turn&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: [grim] Fine.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// COMMENTARY: refusal costs time | STILL correct&gt;</p> <p>[Avyanna exhales.]</p> <p>(Refusal costs time. Not skin.)</p> <hr> <p>[The game continues.]</p> <p>[Avyanna loses turns. Wins turns. Learns the shape of probability as something that exists whether you deserve it or not.]</p> <p>[She starts to see patterns.]</p> <p>[&quot;Weather fee&quot; cards cluster after &quot;insurance&quot; cards.]</p> <p>[&quot;Voluntary disclosure&quot; cards show up when &quot;assistance desk&quot; pieces are near.]</p> <p>[She starts moving her piece not toward the shortest route, but toward the route that keeps options open.]</p> <p>[Bubbles watches her with the quiet intensity of someone guarding a new habit.]</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// OBSERVATION: trainee optimizing for exits instead of speed | INFERENCE: safety prioritization | CONFIDENCE: 0.82&gt;</p> <hr> <p>[Near the end, Avyanna draws:]</p> <p>&gt; **LUCKY BREAK** &gt; &gt; *Gain two turns. No cost.*</p> <p>[She stares at it.]</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s unrealistic.</p> <p>Elia: [grinning] The universe does occasionally misplace mercy.</p> <p>Avyanna: Who enforces it.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// ANSWER: nobody | NOTE: accept the good without paying it back&gt;</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens.]</p> <p>(Accept the good without paying it back.)</p> <p>[She takes the two turns.]</p> <p>[Nothing explodes.]</p> <p>[No one makes her apologize for being lucky.]</p> <p>[She finishes the route one turn later, not first, but intact.]</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// ANNOUNCEMENT: trainee completed route | STATUS: not dead | EVALUATION: acceptable&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: [laughing despite herself] Acceptable.</p> <p>Elia: That&#x27;s the nicest thing Waffle&#x27;s ever said.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// CORRECTION: i have said &quot;delightful&quot; before&gt;</p> <p>Elia: [to Avyanna] See. Games can be fun.</p> <p>Avyanna: It was... annoying.</p> <p>Elia: That&#x27;s fun on this ship.</p> <p>[Bubbles bumps Avyanna&#x27;s shoulder again, a little more familiar this time.]</p> <p>Bubbles: You played.</p> <p>Avyanna: Yes.</p> <p>Bubbles: And nothing bad happened.</p> <p>[Avyanna swallows.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] Yes.</p> <p>[She files that too.]</p> <hr> <p>[Waffle declares a &quot;bonus round&quot; with the smugness of an algorithm that thinks it invented generosity.]</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// ANNOUNCEMENT: BONUS ROUND | PURPOSE: prove that probability is not morality&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: Probability isn&#x27;t-</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// INTERRUPTION: i know | YOU need to know&gt;</p> <p>[Elia returns like she can&#x27;t resist a spectacle.]</p> <p>Elia: Bonus round? Who are we humiliating.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// RESPONSE: nobody | COMMENTARY: humiliation is optional&gt;</p> <p>Elia: [disappointed] Tragic.</p> <p>[Elisira appears in the doorway, sees Elia, and makes the face of someone reconsidering every life choice.]</p> <p>Elisira: Do not teach her to set fires for fun.</p> <p>Elia: [innocent] I would never.</p> <p>Elisira: You did it yesterday.</p> <p>Elia: For science.</p> <p>Elisira: [flat] For boredom.</p> <p>Elia: Both.</p> <p>[Elisira leaves before she becomes part of the game by force.]</p> <hr> <p>[Waffle rearranges the board.]</p> <p>[Same grid. Different objective.]</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// RULESET UPDATE: &quot;DRIFT &amp; DEBT&quot; (variance drill) | GOAL: reach endpoint with maximum optionality preserved&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: Optionality.</p> <p>Elia: Exits.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] Yes.</p> <p>[Waffle points at Avyanna again.]</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// INSTRUCTION: you choose your opponent&gt;</p> <p>[Avyanna looks at the circle.]</p> <p>[Bubbles: steady.]</p> <p>[Cinnamon: sharp.]</p> <p>[Souffle: curious.]</p> <p>[Elia: grinning like a knife that learned humor.]</p> <p>(Choose. Choosing is allowed.)</p> <p>Avyanna: Elia.</p> <p>Elia: [delighted] YES.</p> <p>Elia: I will teach you despair through cardboard.</p> <p>Avyanna: [dry, surprising herself] You already do that with hallway comments.</p> <p>[Elia clutches her chest theatrically.]</p> <p>Elia: She&#x27;s learning banter. I&#x27;m so proud I could commit a felony.</p> <hr> <p>[They take seats opposite each other.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s palms sweat.]</p> <p>(Games mean winners and losers. Winners get to eat. Losers get to hurt.)</p> <p>[The mine tries to overlay that logic.]</p> <p>[Waffle&#x27;s token tray clinks, a tiny reminder: this is plastic. Not rations. Not pain.]</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// OBSERVATION: trainee tension spike | INFERENCE: competition trigger | CONFIDENCE: 0.86&gt;</p> <p>Bubbles: You can stop.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quick] I don&#x27;t want to stop.</p> <p>[The sentence feels like stepping off a ledge and landing safely.]</p> <hr> <p>[First draw.]</p> <p>[Elia gets:]</p> <p>&gt; **WEATHER FEE** &gt; &gt; *Pay one token to proceed.*</p> <p>Elia: [bright] No.</p> <p>[She flicks the card away like it insulted her.]</p> <p>Avyanna: You can&#x27;t just-</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// COMMENTARY: she can | penalties apply&gt;</p> <p>[Waffle spins the board one notch.]</p> <p>&gt; *Penalty: lose one turn. Gain one &quot;reputation&quot; marker.*</p> <p>Avyanna: Reputation.</p> <p>Elia: [cheerful] See? Refusal costs time and buys fear. Useful.</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s... horrible.</p> <p>Elia: Welcome to reality. But with snacks.</p> <p>[Avyanna draws.]</p> <p>&gt; **VOLUNTARY DISCLOSURE** &gt; &gt; *Answer a question. If you refuse, lose one token.*</p> <p>[Her throat tightens.]</p> <p>(Answer a question. That&#x27;s how it starts.)</p> <p>Avyanna: Who enforces it.</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// RESPONSE: &quot;the rules&quot; (simulation)&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: So I can refuse.</p> <p>Elia: Yes.</p> <p>Elia: But don&#x27;t refuse because you&#x27;re scared. Refuse because you decided.</p> <p>[That&#x27;s... different.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I refuse.</p> <p>[Waffle removes a token from her pile with dramatic ceremony.]</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// COMMENTARY: refusal recorded | NOTE: you are still alive&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: [breathing] Still alive.</p> <p>Elia: That&#x27;s our brand.</p> <hr> <p>[They play.]</p> <p>[Elia takes wild risks, refuses anything that smells like leverage, and somehow lands on safe tiles because she treats probability like something she can bully.]</p> <p>[Avyanna plays cautiously at first, then learns to take one calculated risk when the risk buys her two exits later.]</p> <p>[She watches Elia&#x27;s face light up when the rules bend.]</p> <p>(Elia doesn&#x27;t just break rules because she&#x27;s bored. She breaks them because it proves they were never laws.)</p> <p>[At one point, Avyanna draws &quot;LUCKY BREAK&quot; again.]</p> <p>[She freezes.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I didn&#x27;t earn-</p> <p>Elia: Stop that.</p> <p>[No humor. Just a command that isn&#x27;t a threat.]</p> <p>Elia: Don&#x27;t you dare turn luck into guilt.</p> <p>Avyanna: [small] Why.</p> <p>Elia: Because that&#x27;s how they keep you small.</p> <p>Elia: Take the break. Then use it to build something.</p> <p>[Avyanna swallows.]</p> <p>[She takes it.]</p> <p>[She uses the extra turn to move into a better position and-for the first time-blocks Elia&#x27;s path without apologizing.]</p> <p>Elia: [eyes widening, delighted] Oh.</p> <p>Elia: You read me.</p> <p>Avyanna: [heart racing] I-</p> <p>Elia: Again.</p> <p>[Avyanna laughs. It&#x27;s thin with adrenaline, but it&#x27;s real.]</p> <hr> <p>[The bonus round ends with Elia winning by doing something ridiculous that shouldn&#x27;t work and does anyway.]</p> <p>Elia: Chaos wins.</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// CORRECTION: probability won&gt;</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// ADDENDUM: chaos is a probability distribution&gt;</p> <p>Elia: [pointing] See? Even the computers agree I&#x27;m art.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s chest aches, but not from fear.]</p> <p>(I played. I lost. I&#x27;m still here. No one took food away. No one hit me.)</p> <p>Bubbles: Do you want to play again later.</p> <p>[Avyanna hesitates.]</p> <p>[Then decides to practice wanting in a place where wanting is safe.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Yes.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// DELIGHT: YES&gt;</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// NOTE: progress&gt;</p> <p>[Elia grins at her like she just watched a door unlock.]</p> <p>Elia: Good.</p> <p>Elia: We&#x27;ll make you a menace yet.</p> </div> * [[Continue to Chapter 13: Shipboard Physics->Act1 13 Shipboard Physics]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Chapter 13: Shipboard Physics</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 13_shipboard_physics.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **LUMEN THIEF - TRAINING PROTOCOL (AMENDED):** &gt; *Subject: Shipboard Physics* &gt; *Summary: Gravity is honest. So are we.* &gt; *Rule 1: Learn how to fall without breaking.* &gt; *Rule 2: Learn how to prove what happened.* &gt; *Rule 3: Learn the difference between power and strain.* &gt; *Addendum: Floors first. Peaks later. If ever.*</p> <hr> <p>[The cargo bay is the ship&#x27;s largest lie.]</p> <p>[It looks like a place for crates and contraband and emergency repairs.]</p> <p>[It is also where you teach someone how to survive being thrown to the floor.]</p> <p>[The deck plating is cold through Avyanna&#x27;s socks. The air tastes faintly of polymer and recycled coffee. A vibration runs up her shins with each pulse of the engines-a steady reminder that the ship is moving, that motion is being maintained by people she now has names for.]</p> <p>(This is still a workplace.)</p> <p>(Different rules. Elia said that. Different place.)</p> <p>[Elia is already there, barefoot, hair tied back like it&#x27;s about to commit a crime. No blade in her hands. No armor. Just a woman built out of decisions.]</p> <p>Elia: [nodding once] Good. You showed up.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s spine tries to lock, the old reflex-attendance as survival. She forces a slow breath instead. In. Out. Count four. Count the exits.]</p> <p>[Cargo bay door. Corridor. Maintenance ladder.]</p> <p>[Elia watches her eyes move.]</p> <p>Elia: Don&#x27;t stop doing that. Just don&#x27;t let it freeze you.</p> <p>Avyanna: [automatic, too formal] Yes-</p> <p>Elia: [flat] No &quot;yes.&quot; Not here. If you don&#x27;t understand, you say so.</p> <p>[Elia lifts a hand, palm up-stop signal, not threat.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s mouth closes. The automatic agreement dies before it escapes.]</p> <p>Elia: That. Right there. That&#x27;s the reflex we&#x27;re breaking.</p> <p>Avyanna: [careful] I was agreeing with you.</p> <p>Elia: Were you? Or were you just saying what you think keeps you safe?</p> <p>[The question hits like cold water.]</p> <p>[Avyanna doesn&#x27;t answer. Can&#x27;t answer. Because Elia&#x27;s right.]</p> <p>Elia: At the mine, &quot;yes&quot; meant survival. Here, &quot;yes&quot; when you mean &quot;I don&#x27;t know&quot; gets people killed.</p> <p>[Elia sits on the edge of the console, making herself lower, less looming.]</p> <p>Elia: So we&#x27;re going to practice. Every time I give you an instruction, you&#x27;re going to do three things before you move.</p> <p>[She holds up fingers as she counts.]</p> <p>Elia: One. State the assumption.</p> <p>Elia: Two. Ask if the assumption is true.</p> <p>Elia: Three. If you&#x27;re still not sure, say &quot;I don&#x27;t understand.&quot;</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] That&#x27;s going to take forever.</p> <p>Elia: Good. Fast obedience is how they trained you. Slow thinking is how you survive.</p> <p>[She gestures at the console.]</p> <p>Elia: Let&#x27;s start. I&#x27;m going to tell you to check the reactor pressure. What&#x27;s your assumption?</p> <p>Avyanna: [hesitant] That... the reactor pressure needs checking?</p> <p>Elia: Why do you assume that?</p> <p>Avyanna: Because you told me to.</p> <p>Elia: [grin] And if I&#x27;m wrong? If I&#x27;m tired, or distracted, or testing you?</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s hands tighten.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Then I&#x27;d check something that doesn&#x27;t need checking.</p> <p>Elia: Exactly. So what do you ask?</p> <p>Avyanna: [slow, trying it out] Why are we checking reactor pressure?</p> <p>Elia: [approving] Good. And I&#x27;d answer: because Jalen flagged an anomaly, or because it&#x27;s on the maintenance schedule, or because I want you to learn where the readout is.</p> <p>Elia: Now you know. Now &quot;yes&quot; means something.</p> <p>[Avyanna processes that.]</p> <p>Avyanna: But what if there&#x27;s no time? What if it&#x27;s an emergency?</p> <p>Elia: Then I don&#x27;t give you a choice. I say &quot;Avyanna, do this, now, I&#x27;ll explain later.&quot;</p> <p>[She leans forward.]</p> <p>Elia: And you&#x27;ll know it&#x27;s real because I never pull that card unless it&#x27;s life or death. Because I don&#x27;t train you to obey blindly and then suddenly demand critical thinking when the ship&#x27;s on fire.</p> <p>Avyanna: [very quiet] The overseers did that.</p> <p>Elia: I know. That&#x27;s why we&#x27;re doing this.</p> <p>[She stands, offers a hand up.]</p> <p>Elia: Three questions. Every instruction. Until it&#x27;s reflex.</p> <p>Avyanna: [taking the hand] What. Why. What if.</p> <p>Elia: [sharp grin] Fast learner. Let&#x27;s break some more habits.</p> <p>[Elia lifts a hand, palm up-stop signal, not threat.]</p> <p>Elia: Three questions.</p> <p>[She holds up fingers as she counts, like she&#x27;s teaching a child and a soldier at the same time.]</p> <p>Elia: What are we doing.</p> <p>Elia: Why are we doing it.</p> <p>Elia: What happens if you do it wrong.</p> <p>[Avyanna stares.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [automatic] So I-</p> <p>Elia: Not &quot;so I can obey.&quot; So you can decide.</p> <p>[The sentence lands hard. Decision as a thing Avyanna is allowed to have.]</p> <p>Elia: If you can&#x27;t answer those three, you don&#x27;t move. You ask. You stall. You buy time.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] In the Kennel, stalling meant-</p> <p>Elia: I know.</p> <p>[Elia&#x27;s gaze is steady in the way a bulkhead is steady. No pity. No softness performance. Just a rule.]</p> <p>Elia: Here, stalling is how you stay alive.</p> <p>Avyanna: [swallows] What if you tell me to do something and I don&#x27;t understand.</p> <p>Elia: Then you say: &quot;Explain.&quot;</p> <p>Avyanna: And if you get angry.</p> <p>Elia: Then you&#x27;ll have learned something about me that needs correcting.</p> <p>[Cooling beat: Elia&#x27;s mouth quirks, almost a smile.]</p> <p>Elia: I&#x27;m not allergic to being wrong, Avyanna. I&#x27;m allergic to rot.</p> <p>Elia: Confusion you say out loud is fixable. Silence isn&#x27;t.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s pulse is loud in her ears.]</p> <p>(Ask. Out loud. Like it won&#x27;t cost me skin.)</p> <p>Elia: Try it.</p> <p>Avyanna: [a breath] What are we doing.</p> <p>Elia: Teaching you how to hit the floor without letting it collect you.</p> <p>Avyanna: Why.</p> <p>Elia: Because corridors throw people. Bullets throw people. Panic throws people. You don&#x27;t get to pick when.</p> <p>Avyanna: What happens if I do it wrong.</p> <p>Elia: You get hurt.</p> <p>Elia: Or you die.</p> <p>Elia: So we learn it on purpose. In here. With mats. With me watching. With you allowed to stop.</p> <p>[Avyanna nods once. It isn&#x27;t agreement. It&#x27;s comprehension. That&#x27;s different.]</p> <p>[Silence, the kind Avyanna used to live inside. Elia waits it out like it&#x27;s weather.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] I understand.</p> <p>Elia: We&#x27;ll see.</p> <hr> <p>[Elia walks a slow circle around her-distance kept deliberate, not crowding. Evaluating stance the way Vesper evaluates contracts: looking for the hidden clause that kills you.]</p> <p>Elia: You&#x27;re not going to be a fighter.</p> <p>[The words land clean and hard. Avyanna&#x27;s stomach drops anyway.]</p> <p>(Not useful.)</p> <p>Elia: [matter-of-fact] That&#x27;s fine. I don&#x27;t need another blade. I need you alive.</p> <p>[Avyanna swallows. Her left hip aches, a reminder from the mine that bodies keep receipts even when you don&#x27;t.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I can learn.</p> <p>Elia: You can learn to survive. That&#x27;s what this is.</p> <p>[Elia steps in. Not fast. Not threatening. A demonstration of how little warning &quot;not fast&quot; can be.]</p> <p>Elia: Stance. Feet under you. Weight where you can move it.</p> <p>[She adjusts Avyanna&#x27;s shoulders with two fingers-light touch, no claiming. Just alignment.]</p> <p>Elia: Knees unlocked. You lock them, you fall wrong. You fall wrong, you break.</p> <p>[Avyanna tries. Her body wants the mine&#x27;s posture-small, contained, invisible. Elia&#x27;s hands hover, correct, withdraw.]</p> <p>Elia: Good. Now fall.</p> <p>Avyanna: [blinking] What?</p> <p>Elia: On purpose. Better you learn it here than in a corridor with bullets.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat goes dry.]</p> <p>(In the Kennel, falling meant someone stepped on you.)</p> <p>Avyanna: I-</p> <p>Elia: [cutting in] It&#x27;s controlled. I&#x27;m here. If it hurts, we adjust. If you&#x27;re scared, you say it.</p> <p>[The deck hums. The ship keeps going. Elia&#x27;s face stays steady-no softness performance, no cruelty either. Just the fact of her attention.]</p> <p>[Avyanna lowers herself, awkward. Her palms slap the mat Elia must have laid out when she wasn&#x27;t looking.]</p> <p>Pain spikes up her wrists.</p> <p>Elia: Wrong.</p> <p>Avyanna: [breathing through it] I-</p> <p>Elia: Again.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// OBSERVATION: pain response elevated | INFERENCE: old injury fear-loop | CONFIDENCE: 0.73&gt;</p> <p>[Avyanna stares at the angle-brackets on the wall display. The ship, watching. Not like the Kennel&#x27;s cameras-hungry, punitive. This feels... clinical. Protective. Weirdly proud.]</p> <p>Elia: [dry] Don&#x27;t take advice from the gremlin unless it&#x27;s about not dying.</p> <p>Waffle.bat: [over the bay speakers, offended sincerity] I can do both.</p> <p>[Cooling beat: Elia&#x27;s mouth twitches. Avyanna&#x27;s hands flex once, then settle.]</p> <p>Elia: Roll your shoulder. Turn. Let the floor take the force, not your bones.</p> <p>[They do it again. Again. Again.]</p> <p>[Avyanna learns the strange intimacy of impact in a place where nobody uses it as a lever.]</p> <p>[She learns that falling can be a skill instead of a failure.]</p> <hr> <p>[An hour later, her muscles shake with new kinds of exhaustion. Elia calls a stop like she calls everything: no ceremony, just decision.]</p> <p>Elia: That&#x27;s enough.</p> <p>Avyanna: [hoarse] I can-</p> <p>Elia: [flat] You can&#x27;t. That&#x27;s not a moral weakness. It&#x27;s a body.</p> <p>[Avyanna sits on the mat, sweat cooling fast in the recycled air. Her hip throbs, but it&#x27;s a clean throb-use, not damage.]</p> <p>Elia: Your job is to not die long enough for us to reach you.</p> <p>[It&#x27;s not comforting. It&#x27;s worse.]</p> <p>[It&#x27;s structure.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [small] And if you don&#x27;t reach me?</p> <p>[Elia&#x27;s eyes sharpen-annoyed at the question, not at Avyanna. Like someone just suggested a system without failure absorbers.]</p> <p>Elia: Then we did something wrong. And we fix it. We don&#x27;t write you off.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s chest tightens. Something stuck there that won&#x27;t go down.]</p> <p>Elia: [standing, offering her hand up] Walk it off. Hydrate. Then you&#x27;re with Vesper.</p> <p>[The offered hand is bare. No glove. No command.]</p> <p>[Avyanna takes it. The pull is firm, efficient. Not ownership. Not rescue. Just assistance-one person helping another get back to their feet.]</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s workspace is a corner of the common area that pretends it&#x27;s casual.]</p> <p>[A chair angled for the best line of sight to both corridor entrances. A stack of tablets that look like clutter until you realize they&#x27;re sorted by threat.]</p> <p>[The ship&#x27;s lights are slightly warmer here. Cinnamon&#x27;s tell, Avyanna remembers. When Cinnamon is pleased, the ship glows like it&#x27;s letting itself feel it.]</p> <p>Vesper: [without looking up] Sit.</p> <p>[Avyanna sits.]</p> <p>Vesper: Good. Now: evidence.</p> <p>[She slides a sealed pouch across the table. Inside, a shard of scorched plating, edges jagged. A tiny thing to be carrying so carefully.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [careful] From the mine?</p> <p>Vesper: [finally looking at her] From the mine. From the dock. From any place that will lie about what it did.</p> <p>Avyanna: [swallowing] Aurum will deny everything.</p> <p>Vesper: Yes.</p> <p>[A beat. Vesper&#x27;s gaze goes past Avyanna-cataloging, checking angles, then returning.]</p> <p>Vesper: So we build receipts that survive denial.</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper taps her tablet. A template opens: timestamps, hashes, custody chains, signatures.]</p> <p>[Vesper turns the tablet so Avyanna can see the top line.]</p> <p>Vesper: First: time.</p> <p>Avyanna: [frowning] That&#x27;s... a date.</p> <p>Vesper: It&#x27;s a claim.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s stomach twists.]</p> <p>Vesper: Institutions don&#x27;t only lie with words. They lie with clocks.</p> <p>Vesper: If they can move the timestamp, they can move the story.</p> <p>Avyanna: [careful] Like the debt clock.</p> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s eyes flick up-approval, and a flash of something sharper.]</p> <p>Vesper: Exactly like the debt clock.</p> <p>Vesper: A number that pretends to be physics. A number that is actually policy.</p> <p>[She taps two fields.]</p> <p>Vesper: We record ship time.</p> <p>Vesper: We record station time.</p> <p>Vesper: We record who owns the clock.</p> <p>Avyanna: Who owns-</p> <p>Vesper: The jurisdiction.</p> <p>[Vesper says it like &quot;gravity.&quot; Not dramatic. Just reality.]</p> <p>Vesper: If a station&#x27;s logs say &quot;you arrived after the incident,&quot; they&#x27;ll call your evidence fabricated.</p> <p>Vesper: If your ship logs say &quot;we arrived before,&quot; they&#x27;ll call your logs tampered.</p> <p>Avyanna: Then what&#x27;s the point.</p> <p>Vesper: The point is redundancy.</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t smile, but something in the cadence sounds like she&#x27;s enjoying the teaching.]</p> <p>Vesper: Two independent clocks. Two independent hashes. Witness signatures. Sensor IDs.</p> <p>Vesper: You build a chain they have to cut in multiple places.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet, hungry] So you make it expensive.</p> <p>Vesper: Yes.</p> <p>[Avyanna looks down at the fields. The template is a trap turned inside out.]</p> <p>Avyanna: What&#x27;s the assumption.</p> <p>Vesper: [still] Which one.</p> <p>Avyanna: The one they want you to swallow when they say &quot;contamination.&quot;</p> <p>[A beat. Vesper&#x27;s gaze goes past Avyanna-cataloging, checking angles, then returning.]</p> <p>Vesper: The assumption is that their record is neutral.</p> <p>Vesper: That their clock is truth.</p> <p>Vesper: It&#x27;s not.</p> <p>Vesper: So we annotate everything. We don&#x27;t let them pretend context didn&#x27;t exist.</p> <p>Avyanna: [small] I like this.</p> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s mouth tightens like she might want to be amused and refuses.]</p> <p>Vesper: Good.</p> <p>Vesper: You should.</p> <p>Vesper: Chain of custody is a spell you cast with bureaucracy. If you do it right, it binds liars to the same reality as everyone else.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] In the Kennel, paperwork was how they hurt us.</p> <p>Vesper: [razor-polished] I know.</p> <p>[Cooling beat: Vesper&#x27;s thumb rubs once along the edge of her tablet. A private tell. Grief, maybe. Anger. Something controlled.]</p> <p>Vesper: That&#x27;s why we take it back.</p> <p>Vesper: A receipt that can be dismissed is worse than no receipt. It gives them ammunition.</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t raise her voice. She doesn&#x27;t have to. The sentence is a blade without flourishes.]</p> <p>Avyanna: How do they dismiss it?</p> <p>Vesper: With &quot;contamination.&quot; With &quot;tampering.&quot; With &quot;no provenance.&quot; With &quot;you can&#x27;t prove where this came from.&quot;</p> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s fingers move. The template fills with new fields: location tags, sensor IDs, witness names.]</p> <p>Vesper: So you prove it.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s hands take the pouch. Her fingers know carefulness; her whole childhood was built out of not dropping breakable things.]</p> <p>[She reads the fields, starts entering data, pauses, corrects herself, and corrects again. The rhythm is familiar: inventory, audits, quotas-only this time the count is meant to protect.]</p> <p>(Same skills. Different purpose.)</p> <p>[Vesper watches her work like someone watching a door seal.]</p> <p>Vesper: You see details others miss.</p> <p>[Avyanna freezes, the compliment landing like a threat.]</p> <p>Vesper: [dry, as if annoyed at her own sincerity] That&#x27;s rare. That&#x27;s valuable.</p> <p>[Avyanna forces a nod. The mine taught her praise meant someone had noticed you, and noticing was never safe.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I&#x27;m just... careful.</p> <p>Vesper: Careful is an ethic. Keep it.</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// OBSERVATION: documentation compliance improving | INFERENCE: safety-through-structure behavior | CONFIDENCE: 0.84 | COMMENT: receipts as hugs. humans are strange.&gt;</p> <p>[Avyanna doesn&#x27;t ask what a &quot;hug&quot; is supposed to feel like from a spreadsheet, but she feels something in her sternum ease anyway-like a debt ledger being edited without permission.]</p> <p>Vesper: [standing] Good. Bag it. Seal it. Log it. Then eat something with salt. You&#x27;re shaking.</p> <p>Avyanna: [looking down] I&#x27;m not-</p> <p>Vesper: [flat] You are. It&#x27;s fine.</p> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s hand hesitates over Avyanna&#x27;s shoulder-then withdraws. Consent enacted in absence as much as in touch.]</p> <p>Vesper: Nyx after lunch. Don&#x27;t run from them. They&#x27;ll find you.</p> <p>Avyanna: [a reluctant flicker of humor] Like the ship does?</p> <p>Vesper: The ship is nicer about it.</p> <p>[Cooling beat: Avyanna seals the pouch. The adhesive strip makes a small, final sound.]</p> <hr> <p>[Medbay smells like antiseptic and something herbal Nyx insists is &quot;calming.&quot; Avyanna doesn&#x27;t find it calming. She finds it strange.]</p> <p>[Nyx is at the main console, sleeves rolled up, hair a mess that looks deliberate until you notice the tiredness under their eyes.]</p> <p>Nyx: [without preamble] Tell me what you think the Lattice is.</p> <p>[Avyanna stops in the doorway. The question is too big. The Kennel taught her to answer small questions only. &quot;How many kilos?&quot; &quot;How many hours?&quot; &quot;How much pain?&quot;]</p> <p>Avyanna: It&#x27;s... the thing people use to make the machines work.</p> <p>Nyx: [nodding] Good. And what else?</p> <p>Avyanna: [searching] The thing that... breaks. The AMBER days. The flicker.</p> <p>Nyx: Better.</p> <p>[They gesture to the display. A graph blooms: a line climbing, jagged spikes like a heartbeat on a bad day.]</p> <p>Nyx: Regional coherence load. The ship&#x27;s word for it is CLI.</p> <p>Avyanna: [careful] Like a quota?</p> <p>Nyx: [a soft, humorless laugh] Like a carrying capacity. Like air in a sealed room. You can only take so much before it goes sour.</p> <hr> <p>[Nyx brings up footage from the mine: lights stuttering, equipment failing, supervisors calling it &quot;weather.&quot;]</p> <p>Nyx: Every effect pulls from the local pool. People talk like magic is infinite. It isn&#x27;t.</p> <p>[They tap the jagged spikes.]</p> <p>Nyx: When you run hot, you don&#x27;t just fry yourself. You destroy the ground you&#x27;re standing on. Literally.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s mouth tastes metallic. The mine&#x27;s flicker days come back with the smell of dust and overheated wiring.]</p> <p>Avyanna: They kept making us work through it.</p> <p>Nyx: Yes.</p> <p>[The single syllable holds an entire indictment and doesn&#x27;t spend any extra words on it.]</p> <p>Nyx: They treated strain like it was free.</p> <p>Avyanna: And the cost?</p> <p>Nyx: Paid by everyone in the region. Paid later. Paid in failures that look like accidents until you understand the math.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s fingers curl. She thinks of workers collapsing. Of machines that &quot;randomly&quot; failed. Of the way the Kennel always felt one bad day away from becoming a tomb.]</p> <p>Nyx: This is why we don&#x27;t spike the CLI for convenience. Not for speed. Not for ego. Not because someone dared us.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] Because it hurts people who never agreed.</p> <p>Nyx: [a glance, sharp approval] Exactly.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens around the next question. She hates needing. She hates being the variable that breaks the system.]</p> <p>Avyanna: What about... me.</p> <p>[Nyx&#x27;s eyes flick to her spine, as if they can see through skin.]</p> <p>Avyanna: The shard. The thing in me. Does it... raise the load.</p> <p>[Nyx&#x27;s hands still over the console.]</p> <p>Nyx: No.</p> <p>Avyanna: [barely breathing] How do you know?</p> <p>Nyx: Because we&#x27;ve been measuring. Because the ship has been watching. Because if you were running hot, Cinnamon would already be yelling at me in three different currencies.</p> <p>Nyx: [softer, careful] The entity within you is... remarkably efficient.</p> <p>[Avyanna feels warmth at the base of her skull, a quiet pulse like acknowledgement. Geometric shimmer at the edge of vision, then gone.]</p> <p>(It hears you.)</p> <p>Nyx: It&#x27;s not spiking regional strain. If anything- [they hesitate, honest about uncertainty] -it seems to smooth noise. Like it knows how to take less than it needs.</p> <p>Avyanna: [small] Like it&#x27;s being polite.</p> <p>Nyx: [a thin smile] Like it&#x27;s refusing to be extractive.</p> <p>[Avyanna doesn&#x27;t know what to do with that. She doesn&#x27;t know what to do with anything that refuses extraction as a default.]</p> <p>Nyx: This lesson is simple. Power without discipline makes ruins.</p> <p>[They turn the display off with a decisive tap.]</p> <p>Nyx: Go eat. Waffle wants to argue with your cortisol again.</p> <hr> <p>[After lunch, Avyanna finds her tablet pinging with a new schedule that looks like a contract and feels like a promise.]</p> <p>[Combat. Documentation. Medbay study. Maintenance observation. Rest blocks enforced.]</p> <p>[A note at the bottom, in Waffle&#x27;s blunt little font:]</p> <p>WAFFLE.BAT: [message] Training is now a THING. Please do not attempt to become a blade. We already have one. :)</p> <p>[Avyanna stares at the smiley face like it&#x27;s a foreign object.]</p> <p>(Is that... humor.)</p> <p>[The ship&#x27;s intercom clicks. Waffle&#x27;s voice, bright and unreasonably earnest, fills the corridor.]</p> <p>Waffle.bat: Avyanna Lagrange, please report for training calibration. This is not a punishment. This is a gift. Please do not hide inside the vents.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s cheeks go hot.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [to empty air] I didn&#x27;t-</p> <p>Waffle.bat: I know. I modeled it. You considered it.</p> <p>[Cooling beat: Avyanna exhales, then starts walking before her courage can dissolve.]</p> <hr> <p>[The cargo bay again. This time, Elia is gone. The mats are still there, rolled neatly like someone respects their own tools.]</p> <p>[A wall panel glows. Waffle&#x27;s telemetry pulses in clean lines, as if the ship is clearing its throat.]</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// TRAINING-PLAN: active | PRIORITY: &quot;floors&quot; | ERROR: Avyanna attempts to skip rest blocks | SOLUTION: I will prevent this&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: [stopping in front of the panel] You can... prevent it?</p> <p>Waffle.bat: Yes. I can lock doors. I can dim lights. I can summon Bubbles&#x27; drones to hover disapprovingly. I prefer diplomacy.</p> <p>Avyanna: [suspicious] Why.</p> <p>[A pause.
