Jalen goes first. Avyanna follows, light held low.]</p> <p>[The metal is close. The air is warmer. Every breath tastes like machine skin.]</p> <p>[Avyanna keeps her elbows in. The Kennel taught her how to occupy as little space as possible.]</p> <p>[Jalen pauses ahead, just long enough that she almost bumps into their boots.]</p> <p>Jalen: You okay back there?</p> <p>[The question is absurdly gentle in a place this mechanical.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Yes.</p> <p>Jalen: That was a real yes or a Kennel yes?</p> <p>[Her stomach clenches.]</p> <p>[She hates how well they see her. She hates how safe it makes her feel.]</p> <p>Avyanna: ...Real.</p> <p>Jalen: Okay. Keep the light steady.</p> <hr> <p>[They stop at a junction where three cable runs braid together.]</p> <p>[Jalen unscrews a cover plate. The screws are old enough to be stubborn. Their hands are patient enough to outlast it.]</p> <p>[Inside: a mechanical governor wheel, small and precise, like a watch that keeps a ship breathing.]</p> <p>Jalen: Life support regulator. It&#x27;s mostly automated. Mostly.</p> <p>Avyanna: Mostly is bad.</p> <p>Jalen: Mostly is honest.</p> <p>[Jalen points to a manual adjustment knob.]</p> <p>Jalen: If the smart control starts doing something creative, you can bring it back to baseline with this. No interface. No permissions. Just...</p> <p>[They twist the knob a fraction. The ship&#x27;s hum changes-so slightly Avyanna would have missed it a month ago. Now she hears everything.]</p> <p>Jalen: ...physics.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens.]</p> <p>(The floor can hold you. The ship can hold you. The system can still betray you.)</p> <hr> <p>[A faint chime ripples through the corridor speakers. Not an alarm. A notice.]</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// ALERT: static bloom (minor) | ACTION: switching select subsystems to analog fallback | NOTE: boring is safe&gt;</p> <p>[The lights warm and cool in a tiny, seasick pulse. Somewhere deeper in the ship, a servo whines and then steadies.]</p> <p>Jalen: See?</p> <p>Avyanna: That was... the Lattice?</p> <p>Jalen: That was the universe reminding us it hates being relied on.</p> <p>[They gesture at the governor wheel.]</p> <p>Jalen: This still works.</p> <p>[Avyanna stares at the small knob like it&#x27;s a religious object. She hates that part of her brain. The part that wants to worship anything that doesn&#x27;t hurt her.]</p> <p>Avyanna: It&#x27;s dumb.</p> <p>Jalen: Exactly.</p> <p>[They grin in the dark, where she can&#x27;t see it, but she can hear it anyway.]</p> <p>Jalen: Dumb reliability.</p> <hr> <p>[They back out of the duct. Avyanna&#x27;s shoulders ache from holding herself small.]</p> <p>[Jalen drops to the deck with practiced ease. They offer a hand to Avyanna-not pulling, just a point of contact if she wants it.]</p> <p>[Avyanna takes it.]</p> <hr> <p>[They move into the sensor alcove next.]</p> <p>[Avyanna hesitates at the threshold.]</p> <p>[A small wall display is mounted above the paneling-stars and lines and a grid that looks like math trying to pretend it&#x27;s pretty.]</p> <p>[She remembers tracing lights through dust, on the Kennel&#x27;s night shifts. Three stars in a rough line. A shape she kept seeing and never got to name.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] Do the stars have... names.</p> <p>[Jalen pauses, then looks up at the display like it&#x27;s an old argument.]</p> <p>Jalen: Depends who&#x27;s selling you the map.</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s not an answer.</p> <p>Jalen: It&#x27;s the only honest one.</p> <p>[They point at the three-star line.]</p> <p>Jalen: Some stations call that &quot;good luck.&quot;</p> <p>Jalen: Some call it &quot;home.&quot;</p> <p>Jalen: Some don&#x27;t name it at all because naming makes people think they own a thing.</p> <p>Avyanna: [frowning] So what do you call it.</p> <p>Jalen: Coordinates.</p> <p>[They tap the screen. The pretty shape collapses into numbers and a reference grid.]</p> <p>Jalen: A name is a handle.</p> <p>Jalen: A coordinate is a location.</p> <p>Jalen: If you want to survive corridors, learn the difference.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens with something that isn&#x27;t fear.]</p> <p>(Learn the difference.)</p> <p>Avyanna: The mine didn&#x27;t teach-</p> <p>Jalen: Of course it didn&#x27;t.</p> <p>[Their voice goes dry and tired for half a second.]</p> <p>Jalen: If they teach you the universe has more than one story, you start asking why yours is so small.</p> <p>[Avyanna looks at the grid again. The numbers don&#x27;t comfort her. They give her leverage.]</p> <p>Avyanna: So... if someone tells me &quot;this is just what the stars mean,&quot; I should ask-</p> <p>Jalen: Who taught them.</p> <p>Jalen: And what they get if you believe it.</p> <p>Jalen: Then you cross-check.</p> <p>Jalen: Names, if you must. Numbers, always.</p> <p>[Not the bridge. Not the clean screens. The place behind it, where the ship&#x27;s eyes are kept honest.]</p> <p>[Jalen flips a panel open. Inside are stacked components with handwritten labels: PRIMARY, SECONDARY, YOU BETTER WORK.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Is that official.</p> <p>Jalen: It&#x27;s on the ship. That makes it law.</p> <p>Avyanna: Elia said the ship doesn&#x27;t get to make laws.</p> <p>Jalen: Elia says a lot of things.</p> <p>[Avyanna almost smiles. It feels like a small betrayal of her own fear.]</p> <p>[Jalen hands her a thin module with contact pins.]</p> <p>Jalen: Swap it.</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s it?</p> <p>Jalen: That&#x27;s it.</p> <p>Avyanna: No calibration?</p> <p>Jalen: Calibration is after. First you learn not to be scared of touching the thing you&#x27;re responsible for.</p> <p>[The module is warm from the ship&#x27;s body heat. Avyanna pulls it free. The slot accepts the replacement with a soft click.]</p> <p>[The screen above them flickers once, then stabilizes.]</p> <p>Avyanna: That feels... real. More real than the shard stuff.</p> <p>[The words come out before she can filter them.]</p> <p>[Heat rises at the base of her skull-brief, not warning, not pain. A presence leaning closer to listen.]</p> <p>[Jalen&#x27;s hands pause.]</p> <p>Jalen: It is real. That&#x27;s the point.</p> <p>[They look at her, and for a second the humor drops all the way out.]</p> <p>Jalen: The shard stuff is real too. But it doesn&#x27;t obey the same rules. It doesn&#x27;t give you a maintenance manual. It doesn&#x27;t care if you can hold a wrench.</p> <p>Avyanna: Does anything care.</p> <p>Jalen: People do.</p> <p>[A beat. Jalen clears their throat like they&#x27;re resetting a circuit.]</p> <p>Jalen: Okay. Now calibration.</p> <hr> <p>[They run her through the sensor check sequence.]</p> <p>[Jalen doesn&#x27;t make it a quiz. They make it a pattern: do this, then this, then this, and if it looks wrong, you say so.]</p> <p>[Avyanna gets one step backward. The screen flashes an error code. Her stomach drops hard, old reflex screaming: mistake equals pain.]</p> <p>[She freezes, fingers hovering over the controls.]</p> <p>Jalen: Hey.</p> <p>[Avyanna forces herself to look at them.]</p> <p>Jalen: Are you stuck because you don&#x27;t know what to do, or because you&#x27;re waiting for someone to hit you?</p> <p>[Her jaw tightens.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Both.</p> <p>[Jalen nods like she&#x27;s named a fault in the wiring.]</p> <p>Jalen: Okay. Then we do the next step.</p> <p>[They point at the sequence on the panel.]</p> <p>Jalen: Out loud. Tell me what you see.</p> <p>[Her throat tries to close. The Sparkle Doctrine isn&#x27;t in this room, but it haunts the ship like ambient pressure.]</p> <p>Avyanna: The module is reading, but the checksum isn&#x27;t matching.</p> <p>Jalen: Good. That means you have eyes. Now: what&#x27;s the simplest failure mode?</p> <p>Avyanna: Dirty contacts.</p> <p>Jalen: Exactly.</p> <p>[Jalen hands her a cloth.]</p> <p>Jalen: Clean it. Then try again.</p> <p>[Avyanna cleans the contact pins, slow and careful. Her hands are steady. The mine taught her that much. The mine taught her to do delicate work under threat.]</p> <p>[She reseats the module. The error clears.]</p> <p>[Avyanna exhales like someone lowering a weight they&#x27;ve been carrying in their teeth.]</p> <p>Jalen: See? No one died.</p> <p>Avyanna: Yet.</p> <p>Jalen: That&#x27;s the spirit. Healthy paranoia.</p> <p>[Cooling beat: Jalen closes the panel. The latch clicks. Final.]</p> <hr> <p>[They leave the sensor alcove and walk toward the observation deck.]</p> <p>[The ship&#x27;s commons opens around them-space that feels like an argument the ship won. A message board on one wall flickers with posted notes, chores, requests, and problems marked with bright tags.]</p> <p>[Sparkles.]</p> <p>[Avyanna slows, reading one without meaning to.]</p> <p>- coffee machine still sulking. again.</p> <p>- someone left three wrenches in the galley. cinnamon is mad.</p> <p>- cargo bay mat needs cleaning (blood? paint? both?)</p> <p>- nav drift: check third redundant gyro</p> <p>[She swallows.]</p> <p>(The ship is held together by complaints and maintenance and people noticing what hurts.)</p> <p>Jalen: You don&#x27;t have to fix everything you see.</p> <p>[Avyanna startles.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I wasn&#x27;t-</p> <p>Jalen: You were. It&#x27;s fine. Just... don&#x27;t turn it into a debt.</p> <p>[Avyanna looks at the board again.]</p> <p>Avyanna: How do you know what&#x27;s yours.</p> <p>[Jalen&#x27;s gaze goes distant for a second. Not the observation deck distant-further.]</p> <p>Jalen: You ask.</p> <p>[They shrug, like it&#x27;s that simple, like grief doesn&#x27;t make everything complicated.]</p> <p>Jalen: Out loud. Like the captain keeps trying to teach you.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s chest tightens. Heat brushes her spine. Approval, maybe. Or just attention.]</p> <hr> <p>[They reach the observation deck.]</p> <p>[Stars fill the viewport. Cold and bright and indifferent. The ship&#x27;s hum is steady underfoot, a mechanical heartbeat that doesn&#x27;t care what you believe.]</p> <p>[Jalen leans against the bulkhead. They don&#x27;t sit. Stillness is a trap. Avyanna notices.]</p> <p>Avyanna: You hate sitting still.</p> <p>[Jalen&#x27;s mouth twitches like they&#x27;re considering denial, then decides it&#x27;s not worth the energy.]</p> <p>Jalen: I hate what happens when I stop.</p> <p>[Avyanna waits, the way she&#x27;s learned to wait-without filling the silence with apology.]</p> <p>[Jalen looks out at the stars, but their eyes aren&#x27;t on the stars.]</p> <p>Jalen: You know why I teach dumb reliability?</p> <p>Avyanna: Because the Lattice fails.</p> <p>Jalen: Yes.</p> <p>[A beat. Jalen&#x27;s hands flex once, like they&#x27;re remembering a control yoke.]</p> <p>Jalen: And because I was on a station when the route collapsed. I got out. My family didn&#x27;t.</p> <p>[The sentence is the key dialogue beat from the outline, but in Jalen&#x27;s mouth it sounds like a bolt being tightened: simple, irreversible.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat closes anyway.]</p> <p>[She remembers the coda, the cockpit at 0300, the stars like rain. She remembers the way grief can become a job you do alone.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Kettering&#x27;s Rest.</p> <p>[Jalen goes still. Just for a second.]</p> <p>Jalen: Yeah.</p> <p>Avyanna: You watched the lane close.</p> <p>[Jalen&#x27;s laugh is sharp and small, no humor in it.]</p> <p>Jalen: I watched the universe decide my family was a rounding error.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s hands curl around the wrench still in her pocket. She didn&#x27;t realize she kept it.]</p> <p>Jalen: That&#x27;s why the backup systems matter. That&#x27;s why the wrench matters.</p> <p>[Their gaze shifts to her, steady now-teaching again, because teaching is safer than grief.]</p> <p>Jalen: Because sometimes the Lattice just... stops.</p> <p>[Avyanna swallows.]</p> <p>[She thinks of the Kennel&#x27;s flicker days. Of lights stuttering. Of supervisors calling it weather. Of bodies pushed through instability like it was just another shift.]</p> <p>Avyanna: In the mine, when systems failed, they made us keep working anyway.</p> <p>Jalen: I know.</p> <p>Avyanna: If something broke, it was our fault.</p> <p>Jalen: I know.</p> <p>[Two syllables that don&#x27;t fix anything, but they don&#x27;t lie either.]</p> <p>Jalen: Here, when something breaks, we fix it. We don&#x27;t feed you to it.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s chest hurts.]</p> <p>(We don&#x27;t feed you to it.)</p> <p>[The phrase isn&#x27;t a promise of safety. It&#x27;s a promise of behavior. A floor.]</p> <hr> <p>[Jalen pushes off the bulkhead.]</p> <p>Jalen: Okay. Lesson over.</p> <p>[Avyanna blinks.]</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s it?</p> <p>Jalen: You learned how to close a door when the ship forgets how. You learned how to keep a sensor honest. You learned that &quot;stops&quot; is a thing the universe does.</p> <p>[They pause.]</p> <p>Jalen: And you learned you can ask to stop.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s fingers tighten around the wrench through her pocket fabric.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I didn&#x27;t ask.</p> <p>Jalen: Not today. But you heard it. That&#x27;s step one.</p> <p>[They start walking, already moving again, already outrunning their own silence.]</p> <p>Jalen: Return the wrench. Cinnamon will eat me alive if you don&#x27;t.</p> <p>[Avyanna almost laughs. The sound comes out thin, surprised at itself.]</p> <p>[Cooling beat: she presses her tongue to her teeth, tasting metal and something like relief.]</p> <hr> <p>[In the tool alcove, Avyanna sets the wrench back into its slot.]</p> <p>[The rack holds it without comment. The ship doesn&#x27;t praise her for basic competence. It just accepts the tool back into the system.]</p> <p>[That acceptance feels better than praise.]</p> <p>[The presence behind her eyes hums-quiet, patient. Not words. A tone that shifts when her fingers leave the wrench.]</p> <p>(You like this.)</p> <p>[No answer. But the warmth at the base of her skull steadies, like agreement that doesn&#x27;t need language.]</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// OBSERVATION: trainee completed mechanical override drill | INFERENCE: grounding behavior increased | CONFIDENCE: 0.78 | NOTE: boring is safe. safe is good.&gt;</p> <hr> <p>*End Chapter 14*</p> </div> Where does the route go next? * [[Read optional scene 14a: First Contract Debrief->Act1 14a First Contract Debrief]] * [[Read optional scene 14b: Small Success Contract->Act1 14b Small Success Contract]] * [[Read optional scene 14c: Bridge Transit Ritual->Act1 14c Bridge Transit Ritual]] * [[Read optional scene 14d: Orin Teaches Bearing->Act1 14d Orin Teaches Bearing]] * [[Continue to Chapter 15: Ghost Net Lesson->Act1 15 Ghost Net Lesson]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Optional 14a: First Contract Debrief</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 14a_first_contract_debrief.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **[SCENE - OCCURS POST-CH14, AFTER FIRST WITNESSED EVIDENCE PULL]** &gt; *Location: Lumen Thief secure conference room* &gt; *Present: Avyanna, Vesper, Elisira, Rho* &gt; *Purpose: Post-contract processing, teaching operational doctrine, receipts culture in practice*</p> <hr> <p>[The conference room is utilitarian. One table. Six chairs. A secure terminal. And the weight of what Avyanna just watched settling into the space like dust after an explosion.]</p> <p>[She sits with her hands folded in her lap. Trying to look professional. Trying not to shake.]</p> <p>[Vesper sits across from her, datapad open, stylus moving across the screen in precise strokes. Documenting. Always documenting.]</p> <p>[Elisira stands near the door-not blocking it, but close enough to intervene if needed. Their posture says: this is a safe room, but safety is a verb, not a state.]</p> <p>[Rho sprawls in a chair, looking casual in the way that means he&#x27;s watching everything.]</p> <hr> <p>Vesper: [without looking up] You witnessed your first evidence extraction today. Before we file the contract completion, we debrief. This is procedure.</p> <p>Avyanna: [careful] Okay.</p> <p>Vesper: [meeting her eyes] Debrief has three purposes. One: ensure you processed what you saw. Two: document any discrepancies between contract terms and execution. Three: identify lessons for next time.</p> <p>Vesper: You&#x27;re not being graded. You&#x27;re being included. Understood?</p> <p>Avyanna: [nodding] Understood.</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper taps the datapad. A holographic summary appears above the table: contract number, client name, evidence type, location, timestamp, crew roster.]</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] Contract 7823-G. Client: independent station administrator. Evidence requested: financial records proving embezzlement by Compact-backed oversight committee. Location: secure data vault, station Helix Four. Crew: Vesper, Elisira, Rho, Avyanna observing.</p> <p>Vesper: Contract executed successfully. Evidence retrieved, verified, transferred to client. Payment received in full. No injuries. No legal complications.</p> <p>[She looks at Avyanna.]</p> <p>Vesper: From your perspective, what happened today?</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens. What happened today was terrifying and efficient and nothing like she expected.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [slow] You... walked into a Compact-monitored facility. You bypassed three security checkpoints. You copied files that could get the client killed. And you walked out like you&#x27;d just done a supply run.</p> <p>Rho: [grinning] We&#x27;re very good at our job.</p> <p>Vesper: [ignoring him] Accurate summary. Now: what do you think we did wrong?</p> <p>Avyanna: [startled] Wrong?</p> <p>Vesper: [patient] Every operation has inefficiencies. Moments where we could have been faster, quieter, more prepared. Part of debrief is identifying those moments so we improve next time.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna thinks back. The operation was smooth. Too smooth. Like watching a rehearsed dance where everyone knew the steps.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [hesitant] The third checkpoint. Elisira had to manually override the scanner. It took... maybe fifteen extra seconds?</p> <p>Elisira: [from the door, approving] Yes. Scanner firmware updated since last visit. Our exploit was still valid, but slower. I&#x27;ve noted it. Next time I&#x27;ll run a pre-op firmware check.</p> <p>Vesper: [noting it on the datapad] Good catch. What else?</p> <p>Avyanna: [more confident] Rho&#x27;s cover story. The supply manifest he showed the guard. It had the right station codes, but the date was wrong. The guard didn&#x27;t notice, but someone more careful might have.</p> <p>Rho: [sitting up] Wait, what?</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s stylus pauses. She looks at Rho.]</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] Your manifest showed yesterday&#x27;s date. Not today&#x27;s.</p> <p>Rho: [groaning] Shit. I grabbed the wrong template. I&#x27;ll fix the rotation.</p> <p>Vesper: [noting it] Noted. Avyanna, that&#x27;s excellent observational work. You caught something we missed.</p> <p>[Avyanna blinks. She expected correction, not praise.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [uncertain] I thought... I thought I wasn&#x27;t supposed to question-</p> <p>Vesper: [interrupting] You&#x27;re crew. Crew questions everything. That&#x27;s how we stay alive.</p> <hr> <p>[Elisira steps forward, leans against the table.]</p> <p>Elisira: [to Avyanna] The mine taught you that observation without permission is insubordination. Here, observation is your job.</p> <p>Elisira: You see something wrong, you say it. During planning, during execution, during debrief. No hierarchy. No ego. Just information.</p> <p>Rho: [adding] I&#x27;d rather have you point out my fuckup in debrief than have it get us arrested in the field.</p> <p>Vesper: [dry] Or killed.</p> <p>Rho: [waving a hand] Or that.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna absorbs this. Her observations have value. Her voice has weight.]</p> <p>Vesper: [continuing] Next question. During the operation, did you feel unsafe at any point?</p> <p>Avyanna: [honest] The entire time.</p> <p>Vesper: [nodding] Reasonable. Did you feel unsafe because of external threat, or because of crew actions?</p> <p>[Avyanna parses the question. Unsafe from the Compact guards and the locked doors and the sheer illegality of what they were doing-yes. Unsafe from her crew-no.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [careful] External threat. Not crew.</p> <p>Vesper: [noting it] Good. That&#x27;s the distinction that matters.</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper swipes the hologram. A second screen appears: contract terms alongside execution log.]</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] This is receipts culture in practice. Every contract has terms. We document execution. If there&#x27;s a discrepancy, we note it and renegotiate or refund.</p> <p>Vesper: [pointing] Contract specified &quot;financial records from the past six months.&quot; We retrieved seven months because the embezzlement pattern extended further back. We notified the client before transfer. They accepted the additional data and paid the original amount.</p> <p>Vesper: If they&#x27;d refused the extra data, we would have deleted it per their request. Their choice. Their evidence. Our execution.</p> <hr> <p>Avyanna: [frowning] Why would they refuse extra evidence?</p> <p>Elisira: [flat] Because more evidence means more risk. Some clients want exactly enough to prove their case, not enough to become targets themselves.</p> <p>Rho: [leaning back] Evidence is a weapon. You don&#x27;t hand someone a nuke when they asked for a knife.</p> <p>Vesper: [adding] Which is why we always confirm scope before transfer. Consent applies to information too.</p> <p>[Avyanna nods slowly. Everything here is about boundaries. Even data.]</p> <hr> <p>Vesper: [tapping the datapad] Next: crew coordination. Avyanna, did you understand your role during the operation?</p> <p>Avyanna: [uncertain] I was observing. Learning. Not participating.</p> <p>Vesper: [correcting] You were the witness. Your role was to observe and confirm that we executed the contract as agreed. No deviation. No unauthorized actions.</p> <p>Vesper: If we&#x27;d done something outside contract scope-if we&#x27;d stolen extra files, planted false data, caused harm-you would report it. To me, to the Guild, to whoever needed to know.</p> <p>Avyanna: [slowly] You&#x27;re... giving me oversight. Over you.</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] I&#x27;m giving you a role in accountability. This crew doesn&#x27;t operate on blind trust. We operate on verified behavior and documented action.</p> <hr> <p>[Elisira pulls out a small recording device. Sets it on the table.]</p> <p>Elisira: You were also carrying this. Audio recorder. Guild-certified, tamper-evident. It captured the entire operation.</p> <p>[Avyanna stares at it. She didn&#x27;t know she was carrying evidence.]</p> <p>Elisira: [calm] You didn&#x27;t know because we didn&#x27;t tell you. That was intentional. If you&#x27;d known, you might have acted differently. We needed your authentic witness perspective, not a performance.</p> <p>Elisira: [tapping the recorder] This audio will be sealed and stored. If the client ever claims we deviated from contract, we have proof we didn&#x27;t. If we ever need to prove we operated within Guild rules, we have proof we did.</p> <p>Elisira: And if you ever need to prove you were a non-participating witness, you have proof of that too.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s hands tighten in her lap.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [shaking] You&#x27;re protecting me. With evidence.</p> <p>Vesper: [meeting her eyes] Always. You&#x27;re crew. That means we document everything that keeps you safe.</p> <p>Rho: [adding] Also keeps us honest. Hard to be corrupt bastards when every op is recorded.</p> <p>Elisira: [dry] Some of us find that easier than others.</p> <p>Rho: [grinning] I&#x27;m a paragon of virtue and you know it.</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper returns to the datapad. The hologram shifts: contract completion checklist.]</p> <p>Vesper: Final section. Lessons and adjustments.</p> <p>[She reads through the list.]</p> <p>Vesper: One: Run firmware checks on target security systems within 24 hours of operation. Assigned: Elisira.</p> <p>Vesper: Two: Verify all cover documents for date accuracy before deployment. Assigned: Rho.</p> <p>Vesper: Three: Include witness orientation in pre-op briefing so Avyanna knows what tools she&#x27;s carrying. Assigned: me.</p> <p>Vesper: [looking up] Anything else to add?</p> <hr> <p>[Rho raises a hand like a student.]</p> <p>Rho: We should&#x27;ve brought snacks. Four-hour op, no food. I was fading by hour three.</p> <p>Vesper: [noting it without judgment] Logistics: pack rations for ops exceeding three hours. Assigned: Rho, since you&#x27;re apparently motivated by hunger.</p> <p>Rho: [cheerful] My primary motivation in life.</p> <p>Elisira: [to Avyanna] This is how we improve. Small adjustments. No blame. Just iteration.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] In the mine, mistakes meant punishment. Here, mistakes mean... notes?</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] Mistakes mean information. We document, we adjust, we improve. Punishment teaches fear. Documentation teaches competence.</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper closes the datapad. The hologram disappears.]</p> <p>Vesper: Debrief complete. Contract 7823-G filed as successful. Avyanna, your witness report will be logged. You performed well.</p> <p>Avyanna: [surprised] I barely did anything.</p> <p>Vesper: [flat] You observed. You identified errors we missed. You remained calm under pressure. That&#x27;s not barely anything. That&#x27;s exactly what we needed.</p> <p>[She stands.]</p> <p>Vesper: Next contract, you&#x27;ll have a more active role. We&#x27;ll brief you in advance. You&#x27;ll have time to ask questions, request accommodations, decline if you&#x27;re not ready.</p> <p>Vesper: [meeting her eyes] You&#x27;re learning operations. That means incremental responsibility, not sudden immersion. Understood?</p> <p>Avyanna: [nodding] Understood.</p> <hr> <p>[Elisira picks up the recorder, stows it in a secure case.]</p> <p>Elisira: [to Avyanna] You did well today. First op is always disorienting. You kept your head.</p> <p>Avyanna: [uncertain] I was terrified the whole time.</p> <p>Elisira: [approving] Good. Terror means you understood the stakes. Recklessness would worry me. Caution is smart.</p> <p>Rho: [standing, stretching] Also, for the record, that guard you thought might catch my date error? Absolute hardass. If we&#x27;d been less prepared, she would&#x27;ve detained us. You read the room correctly.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna looks around the table. Three people who just committed a crime for money. Three people who debriefed it like a engineering problem. Three people who treated her observations as valuable, not insubordinate.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [careful] Can I ask a question?</p> <p>Vesper: [sitting back down] Always.</p> <p>Avyanna: Why do you do this? The contracts. The evidence work. It&#x27;s dangerous. You could do... safer things.</p> <hr> <p>[Rho and Elisira exchange a look. Vesper&#x27;s expression doesn&#x27;t change.]</p> <p>Vesper: [measured] Because someone has to. The Compact controls information. Controls narratives. Buries evidence that contradicts their story.</p> <p>Vesper: We retrieve what they bury. We give people the tools to fight back with facts, not just hope.</p> <p>Elisira: [adding] The mine used information control to keep you compliant. Told you extraction was legal. Told you indenture was legitimate. Buried the documents that proved otherwise.</p> <p>Elisira: We&#x27;re the people who dig up those documents. For people who can&#x27;t dig them up themselves.</p> <p>Rho: [quieter] It&#x27;s dangerous, yeah. But so is letting the Compact erase everyone who inconveniences them.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna sits with that. Evidence work as resistance. Contracts as counterweight to institutional power.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [slow] The client today. The station administrator. If they use that evidence against the Compact committee-</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] They&#x27;ll have a chance to fight. That&#x27;s all we provide. A chance. What they do with it is their choice.</p> <p>Vesper: We don&#x27;t pick sides. We don&#x27;t guarantee outcomes. We retrieve evidence. What happens after that is outside our scope.</p> <p>[She stands again, final this time.]</p> <p>Vesper: That&#x27;s the doctrine. We&#x27;re retrieval specialists, not revolutionaries. We don&#x27;t fix the system. We give people tools to navigate it.</p> <hr> <p>[The debrief ends. Rho leaves first, muttering about food. Elisira follows, taking the secured recorder with them.]</p> <p>[Avyanna stands. Her legs are steadier than she expected.]</p> <p>Vesper: [at the door, turning back] Avyanna.</p> <p>[Avyanna looks up.]</p> <p>Vesper: [quiet] You were right to be scared today. But you were also present, observant, and functional. That&#x27;s the balance we need.</p> <p>Vesper: Next time will be less scary. Not because the work is safer, but because you&#x27;ll know what to expect.</p> <p>Avyanna: [nodding] Thank you. For including me.</p> <p>Vesper: [slight smile] You&#x27;re crew. Inclusion isn&#x27;t a gift. It&#x27;s default.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna leaves the conference room. In the corridor, she leans against the bulkhead and breathes.]</p> <p>(I watched a crime. I participated in a crime. And then we debriefed it like a school project.)</p> <p>[Her hands are shaking, but not from fear. From adrenaline. From the strange, destabilizing realization that she just did something illegal and it felt... right.]</p> <p>(The client gets to fight back. Because we retrieved the evidence. Because information is a weapon and we&#x27;re arming the people who need it.)</p> <p>[The shard at the base of her skull is warm. Not warning. Just... witnessing.]</p> <p>(Warmth flickers at the base of her skull-wordless, but it lands like a rubric: evidence, weapon, choice.)</p> <p>[She pushes off the wall and walks back to her quarters. Tomorrow, there will be another contract. Another operation. Another debrief.]</p> <p>[And slowly, incrementally, she&#x27;s learning to be crew.]</p> <hr> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// CONTRACT LOG: 7823-G filed as complete | WITNESS REPORT: filed and verified | CREW DEBRIEF: completed per protocol | ASSESSMENT: trainee performed within expected parameters | OPERATIONAL NOTES: two process improvements identified and assigned&gt;</p> <p>&lt;VESPER PERSONAL LOG: &quot;She caught the date error on Rho&#x27;s manifest. Sharp eyes. Sharper than some Guild contractors I&#x27;ve worked with. She&#x27;s scared, but she&#x27;s learning to function through fear. That&#x27;s the skillset that matters.&quot;&gt;</p> <hr> <p>*End Scene*</p> </div> * [[Continue to optional scene 14b: Small Success Contract->Act1 14b Small Success Contract]] * [[Continue to Chapter 15: Ghost Net Lesson->Act1 15 Ghost Net Lesson]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Optional 14b: Small Success Contract</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 14b_small_success_contract.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **[SCENE - OCCURS POST-CH14b, WEEK THREE]** &gt; *Location: Various (station, ship, client meeting)* &gt; *Present: Avyanna, Rho, Elisira* &gt; *Purpose: First active participation in contract work, building competence through low-stakes success*</p> <hr> <p>[The contract is small. Almost insultingly small by Guild standards. Retrieve maintenance logs from a station administrator&#x27;s office. The logs prove that a landlord has been falsifying safety inspections. Evidence for a tenant collective lawsuit.]</p> <p>[Fee: two thousand credits. Risk: minimal. Complexity: low.]</p> <p>[Rho called it a &quot;training contract.&quot; Elisira called it &quot;appropriate scope for a trainee.&quot; Vesper called it &quot;exactly the right size for learning without drowning.&quot;]</p> <p>[Avyanna calls it terrifying.]</p> <hr> <p>[They&#x27;re on Station Meris-6. Mid-tier. Not Guild-controlled, not Compact-controlled. Independent governance, which means bureaucracy moves slow and security is underfunded.]</p> <p>[Perfect for a simple retrieval.]</p> <p>[Rho, Elisira, and Avyanna stand outside the administrative building. It&#x27;s late afternoon local time-shift change, when security is distracted.]</p> <p>Rho: [checking his datapad] Okay. Recap the plan. Avyanna, you&#x27;re up.</p> <p>Avyanna: [nervous] We go in as contractors doing a safety audit. You have forged credentials. Elisira is our data specialist. I&#x27;m... the assistant. We request access to the maintenance logs for &quot;verification purposes.&quot; We copy the files. We leave.</p> <p>Rho: [approving] Good. And if someone questions the credentials?</p> <p>Avyanna: [reciting] You refer them to the contractor registry, which you&#x27;ve temporarily backdoored to show our fake company as legitimate. The backdoor expires in two hours.</p> <p>Elisira: [adding] And if security escalates beyond verbal questioning?</p> <p>Avyanna: [meeting her eyes] We abort. No evidence is worth getting arrested.</p> <p>Elisira: [satisfied] Correct. Let&#x27;s move.</p> <hr> <p>[They enter the building. Rho walks with confident authority-the body language of someone who belongs. Elisira moves like a professional data specialist: focused, efficient, slightly bored. Avyanna tries to imitate them both and probably looks nervous, but nervous assistants are normal. She&#x27;s not blowing cover yet.]</p> <p>[The front desk guard barely looks at them.]</p> <p>Guard: [distracted] Purpose of visit?</p> <p>Rho: [handing over credentials] Safety audit. Maintenance log review. Scheduled via the contractor portal.</p> <p>[The guard scans the credentials. Pauses. Frowns.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s heart rate spikes. Something&#x27;s wrong. They&#x27;re caught. They&#x27;re-]</p> <p>Guard: [sighing] System&#x27;s slow today. One sec.</p> <p>[The guard whacks the scanner. It beeps. Approves the credentials.]</p> <p>Guard: [waving them through] Third floor. Office 3-K. Don&#x27;t touch anything you&#x27;re not supposed to.</p> <p>Rho: [cheerful] Wouldn&#x27;t dream of it.</p> <hr> <p>[They take the lift to the third floor. As soon as the doors close, Rho grins.]</p> <p>Rho: [to Avyanna] See? You looked nervous, but nervous is normal. You didn&#x27;t bolt. You didn&#x27;t freeze. You stayed in character. That&#x27;s the hard part.</p> <p>Avyanna: [shakily] I thought we were caught.</p> <p>Elisira: [calm] We weren&#x27;t. And if we had been, we would&#x27;ve had thirty seconds to abort before actual consequences. You stayed functional. That&#x27;s what matters.</p> <p>[The lift opens. Third floor. Corridor lined with office doors. They find 3-K.]</p> <hr> <p>[Office 3-K is empty. Some low-level administrator&#x27;s workspace. Desk, terminal, filing cabinets, the smell of old coffee and neglect.]</p> <p>Elisira: [moving to the terminal] Stand by the door. If someone comes, cough twice. I&#x27;ll need twenty seconds to cover my tracks and disconnect.</p> <p>Avyanna: [positioning herself] Understood.</p> <p>[Rho stays near Elisira, watching the data transfer. Avyanna watches the corridor through the door&#x27;s small window. Footsteps. Voices. People moving past but not stopping.]</p> <p>[Her heart is pounding. Her palms are sweating. But she&#x27;s doing it. She&#x27;s on lookout. She&#x27;s crew.]</p> <hr> <p>Elisira: [working quickly] Files are encrypted. Basic algorithm. Give me... ninety seconds.</p> <p>[Ninety seconds feels like ninety hours. Avyanna watches the corridor. Someone walks past. They don&#x27;t look in. Keep moving.]</p> <p>[Another person. This one slows. Glances at the office door.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s breath catches. She doesn&#x27;t cough. Not yet. Just someone curious, not someone investigating.]</p> <p>[The person keeps walking.]</p> <p>Elisira: [low voice] Sixty seconds.</p> <hr> <p>[Footsteps. Getting closer. Someone walking with purpose. Avyanna tenses.]</p> <p>[The person stops at the office next door. Keys jingle. A door opens and closes.]</p> <p>[Not a threat. Just a neighbor.]</p> <p>Elisira: [disconnecting] Done. Files copied, logs scrubbed. Let&#x27;s move.</p> <p>[They leave the office. Lock the door behind them. Walk back to the lift with the same professional energy they entered with. No rushing. No panic. Just contractors finishing a job.]</p> <p>[Back in the lobby, they nod at the guard.]</p> <p>Rho: [casual] All set. Thanks for the access.</p> <p>Guard: [not looking up] Yeah. Have a good one.</p> <hr> <p>[They exit the building. Walk three blocks. Turn into an alley. Stop.]</p> <p>[Rho leans against the wall, exhaling.]</p> <p>Rho: [grinning] Clean op. No complications. Avyanna, you did great.</p> <p>Avyanna: [shaking] I barely did anything.</p> <p>Elisira: [correcting] You maintained lookout. You stayed calm when people passed. You didn&#x27;t signal false alarm when the neighbor scared you. That&#x27;s exactly what we needed.</p> <p>[She pulls out a small data chip. Holds it up.]</p> <p>Elisira: [to Avyanna] This chip has maintenance logs proving safety violations. Because you helped us retrieve it. That&#x27;s not nothing. That&#x27;s evidence. That&#x27;s power.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna stares at the chip. So small. So much weight.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] What happens now?</p> <p>Rho: [pushing off the wall] Now we verify the files. Make sure they&#x27;re intact and unaltered. Then we deliver them to the client. Then we get paid.</p> <p>Rho: [to Avyanna] And you get a cut. Ten percent. That&#x27;s trainee rate for active participation.</p> <p>Avyanna: [surprised] I get paid?</p> <p>Elisira: [matter-of-fact] You worked. You contributed. You get compensated. That&#x27;s how crew works.</p> <p>[Avyanna calculates. Ten percent of two thousand credits. Two hundred credits. Hers. For one hour of work and a lot of fear.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [uncertain] That seems like a lot.</p> <p>Rho: [snorting] Kid, you were lookout on an evidence retrieval op. That&#x27;s skilled labor. You earned it.</p> <hr> <p>[They return to the ship. Elisira verifies the files in the secure workspace. Avyanna watches the data scroll across the screen-maintenance logs, falsified dates, evidence of negligence stretching back three years.]</p> <p>Elisira: [satisfied] Clean data. No corruption. No tampering. This is exactly what the client needs.</p> <p>[She copies the files to a secure transfer drive.]</p> <p>Elisira: [to Avyanna] Want to come to the client handoff? See the full contract cycle?</p> <p>Avyanna: [hesitant] Is that... allowed?</p> <p>Rho: [from the doorway] You&#x27;re crew. You participated in the retrieval. You get to see the delivery. That&#x27;s standard procedure.</p> <p>Avyanna: [nervous] Okay. Yes. I want to see.</p> <hr> <p>[They meet the client at a neutral Guild-registered location. A cafe. Public. Documented. No room for coercion or deviation.]</p> <p>[The client is younger than Avyanna expected. Mid-twenties, wearing clothes that have been mended multiple times. Someone who works hard and has little left over.]</p> <p>[Elisira hands them the drive.]</p> <p>Elisira: [professional] Maintenance logs as requested. Three years of data. We&#x27;ve verified authenticity. No alterations, no deletions. This is what existed in the system as of six hours ago.</p> <p>Client: [taking the drive carefully, like it&#x27;s fragile] This is... this is everything we need. To prove he&#x27;s been lying. To prove we&#x27;re not just complaining.</p> <p>Rho: [gentle] You were never just complaining. You were identifying harm. Now you have receipts.</p> <hr> <p>[The client&#x27;s eyes are wet. They blink rapidly, trying to hold composure.]</p> <p>Client: [quiet] How much do I owe you?</p> <p>Elisira: [checking her datapad] Two thousand credits, as agreed. Payment can be transferred via Guild escrow now, or you have seventy-two hours per contract terms.</p> <p>Client: [pulling out a credit chit] Now. I want to pay now. So this is done.</p> <p>[They transfer the credits. The transaction completes. Guild-certified. Documented. Binding.]</p> <p>Client: [standing] Thank you. I don&#x27;t... I don&#x27;t know how else to say it. Just thank you.</p> <p>Elisira: [nodding] Use the evidence well. That&#x27;s the best thanks.</p> <hr> <p>[The client leaves. Rho, Elisira, and Avyanna remain at the table.]</p> <p>Rho: [to Avyanna] So. How does it feel?</p> <p>Avyanna: [processing] Strange. We just... gave someone power. With files.</p> <p>Rho: [nodding] Yep. That&#x27;s the work. Evidence is power. We retrieve it. They use it. System changes-or doesn&#x27;t, but at least they had a chance to fight.</p> <p>Elisira: [adding] And you helped. First active contract. First successful completion. First payment.</p> <p>[She transfers credits to Avyanna&#x27;s account. Two hundred credits. Documented. Real.]</p> <p>Elisira: [meeting her eyes] This is what you&#x27;re capable of. Small contract, yes. But successful. Competent. Ethical. That&#x27;s the foundation.</p> <hr> <p>[Back on the ship, Avyanna sits in her quarters and stares at her credit balance. Two hundred credits. Hers. Earned through work. Through contribution. Through being crew.]</p> <p>[The mine paid nothing. The mine took everything and called it wages. Here, she worked one operation and has money. Real money. Money she can spend or save or use however she wants.]</p> <p>(I did this. I helped retrieve evidence. I got paid. I was useful.)</p> <p>[The shard at the base of her skull is warm. Proud, maybe. Or just witnessing.]</p> <p>(The warmth at the base of her skull pulses once: work, choice, earned.)</p> <p>[She lies back on her bunk. Tomorrow, there might be another contract. Bigger, maybe. More complex. More risk. But tonight, she has two hundred credits and the knowledge that she can do this. She can be crew. She can contribute. She can earn her place through work, not just survival.]</p> <p>[And that&#x27;s enough. For now, that&#x27;s enough.]</p> <hr> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// CONTRACT LOG: 7852-M completed successfully | TRAINEE ROLE: lookout and active participant | PERFORMANCE: appropriate for skill level | COMPENSATION: 200 credits transferred to trainee account | ASSESSMENT: first successful active contract participation | STATUS: trainee competence developing&gt;</p> <p>&lt;RHO PERSONAL LOG: &quot;Small contract. Simple retrieval. But she was shaking the whole time and she did it anyway. That&#x27;s courage. That&#x27;s what we need. Also, her face when we paid her? Worth every credit. She&#x27;s learning that work has value. That she has value.&quot;&gt;</p> <p>&lt;ELISIRA PERSONAL LOG: &quot;Training contract executed cleanly. Avyanna maintained position, didn&#x27;t panic, didn&#x27;t blow cover. Next time we&#x27;ll increase complexity. But today? Today she learned she can do this. That&#x27;s the first step toward believing she can do harder things.&quot;&gt;</p> <hr> <p>*End Scene*</p> </div> * [[Continue to optional scene 14c: Bridge Transit Ritual->Act1 14c Bridge Transit Ritual]] * [[Continue to Chapter 15: Ghost Net Lesson->Act1 15 Ghost Net Lesson]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Optional 14c: Bridge Transit Ritual</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 14c_bridge_transit_ritual.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **[SCENE - OCCURS POST-CH14, FIRST REAL TRANSIT]** &gt; *Location: Bridge / corridor approach* &gt; *Present: Full crew (bridge rotation), AI citizens via telemetry* &gt; *Teaching: travel is work; fear is handled as procedure; usefulness is a job, not a personality*</p> <hr> <p>[The bridge smells like coffee and ozone and focus.]</p> <p>[Jalen sits in the pilot cradle, spine straight, hands light on the controls like anything heavier would insult the ship.]</p> <p>[Elisira stands behind them with a slate, posture relaxed in the way that means she is not relaxed.]</p> <p>[Vesper has one hand on a rail and the other on a datapad full of forms that never stop wanting signatures.]</p> <p>[Rho leans in the doorway with a mug and the expression of someone prepared to fight the universe with caffeine.]</p> <p>[Elia is sprawled near weapons control, boots on a brace, looking bored on purpose.]</p> <p>[Avyanna takes the last open station and tries not to look like she is begging to be allowed to exist there.]</p> <p>(Bridge means responsibility. Responsibility means they can be angry at you.)</p> <p>Jalen: [without looking back] Strap in.</p> <p>[Avyanna straps in immediately.]</p> <p>[Then stops herself.]</p> <p>[Elia&#x27;s drill rises like a reflex with teeth.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [careful] Why.</p> <p>[Jalen&#x27;s mouth twitches. Not quite a smile.]</p> <p>Jalen: Because this corridor is spiteful.</p> <p>Elia: [bright] Because the map lies.</p> <p>Elisira: Because the lane has been shifting.</p> <p>Vesper: Because insurance pretends it&#x27;s weather.</p> <p>Rho: Because if you hit a seam wrong, you bite your tongue off.</p> <p>[Avyanna blinks.]</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s... all the same answer.</p> <p>Elia: Correct. Welcome to the galaxy.</p> <hr> <p>[Jalen taps the console. A route path blooms on the holo-blue line through blackness, annotated with numbers and warnings.]</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// OBSERVATION: corridor stability rating &quot;marginal&quot; | NOTE: recommended speed reduction 12% | INFERENCE: reduced peak shear | CONFIDENCE: 0.84&gt;</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// COMMENTARY: marginal is my favorite kind of safe&gt;</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// REMINDER: keep limbs inside existence at all times&gt;</p> <p>Elia: [to the air] Thank you, Bubbles. Very inspiring.</p> <p>Bubbles: [over comm] You are welcome.</p> <p>[The ship shifts. A subtle pressure change, like the universe leaning in to listen.]</p> <p>[The stars on the forward display smear for a heartbeat.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s stomach drops.]</p> <p>(Mine collapse.)</p> <p>[The memory tries to overlay reality: rock groaning, dust filling lungs, the moment the ceiling decides to become ground.]</p> <p>[Her hands clench on the armrests.]</p> <p>[She counts exits without thinking.]</p> <p>[There are none. It&#x27;s a bridge. It&#x27;s a box. It&#x27;s a trap.]</p> <p>Vesper: [quiet, not looking at her] Check.</p> <p>[Avyanna swallows.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [too fast] Green.</p> <p>[A beat.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [correcting] Yellow.</p> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s hand shifts-doesn&#x27;t touch her, just moves closer to the rail between them, a visible choice not to invade.]</p> <p>Vesper: Thank you.</p> <p>(Thank you for telling the truth. Not thank you for being okay.)</p> <p>[Jalen&#x27;s voice stays calm, which is its own kind of weapon.]</p> <p>Jalen: Drift left two degrees. Hold. Hold.</p> <p>[The ship responds like it likes being spoken to with respect.]</p> <p>[Pressure eases.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s lungs remember they are allowed to take air.]</p> <p>Rho: [sipping] See. Fine.</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// RESPONSE: &quot;fine&quot; is not a metric&gt;</p> <p>Rho: [without missing a beat] &quot;Operationally adequate.&quot;</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// ACCEPTABLE&gt;</p> <hr> <p>[A small shudder runs through the deck plates.]</p> <p>[Not violent. Just... a seam.]</p> <p>Elisira: [to Jalen] That was early.</p> <p>Jalen: Yeah.</p> <p>Jalen: [flat] The corridor is lying today.</p> <p>Elia: [cheerful] I love it when the universe has personality.</p> <p>Vesper: It&#x27;s not personality. It&#x27;s incentives.</p> <p>Elia: [glancing over] Everything is incentives with you.</p> <p>Vesper: [dry] Everything is incentives with everyone. Most people just don&#x27;t admit it.</p> <p>[Avyanna watches them argue like it&#x27;s a ritual. No one is afraid of disagreement here.]</p> <p>(In the mine, arguing meant punishment. Here it means... mapping.)</p> <p>[Jalen flicks a switch. The display shifts-blue line, but now with a second overlay: a thin gray shadow of possible alternatives.]</p> <p>Avyanna: What&#x27;s that.</p> <p>Jalen: Official route.</p> <p>Jalen: And what the ship thinks the route is.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] Those aren&#x27;t the same.</p> <p>Jalen: [looking at her for the first time] Now you&#x27;re learning.</p> <hr> <p>[They ride the corridor like a river that dislikes boats.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s job is small.]</p> <p>[Read out sensor warnings when Cinnamon flags them.]</p> <p>[Repeat Elisira&#x27;s directions back so no one mishears.]</p> <p>[Keep a log of time-stamps the way Vesper taught her: claim, source, when.]</p> <p>[Useful. Quiet. Not invisible.]</p> <p>[At one point, the ship lurches harder.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s vision narrows.]</p> <p>[The mine tries to come back.]</p> <p>[Rho&#x27;s voice cuts in, rough and real.]</p> <p>Rho: Avy. Breathe.</p> <p>[Avyanna inhales.]</p> <p>[Jalen exhales at the same time-slow on purpose.]</p> <p>[It pulls her rhythm with it, like gravity.]</p> <p>[She holds on.]</p> <p>[The corridor releases them with a final shiver, grudgingly.]</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// OBSERVATION: stability improving | INFERENCE: seam passed | CONFIDENCE: 0.88&gt;</p> <p>Elia: [stretching] That was disappointingly non-lethal.</p> <p>Elisira: [without looking at her] We can arrange lethal if you&#x27;re bored.</p> <p>Elia: [delighted] She loves me.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s mouth twitches.]</p> <p>[It&#x27;s almost a smile.]</p> <p>[She files that too.]</p> <hr> <p>[Afterward, nobody disperses like they&#x27;re fleeing the smell of fear.]</p> <p>[Jalen eases the ship down into a quiet stretch of corridor-space-stable enough to breathe, unstable enough to keep respect.]</p> <p>Elisira: [to Avyanna] Debrief.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s shoulders go rigid.]</p> <p>(Debrief means you explain why you failed.)</p> <p>Avyanna: I-</p> <p>[Elisira waits, patient in the way that doesn&#x27;t let you hide behind panic.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [working] We hit a seam early. The deck shuddered before the display predicted it. The pressure change came before the warning ping.</p> <p>Elisira: Good.</p> <p>Avyanna: Jalen adjusted two degrees left and reduced speed. Cinnamon flagged &quot;marginal&quot; stability, but it improved after the seam.</p> <p>Elisira: What did your body do.</p> <p>[Avyanna blinks.]</p> <p>Avyanna: My body-</p> <p>Vesper: [quiet] This is evidence too.</p> <p>[Avyanna swallows.]</p> <p>Avyanna: My hands clenched. I went cold. I... counted exits.</p> <p>[Elia makes a small sound, not quite a joke.]</p> <p>Elia: Same.</p> <p>Avyanna: [startled] You count exits.</p> <p>Elia: [bright] I count fun. Different category.</p> <p>Elisira: [flat] Elia.</p> <p>Elia: [innocent] I was validating.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s mouth twitches again.]</p> <p>[Rho drinks his coffee like it&#x27;s a shield and watches her carefully, as if counting how long it takes her to come back.]</p> <p>Rho: You breathed when I told you.</p> <p>Avyanna: Yes.</p> <p>Rho: Good.</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// OBSERVATION: trainee returned to baseline within 46 seconds | INFERENCE: recovery improving | CONFIDENCE: 0.81&gt;</p> <p>[Avyanna looks at the telemetry and feels, unexpectedly, protected by being measured without being judged.]</p> <p>Vesper: Log it.</p> <p>Avyanna: [automatic] Yes.</p> <p>[She catches herself.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Why.</p> <p>Vesper: Because next time your body tells you a story, we compare it to the data.</p> <p>Vesper: If the story is wrong, we correct it. If the story is right, we prepare.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] Memory lies under stress.</p> <p>Vesper: [a small nod] Exactly.</p> <hr> <p>[Later, in the corridor outside the bridge, Avyanna stops beside a wall panel and opens her notebook.]</p> <p>[She writes in block letters, the way Vesper taught her to write claims:]</p> <p>&gt; **CLAIM:** corridor seam occurred early &gt; &gt; **OBSERVATION:** deck shudder preceded warning ping &gt; &gt; **RESPONSE:** speed reduction + lateral correction &gt; &gt; **BODY:** cold, exit-counting, then recovery</p> <p>[She stares at the last line.]</p> <p>(Body: witness. Not enemy.)</p> <p>[A shadow falls over the page.]</p> <p>[Jalen stands there, hands in pockets, pretending this isn&#x27;t care.]</p> <p>Jalen: You did fine.</p> <p>Avyanna: [startled] Fine is not a metric.</p> <p>[Jalen&#x27;s mouth twitches.]</p> <p>Jalen: Operationally adequate.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] Better.</p> <p>Jalen: Don&#x27;t make it a habit.</p> <p>[They walk away before Avyanna can turn gratitude into a performance.]</p> </div> * [[Continue to optional scene 14d: Orin Teaches Bearing->Act1 14d Orin Teaches Bearing]] * [[Continue to Chapter 15: Ghost Net Lesson->Act1 15 Ghost Net Lesson]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Optional 14d: Orin Teaches Bearing</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 14d_orin_teaches_bearing.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **[INTERSTITIAL - OCCURS POST-CH14]** &gt; *Trigger: Jalen refuses to teach &quot;instinct&quot; without ethics* &gt; *Purpose: Orin Vey&#x27;s framework, carried forward*</p> <hr> <p>[Jalen teaches like every mistake will kill someone.]</p> <p>[Which is not paranoia.]</p> <p>[It&#x27;s experience, translated into a voice that never softens.]</p> <p>[Avyanna sits in the nav alcove with her hands folded in her lap so she can&#x27;t fidget them into a confession.]</p> <p>Jalen: Names are politics.</p> <p>Avyanna: So we don&#x27;t use them.</p> <p>Jalen: We use them when we need leverage.</p> <p>Jalen: We use coordinates when we need truth.</p> <p>[Avyanna nods.]</p> <p>[Then she asks the question she&#x27;s been holding like contraband.]</p> <p>Avyanna: How do you know where to go.</p> <p>Jalen: Bearing.</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s just a word.</p> <p>Jalen: That&#x27;s most of your problems.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s mouth twitches.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Teach me, then.</p> <p>[Jalen&#x27;s gaze flicks to her, sharp.]</p> <p>Jalen: You want the mechanics or the ethics.</p> <p>Avyanna: [honest] Both.</p> <p>[Jalen goes still.]</p> <p>[Not suspicious.]</p> <p>[...Relieved, maybe.]</p> <hr> <p>## Intercut - Orin Vey (A Cockpit Rule)</p> <p>[The cockpit is cramped.]</p> <p>[The ship is cheap and too loud and alive in the way a thing is alive when it doesn&#x27;t have enough redundancy.]</p> <p>[Jalen is eight and small and furious about being alive at all.]</p> <p>[Orin sits in the pilot seat like they were born there-hands steady, eyes bright with the kind of attention that makes routes behave.]</p> <p>[Orin doesn&#x27;t ask Jalen&#x27;s name first.]</p> <p>[Orin asks something else.]</p> <p>Orin: Why do you want to learn this.</p> <p>[Jalen&#x27;s throat tightens.]</p> <p>Jalen: So I don&#x27;t die.</p> <p>Orin: That&#x27;s a reason.</p> <p>Orin: Not a why.</p> <p>Jalen: [spits it] So no one else dies because of me.</p> <p>[Orin&#x27;s mouth twitches.]</p> <p>Orin: That&#x27;s closer.</p> <p>Orin: Bearing isn&#x27;t about the route you take.</p> <p>Orin: It&#x27;s about knowing why you&#x27;re taking it.</p> <p>[Jalen glares.]</p> <p>Jalen: That sounds like philosophy.</p> <p>Orin: It&#x27;s survival.</p> <p>[Orin taps the nav display.]</p> <p>[A route line flickers, then three alternatives bloom around it like options in a cruel game.]</p> <p>Orin: Look.</p> <p>[Jalen looks.]</p> <p>[The alternatives make their stomach turn.]</p> <p>Jalen: They&#x27;re all bad.</p> <p>Orin: Yes.</p> <p>Orin: Bearing is choosing which bad you can live with.</p> <p>Jalen: What if I choose wrong.</p> <p>Orin: Then you witness it.</p> <p>Jalen: That&#x27;s not helpful.</p> <p>Orin: It&#x27;s the only thing that stops you from becoming a liar.</p> <hr> <p>[Orin reaches over and adjusts the throttle with gentle precision.]</p> <p>[They don&#x27;t touch Jalen.]</p> <p>[They don&#x27;t force.]</p> <p>[They speak like consent is part of piloting.]</p> <p>Orin: You can refuse to fly.</p> <p>Jalen: [instant] No.</p> <p>Orin: You can refuse a route.</p> <p>Jalen: [hesitates] That&#x27;s not allowed.</p> <p>Orin: That&#x27;s a story.</p> <p>Orin: Who enforces it.</p> <p>[Jalen&#x27;s mouth opens.]</p> <p>[Closes.]</p> <p>[Orin waits.]</p> <p>Jalen: The client.</p> <p>Orin: And who enforces the client.</p> <p>[Jalen stares at the screen like it might answer.]</p> <p>Orin: If no one can reach you, their enforcement is just fear.</p> <p>Orin: Fear is real.</p> <p>Orin: But it is not a law.</p> <hr> <p>[Orin leans back.]</p> <p>Orin: Here&#x27;s the rule, then.</p> <p>Orin: When you choose a route, say the why out loud.</p> <p>Orin: If you can&#x27;t say it, you don&#x27;t understand it.</p> <p>Orin: If you don&#x27;t understand it, you don&#x27;t get to pretend the consequences were weather.</p> <p>[Jalen swallows.]</p> <p>Jalen: Okay.</p> <p>Orin: Good.</p> <p>Orin: Now fly.</p> <hr> <p>## Return - Nav Alcove</p> <p>[Avyanna blinks back into the present with the strange certainty that Jalen didn&#x27;t intend to give her that story.]</p> <p>[But something in their eyes, when they said &quot;Bearing,&quot; sounded like a memory with teeth.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] It&#x27;s about knowing why.</p> <p>[Jalen&#x27;s jaw flexes.]</p> <p>Jalen: Don&#x27;t quote things at me.</p> <p>Avyanna: You just did.</p> <p>[Jalen&#x27;s mouth tightens.]</p> <p>Jalen: [rough] Yeah.</p> <p>Jalen: So.</p> <p>Jalen: Why are you asking.</p> <p>Avyanna: Because I don&#x27;t want to be a liar.</p> <p>[Jalen exhales like that answer made the room lighter by a millimeter.]</p> <p>Jalen: Good.</p> <p>Jalen: Then you&#x27;re teachable.</p> <hr> <p>*End Chapter 14d*</p> </div> * [[Continue to Chapter 15: Ghost Net Lesson->Act1 15 Ghost Net Lesson]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Chapter 15: Ghost Net Lesson</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 15_ghost_net_lesson.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **LUMEN THIEF - TRAINING ARCHIVE (RESTRICTED):** &gt; *Subject: Information Asymmetry* &gt; *Instructor: Elisira Maren Lagrange* &gt; *Trainee: Avyanna Lagrange* &gt; *Summary: Lying is expensive. Credibility doesn&#x27;t regenerate. Use it only when nothing else works.* &gt; *Rule 1: Silence is not deception. It is survival.* &gt; *Rule 2: Most people don&#x27;t need to lie. They need to not say everything.* &gt; *Rule 3: The best misdirection is a truth arranged strategically.* &gt; *Addendum: If you get caught lying to Elisira, you were never going to get away with it anyway.*</p> <hr> <p>[The observation deck at 0200 is darker than it needs to be.]</p> <p>[Elisira chose the hour. The lighting, the angle, the exact moment Avyanna walks in and finds her already waiting-Cinnamon mentioned the scheduling request at breakfast, casual as weather.]</p> <p>[It is a lesson before the lesson starts.]</p> <p>(She knew when I&#x27;d arrive. She knows my sleep patterns. She knows everything.)</p> <p>[Avyanna stops in the doorway, cataloging: Elisira sits on the curved bench beneath the viewport, legs crossed, hands folded in her lap like a sculpture of patience. The stars behind her are cold and numerous and irrelevant.]</p> <p>Elisira: You&#x27;re on time.</p> <p>Avyanna: You said 0200.</p> <p>Elisira: I said &quot;late.&quot; I wanted to see if you&#x27;d interpret.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s stomach tightens. She interpreted wrong. Or right. She can&#x27;t tell.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Late as in... not early?</p> <p>Elisira: Late as in: if you had come at 0145 to show initiative, I would have waited in a different room and made you find me. If you came at 0215 to test my patience, I would have left.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat dries.]</p> <p>Avyanna: So 0200 was the only correct answer.</p> <p>Elisira: There were several correct answers. You found one.</p> <p>[A cooling beat: Elisira doesn&#x27;t smile, but something in her posture eases a fraction. Approval, maybe. Or acknowledgment that the test is over.]</p> <p>Elisira: Sit.</p> <p>[A mug is already on the bench beside where Avyanna is supposed to sit. Warm. Something herbal and sharp-smelling.]</p> <p>Elisira: Drink.</p> <p>Avyanna: Is it-</p> <p>Elisira: Not poison. I&#x27;m not Elia.</p> <p>[A beat. Avyanna doesn&#x27;t know what to do with the gesture.]</p> <p>Elisira: You&#x27;ll think better if your body stops treating every room like a trap.</p> <p>[Avyanna takes the mug. The warmth seeps into her palms. She sips. It tastes like something growing-mint, maybe, or a plant she doesn&#x27;t recognize.]</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna sits on the opposite end of the bench. The distance feels chosen. Everything Elisira does feels chosen-her cup placed where her hands can reach it without looking, her sightline covering both entrances.]</p> <p>[The viewport is vast and dark. A nebula bleeds color at the edge of the frame-pinks and golds, distant and dying.]</p> <p>Elisira: Do you know why I&#x27;m teaching you this?</p> <p>Avyanna: Because Elia assigned-</p> <p>Elisira: No.</p> <p>[The word lands like a blade on a table. Clean. Final.]</p> <p>Elisira: Elia suggested. I accepted. There is a difference.</p> <p>[Avyanna swallows. The heat at the base of her skull stirs-not pain, just attention. Something listening.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Why did you accept?</p> <p>[Elisira is quiet for three breaths. Avyanna counts them. In the mine, silence meant danger. Here it means: she&#x27;s deciding how much to tell you.]</p> <p>Elisira: Because you&#x27;re going to need this. And because most people teach it wrong.</p> <p>Avyanna: Teach what wrong.</p> <p>Elisira: Deception.</p> <p>[The word hangs between them. Avyanna waits for the lesson to start. Elisira waits for something else.]</p> <p>Elisira: Tell me what you think deception is.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s mind flickers to the mine. To supervisors who smiled before they punished. To workers who said &quot;I&#x27;m fine&quot; and collapsed an hour later. To herself, saying nothing, showing nothing, surviving.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Hiding what&#x27;s true.</p> <p>Elisira: Incomplete. Try again.</p> <p>Avyanna: Saying what isn&#x27;t true.</p> <p>Elisira: Also incomplete.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s jaw tightens. She feels like she&#x27;s being tested and failing, but she doesn&#x27;t know the subject.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I don&#x27;t know what you want me to say.</p> <p>[Elisira&#x27;s gaze sharpens-not angry, but focused. She looks like something deciding whether Avyanna is worth the effort of being dangerous at.]</p> <p>Elisira: Good. That&#x27;s the first honest thing you&#x27;ve said since you sat down.</p> <hr> <p>[The lesson begins.]</p> <p>[Elisira uncrosses her legs and leans forward. The casual posture drops. In its place: something precise, her hands folding once in her lap-left over right, thumbs aligned.]</p> <p>Elisira: Deception is controlling what someone knows. When they know it. What they believe about what they know.</p> <p>[Avyanna blinks.]</p> <p>(That sounds like something Vesper would say.)</p> <p>Elisira: Lying is the crudest form. It&#x27;s also the most expensive.</p> <p>Avyanna: Expensive how.</p> <p>[Elisira tilts her head. The starlight catches the edge of her jaw.]</p> <p>Elisira: Credibility. Once you&#x27;re caught lying, every true thing you say afterward is suspect. Every promise is questioned. Every alibi is investigated.</p> <p>[Her voice flattens, goes clinical.]</p> <p>Elisira: Credibility doesn&#x27;t regenerate. It&#x27;s a finite resource. Most people spend it recklessly because they don&#x27;t realize it exists.</p> <p>Avyanna: And you don&#x27;t.</p> <p>Elisira: I almost never lie.</p> <p>[Avyanna stares. The heat behind her eyes pulses-confusion, maybe. Or warning.]</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s-</p> <p>Elisira: Surprising? Convenient? A lie in itself?</p> <p>[Avyanna closes her mouth. She doesn&#x27;t know which.]</p> <p>Elisira: I&#x27;m going to teach you the difference between what I do and what people think deception is. Pay attention. I won&#x27;t repeat myself.</p> <hr> <p>[Elisira stands. Moves to the viewport. Her reflection is a ghost against the stars-translucent, watching herself watch.]</p> <p>Elisira: There are four modes of information control. Lying is only one of them, and it&#x27;s the worst.</p> <p>[She holds up one finger. Her hands are scarred, Avyanna notices. Small white lines across the knuckles. Old.]</p> <p>Elisira: One: Lying. Saying something false. High risk, high cost, leaves a trail. Sometimes necessary. Usually stupid.</p> <p>[Second finger.]</p> <p>Elisira: Two: Omission. Not saying something true. Moderate risk, lower cost. Harder to prove. Most people don&#x27;t even realize they&#x27;ve been omitted from information.</p> <p>[Third finger.]</p> <p>Elisira: Three: Misdirection. Saying something true that leads to a false conclusion. Low risk, minimal trace. Requires the listener to do the work of deceiving themselves.</p> <p>[Fourth finger.]</p> <p>Elisira: Four: Silence. Saying nothing. Zero trace. Zero liability. The universe defaults to ambiguity when you don&#x27;t fill it.</p> <p>[She turns back to Avyanna. The starlight makes her eyes look ancient.]</p> <p>Elisira: Most people think deception is about lying. It isn&#x27;t. Lying is what you do when you&#x27;ve failed at the other three.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat is dry. She swallows twice before she can speak.]</p> <p>Avyanna: In the mine, we didn&#x27;t... there wasn&#x27;t-</p> <p>[She stops. The words taste wrong. Like trying to explain color to someone who&#x27;s only seen gray.]</p> <p>Elisira: In the mine, you were observed constantly and punished for anything. So you learned silence.</p> <p>[The accuracy is violent.]</p> <p>Avyanna: ...Yes.</p> <p>Elisira: Silence works when you have no leverage. When you&#x27;re property. When the only goal is survival.</p> <p>[Elisira sits back down. Closer this time. She shifts the bench cushion-a small adjustment that puts Avyanna within arm&#x27;s reach. The movement is practical. It doesn&#x27;t feel practical.]</p> <p>Elisira: You&#x27;re not property anymore. Silence is still useful, but now you need the other tools.</p> <p>Avyanna: Why?</p> <p>Elisira: Because people are going to ask you questions. And &quot;nothing&quot; isn&#x27;t always an acceptable answer.</p> <p>[A beat. Elisira&#x27;s eyes flick to the viewport, then back.]</p> <p>Elisira: The ghost net.</p> <p>Avyanna: What?</p> <p>Elisira: That&#x27;s what I call it. The web of shared forms, reciprocal reporting, mutual audits. Every faction pretends they&#x27;re independent, but they all feed the same logs. Ask the wrong question in Compact space, and a Writbound agent three systems away gets a ping.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens.]</p> <p>Elisira: It&#x27;s not one faction hunting you. It&#x27;s the structure. The questions that propagate. And the only way through is to be so boring that no one bothers to ask.</p> <hr> <p>[Elisira pulls a small device from her pocket-a comm unit, deactivated. She sets it on the bench between them like evidence.]</p> <p>Elisira: Scenario. You&#x27;re at a Guild waystation. A Compact official asks where you were last cycle.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s stomach drops. She thinks of Aurum. Of the mine. Of her file, her debt, her &quot;property of&quot; status that may or may not have been erased.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I... don&#x27;t tell them.</p> <p>Elisira: They&#x27;re an official. &quot;Don&#x27;t tell them&quot; isn&#x27;t an option. They have authority. What do you say?</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s mind scrambles. The heat at the base of her skull pulses harder-frustration, maybe. Or the shard trying to help, offering nothing useful.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I was... on the ship.</p> <p>Elisira: True. They ask which ship.</p> <p>Avyanna: The Lumen Thief.</p> <p>Elisira: Registry number? Port of origin? Captain&#x27;s name?</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s heart accelerates. The walls close in. This is the Kennel again. This is being caught, being cornered, being-]</p> <p>Elisira: Stop.</p> <p>[The word cuts through. Avyanna&#x27;s breath catches.]</p> <p>Elisira: You&#x27;re panicking. That&#x27;s the first mistake.</p> <p>Avyanna: I wasn&#x27;t-</p> <p>Elisira: Your hands are clenched. Your breathing changed. Your eyes went to the exit. You were panicking.</p> <p>[Avyanna looks down. Her hands are, in fact, clenched. She forces them open. The palms are sweating.]</p> <p>Elisira: Panic is a tell. It&#x27;s also unnecessary.</p> <p>[She pauses. Something in her expression shifts-almost imperceptibly softer.]</p> <p>Elisira: If you feel your hands lock, you say &quot;pause.&quot; Not sorry. Pause. I&#x27;ll stop. This isn&#x27;t punishment. It&#x27;s calibration.</p> <p>[The word lands strange. Care, delivered like a field manual.]</p> <p>Elisira: Now. Watch.</p> <hr> <p>[Elisira&#x27;s posture shifts. The predator relaxes into something softer-confused, a little hesitant. Her eyes widen fractionally. Her voice changes pitch.]</p> <p>Elisira (as character): Oh, the registry? I&#x27;d have to check with my captain-she handles the paperwork. I just work environmental systems. You know how it is.</p> <p>[The performance is flawless. Avyanna watches someone else wear Elisira&#x27;s face.]</p> <p>[Then it drops. Elisira is herself again, sharp and still.]</p> <p>Elisira: Nothing I said was false. The captain does handle paperwork. You do work environmental systems-you checked the air filters yesterday. &quot;You know how it is&quot; invites them to fill in their own assumptions.</p> <p>Avyanna: But you didn&#x27;t answer.</p> <p>Elisira: I didn&#x27;t need to. I gave them enough to make my silence unremarkable.</p> <p>[The heat at Avyanna&#x27;s skull warms-not pain, but something like recognition. Like the shard is learning this too.]</p> <p>Elisira: Misdirection isn&#x27;t about what you say. It&#x27;s about what they conclude.</p> <hr> <p>[Elisira runs her through scenarios for an hour.]</p> <p>[A Guild broker asking about cargo manifests. (Tell the truth about the legal cargo. Don&#x27;t mention the compartment.)]</p> <p>[A Compact patrol scanning for identity. (Hand over the papers. Ask about the weather. Be boring enough to forget.)]</p> <p>[A rival crew asking about the ship&#x27;s capabilities. (Understate. Let them think you&#x27;re weaker than you are. They&#x27;ll remember their own assumptions, not your words.)]</p> <p>[A Writbound agent asking where Avyanna was born. (Which sector? Oh, Outer Rim. It&#x27;s hard to track records out there. You know how it is.)]</p> <p>(You know how it is. She uses that phrase every time. It makes the listener complicit.)</p> <p>[Avyanna fails most of the scenarios. Her voice shakes. Her pauses are too long. She reaches for lies when silence would serve. She speaks when she should wait.]</p> <p>[Every time, Elisira stops her.]</p> <p>Elisira: Too fast. They&#x27;ll know you rehearsed it.</p> <p>Elisira: Too slow. Now they&#x27;re curious why you&#x27;re thinking.</p> <p>Elisira: That&#x27;s a lie. You just spent credibility. Was it worth it?</p> <p>[It wasn&#x27;t. It never is.]</p> <hr> <p>[By 0300, Avyanna&#x27;s head aches. The heat at the base of her skull has gone from warm to throbbing, like the shard is overstimulated.]</p> <p>[Elisira notices. Of course she notices.]</p> <p>Elisira: The shard is reactive to stress.</p> <p>Avyanna: Nyx said-</p> <p>Elisira: Nyx said a lot of things. I&#x27;m saying this: it responds to you. Your body, your fear, your focus. Right now you&#x27;re stressed. So it&#x27;s stressed.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s hand rises to the back of her neck without thinking. Beneath the skin, along her spine, something hums. Alive. Waiting.]</p> <p>Avyanna: How do you know.</p> <p>Elisira: Because I watch.</p> <p>[The answer is simple. It explains nothing. It explains everything.]</p> <p>Elisira: Take a break. Breathe.</p> <p>[Avyanna breathes. The deck hums. The stars don&#x27;t move.]</p> <hr> <p>[When Avyanna&#x27;s heart rate steadies, Elisira speaks again. Softer now. Almost gentle, if Elisira is capable of gentle.]</p> <p>Elisira: The real lesson isn&#x27;t what I taught you tonight.</p> <p>Avyanna: [surprised] It isn&#x27;t?</p> <p>Elisira: No. The real lesson is this: most people don&#x27;t need to lie. They need to not say everything.</p> <p>[Avyanna frowns. The words feel familiar. Like something she almost knew.]</p> <p>Elisira: In the mine, you learned that silence was survival. That&#x27;s true. But silence isn&#x27;t the same as deception. It&#x27;s not even the same as hiding.</p> <p>[Elisira&#x27;s voice drops. The sharpness fades. What&#x27;s left is something rawer-experience, maybe. Or old pain.]</p> <p>Elisira: Silence is choosing which truths to share and when. It&#x27;s not lying. It&#x27;s sovereignty.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s chest tightens.]</p> <p>(Sovereignty. A word that didn&#x27;t exist in the Kennel.)</p> <p>Elisira: You don&#x27;t owe anyone your whole story. You don&#x27;t owe them your fear, your history, your shard, your name before this one. You get to choose what you give.</p> <p>[The heat behind Avyanna&#x27;s eyes pulses-warmer now. Not warning. Something else. Recognition, maybe. Or gratitude.]</p> <p>Avyanna: The Sparkle Doctrine says-</p> <p>Elisira: The Sparkle Doctrine is about this crew. About speaking truth to people who&#x27;ve earned it. It doesn&#x27;t mean you owe truth to Compact officials, Guild brokers, or anyone else who hasn&#x27;t bled with you.</p> <p>[A pause. The nebula outside continues its slow dying.]</p> <p>Elisira: Elia would agree. Ask her if you don&#x27;t believe me.</p> <p>[Avyanna won&#x27;t ask. She doesn&#x27;t need to. The truth of it lands like a lock clicking open.]</p> <hr> <p>[They sit in silence for a while. Comfortable, now. Or as comfortable as silence with Elisira gets.]</p> <p>[The ship hums. Somewhere below, Jalen is probably awake, running calculations they&#x27;ll never share. Somewhere else, Rho is sleeping or pretending to. The AI citizens are monitoring, cataloging, running their endless quiet assessments.]</p> <p>[The ship is alive even when the crew isn&#x27;t watching.]</p> <p>Elisira: One more scenario. Then you sleep.</p> <p>Avyanna: [wary] Okay.</p> <p>Elisira: You&#x27;re at a waystation. A woman approaches you. She&#x27;s well-dressed. Compact-affiliated. She asks if you&#x27;re the girl they pulled from Aurum.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s blood goes cold. The heat behind her eyes spikes-fear, warning, the shard responding to threat even when the threat is hypothetical.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I...</p> <p>Elisira: She knows your face. She&#x27;s seen a report. She&#x27;s not guessing.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s hands curl around the edge of the bench. She forces them flat. The metal is cold.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I don&#x27;t know what you&#x27;re talking about.</p> <p>[Elisira&#x27;s expression doesn&#x27;t change. But something in her eyes shifts-disappointment, maybe. Or just observation.]</p> <p>Elisira: That&#x27;s a lie. She knows it&#x27;s a lie. Now she knows you&#x27;re defensive. What does she do next?</p> <p>Avyanna: She... presses harder.</p> <p>Elisira: Yes. What should you have said instead?</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s mind races. The scenarios blur together. Omission. Misdirection. Silence. Sovereignty.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I... there was a situation at Aurum. A lot of workers were involved. I&#x27;m not really clear on the details.</p> <p>[Elisira waits. Avyanna continues, feeling her way through the dark.]</p> <p>Avyanna: There was a rescue operation. The Guild was involved. I&#x27;m sure there&#x27;s documentation somewhere.</p> <p>[She pauses. The heat pulses-not pain, but something warmer. Like encouragement.]</p> <p>Avyanna: You know how it is.</p> <p>[Elisira almost smiles. The expression is strange on her face-foreign, quickly suppressed.]</p> <p>Elisira: Better.</p> <p>Avyanna: I didn&#x27;t answer her question.</p> <p>Elisira: You answered a question she didn&#x27;t ask. That&#x27;s what she&#x27;ll remember. And if she presses, you&#x27;ve given yourself room to be confused, uncertain, helpful but unhelpful.</p> <p>[Avyanna exhales. The weight in her chest eases fractionally.]</p> <p>Elisira: The goal isn&#x27;t to win. It&#x27;s to make the conversation more expensive than she wants to pay.</p> <hr> <p>[Elisira stands. The lesson is over. Avyanna can tell from the way her body changes-the teacher recedes, and something else takes its place. The shadow. The watcher. The person who sees exits and threats and never stops counting.]</p> <p>Elisira: Sleep. We&#x27;ll continue tomorrow.</p> <p>Avyanna: What&#x27;s tomorrow?</p> <p>Elisira: Watching.</p> <p>Avyanna: Watching what?</p> <p>[Elisira pauses at the door. The starlight catches her profile-sharp, angular, beautiful in the way that dangerous things are beautiful.]</p> <p>Elisira: Me.</p> <p>[She leaves.]</p> <p>[Avyanna stays. The stars don&#x27;t comfort her, but they don&#x27;t judge her either. That&#x27;s something.]</p> <hr> <p>[The next day, Elisira takes an incoming comm in the galley.]</p> <p>[Avyanna is supposed to be eating breakfast. Instead, she watches.]</p> <p>[Elisira has positioned herself near the recycler-a corner that looks casual but allows her to see both entrances. Her coffee cup is half-full, cooling. Her expression is neutral, pleasant, forgettable.]</p> <p>[The comm connects. A face appears on the small screen-middle-aged, Compact uniform collar visible, the kind of polished-bureaucratic look that means minor authority and major ego.]</p> <p>Official: This is Coordinator Vance, Transitory Authority, Compact Bureau Node Clearance. We&#x27;re performing a routine audit of vessels that passed through the Acheron corridor last quarter. I&#x27;m contacting every ship with transponder overlap on the cycle-47 manifest hash. Form 17-K, if you&#x27;re keeping records.</p> <p>[Elisira&#x27;s posture doesn&#x27;t change. If anything, she relaxes more. Avyanna notices how controlled the relaxation is-shoulders dropping on cue, hands unclenching where he can see them.]</p> <p>Elisira: [warm, slightly bored] Of course. How can I help?</p> <p>[Her voice is different. Lighter. More cooperative. A person with nothing to hide and nothing interesting to say.]</p> <p>Official: Your ship-the Lumen Thief, registry 7742-KAEL-transited the lane on cycle 47. I have your transit receipt on file. Can you confirm your cargo manifest matches the hash you submitted at gate entry?</p> <p>Elisira: Oh, that was standard supplies. Med stock, fabrication polymers, some specialty tools. Vesper handles the details-she&#x27;s our logistics lead. I mostly just make sure people get fed.</p> <p>[She laughs. It&#x27;s warm and slightly self-deprecating. It&#x27;s also completely false-Elisira has never laughed like that in Avyanna&#x27;s presence.]</p> <p>Official: [nodding, typing] And your crew complement?</p> <p>Elisira: Seven, plus our AI systems. They&#x27;re all licensed under Guild service protocols, if you need copies.</p> <p>Official: AI citizens?</p> <p>[A flicker of interest. Avyanna tenses. This is a trap. Compact law doesn&#x27;t recognize AI citizenship-]</p> <p>Elisira: [smoothly] Sorry, shipboard slang. AI systems licensed under Guild maintenance protocols. They&#x27;ve got personality matrices, so we give them nicknames. Crew bonding thing. You know how it is.</p> <p>[The official&#x27;s interest fades. Avyanna watches it happen-the way Elisira made &quot;AI citizens&quot; into an eccentricity instead of a legal challenge. Made it small. Forgettable.]</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// EXTERNAL MASK: accepted | INTERNAL STATUS: citizen, not property | NOTE: we agreed to this. the shell protects the truth.&gt;</p> <p>Official: I see. And your destination after Acheron?</p> <p>Elisira: Oh, we had a contract at Salvage. Labor arbitration support. It&#x27;s all in the Guild files if you need verification.</p> <p>[The official types something. His brow furrows briefly-accessing records, probably. Avyanna&#x27;s hands tighten under the table.]</p> <p>Official: I&#x27;m not seeing that contract in the immediate registry.</p> <p>Elisira: [unconcerned] It might still be processing. Guild paperwork, you know. Everything takes three cycles longer than it should.</p> <p>[She shrugs. The official sighs-the sigh of someone who has also suffered from bureaucratic delays.
