A moment of shared frustration. Bonding.]</p> <p>Official: I&#x27;ll flag it for follow-up. Nothing urgent.</p> <p>Elisira: Of course. Is there anything else?</p> <p>Official: No, that should be sufficient. Thank you for your cooperation.</p> <p>Elisira: Anytime. Safe transits, Coordinator.</p> <p>[The comm disconnects. Elisira&#x27;s face doesn&#x27;t change for three full seconds-holding the mask in case of monitoring-and then it drops.]</p> <p>[What&#x27;s left is sharp, still, predatory. The real Elisira. Avyanna&#x27;s teacher.]</p> <hr> <p>[Elisira doesn&#x27;t look at Avyanna. Doesn&#x27;t acknowledge that she was being watched. But she speaks anyway, quiet enough that only Avyanna can hear.]</p> <p>Elisira: What did you see?</p> <p>[Avyanna thinks. Replays the conversation in her head.]</p> <p>Avyanna: You didn&#x27;t lie.</p> <p>Elisira: No.</p> <p>Avyanna: Everything you said was technically true.</p> <p>Elisira: Yes.</p> <p>Avyanna: But you arranged it so he concluded things that weren&#x27;t true.</p> <p>[Elisira takes a sip of her cold coffee. Her face is neutral again. Patient.]</p> <p>Elisira: What things?</p> <p>Avyanna: That... the AI citizens are just systems with nicknames. That the Salvage contract was routine. That you&#x27;re just a cook who doesn&#x27;t know much.</p> <p>[The last one makes Avyanna&#x27;s chest tighten. The lie that wasn&#x27;t a lie-Elisira as harmless, forgettable, domestic.]</p> <p>Elisira: I never said I was a cook.</p> <p>Avyanna: You implied-</p> <p>Elisira: I said I make sure people get fed. True. I&#x27;m the one who manages food rotation when Elia forgets to eat and Vesper gets lost in casework. I fed you your first real meal on this ship.</p> <p>[The memory surfaces: Avyanna in the med bay, Elisira appearing with a tray, saying nothing, leaving before thanks could be offered.]</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s... true.</p> <p>Elisira: Everything was true. He filled in the rest himself. That&#x27;s what people do.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna sits with that for a long moment. The galley smells like recycled coffee and Jalen&#x27;s attempt at synthetic toast. Someone has scratched &quot;WAFFLE MAKES BETTER TOAST&quot; into the counter near the dispensary. It&#x27;s been there for months.]</p> <p>(People assume. They hear a partial truth and finish the story themselves. And they believe their own conclusions more than they&#x27;d believe mine.)</p> <p>Avyanna: The official will report that we&#x27;re boring.</p> <p>Elisira: He&#x27;ll report that we&#x27;re cooperative, mundane, and not worth investigating further.</p> <p>Avyanna: Even though we&#x27;re not.</p> <p>Elisira: Being interesting is expensive. Being boring is free.</p> <p>[Avyanna turns that over. It sounds like something Vesper would say-or maybe something they both learned from the same source. The ship&#x27;s philosophy, distilled into survival strategy.]</p> <p>Avyanna: What if he&#x27;d pressed harder? About the AI citizens?</p> <p>[Elisira sets down her coffee cup. The movement is precise.]</p> <p>Elisira: Then I would have redirected again. Asked about his day. Mentioned transit schedules. Made myself more trouble than I was worth.</p> <p>Avyanna: And if that didn&#x27;t work?</p> <p>Elisira: Then I would have lied.</p> <p>[The word lands simply. No justification. No excuse.]</p> <p>Elisira: But lying would have cost me. Now, if he ever checks again, my record is: cooperative, boring, compliant. That&#x27;s valuable. That&#x27;s what I protected tonight.</p> <p>[Avyanna nods. The heat at the base of her skull hums-approval, maybe. Or just attention.]</p> <p>[Elisira pauses. For a moment, her hand moves toward Avyanna&#x27;s shoulder-then stops. The gesture aborts mid-motion, replaced by adjusting her own collar.]</p> <p>Elisira: Lying is for when you&#x27;ve failed at everything else. Remember that.</p> <hr> <p>[Elisira pauses at the corridor. Not turning back fully. Just enough to prove she hasn&#x27;t forgotten Avyanna exists.]</p> <p>Elisira: One more thing.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s spine goes rigid on instinct. &quot;One more thing&quot; used to mean surprise charges. Extra shifts. A quota adjustment.]</p> <p>Elisira: Homework.</p> <p>Avyanna: [blinking] Homework.</p> <p>[Elisira slides a thin printout onto the table-station letterhead, a bland bulletin in bureaucratic font. The kind of paper that looks harmless until it kills you.]</p> <p>Elisira: Circle every noun that describes a person.</p> <p>Elisira: Underline every verb that implies ownership.</p> <p>Elisira: Then write, in the margin: what assumption those words are trying to smuggle into your skull.</p> <p>[Avyanna stares at the printout.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Why.</p> <p>Elisira: Because you keep thinking lies are the interesting part.</p> <p>[She finally looks at Avyanna, eyes flat and sharp.]</p> <p>Elisira: Lies are easy.</p> <p>Elisira: Assumptions are infrastructure.</p> <p>Elisira: If you can see the assumptions, you can predict the whole system&#x27;s next move.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s chest tightens. Not fear-focus.]</p> <p>Avyanna: And then what.</p> <p>Elisira: Then you write one sentence that refuses the assumption without starting a fight you can&#x27;t win.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] Like you did with the Compact official.</p> <p>Elisira: Yes.</p> <p>[Elisira adjusts her collar-mask back on, already leaving.]</p> <p>Elisira: Bring it to me tomorrow.</p> <p>Elisira: If your margin notes are good, I&#x27;ll give you harder paper.</p> <p>[It&#x27;s almost a joke.]</p> <p>[It&#x27;s almost encouragement.]</p> <p>[It lands anyway.]</p> <p>[Later, in her bunk, Avyanna stares at the ceiling and lets the lesson settle.]</p> <p>(Silence is sovereignty. Truth can be arranged. Boring is free.)</p> <p>[The heat along her spine settles into a low, steady hum-not words, but warmth. Agreement, maybe. Or just presence.]</p> <p>[She thinks of the mine. The constant surveillance. The way speaking meant danger and silence meant survival. She&#x27;d thought she knew about hiding.]</p> <p>(I didn&#x27;t know anything. I knew how to disappear. Elisira knows how to be seen and still keep everything that matters.)</p> <p>[There&#x27;s a difference. A huge one.]</p> <p>[Avyanna rolls onto her side. The ship&#x27;s hum is steady. Someone-Rho, probably-is moving in the corridor, boots heavy and unhurried.]</p> <p>(I don&#x27;t owe anyone my whole story.)</p> <p>[The thought feels like a door opening. Small, but real.]</p> <p>[For years, every truth about herself was extracted. Forced. Demanded by forms and supervisors and the endless ledger of her debt. Her history belonged to Aurum. Her future belonged to the balance.]</p> <p>(Here, I get to choose.)</p> <p>[The warmth at her skull spreads-down her spine, into her chest. Not pain. Not warning. Something else.]</p> <p>(I get to choose what I give.)</p> <p>[She closes her eyes. For the first time in weeks, sleep comes easily.]</p> <hr> <p>[The next morning, Vesper catches her in the commons. Her datapad is in hand. Her expression is neutral, but something in her eyes is sharper than usual.]</p> <p>Vesper: Elisira says you&#x27;re learning.</p> <p>Avyanna: [carefully] She said that?</p> <p>Vesper: She said &quot;not hopeless.&quot; For Elisira, that&#x27;s high praise.</p> <p>[Avyanna doesn&#x27;t smile, but something in her chest loosens.]</p> <p>Vesper: How do you feel?</p> <p>[The question is genuine. Vesper always asks genuine questions, even when the answers are inconvenient.]</p> <p>[Avyanna considers. How does she feel?]</p> <p>(Tired. Scared. Slightly less terrified than yesterday.)</p> <p>[But that&#x27;s not what she says.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I feel like I understand something I didn&#x27;t before.</p> <p>Vesper: What&#x27;s that?</p> <p>[Avyanna meets her eyes. The heat hums. The lesson settles into her bones.]</p> <p>Avyanna: That silence isn&#x27;t weakness. It&#x27;s a choice.</p> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s expression shifts-just slightly. Something like pride. Or recognition.]</p> <p>Vesper: Good.</p> <p>[She turns to leave. Pauses at the door.]</p> <p>Vesper: For what it&#x27;s worth: Elisira doesn&#x27;t teach people she doesn&#x27;t believe in. If she&#x27;s spending time on you, it means something.</p> <p>[Then she&#x27;s gone.]</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna sits in the commons alone, surrounded by the ship&#x27;s quiet breathing.]</p> <p>[On the message board, someone has added a new note: *Nav check tomorrow, 0800. Jalen will be grumpy. Bring offerings (coffee).*]</p> <p>[Below it: *WHAT COUNTS AS COFFEE ON THIS SHIP IS AN OPEN PHILOSOPHICAL QUESTION - E*]</p> <p>[Below that, in smaller handwriting: *the recycler&#x27;s attempt is a war crime - R*]</p> <p>[Avyanna reads them. Rereads them. Small complaints. Small life. The ship continuing its existence through bickering and maintenance and people noticing what matters.]</p> <p>[She gets up. Goes to the galley. Makes coffee-or the closest thing the dispensary can manage.]</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t drink it. She leaves it outside Jalen&#x27;s door with a note: *For 0800. A. L.*]</p> <p>[It&#x27;s a small thing. It&#x27;s a choice.]</p> <p>[The heat at her skull warms. Not approval-something simpler. Just: awareness. Just: the shard noticing that she&#x27;s changing.]</p> <p>[She walks back to her bunk, boots quiet on the deck.]</p> <p>(I&#x27;m learning.)</p> <p>[The thought doesn&#x27;t scare her anymore.]</p> <hr> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// OBSERVATION: trainee left offering outside pilot&#x27;s quarters | INFERENCE: bonding behavior | CONFIDENCE: 0.72 | NOTE: she&#x27;s learning which debts are real.&gt;</p> <hr> <p>*End Chapter 15*</p> </div> Where does the route go next? * [[Read optional scene 15a: Guild Station Protocols->Act1 15a Guild Station Protocols]] * [[Read optional scene 15b: First Purchase Decision->Act1 15b First Purchase Decision]] * [[Read optional scene 15c: Station Micro Errand Paperwork And Predators->Act1 15c Station Micro Errand Paperwork And Predators]] * [[Read optional scene 15d: Rival Crew Probe->Act1 15d Rival Crew Probe]] * [[Read optional scene 15e: Maren Blocks->Act1 15e Maren Blocks]] * [[Continue to Chapter 16: Breach Hymn->Act1 16 Breach Hymn]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Optional 15a: Guild Station Protocols</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 15a_guild_station_protocols.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **[SCENE - OCCURS POST-CH15, FIRST PROPER GUILD STATION VISIT]** &gt; *Location: Guild Station Kael-7, public concourse* &gt; *Present: Avyanna, Vesper, Waffle (AI drone escort)* &gt; *Teaching: Guild culture, professional boundaries, station navigation*</p> <hr> <p>[Kael-7 smells different than civilian stations.]</p> <p>[Less desperation. More order. The air is cleaner, the lights are brighter, and the people move with purpose instead of panic. This is Guild space-not Compact-controlled, not corporate-owned. Neutral ground with teeth.]</p> <p>[Avyanna walks beside Vesper through the main concourse. Waffle drifts overhead, scanning the crowd with benign curiosity. Around them: contractors in mismatched gear, station personnel in gray uniforms, traders negotiating in quiet corners, and the ever-present hum of people doing business.]</p> <p>[Vesper walks with professional ease. Not hurried. Not casual. Moving like someone who belongs and knows it.]</p> <hr> <p>Vesper: [not looking at Avyanna] First rule. Guild stations are neutral territory. That means Compact agents, corporate security, and independent operators all use the same space. They don&#x27;t fight here. Fighting means blacklist.</p> <p>Avyanna: [careful] So... it&#x27;s safe?</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] It&#x27;s safer. Not safe. There&#x27;s a difference.</p> <p>[She nods toward a corner where two armored figures are having a conversation that looks civil but has the body language of mutual threat.]</p> <p>Vesper: Those two probably tried to kill each other last week. Here, they drink coffee and negotiate contracts. Because Guild neutrality is enforced with permanent bans, frozen accounts, and very enthusiastic security drones.</p> <p>[Waffle chirps.]</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// CORRECTION: i am not enthusiastic | I AM THOROUGH | ALSO I HAVE NEVER PARTICIPATED IN GUILD ENFORCEMENT | I AM SHIP CREW NOT STATION SECURITY&gt;</p> <p>Vesper: [dry] Apologies. Waffle is thorough, not enthusiastic.</p> <hr> <p>[They pass a vendor stall selling data chips. Another selling ship parts. Another selling something Avyanna can&#x27;t identify but suspects is illegal somewhere.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] How do I know who&#x27;s... who? Compact, corporate, Guild, independent?</p> <p>Vesper: [approving the question] Good instinct. You can&#x27;t always know. But there are tells.</p> <p>[She slows, lets Avyanna observe the crowd.]</p> <p>Vesper: Compact personnel dress formal. Uniforms or suits, even when they&#x27;re off-duty. They project authority. They want you to know they&#x27;re Compact.</p> <p>[She points subtly.]</p> <p>Vesper: See the woman by the transit board? Gray suit, polished boots, posture like she owns the station? Compact administrative corps. Not a threat to us, but she&#x27;ll report anything irregular she sees.</p> <hr> <p>Avyanna: [watching] What about corporate?</p> <p>Vesper: [scanning] Corporate security is obvious-branded armor, company logos, moving in pairs. They&#x27;re here to protect assets, not enforce law.</p> <p>[She nods toward two figures in dark armor with a stylized sun emblem.]</p> <p>Vesper: Helios extraction security. Rival company to Aurum. If they recognized you as ex-Aurum, they wouldn&#x27;t care. You&#x27;re not their asset to recover.</p> <p>Avyanna: [tense] But Aurum security-</p> <p>Vesper: [flat] Would be banned from approaching you on Guild neutral ground. If they tried, station security would intervene before we had to.</p> <p>[Waffle chirps again.]</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// ADDENDUM: if station security is slow i am authorized to intervene | STUN SETTINGS ONLY | GUILD RULES ARE CLEAR RE: PROPORTIONAL RESPONSE&gt;</p> <hr> <p>[They reach a quieter section of the concourse. Vesper stops at a public board displaying contract postings.]</p> <p>Vesper: Second rule. Everything on a Guild station is documented. Every transaction. Every contract. Every dispute.</p> <p>[She pulls up a screen.]</p> <p>Vesper: You want to post a contract? You register it here. Terms, payment, duration, scope. All public. All verified by Guild AI arbitration systems.</p> <p>Vesper: You accept a contract? It&#x27;s logged. You complete it? Logged. You fail it? Logged. Your reputation is your currency here.</p> <p>Avyanna: [frowning] What if someone lies about completing a contract?</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] They can&#x27;t. Guild stations require evidence of completion. No evidence, no payment release, no reputation credit.</p> <p>Vesper: That&#x27;s why we document everything. Not just for our protection. For our reputation.</p> <hr> <p>[She swipes to another screen: contractor profiles. Names, ratings, specializations, completed contracts, disputes.]</p> <p>Vesper: [pointing] This is us. Lumen Thief crew. Specialty: evidence retrieval and secured data extraction. Rating: 4.7 out of 5. Contracts completed: two hundred forty-three. Disputes: three, all resolved in our favor.</p> <p>Avyanna: [reading] Why not five out of five?</p> <p>Vesper: [dry] Because perfection is suspicious. We&#x27;ve had clients who were dissatisfied with our refusal to break contract scope. They rated us poorly. We kept the rating. It&#x27;s honest.</p> <p>Vesper: [meeting her eyes] Reputation isn&#x27;t about perfection. It&#x27;s about documented reliability. We do what we say we&#x27;ll do. That&#x27;s worth more than a perfect score built on lies.</p> <hr> <p>[Waffle descends to eye level, projecting a small hologram.]</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// EDUCATIONAL MOMENT: reputation systems are mutual accountability | CLIENTS RATE CONTRACTORS | CONTRACTORS RATE CLIENTS | BAD CLIENTS GET FLAGGED | EXAMPLE: client who demands contract violations = flagged as high-risk&gt;</p> <p>Vesper: [nodding] Which is why we turn down contracts from bad clients. Not worth the risk to our reputation or safety.</p> <p>Avyanna: [slow] So the Guild protects contractors, not just clients.</p> <p>Vesper: [approving] Exactly. The Guild is a mutual aid structure. Everyone participates. Everyone benefits. Everyone is held accountable.</p> <p>[She closes the screen.]</p> <p>Vesper: Third rule. Personal boundaries are law here. If someone violates yours, you report it. Guild security takes that seriously.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s shoulders tense.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [careful] What counts as a violation?</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] Unwanted touch. Unwanted following. Verbal harassment. Coercion of any kind. Refusal to accept &quot;no.&quot;</p> <p>Vesper: If someone makes you uncomfortable, you say &quot;you&#x27;re violating Guild neutrality protocols&quot; and you report them to station security. They&#x27;ll intervene.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// ADDENDUM: if you are unsure whether behavior is violation i can assess and advise | ALSO I CAN RECORD EVIDENCE | ALSO I CAN DEPLOY EXTREMELY LOUD ALARM&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: [almost smiling] You&#x27;d alarm for me?</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// AFFIRMATIVE: you are crew | CREW PROTECTION IS PRIMARY FUNCTION | ALSO I ENJOY ALARMING | IT IS VERY SATISFYING&gt;</p> <hr> <p>[They walk through the market sector. Vesper stops at a food stall, orders two drinks without asking what Avyanna wants. Sets one in front of her.]</p> <p>Vesper: [matter-of-fact] Fourth rule. You don&#x27;t have to explain yourself to strangers. If someone asks intrusive questions, you say &quot;not your concern&quot; and move on.</p> <p>[She takes a sip of her drink-something dark and bitter.]</p> <p>Vesper: The mine taught you to answer every question from authority. Here, most people aren&#x27;t authority. They&#x27;re strangers. Strangers don&#x27;t get your history.</p> <p>Avyanna: [holding her drink] What if they keep asking?</p> <p>Vesper: [flat] Then they&#x27;re violating rule three. You report them.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna sips her drink. It&#x27;s sweet and cold and tastes like fruit she&#x27;s never encountered.]</p> <p>[A figure approaches the stall. Human, older, wearing contractor gear. They nod at Vesper.]</p> <p>Contractor: [familiar] Vesper. Still pulling impossible contracts?</p> <p>Vesper: [neutral] Still charging appropriately for them. You?</p> <p>Contractor: [grinning] Retired. Sort of. These days I just consult.</p> <p>[Their eyes flick to Avyanna. Curious, not threatening.]</p> <p>Contractor: New crew?</p> <p>Vesper: [clipped] Yes.</p> <p>Contractor: [to Avyanna] Welcome to Guild space. Vesper&#x27;s crew is solid. You&#x27;re in good hands.</p> <p>[They don&#x27;t ask her name. Don&#x27;t ask her history. Just nod and leave.]</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna watches them go.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] They didn&#x27;t ask about me.</p> <p>Vesper: [approving] Because it&#x27;s not their concern. Professionalism means respecting boundaries. They recognized you as crew. That&#x27;s enough.</p> <p>[She finishes her drink, disposes of the cup in a recycling port.]</p> <p>Vesper: Fifth rule. Guild stations operate on resource-sharing principles. Water, air, power, food-all allocated fairly. No artificial scarcity. No rationing as control.</p> <p>Vesper: You&#x27;re thirsty? You drink. Hungry? You eat. Tired? You rest. The station supports that. It&#x27;s in everyone&#x27;s interest to keep people functional, not desperate.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna processes that. The opposite of extraction logic. The opposite of the mine.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [hesitant] What happens if someone abuses it? Takes more than they need?</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] Station AI monitors resource use. If someone&#x27;s consumption is disproportionate, they&#x27;re flagged and asked to explain. If it&#x27;s legitimate need, they get support. If it&#x27;s hoarding or exploitation, they&#x27;re fined or banned.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// CLARIFICATION: AI monitors patterns not individuals | PRIVACY PRESERVED | COLLECTIVE RESOURCE HEALTH TRACKED | INTERVENTION ONLY WHEN HARM DETECTED&gt;</p> <p>Vesper: [adding] The system assumes good faith unless proven otherwise. That&#x27;s the foundation. Trust with accountability.</p> <hr> <p>[They walk through a residential sector. Quieter. Crew quarters for long-term contractors. Small apartments with windows overlooking the docking bay.]</p> <p>Vesper: [slowing] Some contractors live on stations full-time. They&#x27;re not ship crew, but they&#x27;re Guild-affiliated. They have the same rights, same access, same protections.</p> <p>Vesper: If you ever wanted to live station-side instead of ship-side, you could. Your choice.</p> <p>[Avyanna looks at the apartments. Stable. Stationary. Safe.]</p> <p>[And suffocating.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] I think... I think I need the ship. For now.</p> <p>Vesper: [nodding] Understood. The option remains available if you change your mind.</p> <hr> <p>[They reach the docking bay. The Lumen Thief is visible through the viewport-scarred, asymmetrical, home.]</p> <p>Vesper: [turning to face Avyanna] Last rule. The most important one.</p> <p>[Avyanna waits.]</p> <p>Vesper: [meeting her eyes] Guild space is safer than most places. But safe is relative. Stay aware. Trust your instincts. If something feels wrong, it probably is.</p> <p>Vesper: You&#x27;re learning to function here. That doesn&#x27;t mean you stop being cautious. Caution kept you alive in the mine. It&#x27;ll keep you alive here too.</p> <p>Avyanna: [nodding] Caution. Awareness. Trust instincts.</p> <p>Vesper: [approving] And if you&#x27;re ever uncertain, ask. Crew, station personnel, Guild AI systems. Resources exist. Use them.</p> <hr> <p>[Waffle hovers beside Avyanna.]</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// PERSONAL NOTE: i am also resource | IF YOU ARE LOST OR SCARED OR UNSURE I WILL ASSIST | ALSO I GIVE EXCELLENT DIRECTIONS | ALSO I KNOW WHERE ALL THE GOOD FOOD VENDORS ARE&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: [almost smiling] Thank you, Waffle.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// ACKNOWLEDGED: you are crew | CREW SUPPORT IS NOT GRATITUDE-BASED | IT IS STRUCTURAL&gt;</p> <p>[But the drone&#x27;s lights pulse warm anyway. Affection, even if it won&#x27;t name it as such.]</p> <hr> <p>[They board the ship. The airlock hisses closed behind them. Avyanna&#x27;s shoulders relax incrementally. Station space is safer than Compact space. But ship space is home.]</p> <p>Vesper: [removing her outer jacket] You did well today. Observed. Asked questions. Stayed aware.</p> <p>Avyanna: [uncertain] I barely did anything.</p> <p>Vesper: [hanging the jacket] You navigated Guild space for the first time without panic. You absorbed protocols. You made choices about your comfort level.</p> <p>Vesper: That&#x27;s not barely anything. That&#x27;s exactly what I needed you to do.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna heads toward her quarters. In the corridor, she pauses.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [turning back] Vesper?</p> <p>Vesper: [at her own door] Yes?</p> <p>Avyanna: [careful] The contractor who said I was in good hands. Were they right?</p> <p>[Vesper pauses. Considers the question with the seriousness it deserves.]</p> <p>Vesper: [quiet] Yes. You are. We&#x27;re not perfect. But we&#x27;re committed to your safety and autonomy. That commitment is documented and verified.</p> <p>Vesper: [meeting her eyes] You&#x27;re in good hands. And you&#x27;re learning to trust that.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna nods. Continues to her quarters. Closes the door behind her.]</p> <p>[She sits on her bunk. Pulls out the data chip Vesper gave her-three identities, encrypted, hers to control.]</p> <p>(Guild station protocols. Neutral ground. Reputation as currency. Boundaries as law.)</p> <p>[The shard at the base of her skull is warm. Content.]</p> <p>(A quiet hum at the base of her skull: guild, floor, safe.)</p> <p>[She tucks the chip back into her pocket. Tomorrow, there will be another station. Another set of protocols to learn. Another layer of the galaxy revealing itself.]</p> <p>[And slowly, incrementally, she&#x27;s learning to move through it without fear.]</p> <hr> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// STATION VISIT LOG: Guild Station Kael-7 | DURATION: 3.2 hours | TRAINEE BEHAVIOR: appropriate and observant | PROTOCOLS LEARNED: neutrality enforcement, reputation systems, boundary protection, resource sharing | ASSESSMENT: integration progressing&gt;</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// OBSERVATION: trainee asked good questions | TRAINEE ACCEPTED BOUNDARIES EDUCATION WITHOUT RESISTANCE | TRAINEE IDENTIFIED SHIP AS HOME | ASSESSMENT: crew bonding effective&gt;</p> <p>&lt;VESPER PERSONAL LOG: &quot;She walked through Guild space like she expected someone to grab her at any moment. But she kept walking. Asked the right questions. Recognized that safety is verb, not noun. She&#x27;s learning.&quot;&gt;</p> <hr> <p>*End Scene*</p> </div> * [[Continue to optional scene 15b: First Purchase Decision->Act1 15b First Purchase Decision]] * [[Continue to Chapter 16: Breach Hymn->Act1 16 Breach Hymn]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Optional 15b: First Purchase Decision</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 15b_first_purchase_decision.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **[SCENE - OCCURS POST-CH15b after first contract payment]** &gt; *Location: Mid-tier station market* &gt; *Present: Avyanna, Bubbles* &gt; *Purpose: Learning to spend money autonomously, distinguishing wants from needs*</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna has two hundred credits in her account. Money she earned. Not given. Not stolen. Earned through work.]</p> <p>[The concept is still strange. The mine took everything and gave nothing. Here, she worked one contract and has enough money to buy... things. Whatever things she wants. Or needs. Or both.]</p> <p>[That&#x27;s the problem. She doesn&#x27;t know the difference yet.]</p> <p>[She stands in the market district of Station Kael-7 with Bubbles hovering beside her. She&#x27;s supposed to be buying things. Vesper said: &quot;You have money. You need supplies. Go buy them. It&#x27;s good practice.&quot;]</p> <p>[But what does she need? And what does she want? And how does she tell them apart?]</p> <hr> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// OBSERVATION: trainee has been stationary for 4.7 minutes | QUERY: do you require assistance&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: [uncertain] I don&#x27;t know what to buy.</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// CLARIFICATION REQUIRED: you don&#x27;t know what to buy because you lack information about available goods, or because you are uncertain of your preferences&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: [frustrated] Both. Neither. I just... I have money and I don&#x27;t know what to do with it.</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// ACKNOWLEDGED: first autonomous purchase decision is difficult | SUGGESTION: start with needs, then proceed to wants | NEEDS = items required for survival/function | WANTS = items that increase comfort/pleasure but are not strictly necessary&gt;</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna processes that. Needs first. She can do needs. Needs are objective.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [to Bubbles] What do I need?</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// ASSESSMENT: basic hygiene supplies running low | CLOTHING: adequate but minimal variety | NUTRITION: ship provides meals but personal snack stores are recommended for autonomy | COMFORT ITEMS: worry stone acquired, other grounding tools may be beneficial&gt;</p> <p>[Bubbles projects a small list in the air.]</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// RECOMMENDED PURCHASES: - Hygiene supplies (soap, cleaning cloths, personal care items): ~15 credits - Additional clothing (second set for rotation): ~30 credits - Personal food storage (non-perishable snacks): ~20 credits - Grounding tools (if desired): ~10 credits TOTAL: ~75 credits | REMAINING BUDGET: 125 credits for discretionary use&gt;</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna reads the list. Seventy-five credits for needs. One hundred twenty-five credits left over. For wants.]</p> <p>[The concept of having money left over after needs feels impossible.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] What do I do with the rest?</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// OPTIONS: 1. Save for future needs 2. Purchase comfort/pleasure items 3. Combination of both ALL OPTIONS ARE VALID | YOUR CHOICE&gt;</p> <p>[Her choice. Everything here is her choice.]</p> <p>[She starts with needs. Easier to think about.]</p> <hr> <p>**Hygiene Supplies**</p> <p>[The vendor has twenty different kinds of soap. TWENTY. Avyanna stares at them like they&#x27;re a threat.]</p> <p>Vendor: [helpful] Looking for anything specific?</p> <p>Avyanna: [overwhelmed] Just... soap.</p> <p>Vendor: [nodding] Bar or liquid? Scented or unscented? Antibacterial or moisturizing?</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s brain shorts out. Too many options. Too many variables.]</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// ASSISTANCE: recommend unscented, moisturizing, bar form | REASONING: less likely to trigger scent-based trauma memories, easy to store, station-standard quality&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: [to vendor] That one. The unscented moisturizing bar.</p> <p>Vendor: [handing it to her] Good choice. Anything else?</p> <hr> <p>[She adds cleaning cloths. Toothpaste. Things that are universal. Things that don&#x27;t require decisions beyond &quot;do I need this? yes.&quot;]</p> <p>[Total: fourteen credits. She pays. The transaction is simple. No haggling. No games. Just goods exchanged for money at the stated price.]</p> <p>[One down. Three to go.]</p> <p>**Clothing**</p> <p>[The clothing stalls are worse. So many colors. So many styles. So many ways to be wrong.]</p> <p>[She gravitates toward gray. Safe. Familiar. The mine color. But then she remembers-she doesn&#x27;t have to wear gray anymore. Gray isn&#x27;t mandatory. Gray is just habit.]</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// OBSERVATION: trainee selecting gray garments | QUERY: is this preference or default trauma response&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: [pausing] I don&#x27;t know.</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// SUGGESTION: test alternative | SELECT ONE ITEM IN DIFFERENT COLOR | COMPARE EMOTIONAL RESPONSE | DETERMINE IF GRAY IS PREFERENCE OR SAFETY BEHAVIOR&gt;</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna picks up a gray shirt. Then a blue one. The blue reminds her of Jalen&#x27;s nav displays. Of the first coffee ritual. Of sky that isn&#x27;t filtered through mine dust.]</p> <p>[She holds both. Compares. The gray feels safe. The blue feels like... possibility?]</p> <p>Vendor: [nearby] Try them on if you want. Changing room&#x27;s open.</p> <p>[Avyanna tries the blue shirt. It fits. The color is bright against her skin in a way that feels foreign but not wrong.]</p> <p>[She buys both. Gray for safe days. Blue for days when she wants to remember that she&#x27;s not in the mine anymore.]</p> <p>[Cost: thirty-two credits. Within budget.]</p> <hr> <p>**Personal Food Storage**</p> <p>[The food vendors sell pre-packaged snacks. Protein bars. Dried fruit. Nut mixes. Things that keep without refrigeration. Things you can eat when ship meals don&#x27;t align with hunger.]</p> <p>[Avyanna picks items based on Bubbles&#x27; nutritional recommendations and her own tentative preferences. She&#x27;s learning that she likes sweet things. That salt is sometimes too much. That texture matters as much as taste.]</p> <p>[She fills a small bag with selections. Enough for two weeks if she rations. Or one week if she doesn&#x27;t.]</p> <p>[Cost: eighteen credits.]</p> <hr> <p>**Grounding Tools**</p> <p>[This is the hard one. This is wants, not needs. She already has the worry stone. She doesn&#x27;t *need* more tools.]</p> <p>[But Nyx gave her the weighted stone and it helped. Maybe other things would help too.]</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// OBSERVATION: trainee hesitating at grounding tools vendor | REMINDER: comfort items are not frivolous | MENTAL HEALTH TOOLS ARE VALID PURCHASES | YOU ARE ALLOWED TO INVEST IN YOUR OWN WELL-BEING&gt;</p> <p>[Avyanna approaches the vendor. Looks at the options. Fidget rings. Textured squares. Small puzzles. Things designed to give restless hands something to do.]</p> <p>[She picks up a smooth metal ring. It spins. The motion is satisfying. Mindless. Something to do when her thoughts get too loud.]</p> <p>Vendor: [knowledgeable] That&#x27;s a spinner ring. Good for anxiety. Fidget without being disruptive.</p> <p>Avyanna: [testing it] How much?</p> <p>Vendor: Eight credits.</p> <p>[Eight credits. For something she doesn&#x27;t strictly need. For comfort. For mental health. For herself.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [decision made] I&#x27;ll take it.</p> <hr> <p>[She pays. The ring is hers. She immediately starts spinning it on her finger. The motion is soothing.]</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// OBSERVATION: trainee purchased non-essential comfort item autonomously | ASSESSMENT: developing ability to identify and meet own comfort needs | THIS IS PROGRESS&gt;</p> <p>[Total spent on needs: sixty-four credits. One hundred thirty-six credits remaining.]</p> <p>[Avyanna stands in the market with her purchases and more money than she expected to have left. The money is hers. She can save it. She can spend it. She can do whatever she wants with it.]</p> <p>[The freedom is terrifying and exhilarating in equal measure.]</p> <hr> <p>**Discretionary Purchase**</p> <p>[She walks past vendors selling things she doesn&#x27;t understand. Art. Music chips. Decorative objects. Things that exist for pleasure, not function.]</p> <p>[She stops at a stall selling plants. Real plants. Tiny ones, meant for small spaces. Hardy varieties that can survive ship environments.]</p> <p>[There&#x27;s a small succulent. Dark green with purple edges. Sturdy. Low-maintenance. The vendor says it needs water once a week and tolerates low light.]</p> <p>[Avyanna stares at it.]</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// QUERY: interest in plant acquisition?&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] I&#x27;ve never had a plant before.</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// OBSERVATION: plants provide psychological benefits | CARING FOR LIVING THING DEVELOPS NURTURING CAPACITY | ALSO: AESTHETIC BENEFIT | ALSO: PLANTS PRODUCE OXYGEN (MINIMAL BUT NON-ZERO)&gt;</p> <hr> <p>Vendor: [to Avyanna] First plant?</p> <p>Avyanna: [nodding] Yes.</p> <p>Vendor: [gentle] That&#x27;s a good starter. Very hard to kill. Forgiving of mistakes. If you forget to water it, it&#x27;ll recover. If you overwater it, just let it dry out. It wants to survive.</p> <p>[The phrase hits different. &quot;It wants to survive.&quot;]</p> <p>[She buys the plant. Twenty credits. More than the spinner ring. More than the soap. But it&#x27;s alive. It&#x27;s something that exists with her, not just for her.]</p> <p>[Cost: twenty credits.]</p> <hr> <p>[She returns to the ship with Bubbles carrying her bags. The plant sits carefully in her hands. Fragile. Trusting. Dependent on her not to kill it through neglect or overcare.]</p> <p>[In her quarters, she sets the plant on the small desk. Finds a spot where it gets indirect light from the corridor. Not too much. Not too little.]</p> <p>[She sits on her bunk and looks at it. A living thing. In her space. Proof that she can care for something. That she has enough stability to commit to keeping another living thing alive.]</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// OBSERVATION: trainee purchased needs (64 credits) and wants (20 credits) | SAVED: 116 credits | FINANCIAL AUTONOMY: demonstrated | EMOTIONAL REGULATION TOOLS: acquired | LIVING THING: acquired | ASSESSMENT: successful first autonomous purchase experience&gt;</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna spins the ring on her finger. Looks at the plant. Smells the new soap. Sees the blue shirt hanging in her small closet next to the gray one.]</p> <p>(I earned money. I bought things. I chose what to buy. I have money left over. I have a plant.)</p> <p>[The concepts are still strange. But they&#x27;re real. Documented. Hers.]</p> <p>[The shard at the base of her skull is warm.]</p> <p>(Warmth gathers at the base of her skull, like a stamp: earned, chose, kept.)</p> <p>[She waters the plant. Just a little. The vendor said it wants to survive. So does she. Maybe they can figure it out together.]</p> <hr> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// PURCHASE LOG: trainee spent 84 of 200 credits | CATEGORIES: hygiene, clothing, food, comfort items, living plant | FINANCIAL DECISIONS: appropriate and balanced | REMAINING FUNDS: saved for future use | ASSESSMENT: trainee developing economic autonomy and purchasing confidence&gt;</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// PERSONAL NOTE: she bought a plant | THIS IS SIGNIFICANT | PLANTS REQUIRE ONGOING CARE AND COMMITMENT | SHE IS BUILDING CAPACITY FOR SUSTAINED RESPONSIBILITY | ALSO THE PLANT IS VERY CUTE | I AM PLEASED&gt;</p> <hr> <p>*End Scene*</p> </div> * [[Continue to optional scene 15c: Station Micro Errand Paperwork And Predators->Act1 15c Station Micro Errand Paperwork And Predators]] * [[Continue to Chapter 16: Breach Hymn->Act1 16 Breach Hymn]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Optional 15c: Station Micro Errand Paperwork And Predators</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 15c_station_micro_errand_paperwork_and_predators.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **[SCENE - OCCURS POST-CH15, FIRST STATION ERRAND]** &gt; *Location: Station concourse (unnamed)* &gt; *Present: Avyanna, Elisira* &gt; *Teaching: &quot;helpful&quot; is not always help; politeness is wrapper; forms are teeth*</p> <hr> <p>[The concourse smells like hot oil, cheap perfume, and the particular tang of too many bodies pretending not to need each other.]</p> <p>[Avyanna walks half a step behind Elisira, trying to match her pace.]</p> <p>[Elisira moves like she belongs. Not because she&#x27;s loud. Because she&#x27;s certain.]</p> <p>(How do you look like you belong when you&#x27;re always waiting to be corrected.)</p> <p>Elisira: Stay close enough that you can hear me.</p> <p>Avyanna: [automatic] Yes.</p> <p>[Avyanna catches herself.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Why.</p> <p>[Elisira glances at her, a tiny flicker of approval.]</p> <p>Elisira: Because this is a funnel.</p> <p>[She gestures without moving her hands-just a small tilt of chin toward a cluster of kiosks, a queue, and a row of &quot;assistance desks&quot; with people who look too eager.]</p> <p>Elisira: Funnels are where predators hunt.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] Like the mine.</p> <p>Elisira: Like anywhere people are scared and tired.</p> <hr> <p>[They&#x27;re here for a simple errand: pick up a stamped copy of a contract addendum. A paper artifact. A thing the Guild recognizes even if systems &quot;glitch.&quot;]</p> <p>[Avyanna hates that paper matters.]</p> <p>(Paper matters because someone can take it away. Paper matters because it can be burned.)</p> <p>[Elisira doesn&#x27;t hate it. Elisira treats paper like a weapon with a handle.]</p> <p>[They approach the counter.]</p> <p>[A clerk smiles too widely.]</p> <p>Clerk: Can I help you, miss-</p> <p>[Her eyes flick to Avyanna&#x27;s collar tag. Too fast to be casual.]</p> <p>Clerk: -traveler.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s skin crawls.]</p> <p>Elisira: [pleasant] We&#x27;re here for a pickup. Code is on the slate.</p> <p>[She slides the slate over. Not close enough for the clerk to snatch it.]</p> <p>[The clerk&#x27;s smile tightens.]</p> <p>Clerk: We&#x27;ll need to verify identity.</p> <p>Avyanna: [tensing] Which-</p> <p>Elisira: [cutting in, still pleasant] Under which jurisdiction.</p> <p>[The clerk blinks.]</p> <p>Clerk: Excuse me?</p> <p>Elisira: You said &quot;need.&quot; I&#x27;m asking: need by which rule. Enforced by whom.</p> <p>[The clerk&#x27;s eyes flick toward a security camera. Toward a uniform across the hall.]</p> <p>[Then back.]</p> <p>Clerk: It&#x27;s... standard.</p> <p>Elisira: Standard is not enforceable.</p> <p>[The clerk&#x27;s stylus taps twice against the counter.]</p> <p>Clerk: Fine. Sign here.</p> <p>[She prints a slip with a pen attached by chain.]</p> <p>[The slip is a trap with polite edges: it asks for name, origin, and &quot;additional contacts.&quot;]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens.]</p> <p>(Additional contacts means: who can we threaten.)</p> <p>Elisira: [to Avyanna, quiet] You don&#x27;t sign anything you didn&#x27;t request.</p> <p>Avyanna: [whisper] I didn&#x27;t request this.</p> <p>Elisira: Correct.</p> <p>[Elisira takes the pen-not from the clerk&#x27;s hand, from the counter.]</p> <p>Elisira: [pleasant] We&#x27;re declining voluntary disclosure.</p> <p>Clerk: It&#x27;s required.</p> <p>Elisira: Then cite it.</p> <p>[Silence.]</p> <p>[The clerk&#x27;s smile becomes brittle.]</p> <p>Clerk: If you don&#x27;t cooperate, the pickup may be delayed.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s heart rate spikes.]</p> <p>(Delay means loss. Loss means punishment. Loss means hunger.)</p> <p>Elisira: Then delay it.</p> <p>[She says it like: we have all day. Like time can&#x27;t be weaponized here.]</p> <p>Elisira: We&#x27;ll file a complaint with the arbitration desk. We&#x27;ll note the attempted coercion. We&#x27;ll request the security footage.</p> <p>[The clerk&#x27;s eyes widen, just a fraction.]</p> <p>[She reaches under the counter and produces the stamped packet like it&#x27;s always been there.]</p> <p>Clerk: No need to escalate.</p> <p>Elisira: Good.</p> <hr> <p>[They step away.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s knees feel like they&#x27;re trying to unlock.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [breathing] You can just... do that.</p> <p>Elisira: Yes.</p> <p>Avyanna: Why didn&#x27;t she-</p> <p>Elisira: Because the clerk wasn&#x27;t enforcing policy. She was testing a boundary.</p> <p>Avyanna: Like a-</p> <p>Elisira: Predator.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s hands tremble as she clutches the packet.]</p> <p>Avyanna: What if she had called security.</p> <p>Elisira: Then we&#x27;d have receipts.</p> <p>Avyanna: That sounds like Vesper.</p> <p>Elisira: Vesper taught me half of it. I taught her the other half. It&#x27;s incestuous.</p> <p>Avyanna: [a startled laugh] That&#x27;s not-</p> <p>Elisira: [deadpan] It&#x27;s a metaphor. Do not repeat it.</p> <hr> <p>[A man steps into their path.]</p> <p>[Not uniformed. Not armed. Just... friendly.]</p> <p>Man: Hey. You look lost. I know where the arbitration wing is. I can-</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s mouth opens.]</p> <p>[The mine reflex tries to offer politeness as payment.]</p> <p>Elisira: No thank you.</p> <p>Man: I&#x27;m just helping.</p> <p>Elisira: We did not request help.</p> <p>Man: Wow. Hostile.</p> <p>Elisira: [pleasant] Correct.</p> <p>[They walk around him.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s chest tightens as if waiting for the hit that follows refusal.]</p> <p>[None comes.]</p> <p>[The man mutters something under his breath and moves on, scanning for softer prey.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] No thank you.</p> <p>Elisira: Yes.</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s... allowed.</p> <p>Elisira: It&#x27;s required.</p> <p>[Avyanna looks at her.]</p> <p>Elisira: For survival.</p> <hr> <p>[Back at the docking tunnel, Avyanna exhales like she&#x27;s been holding her breath since they left the ship.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I kept thinking... if I&#x27;m rude, something bad happens.</p> <p>Elisira: That&#x27;s because something bad used to happen.</p> <p>Avyanna: Does it ever stop feeling like-</p> <p>Elisira: [cutting in, softer than usual] Yes.</p> <p>[A pause.]</p> <p>Elisira: Not fast. But yes.</p> <p>[Avyanna nods.]</p> <p>[She presses the stamped packet to her chest like it can keep her safe.]</p> <p>(Paper is teeth. But sometimes it bites the right thing.)</p> <hr> <p>[They don&#x27;t go straight back.]</p> <p>[Elisira takes the long route through the concourse on purpose.]</p> <p>[Not because she likes crowds. Because she likes information.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Why are we-</p> <p>Elisira: Watching.</p> <p>[They pause near a wall of public postings.]</p> <p>[Not a bulletin board. A contract board. Smaller than the Guild&#x27;s, but the same bones: jobs, fees, penalties, a thousand ways to make desperation look like choice.]</p> <p>[Avyanna reads a posting aloud without thinking.]</p> <p>Avyanna: &quot;Processing surcharge applies to late submissions.&quot;</p> <p>[Her stomach twists.]</p> <p>(Processing surcharge. Mine word. Station word. Same shape.)</p> <p>Elisira: What does that mean.</p> <p>Avyanna: [automatic] It means... pay.</p> <p>Elisira: That&#x27;s the effect. Not the meaning.</p> <p>Avyanna: [working] It means they&#x27;re charging for time they didn&#x27;t lose. They&#x27;re charging for... being behind.</p> <p>Elisira: Who benefits.</p> <p>Avyanna: The office. The broker. Whoever owns the desk.</p> <p>Elisira: Who enforces it.</p> <p>Avyanna: The clerk. The system. The fact that if you miss a submission, you lose your claim.</p> <p>Elisira: Good.</p> <hr> <p>[A woman approaches them.]</p> <p>[Mid-thirties, expensive coat, smile tuned for crowds. She carries a badge that looks real enough to be dangerous.]</p> <p>Woman: You look like you&#x27;re having trouble with the boards.</p> <p>Elisira: No.</p> <p>Woman: I can help you file. I know the system. I can get you processed faster.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s mouth goes dry.]</p> <p>(Faster means: I own you now.)</p> <p>[The woman&#x27;s gaze flicks to Avyanna again. Too curious.]</p> <p>Woman: You&#x27;re new. You don&#x27;t want to get tripped up by bureaucracy. It&#x27;s such a shame when people lose claims over small mistakes.</p> <p>[She smiles wider, as if pity is a gift.]</p> <p>[Avyanna feels her body prepare the mine-script: apologize, agree, accept the hook.]</p> <p>[Elisira doesn&#x27;t raise her voice.]</p> <p>Elisira: No thank you.</p> <p>Woman: I&#x27;m not charging.</p> <p>Elisira: Then you are lying.</p> <p>[The woman&#x27;s smile tightens.]</p> <p>Woman: Excuse me?</p> <p>Elisira: People who do labor for free in systems like this are buying something else.</p> <p>Woman: I&#x27;m just-</p> <p>Elisira: Helping.</p> <p>Elisira: We did not request help.</p> <p>[The woman&#x27;s eyes harden, just for a blink. A tell.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet, to Elisira] Anger at refusal.</p> <p>[Elisira&#x27;s gaze flicks to her. Approval without softness.]</p> <p>Elisira: Correct.</p> <p>[The woman recovers her smile.]</p> <p>Woman: You don&#x27;t have to be rude.</p> <p>Elisira: I do.</p> <p>Woman: That&#x27;s going to make you enemies.</p> <p>Elisira: It&#x27;s a public concourse. Everyone is already an enemy. The difference is which ones pretend.</p> <p>[The woman&#x27;s smile drops entirely.]</p> <p>Woman: Fine. Good luck.</p> <p>[She walks away quickly, scanning for someone who will trade fear for assistance.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [breathing] I almost-</p> <p>Elisira: Took the hook.</p> <p>Avyanna: Yes.</p> <p>Elisira: That&#x27;s normal.</p> <p>Avyanna: It doesn&#x27;t feel normal.</p> <p>Elisira: It feels like training data.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Am I... always going to be that easy.</p> <p>Elisira: No.</p> <p>Elisira: You just proved it.</p> <hr> <p>[They walk back toward the docking tunnel.]</p> <p>[Avyanna clutches the stamped packet less like a lifeline and more like evidence.]</p> <p>(Politeness is wrapper. Evaluate the ask.)</p> <p>[She repeats it silently until the words stop feeling like a borrowed tool and start feeling like hers.]</p> </div> * [[Continue to optional scene 15d: Rival Crew Probe->Act1 15d Rival Crew Probe]] * [[Continue to Chapter 16: Breach Hymn->Act1 16 Breach Hymn]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Optional 15d: Rival Crew Probe</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 15d_rival_crew_probe.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **LUMEN THIEF - GUILD STATION NOTE (INTERNAL):** &gt; *Topic: curiosity is a form of targeting* &gt; *Rule: understate; let them remember their own assumptions*</p> <hr> <p>[The Guild concourse is loud in a way the mine never was.]</p> <p>[The mine&#x27;s noise was work.]</p> <p>[This noise is choice.]</p> <p>[Vendors. Bounties. Arguments that end without blood.]</p> <p>[People laughing like it doesn&#x27;t cost them.]</p> <p>[Avyanna keeps her back to a support column and watches the flow of crews.]</p> <p>[Not because she&#x27;s scared.]</p> <p>[Because she learned that crowds are ledgers.]</p> <p>[Everyone here has an invoice attached to their posture.]</p> <hr> <p>[The Lumen Thief crew draws eyes even when they try not to.]</p> <p>[Not because they&#x27;re loud.]</p> <p>[Because they don&#x27;t look like they&#x27;re hunting for permission.]</p> <p>[Because Elia moves like she owns the air, and Elisira moves like the air asked her to.]</p> <p>[Because Vesper&#x27;s stillness reads like a warning sign to anyone with sense.]</p> <p>[Because Rho carries a parts crate like it weighs nothing and his eyes like it weighs everything.]</p> <p>[Because the AIs talk where everyone can hear them and nobody shushes them.]</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// OBSERVATION: external attention density elevated within 8m radius | INFERENCE: reputation gradient interacting with local rumor flow | CONFIDENCE: 0.74&gt;</p> <hr> <p>[Ten people approach in a loose wedge.]</p> <p>[A full-sized crew.]</p> <p>[Uniform jackets. Patches. Matching boots.]</p> <p>[Roles visible even to Avyanna&#x27;s untrained eye: lead in front, two heavies, a tech, a pilot, a medic, an admin with a slate, and extra bodies for redundancy.]</p> <p>[They are built to win by occupying space.]</p> <p>[Avyanna recognizes the technique.]</p> <p>[The mine did it with supervisors.]</p> <p>[This is the same math in cleaner fabric.]</p> <hr> <p>Their Lead: [smiling] Lumen Thief.</p> <p>[Not a greeting.]</p> <p>[A label tossed like bait.]</p> <p>Elia: [bright] Hi.</p> <p>Their Admin: [pleasant, to Vesper] You&#x27;re the fixer, right.</p> <p>Vesper: [mild] Define &quot;fixer.&quot;</p> <p>Their Admin laughs like it&#x27;s a joke because admitting it isn&#x27;t would be rude.</p> <p>Their Lead: We don&#x27;t mean anything by it. People talk.</p> <p>Elisira: [flat] People always talk.</p> <p>Their Lead: They say you take jobs nobody else can.</p> <p>Rho: [dry] People say the corridor weather is natural.</p> <p>Their Lead: [smile tightening] So it&#x27;s true.</p> <p>Vesper: What&#x27;s your office.</p> <p>Their Lead: Excuse me.</p> <p>Vesper: Your office. Your authority. The thing you represent when you say &quot;we.&quot; Which jurisdiction are you trying to drag us into by talking to us like this.</p> <p>[The lead&#x27;s smile wavers.]</p> <p>Their Admin: We&#x27;re just contractors.</p> <p>Elisira: That&#x27;s a credential. Not an office.</p> <p>[The admin glances down at their slate. Not nervous. Logging.]</p> <p>[Avyanna watches the motion and feels her skin crawl.]</p> <p>(A list.)</p> <hr> <p>Their Lead: [recovering, to Avyanna] You&#x27;re new.</p> <p>[He says it like an advantage.]</p> <p>[Avyanna keeps her face neutral.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Define &quot;new.&quot;</p> <p>[Elia&#x27;s mouth twitches.]</p> <p>[Elisira doesn&#x27;t look at Avyanna, but her posture shifts by a fraction: approval, filed.]</p> <p>Their Lead: [laughing] Okay. Fair.</p> <p>Their Lead: We heard you run a small crew.</p> <p>Rho: We run a sufficient crew.</p> <p>Their Lead: Seven bodies.</p> <p>Their Pilot: [low] Plus their drones.</p> <p>Their Medic: And the ship systems. You can&#x27;t count them as bodies.</p> <p>Elia: [cheerful] You can if they yell at you.</p> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// OBSERVATION: external crew attempting informal intimidation via headcount | COUNTERMEASURE: ignoring | CONFIDENCE: 0.92 | COMMENTARY: numbers are not competence&gt;</p> <p>Their Tech: [to Cinnamon&#x27;s voice source] Is that your ship AI.</p> <p>Cinnamon doesn&#x27;t answer questions from strangers.</p> <p>Vesper: She has a name.</p> <p>Their Tech: What is it.</p> <p>Vesper: Not your business.</p> <hr> <p>[Their lead spreads their hands.]</p> <p>Their Lead: We&#x27;re not trying to steal anything.</p> <p>Their Lead: We&#x27;re offering to coordinate. Guild&#x27;s getting crowded. Contracts are getting weird. You know how it is.</p> <p>Elisira: [flat] We do.</p> <p>Their Lead: We can share intel. Share lanes. Keep from stepping on each other.</p> <p>Rho: [dry] Define &quot;step.&quot;</p> <p>Their Lead: [smile thinning again] You know. Compete.</p> <p>Vesper: Then file a co-op agreement.</p> <p>Their Lead: That&#x27;s-</p> <p>Vesper: That&#x27;s how coordination works if you&#x27;re not trying to hide your leverage.</p> <p>[The lead&#x27;s gaze slides to Avyanna again.]</p> <p>Their Lead: How old are you.</p> <p>[Silence drops like a tool hitting metal.]</p> <p>[Not because the question is illegal.]</p> <p>[Because it&#x27;s diagnostic.]</p> <p>Vesper: [cold] Leave.</p> <p>Their Lead: I&#x27;m not-</p> <p>Elia: [still bright] She is.</p> <p>Elia: You&#x27;re doing math in public.</p> <p>Elia: We don&#x27;t like your math.</p> <p>Their heavy shifts their weight.</p> <p>[Elia smiles wider.]</p> <p>[It isn&#x27;t friendly.]</p> <p>It&#x27;s delighted.</p> <p>Elia: If you want to learn how old she is, go ask the Guild for her file and see what they charge you.</p> <p>Their Medic: [murmuring] That&#x27;s not fair.</p> <p>Rho: [to the medic, almost kind] The galaxy isn&#x27;t.</p> <p>Rho: That&#x27;s why we build floors anyway.</p> <hr> <p>[Their admin finally looks up from the slate.]</p> <p>Their Admin: We&#x27;re not a threat.</p> <p>Elisira: Then stop asking threat questions.</p> <p>Their Admin: We were curious.</p> <p>Elisira: Curiosity is not neutral.</p> <p>Elisira: Curiosity is a direction you walk in.</p> <hr> <p>[The other crew backs up a step.]</p> <p>[Not fear.]</p> <p>[Calculation.]</p> <p>[Elia is smiling.]</p> <p>[Vesper is not.]</p> <p>[Elisira looks like a locked door.]</p> <p>[And the AIs are listening like witnesses.]</p> <p>Ten bodies doesn&#x27;t feel like power anymore.</p> <p>It feels like liability.</p> <p>Their Lead: [hands up] Okay. Okay. Message received.</p> <p>Their Lead: We&#x27;ll keep our distance.</p> <p>Vesper: Good.</p> <p>Their Lead: People are going to keep talking.</p> <p>Vesper: They were going to anyway.</p> <p>Their Lead: You&#x27;re not worried.</p> <p>Rho: We&#x27;re always worried.</p> <p>Rho: We just don&#x27;t let it run the ship.</p> <hr> <p>[The other crew leaves.]</p> <p>[Ten bodies peeling away into the concourse noise.]</p> <p>[Avyanna watches their admin&#x27;s slate as they go.]</p> <p>[Not the writing. The habit.]</p> <p>[The way they cataloged her like a salvage item.]</p> <p>(Curiosity is targeting.)</p> <p>[Elisira touches Avyanna&#x27;s elbow once as they fall back into motion.]</p> <p>[Not a drag.]</p> <p>[A check-in disguised as navigation.]</p> <p>Elisira: Green.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] Green.</p> <p>Elia: [cheerful] See.</p> <p>Elia: We can do public spaces without murder. Growth.</p> <p>Vesper: [dry] Don&#x27;t jinx it.</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// OBSERVATION: trainee held boundary under social pressure | INFERENCE: integration improving | CONFIDENCE: 0.86 | NOTE: good work&gt;</p> <p>[Avyanna doesn&#x27;t smile.]</p> <p>[But she feels something settle in her chest.]</p> <p>[Not safety.]</p> <p>[A tool.]</p> <p>[A script that isn&#x27;t obedience.]</p> <hr> <p>[As they pass a bounty board, Avyanna sees the reflection of the other crew&#x27;s lead watching them from across the concourse.]</p> <p>[Not approaching.]</p> <p>[Just... measuring.]</p> <p>[Avyanna keeps walking.]</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t look back.]</p> <p>[She lets them keep their assumptions.]</p> <p>[Because the ship taught her that being underestimated is sometimes the only affordable cloak.]</p> </div> * [[Continue to optional scene 15e: Maren Blocks->Act1 15e Maren Blocks]] * [[Continue to Chapter 16: Breach Hymn->Act1 16 Breach Hymn]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Optional 15e: Maren Blocks</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 15e_maren_blocks.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **[INTERSTITIAL - OCCURS POST-CH15]** &gt; *Trigger: Elisira teaches &quot;pattern&quot; like it&#x27;s a language* &gt; *Purpose: show Maren Sova&#x27;s foundation without confirming her fate*</p> <hr> <p>[Elisira&#x27;s lessons always look like paperwork until you realize they&#x27;re knives.]</p> <p>[After the ghost net drill, Avyanna finds Elisira at the galley table with a small tin of colored blocks spread before her.]</p> <p>[Not children&#x27;s toys.]</p> <p>[Something more deliberate-hard-edged pieces, each one marked with a tiny notch.]</p> <p>[Elisira sorts them into rows.]</p> <p>[Blue.]</p> <p>[Red.]</p> <p>[Yellow.]</p> <p>[Then she flips two pieces without changing her expression.]</p> <p>[The whole row reads differently.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [cautious] What is that.</p> <p>Elisira: Encryption.</p> <p>Avyanna: With blocks.</p> <p>Elisira: With patterns.</p> <p>[Avyanna watches her hands.]</p> <p>[They&#x27;re steady.]</p> <p>[Not the steady of calm.]</p> <p>[The steady of someone who learned to survive by making systems behave.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Who taught you.</p> <p>[Elisira&#x27;s hands pause.]</p> <p>[A beat too long.]</p> <p>Elisira: Someone.</p> <hr> <p>## Intercut - Age Eleven (Maren&#x27;s Table)</p> <p>[The apartment is small.]</p> <p>[Station-small: air that tastes like recycled promises, walls thin enough that you can hear the neighbors&#x27; arguments through them.]</p> <p>[Elisira sits at a table with her feet not quite reaching the floor.]</p> <p>[Her hands are stained with marker ink.]</p> <p>[Across from her, Maren Sova lays down three colored blocks like she&#x27;s dealing cards.]</p> <p>Maren: Rule one.</p> <p>Elisira: [automatic] Don&#x27;t trust a label.</p> <p>[Maren&#x27;s mouth twitches.]</p> <p>Maren: Good.</p> <p>Maren: Rule two.</p> <p>Elisira: Don&#x27;t trust a story.</p> <p>Maren: Better.</p> <p>Maren: Rule three.</p> <p>[Elisira hesitates.]</p> <p>[Maren waits.]</p> <p>[Not impatient.]</p> <p>[Just present, the way a good guardian is present-no pressure, no punishment, only expectation.]</p> <p>Elisira: Trust the pattern.</p> <p>Maren: Yes.</p> <hr> <p>[Maren taps the blocks.]</p> <p>Maren: This is a message.</p> <p>Elisira: It&#x27;s colors.</p> <p>Maren: Colors are symbols.</p> <p>Maren: Symbols become rules when someone enforces them.</p> <p>Maren: Who enforces this.</p> <p>Elisira: [frowning] You.</p> <p>Maren: Here.</p> <p>[Maren slides the blocks across the table, out of her own reach.]</p> <p>Maren: Now who enforces it.</p> <p>[Elisira stares.]</p> <p>[The answer lands slow and bright.]</p> <p>Elisira: Me.</p> <p>[Maren nods.]</p> <p>Maren: Good.</p> <p>Maren: Remember that.</p> <p>Maren: Most cages are built from things people agree to enforce for free.</p> <hr> <p>[Elisira&#x27;s throat tightens.]</p> <p>Elisira: Why are you teaching me this.</p> <p>Maren: Because you notice.</p> <p>Maren: And because one day you&#x27;ll need to hide in plain sight.</p> <p>Elisira: That sounds like leaving.</p> <p>[Maren&#x27;s eyes flicker-too fast to be a confession, too slow to be nothing.]</p> <p>Maren: It sounds like being prepared.</p> <p>Elisira: Prepared for what.</p> <p>Maren: [quiet] For a galaxy that lies.</p> <hr> <p>[Maren reaches over and brushes Elisira&#x27;s hair back from her forehead.]</p> <p>[Touch offered, not taken.]</p> <p>Maren: You can hate me if I disappear.</p> <p>[Elisira freezes.]</p> <p>Elisira: What.</p> <p>Maren: You can decide I abandoned you.</p> <p>Maren: If that story keeps you alive.</p> <p>[Elisira&#x27;s eyes sting.]</p> <p>Elisira: Don&#x27;t say that.</p> <p>Maren: I&#x27;m saying it because I don&#x27;t get to control what you need later.</p> <p>Maren: I only get to give you tools now.</p> <hr> <p>## Return - Galley (Now)</p> <p>[Elisira&#x27;s fingers move again.]</p> <p>[She flips a block.]</p> <p>[The pattern changes.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] Is it easier when it&#x27;s colors.</p> <p>Elisira: It was.</p> <p>Avyanna: Was.</p> <p>[Elisira looks at her for one second.]</p> <p>Elisira: It&#x27;s easier when the rules are honest.</p> <p>Avyanna: The galaxy isn&#x27;t.</p> <p>Elisira: [flat] Correct.</p> <p>[Elisira turns a block over in her fingers, considering its weight.]</p> <p>Elisira: That&#x27;s why we make our own.</p> <p>[She pushes one block toward Avyanna.]</p> <p>Elisira: Your turn.</p> <p>[Avyanna hesitates.]</p> <p>[Then she touches it.]</p> <p>[Not a toy.]</p> <p>[A tool.]</p> <p>[A small, heavy proof that people can teach you how to disappear without telling you to vanish.]</p> <hr> <p>*End Chapter 15e*</p> </div> * [[Continue to Chapter 16: Breach Hymn->Act1 16 Breach Hymn]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Chapter 16: Breach Hymn</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 16_breach_hymn.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **LUMEN THIEF - DEMOLITIONS TRAINING MANUAL (RESTRICTED):** &gt; *Subject: Controlled Destruction* &gt; *Instructor: Rho Sable* &gt; *Trainee: Avyanna Lagrange* &gt; *Summary: Making things fall is easy. Making them fall where you want is the job.* &gt; *Rule 1: Know what&#x27;s on the other side of the wall.* &gt; *Rule 2: Precision is slower. Precision is the point.* &gt; *Rule 3: The shortcut always costs more than it saves.* &gt; *Addendum: If you&#x27;re in a hurry, you&#x27;re already losing.*</p> <hr> <p>[The range is in the cargo hold&#x27;s lower section-a reinforced bay that smells like ozone and old polymer and something sharp that catches in the back of Avyanna&#x27;s throat. The walls are analog-hardened against corridor drift; someone has scratched &quot;NO LATTICE INTERFERENCE&quot; into the blast door frame.]</p> <p>[Rho is already there when she arrives. He&#x27;s setting up a simulation grid: holographic walls projected onto a metal frame, sensors embedded in the deck to track debris patterns.]</p> <p>[He doesn&#x27;t look up.]</p> <p>Rho: You&#x27;re early.</p> <p>Avyanna: Elisira&#x27;s lesson ran short.</p> <p>Rho: Elisira&#x27;s lessons never run short. She let you think it did.</p> <p>[Avyanna blinks. He&#x27;s right, probably. Everything Elisira does is deliberate.]</p> <p>[Rho finishes calibrating the grid. The holographic walls flicker-gray and solid-looking, like real bulkheads. He steps back, wipes his hands on his thighs, and finally meets her eyes.]</p> <p>Rho: Ready to learn how to break things?</p> <p>Avyanna: I&#x27;ve broken things before.</p> <p>Rho: Yeah. That&#x27;s not the same.</p> <hr> <p>[The range setup is simple: three simulated walls, each with different structural compositions displayed on a floating readout. Behind each wall, a cluster of red dots-simulated personnel. Hostiles. Civilians. Unknowns.]</p> <p>[Rho hands her a pair of safety lenses. They&#x27;re scratched and heavy.]</p> <p>Rho: The goal isn&#x27;t destruction. The goal is *access*. You want through a wall, not through whatever&#x27;s behind it.</p> <p>[Avyanna puts on the lenses. The world tints amber.]</p> <p>Avyanna: What if what&#x27;s behind it is hostile?</p> <p>Rho: Then you figure that out *before* you blow the wall. Not after.</p> <p>[He taps a control. The first simulated wall highlights-standard station composite, internal bracing visible in the overlay.]</p> <p>Rho: First lesson: reading structure. Every wall tells you how it wants to fall.</p> <hr> <p>[He walks her through the basics.]</p> <p>[Stress points. Load-bearing vs cosmetic. The difference between &quot;opens cleanly&quot; and &quot;collapses in a cascade that kills everyone in the next three rooms.&quot;]</p> <p>[Avyanna listens. Her hands itch for something to hold-a wrench, a tool, anything that makes her feel less like a student and more like someone who belongs here.]</p> <p>[Rho talks with his hands. Big gestures, precise despite their size. She notices the scars on his knuckles-different from Elisira&#x27;s. These are burns, not cuts. Chemical or thermal, healed rough.]</p> <p>Rho: See that brace? If you hit it wrong, the whole section torques. Clean breach becomes a hull compromise.</p> <p>Avyanna: And if it&#x27;s an emergency? If there&#x27;s no time to read the structure?</p> <p>[Rho&#x27;s jaw tightens. Just for a second. The grin flickers-not armor snapping into place, but armor *slipping*. He catches it. Resets it. Too slow for casual.]</p> <p>Rho: Then you&#x27;re already in trouble. And trouble means people die.</p> <hr> <p>[He sets up the first exercise: a simulated door, reinforced, with a lock mechanism and a timer.]</p> <p>Rho: Standard Guild security door. You&#x27;ve got thirty seconds to get through it without triggering the alarm on the other side.</p> <p>Avyanna: Thirty seconds isn&#x27;t-</p> <p>Rho: Yeah. It isn&#x27;t. That&#x27;s the point.</p> <p>[He hands her a kit-small charges, placement tools, a detonator with a physical switch. No smart interface. Dumb reliability. A manifest tag on the case reads &quot;ALLOCATION: TRAINING-VESPER APPROVED (GRUDGINGLY).&quot;]</p> <p>Rho: Go.</p> <p>[Avyanna moves. Her hands are steadier than she expected. The mine taught her to work fast, to work small, to occupy as little space and attention as possible.]</p> <p>[She finds the hinges. Places the first charge. Too high-she adjusts. The second charge goes on the lock mechanism. The timer ticks.]</p> <p>[She triggers the detonator.]</p> <p>[The door simulation fractures-but wrong. The debris pattern sprays backward, toward her position. Red warning lights flash across the safety lenses.]</p> <p>Rho: Dead.</p> <p>Avyanna: What?</p> <p>Rho: You shaped the charge to push inward. But you didn&#x27;t account for the lock&#x27;s reinforcement. It redirected the blast.</p> <p>[He taps the control. The simulation resets.]</p> <p>Rho: Try again.</p> <hr> <p>[She tries again.]</p> <p>[And again.]</p> <p>[The fourth attempt, the door opens cleanly. No debris spray. No alarm trigger. The red dots behind the door remain stationary-undisturbed.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s hands are shaking slightly. Not fear. Adrenaline. The shard hums at the base of her skull-warm, attentive.]</p> <p>Rho: Better.</p> <p>Avyanna: It took four tries.</p> <p>Rho: First time I did this drill, it took me eleven.</p> <p>[She stares at him.]</p> <p>Rho: [shrugging] What? I&#x27;m not naturally talented. I just practice until I stop being terrible.</p> <p>[The honesty is disarming. Avyanna doesn&#x27;t know what to do with it.]</p> <hr> <p>[They move to the second exercise: a wall with civilians on the other side.]</p> <p>[The simulation shows them as blue dots-clustered, unaware, positioned where a careless breach would kill them.]</p> <p>Rho: Same wall. Different problem. How do you get through without casualties?</p> <p>Avyanna: [studying the layout] Move them first?</p> <p>Rho: Can&#x27;t. They don&#x27;t know you&#x27;re coming. Moving them tips off the hostiles in the next section.</p> <p>[Avyanna frowns. The geometry is wrong. Every angle she can see puts the breach zone too close to the civilians.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Then you don&#x27;t go through this wall.</p> <p>[Rho&#x27;s eyebrows lift. Interest, not approval-not yet.]</p> <p>Rho: Go on.</p> <p>Avyanna: You find a different entry point. One that doesn&#x27;t put them at risk.</p> <p>Rho: What if this is the only entry point?</p> <p>Avyanna: Then you wait. Or you change the mission parameters. Or you-</p> <p>[She stops. The heat at the base of her skull pulses-frustration, maybe. The shard doesn&#x27;t like constraints it can&#x27;t solve.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I don&#x27;t know.</p> <p>Rho: That&#x27;s the right answer.</p> <p>[He taps the control. The simulation zooms out, showing the full layout-multiple entry vectors, timing windows, patrol patterns.]</p> <p>Rho: There&#x27;s always another way. The shortcut is the lie that says there isn&#x27;t.</p> <hr> <p>[They take a break. Rho produces a thermos from somewhere-coffee, or the ship&#x27;s approximation of it. He pours two cups.]</p> <p>[Avyanna takes hers. The warmth is grounding. The shard settles from frustrated to merely present.]</p> <p>Rho: You&#x27;re doing better than I expected.</p> <p>Avyanna: [wary] Is that a compliment?</p> <p>Rho: It&#x27;s an observation. Compliments are Vesper&#x27;s department.</p> <p>[He drinks. The silence stretches. Comfortable, almost. The range hums with residual charge.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Why do you do this?</p> <p>Rho: Teach?</p> <p>Avyanna: Demolitions. Breaking things. Making them fall.</p> <p>[Rho&#x27;s cup pauses halfway to his mouth. The grin doesn&#x27;t fade, but something behind it shifts-like a door closing, then forcing itself open again.]</p> <p>Rho: Because I&#x27;m good at it. And because someone has to be.</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s not an answer.</p> <p>Rho: It&#x27;s the one I&#x27;ve got.</p> <hr> <p>[The next exercise is harder.]</p> <p>[Rho sets up a scenario: a hull breach in progress, structural integrity failing, hostiles on one side and crew on the other. The timer starts at sixty seconds.]</p> <p>Rho: You can seal the breach with a shaped charge-collapse the corridor, buy time for evacuation. But the collapse will kill the hostiles.</p> <p>Avyanna: They&#x27;re hostiles. They&#x27;re trying to-</p> <p>Rho: They&#x27;re people. Maybe hired. Maybe coerced. Maybe desperate. Doesn&#x27;t matter. They&#x27;re still people.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens.]</p> <p>Avyanna: So what&#x27;s the other option?</p> <p>Rho: Slower. More precise. You shape the charge differently-collapse only the section you need, leave an exit route for them. Evacuation takes longer. More risk to the crew.</p> <p>[He watches her. No guidance. No hints.]</p> <p>Rho: Choose.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna stares at the simulation. The timer ticks. Fifty-three seconds. Fifty-two.]</p> <p>[The shard pulses-not heat, not cold. Something else. Pressure. Like it&#x27;s waiting to see what she&#x27;ll do.]</p> <p>[She thinks of the mine. Of supervisors who made choices like this every day. &quot;Acceptable losses.&quot; &quot;Necessary sacrifices.&quot; Words that meant: *someone else pays so I don&#x27;t have to.*]</p> <p>(In the Kennel, I was the acceptable loss. I was the necessary sacrifice. I will not be the one who decides someone else is.)</p> <p>Avyanna: The slower way.</p> <p>Rho: Even with the risk?</p> <p>Avyanna: Even with the risk.</p> <p>[Rho nods. His face is unreadable.]</p> <p>Rho: Do it.</p> <hr> <p>[She fails the first attempt. The collapse is too wide-one of the blue dots winks out. Civilian casualty.]</p> <p>[She fails the second attempt. The breach isn&#x27;t sealed in time. The timer hits zero.]</p> <p>[The third attempt, she gets it right. The hostiles escape through their exit route. The crew evacuates. The breach holds.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s hands are shaking again. Not adrenaline this time. Something else.]</p> <p>Rho: Slower is harder. Slower takes practice. But slower is what we do.</p> <p>Avyanna: Why?</p> <p>[The question comes out sharper than she intended. The shard&#x27;s pressure hasn&#x27;t faded-it&#x27;s watching, listening, *waiting.*]</p> <p>Avyanna: Why does it matter? If they&#x27;re hostile-if they&#x27;re trying to kill us-why does it matter if they have an exit?</p> <hr> <p>[Rho is quiet for a long moment. The range hums. Somewhere in the ship, a ventilation system cycles.]</p> <p>[When he speaks, the humor is gone. His voice is flat, careful, like he&#x27;s reciting something he&#x27;s said before-to himself, in the dark, when no one else was listening.]</p> <p>Rho: Because shortcuts have a cost. And the cost is never the one you expect.</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s not-</p> <p>Rho: I killed thirty-seven people with a shortcut.</p> <p>[The words land like a physical weight. Avyanna&#x27;s mouth closes.]</p> <p>[Rho doesn&#x27;t look at her. He&#x27;s looking at the simulation grid-at the blue dots, the red dots, the debris patterns that mark where walls used to be.]</p> <p>Rho: I was Compact military. Then rim. Then mercenary. Whatever paid, wherever the work was. I was good at it. Fast. Efficient.</p> <p>[His hands curl around the thermos. The metal creaks.]</p> <p>Rho: There was a situation. Andros Station. Under siege. Hostages in one section, hostiles in another. Command said we had a window-forty seconds to breach, extract, and evac before the station&#x27;s core went critical.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] What happened?</p> <p>Rho: I took the shortcut. Shaped charge through the main corridor. Fast. Clean. The hostiles went down. The window held.</p> <p>[A beat.]</p> <p>Rho: Except the corridor connected to a secondary habitation module. Civilians. Families. The shockwave killed them before they knew we were there.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s chest tightens. The shard goes cold-not pain, but something like recognition. Like it knows this shape of grief.]</p> <p>Rho: Thirty-seven people. Twelve of them were children.</p> <hr> <p>[The silence stretches. The range&#x27;s ambient hum fills the space between them.]</p> <p>[Avyanna doesn&#x27;t know what to say. In the mine, people died all the time. You learned not to look. Not to count. Not to carry the weight of faces you couldn&#x27;t save.]</p> <p>[But Rho carries them. She can see it now-in the set of his shoulders, the tightness around his eyes, the way his jokes deflect before anyone can ask.]</p> <p>Avyanna: The crew knows?</p> <p>Rho: Elia knows. Elisira figured it out. Vesper has access to records she shouldn&#x27;t. Jalen... Jalen lost people too. Different way. Same weight.</p> <p>Avyanna: And they still-</p> <p>Rho: They still trust me.</p> <p>[He says it like he doesn&#x27;t understand it. Like every day he wakes up expecting them to finally realize what he is.]</p> <p>Rho: Elia gave me a rule. *Precision is the only absolution.* She didn&#x27;t forgive me. She just... kept trusting me anyway. Made me earn it.</p> <p>[He looks at Avyanna. Really looks-not the casual assessment of a teacher, but something rawer. Someone deciding how much of themselves to show.]</p> <p>Rho: That&#x27;s why we choose the slower way. That&#x27;s why shortcuts cost more than they save. Because the people behind those walls-the blue dots, the red dots, whoever they are-they&#x27;re not numbers. They&#x27;re faces you&#x27;ll carry forever.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat is tight. The heat at the base of her skull has shifted-not cold anymore, but not warm either. Present. Witnessing.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I&#x27;m sorry.</p> <p>Rho: [shaking his head] Don&#x27;t be. Just... remember.</p> <p>Avyanna: Remember what?</p> <p>Rho: That the shortcut always lies. It says it&#x27;s faster. It says it&#x27;s cleaner. It says the cost is acceptable.</p> <p>[He sets down the thermos. His hands are steady again-the mask reassembling, the armor clicking back into place.]</p> <p>Rho: The cost is never acceptable. It&#x27;s just the one you haven&#x27;t paid yet.</p> <hr> <p>[They run through two more exercises. Avyanna&#x27;s focus is sharper now-not just technical, but moral. She reads the simulations differently. The blue dots aren&#x27;t abstractions anymore. They&#x27;re faces she hasn&#x27;t met yet.]</p> <p>[Rho&#x27;s teaching shifts too. Less joking. More direct. He points out her mistakes without softening them, but he also names what she&#x27;s doing right.]</p> <p>Rho: Good. You&#x27;re thinking about the exit before you think about the entry.</p> <p>Avyanna: You said there&#x27;s always another way.</p> <p>Rho: There is. The trick is being patient enough to find it.</p> <hr> <p>[At the end of the session, Rho packs up the simulation grid. The cargo hold returns to its default state-crates, cargo nets, the quiet hum of environmental systems.]</p> <p>[Avyanna helps without being asked. The work is familiar-lifting, stacking, fitting pieces into spaces where they belong. The mine taught her this much.]</p> <p>Rho: Same time tomorrow?</p> <p>Avyanna: If you want.</p> <p>Rho: I want.</p> <p>[He pauses, one hand on a crate.]</p> <p>Rho: Avyanna.</p> <p>[She looks up.]</p> <p>Rho: You asked why I do this. Breaking things. Making them fall.</p> <p>[His voice is lighter now-the armor back, the jokes hovering at the edge. But underneath, something true.]</p> <p>Rho: I do it because I already know what happens when you do it wrong. And I&#x27;d rather teach someone to do it right than watch them learn the way I did.</p> <hr> <p>[Later, in the corridor outside the cargo hold, Avyanna pauses.]</p> <p>[The shard hums-low, steady. Not pain. Not pressure. Just... presence. Acknowledgment.]</p> <p>(He carries thirty-seven faces.)</p> <p>[The thought settles into her like a new weight. Not crushing. Just there.]</p> <p>(I carry the Kennel. He carries the corridor. Jalen carries Kettering&#x27;s Rest. Everyone on this ship carries something.)</p> <p>[She thinks of Elia&#x27;s rule: *Precision is the only absolution.*]</p> <p>[It doesn&#x27;t sound like forgiveness. It sounds like a job. Something you do every day, whether you feel like it or not. A discipline instead of a pardon.]</p> <p>(Maybe that&#x27;s what floors are. Not absolution. Just... the work that makes you worth trusting.)</p> <p>[The heat along her spine spreads-warmer now. Agreement, maybe. Or just the shard learning alongside her.]</p> <p>[Avyanna walks back toward her bunk. The ship hums. Somewhere ahead, someone is arguing about the coffee machine again.]</p> <p>[She almost smiles.]</p> <hr> <p>&lt;WAFFLE.BAT// OBSERVATION: trainee completed demolitions drill | INFERENCE: moral framework strengthening | CONFIDENCE: 0.81 | NOTE: slower is harder. slower is what we do.&gt;</p> <hr> <p>## Coda - Rho&#x27;s Ledger</p> <p>[0300. The cargo hold is empty. The simulation grid is packed away. The deck plates are cold under Rho&#x27;s boots.]</p> <p>[He sits on a crate in the dark, datapad in hand. The screen&#x27;s glow is the only light-blue and clinical, washing out the color in his face.]</p> <p>[The file is encrypted. Three layers. Cinnamon could break it-she&#x27;s broken harder-but she won&#x27;t. Trust boundary. Some doors stay closed because someone asks, not because they&#x27;re locked.]</p> <p>**LEDGER - PERSONAL - RHO SABLE**</p> <p>[The entries are names.]</p> <p>[Thirty-seven of them at the top. Andros Station. The corridor. The shortcut that wasn&#x27;t.]</p> <p>[Below that: others. People who died because he was fast instead of careful. People who died because he followed orders. People who died because he was young and stupid and believed that &quot;acceptable losses&quot; was a real thing.]</p> <p>[He doesn&#x27;t read the names. He knows them. They&#x27;re carved into the back of his skull, behind his eyes, in the place where dreams should be.]</p> <p>[Tonight, he adds a new entry. Not a name. A number.]</p> <p>**Days since last shortcut: 847**</p> <p>[He stares at it. The number feels fragile. Tomorrow it will be 848. Then 849. Then a thousand, maybe. Or zero, if he screws up.]</p> <p>[The jokes are armor. Everyone knows that. Rho knows they know. It doesn&#x27;t matter. The armor works because it gives people something to react to-something easier than the truth.]</p> <p>[The truth is this: the ledger is discipline. The count is work. Every day he doesn&#x27;t take the shortcut is a floor under someone else&#x27;s feet.]</p> <p>*Precision is the only absolution.*</p> <p>[He&#x27;d laughed when she said it. A short, bitter sound. &quot;I&#x27;m not looking for absolution,&quot; he&#x27;d told her.]</p> <p>[And Elia-small and calm and carrying her own weight of faces-had just looked at him.]</p> <p>*&quot;Then what are you looking for?&quot;*</p> <p>[He still doesn&#x27;t have an answer. But he has the ledger. He has the count. He has tomorrow, and the day after, and every day after that where he doesn&#x27;t take the shortcut.]</p> <p>[That&#x27;s not absolution. That&#x27;s not forgiveness. That&#x27;s just... staying alive long enough to do the work.]</p> <hr> <p>[Rho closes the datapad. The cargo hold goes dark.]</p> <p>[Somewhere in the ship, someone laughs. Jalen, probably-or Nyx, if they&#x27;re feeling bold. The sound is muffled, distant, real.]</p> <p>[He sits with it for a moment. The laughter. The hum of the ship. The weight of the faces he carries.]</p> <p>[Then he stands. Rolls his shoulders.
