The mine uses stress like a tool because it works.)</p> <p>Avyanna: How do you know the conditions are supportive.</p> <p>Grove: We check.</p> <p>[Grove points to sensors embedded in the soil bed.]</p> <p>Grove: Water. Light. Minerals. Temperature. Fungal balance.</p> <p>Grove: We do not guess.</p> <p>Avyanna: [soft] That sounds like Vesper.</p> <p>Grove: Vesper and I agree on method.</p> <p>Grove: Vesper is sharper about adversaries.</p> <p>Avyanna: And you&#x27;re... sharper about leaves.</p> <p>Grove: Correct.</p> <hr> <p>[Grove offers Avyanna the mint cutting like it&#x27;s a fragile weapon.]</p> <p>Grove: Your turn.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s hands hover.]</p> <p>(My hands have only ever been used to take or to work. Not to... help something live.)</p> <p>Avyanna: What if I kill it.</p> <p>Grove: Then we try again.</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s it.</p> <p>Grove: That is it.</p> <p>[Avyanna takes the cutting with two fingers, too careful.]</p> <p>Grove: Hold it like you trust it.</p> <p>Avyanna: I don&#x27;t trust it.</p> <p>Grove: Hold it like you trust yourself.</p> <p>[That&#x27;s worse.]</p> <p>[Avyanna exhales.]</p> <p>[She adjusts her grip.]</p> <p>[The plant doesn&#x27;t break.]</p> <p>[It sits in her hand like a small promise that doesn&#x27;t demand anything.]</p> <hr> <p>[Grove guides her through steps.]</p> <p>[Cut at an angle.]</p> <p>[Strip the lower leaves.]</p> <p>[Set it in water, then soil when roots appear.]</p> <p>[Avyanna repeats each step out loud, because saying things out loud makes them real and less likely to be stolen by fear.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Angle cut. Leaves off. Water.</p> <p>Grove: Excellent.</p> <p>[Avyanna places the cutting in the jar.]</p> <p>[It floats for a second, then settles.]</p> <p>[Her chest tightens anyway.]</p> <p>(I did something and it didn&#x27;t immediately punish me.)</p> <hr> <p>[Nyx steps into the garden deck like they belong to quiet.]</p> <p>Nyx: You&#x27;re gardening.</p> <p>Avyanna: [startled] I&#x27;m... attempting.</p> <p>Nyx: Attempting is sufficient.</p> <p>Grove: She is propagating.</p> <p>Nyx: [a faint, almost-smile] Propagation as doctrine.</p> <p>Avyanna: What.</p> <p>Nyx: You cut a thing, and it becomes more without becoming owned.</p> <p>Nyx: The galaxy could learn.</p> <p>[Avyanna looks down at the jar.]</p> <p>Avyanna: The mine would call it &quot;resource duplication.&quot;</p> <p>Grove: The mine is a bad poet.</p> <p>Avyanna: [a real laugh] Yes.</p> <hr> <p>[Nyx watches her hands for a moment.]</p> <p>Nyx: Does your body feel different in here.</p> <p>[Avyanna hesitates.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I... don&#x27;t know.</p> <p>Nyx: Then tell me what you can observe without guessing.</p> <p>Avyanna: My shoulders are lower.</p> <p>Avyanna: My teeth don&#x27;t hurt.</p> <p>Avyanna: I&#x27;m not counting exits as fast.</p> <p>Nyx: Good.</p> <p>Nyx: You&#x27;re learning that safety can be boring.</p> <p>Avyanna: Boring sounds... nice.</p> <p>Nyx: It is.</p> <p>Nyx: Boring is the foundation everything else can stand on.</p> <hr> <p>[Bubbles floats through the doorway carrying a small label maker.]</p> <p>Bubbles: Do you want to label the jar.</p> <p>Avyanna: Why.</p> <p>Bubbles: Because labeling reduces loss.</p> <p>[Avyanna swallows.]</p> <p>(Loss is always waiting.)</p> <p>Avyanna: Yes.</p> <p>[Bubbles prints a label and sticks it on the jar.]</p> <p>&gt; **MINT - AVYANNA**</p> <p>[Avyanna stares at her own name on something living.]</p> <p>(This is mine. Not as property. As responsibility.)</p> <p>Avyanna: [hoarse] It says my name.</p> <p>Bubbles: Yes.</p> <p>Avyanna: I didn&#x27;t earn it.</p> <p>Bubbles: You chose it.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens.]</p> <p>Nyx: Choosing is the only thing that matters, sometimes.</p> <hr> <p>[Grove adjusts the grow lights, dimmer and warmer.]</p> <p>Grove: Now we wait.</p> <p>Avyanna: Waiting is hard.</p> <p>Grove: Yes.</p> <p>Grove: Waiting is also a skill.</p> <p>[Avyanna looks at the jar, then at the soil beds, then at Grove&#x27;s careful hands.]</p> <p>Avyanna: In the mine, if you waited you died.</p> <p>Grove: In the mine, waiting was imposed.</p> <p>Grove: Here, waiting is chosen.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] That&#x27;s different.</p> <p>Grove: It is.</p> <p>[Avyanna sits on the bench and watches her cutting float in water like a tiny ship learning to exist without fear.]</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t know how long she sits.]</p> <p>[No one tells her to move.]</p> <p>[No one charges her a fee for being still.]</p> <hr> <p>[The next day, she comes back.]</p> <p>[Not because anyone told her to. Because she can&#x27;t stop thinking about the jar with her name on it.]</p> <p>(If I leave it alone, will it die.)</p> <p>(If it dies, is that my fault.)</p> <p>[Grove is already there.]</p> <p>Grove: Welcome back.</p> <p>Avyanna: [defensive] I&#x27;m checking.</p> <p>Grove: Yes.</p> <p>Grove: Checking is care.</p> <p>[Avyanna peers into the jar.]</p> <p>[No roots yet. Just green in water.]</p> <p>Avyanna: It looks the same.</p> <p>Grove: Correct.</p> <p>Avyanna: That feels... unproductive.</p> <p>Grove: Growth is not always visible.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s jaw tightens.]</p> <p>(In the mine, if you weren&#x27;t visibly productive you were punished.)</p> <p>Avyanna: How do you know it&#x27;s working.</p> <p>Grove: We observe.</p> <p>[Grove points to the mint stem.]</p> <p>Grove: Leaf color stable. Stem firm. No rot. Water clear.</p> <p>Grove: These are indicators.</p> <p>Avyanna: So you don&#x27;t... panic.</p> <p>Grove: Panic is a form of guessing.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] I guess a lot.</p> <p>Grove: Yes.</p> <p>Grove: You learned that guessing is safer than asking.</p> <p>[Avyanna flinches.]</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s not-</p> <p>Grove: It is not a moral failure.</p> <p>Grove: It is training data from a hostile environment.</p> <p>[Avyanna exhales.]</p> <p>(Training data. Not destiny.)</p> <hr> <p>[Bubbles arrives with a small notebook.]</p> <p>Bubbles: I brought you paper.</p> <p>Avyanna: [startled] Why.</p> <p>Bubbles: Because you like logs.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I do.</p> <p>[Bubbles sets the notebook beside the jar.]</p> <p>&gt; **MINT LOG** &gt; &gt; *Day:* &gt; &gt; *Observation:* &gt; &gt; *Action taken (if any):*</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s... adorable.</p> <p>Bubbles: It is functional.</p> <p>Avyanna: Same thing.</p> <p>[Bubbles&#x27; fans shift-pleased.]</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna writes.]</p> <p>&gt; **DAY 1** &gt; &gt; *Observation: still alive. No roots.* &gt; &gt; *Action: none.*</p> <p>[Her pen hovers.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Action: none.</p> <p>(Doing nothing feels like a crime.)</p> <p>Grove: Correct.</p> <p>Grove: Sometimes doing nothing is the correct action.</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s- [she searches for a word] -wild.</p> <p>Grove: Thank you.</p> <hr> <p>[Nyx appears again, as if drawn by the scent of a nervous system trying to learn calm.]</p> <p>Nyx: You returned.</p> <p>Avyanna: [defensive] I wanted to make sure-</p> <p>Nyx: Yes.</p> <p>Nyx: Wanting to make sure is allowed.</p> <p>[Avyanna swallows.]</p> <p>Avyanna: In the mine, wanting was... dangerous.</p> <p>Nyx: In the mine, wanting was leverage.</p> <p>Nyx: Here, wanting is information about what keeps you alive.</p> <p>[Avyanna looks at the jar.]</p> <p>Avyanna: This keeps me alive?</p> <p>Nyx: Not the mint.</p> <p>Nyx: The practice.</p> <p>Nyx: You are learning to care for something without turning it into a test.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I&#x27;m failing at that.</p> <p>Nyx: You are practicing at that.</p> <hr> <p>[Nyx gestures toward the bench.]</p> <p>Nyx: Sit.</p> <p>[Avyanna sits.]</p> <p>Nyx: Put your feet on the deck.</p> <p>[Avyanna does.]</p> <p>Nyx: Tell me five things you can see.</p> <p>[Avyanna blinks.]</p> <p>Avyanna: The mint jar. The soil beds. The grow lights. Your hands. Bubbles&#x27; fans.</p> <p>Nyx: Four things you can feel.</p> <p>Avyanna: The bench. The air. The warmth from the lights. The vibration of the ship.</p> <p>Nyx: Three things you can hear.</p> <p>Avyanna: Water in the irrigation line. The fans. My own breathing.</p> <p>Nyx: Two things you can smell.</p> <p>Avyanna: Soil. Mint.</p> <p>Nyx: One thing you can taste.</p> <p>Avyanna: [a tiny pause] Tea.</p> <p>Nyx: Good.</p> <p>Nyx: That&#x27;s a grounding tool.</p> <p>Nyx: Not mysticism. Nervous system maintenance.</p> <p>[Avyanna swallows.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Maintenance.</p> <p>Nyx: Everything is maintenance.</p> <p>[Grove hums like agreement.]</p> <p>Grove: Everything that lives requires maintenance.</p> <hr> <p>[Nyx leaves without making it heavy.]</p> <p>[Bubbles lingers a moment longer.]</p> <p>Bubbles: You can name the jar something else if you want.</p> <p>Avyanna: Why would I.</p> <p>Bubbles: Some humans like to name things they care for.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] Naming feels like claiming.</p> <p>Bubbles: It can be.</p> <p>Bubbles: It can also be honoring.</p> <p>[Avyanna looks at the jar.]</p> <p>[The label says MINT - AVYANNA.]</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t change it.]</p> <p>[Not yet.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Maybe later.</p> <p>Bubbles: Accepted.</p> <p>[Avyanna sits in the garden deck for a while, watching something not change and learning that not changing is not the same as dying.]</p> </div> * [[Continue to Chapter 19: Contract Station Justice->Act1 19 Contract Station Justice]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Chapter 19: Contract Station Justice</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 19_contract_station_justice.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **GUILD ARBITRATION NOTICE - FORM 77-K (EXPEDITED):** &gt; *Re: Disputed Cargo Manifest, Registry 4412-HELM* &gt; *Arbiter Assigned: Pending (Lumen Thief crew, observer capacity)* &gt; *Summary: Both parties claim ownership. Neither party has clean hands.* &gt; *Note: The truth is a receipt. Everything else is negotiation.*</p> <hr> <p>[Helder Weir Station smells like recycled air and old grievances.]</p> <p>[Avyanna stands in the observation gallery overlooking the arbitration floor-a circular chamber with tiered seating, holographic evidence displays, and a raised platform where a Guild arbiter sits like a judge in a court that never quite stopped being a market.]</p> <p>[Eight months on the Lumen Thief. Eight months of learning to move differently, speak differently, see differently. The shard at the base of her skull hums-present but not pressing. Almost comfortable.]</p> <p>(I&#x27;m getting used to this. That should worry me more than it does.)</p> <p>Vesper: [beside her, voice low] Watch the claimants. Not the arbiter.</p> <p>Avyanna: Why?</p> <p>Vesper: Because the arbiter already knows who&#x27;s lying. The question is which lie they&#x27;ll decide to believe.</p> <hr> <p>[The case is simple on paper: two shipping consortiums disputing ownership of a cargo pod retrieved from a static zone. Standard salvage law says the finder keeps it. Standard contract law says the original owner retains claim.]</p> <p>[The complication: the cargo pod contains medical supplies that were never meant to reach their destination. Diverted. Stolen. Relabeled. The original &quot;owner&quot; is a Compact logistics house that sold the supplies twice-once legitimately, once to a black market intermediary who died in the static bloom that stranded the pod.]</p> <p>[The finder is a rim scavenger crew who pulled the pod from wreckage and claims salvage rights.]</p> <p>[Neither party is innocent. Both are lying. The medical supplies are needed by a station three systems away where children are dying of a preventable infection.]</p> <p>[This is the job.]</p> <hr> <p>Vesper: [studying a datapad] The logistics house has better documentation. Cleaner chain of custody. More lawyers.</p> <p>Avyanna: So they&#x27;ll win?</p> <p>Vesper: They&#x27;ll win unless we make the cost of winning higher than the cost of losing.</p> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s fingers move across the datapad-cross-referencing manifests, timestamps, comm logs. Her expression is neutral, focused. This is what she does: finds the seams in other people&#x27;s stories.]</p> <p>Avyanna: What are you looking for?</p> <p>Vesper: The thing they don&#x27;t want found.</p> <hr> <p>[The arbitration proceeds. Testimony. Counter-testimony. Legal precedent cited with the reverence of scripture.]</p> <p>[Avyanna watches the claimants. The logistics house representative-a woman in Compact corporate attire, smile professional, hands steady. The scavenger captain-weathered, defensive, obviously outmatched by the legal theater.]</p> <p>[The arbiter listens. Takes notes. Asks clarifying questions that feel like traps.]</p> <p>[Vesper doesn&#x27;t speak. She&#x27;s not here to argue. She&#x27;s here to observe-and to deliver a receipt at the precise moment it will matter most.]</p> <hr> <p>[During a recess, Vesper pulls Avyanna into a side corridor.]</p> <p>Vesper: The logistics house has a problem.</p> <p>Avyanna: Which one?</p> <p>Vesper: [almost smiling] Good question. They have several. But the one that matters is this: the cargo manifest they submitted as evidence was modified three days ago.</p> <p>[She shows Avyanna the datapad. Two versions of the same document-identical except for a single line entry that changes &quot;medical grade biosealant&quot; to &quot;industrial adhesive.&quot;]</p> <p>Avyanna: They changed the cargo description?</p> <p>Vesper: They changed what they&#x27;re claiming ownership of. The original manifest-the one filed at origin-lists specific medical compounds. The version they submitted here lists generic industrial materials.</p> <p>Avyanna: Why would they-</p> <p>[She stops. Thinks. The shard pulses-not guidance, just attention. It wants her to figure this out.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Because the medical compounds were never supposed to be in that pod in the first place.</p> <p>Vesper: [nodding] The pod was diverted from a legitimate shipment. The supplies were stolen from a hospital contract and sold to the intermediary. If the original manifest is introduced, it proves the logistics house committed fraud.</p> <p>Avyanna: So they falsified evidence to hide a crime.</p> <p>Vesper: They falsified evidence to hide *this* crime. The crime itself is somewhere else-buried in accounting they thought no one would ever see.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s chest tightens. She thinks of the station three systems away. The children. The preventable infection.]</p> <p>Avyanna: If they win, they get the supplies back. And then what?</p> <p>Vesper: Then the supplies disappear into a warehouse somewhere. Resold. Relabeled again. The station doesn&#x27;t get them.</p> <p>Avyanna: And if the scavengers win?</p> <p>Vesper: They sell to whoever pays. Maybe the station. Maybe not.</p> <p>[The answer Avyanna wants doesn&#x27;t exist. There&#x27;s no outcome where the right people automatically get what they need.]</p> <p>Vesper: [watching her] This is the part where you learn that evidence isn&#x27;t justice. It&#x27;s leverage. What you do with the leverage-that&#x27;s where the ethics live.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Then why have a court.</p> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s gaze stays on the chamber below. The arbiter. The parties. The performative politeness.]</p> <p>Vesper: Because people like believing that truth automatically becomes consequence.</p> <p>Vesper: Courts sell that belief.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] Does it work.</p> <p>Vesper: Sometimes.</p> <p>[A pause.]</p> <p>Vesper: More often, it delays.</p> <p>Avyanna: And delay kills the station with the kids.</p> <p>[Vesper looks at her now. Direct.]</p> <p>Vesper: Yes.</p> <p>Vesper: So your first civics lesson is not &quot;who is right.&quot;</p> <p>Vesper: It&#x27;s: who can move the world in time.</p> <p>[Avyanna stares.]</p> <p>Vesper: Ask three questions.</p> <p>Vesper: Who has the authority.</p> <p>Vesper: Who has the force.</p> <p>Vesper: Who has the clock.</p> <p>Avyanna: The clock.</p> <p>Vesper: The timeline.</p> <p>Vesper: If we turn this into a criminal investigation, the supplies sit in evidence lockup for months while everyone argues about procedure.</p> <p>Vesper: The children don&#x27;t get months.</p> <p>Avyanna: So we stay in arbitration.</p> <p>Vesper: We use the venue that can move the cargo inside forty-eight hours.</p> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s mouth tightens like she hates that this is how the universe is arranged.]</p> <p>Vesper: It&#x27;s not justice.</p> <p>Vesper: It&#x27;s harm reduction.</p> <p>Avyanna: [after a beat] And then we build a bigger case later.</p> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s eyes flick-a tiny approval.]</p> <p>Vesper: Yes.</p> <hr> <p>[When the arbitration resumes, Vesper requests permission to submit supplementary evidence.]</p> <p>[The arbiter-a Guild veteran with the tired eyes of someone who&#x27;s seen every trick-checks her registry.]</p> <p>Arbiter: Lumen Thief is listed as a Guild-certified evidence contractor. Amicus submission is permitted under Protocol 31-A.</p> <p>[She grants the request with obvious skepticism, but the permission is real. The logistics representative&#x27;s counsel objects anyway-overruled before they finish speaking.]</p> <p>[Vesper stands. Her posture is relaxed, her voice conversational. She doesn&#x27;t look like someone about to detonate a case.]</p> <p>Vesper: Arbiter, I&#x27;d like to submit the original cargo manifest from origin station Taelios Forge, timestamp-verified and chain-of-custody confirmed through Guild archive protocols.</p> <p>[The logistics representative&#x27;s smile flickers. Just for a moment.]</p> <p>Representative: Objection. The manifest we submitted is the controlling document.</p> <p>Vesper: The manifest you submitted was modified three days ago. Guild archive protocol 12-C requires preservation of all versions. I have the original.</p> <p>[She transfers the file. The holographic display updates. Two manifests, side by side. The difference highlighted in red.]</p> <p>Vesper: The original lists medical compounds specific to pediatric immunization protocols. The modified version lists industrial adhesive. The question, Arbiter, is why a logistics house would alter evidence to obscure the nature of cargo they claim to own.</p> <hr> <p>[The silence is heavy. The arbiter studies the documents. The logistics representative is speaking rapidly with her legal counsel-whispers sharp enough to cut.]</p> <p>[The scavenger captain looks confused but hopeful. He doesn&#x27;t understand what&#x27;s happening, but he understands momentum shifting.]</p> <p>[Avyanna watches Vesper. The calm. The precision. No flourish, no drama-just the receipt, delivered at the moment it mattered most.]</p> <p>(This is what she does. She makes the truth visible. And then she waits for the weight of it to do the work.)</p> <hr> <p>[The arbiter calls another recess. This one feels different-charged, inevitable.]</p> <p>[In the corridor, the logistics representative approaches Vesper. Her professional smile is gone. What replaces it is something colder-calculation without mask.]</p> <p>Representative: You&#x27;ve made your point. What do you want?</p> <p>Vesper: The supplies delivered to Eirene Reach Station within forty-eight hours. Full manifested value. No relabeling.</p> <p>Representative: That&#x27;s not-</p> <p>Vesper: I have the original contract between your company and the intermediary. The one that shows you sold stolen medical supplies to a black market contact. Would you like me to submit that as well?</p> <p>[The representative&#x27;s jaw tightens.]</p> <p>Vesper: [mild] The Guild takes document fraud seriously. So does the Compact regulatory board. So do the hospitals whose patients died because supplies never arrived.</p> <p>[The representative&#x27;s datapad creaks in his grip.]</p> <p>Representative: This is extortion.</p> <p>Vesper: This is leverage. The difference is I&#x27;m giving you a choice.</p> <hr> <p>[The resolution happens quickly after that.]</p> <p>[The logistics house &quot;voluntarily&quot; releases the cargo to humanitarian transit. The scavenger crew receives salvage compensation-less than they wanted, more than they expected. The station at Eirene Reach will have the medical supplies within forty-eight hours.]</p> <p>[No one is happy. Everyone has lost something. But children three systems away will live who would have died.]</p> <p>[Avyanna stands in the observation gallery as the chamber empties. The shard hums-warm, present. Not approval exactly. Acknowledgment.]</p> <hr> <p>Vesper: [joining her] Questions?</p> <p>Avyanna: You knew the whole time. What they did. What the outcome needed to be.</p> <p>Vesper: I knew the evidence. The outcome was... negotiable.</p> <p>Avyanna: You could have submitted everything at the start. Blown the whole case open.</p> <p>Vesper: And then what? The arbitration becomes a criminal investigation. The supplies sit in evidence lockup for months. The station runs out of time.</p> <p>[She turns to face Avyanna directly. Her eyes are steady, patient-the same way she looks when teaching, when explaining, when waiting for understanding to land.]</p> <p>Vesper: The goal was never punishment. The goal was the supplies. Everything else-the leverage, the timing, the pressure-was in service of that.</p> <p>Avyanna: But they got away with it. The fraud. The theft. The people who died.</p> <p>Vesper: [quietly] They didn&#x27;t get away with it. They just paid a different price. One that served the people who needed help now.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna is silent. The gallery empties around them. Station lights cycle toward evening mode-dimmer, warmer, the artificial rhythm of a day that doesn&#x27;t exist.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Is that how it always works?</p> <p>Vesper: No. Sometimes the leverage isn&#x27;t enough. Sometimes the timing is wrong. Sometimes you have all the receipts in the world and the people with power just... don&#x27;t care.</p> <p>[She looks out at the empty arbitration floor.]</p> <p>Vesper: But you build anyway. Document anyway. Create the receipts even when no one&#x27;s watching. Because one day, the timing will be right. And when it is, you&#x27;ll be ready.</p> <hr> <p>[They leave the station together. The Lumen Thief is docked at berth seven-a familiar silhouette against the star-scattered black.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s mind is full. The case. The evidence. The children who will live because Vesper knew when to deploy a truth.]</p> <p>(Justice isn&#x27;t a verdict. It&#x27;s leverage used for the right thing.)</p> <p>[The shard pulses-warm, settled. Learning alongside her.]</p> <p>(I&#x27;m starting to understand what Vesper does. What receipts are for. Why evidence matters even when the system is broken.)</p> <p>[She glances at Vesper. The older woman is already reviewing something on her datapad-the next case, probably. The next receipt to build.]</p> <p>(She never stops. Even when it doesn&#x27;t work. Even when the leverage isn&#x27;t enough. She just... keeps building.)</p> <hr> <p>[Back on the ship, Elia is in the common area with Rho. They&#x27;re arguing about something trivial-coffee rationing, maybe, or the proper calibration of the galley&#x27;s temperature settings. The kind of argument that means nothing and everything.]</p> <p>Elia: [noticing them] How&#x27;d it go?</p> <p>Vesper: Supplies are en route to Eirene Reach. The logistics house is reconsidering their business practices.</p> <p>Elia: [dry] Reconsidering or actually changing?</p> <p>Vesper: Reconsidering with prejudice.</p> <p>[Elia&#x27;s mouth quirks. Almost a smile.]</p> <p>Elia: And the trainee?</p> <p>[She looks at Avyanna. The gaze is assessing but not unkind-the same way she looks at everyone, cataloging capability and cost.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I learned that evidence isn&#x27;t justice.</p> <p>Elia: [nodding slowly] That&#x27;s the first half.</p> <p>Avyanna: What&#x27;s the second half?</p> <p>Elia: That you make it justice anyway. Even when the system doesn&#x27;t cooperate.</p> <hr> <p>[That night, Avyanna lies in her bunk and stares at the ceiling.]</p> <p>[The case replays in her mind. The manifests. The timing. The moment Vesper&#x27;s receipt landed like a controlled demolition-precise, devastating, exactly where it needed to be.]</p> <p>(Rho taught me how to make walls fall without hurting people behind them. Vesper taught me how to make truth land without losing the goal.)</p> <p>[The shard hums. Warm. Present.]</p> <p>(Different skills. Same principle. Precision in service of something that matters.)</p> <p>[She thinks of the children at Eirene Reach. Faces she&#x27;ll never see. Lives she helped save without being in the room.]</p> <p>(Is this what the crew does? Build floors for people they&#x27;ll never meet?)</p> <hr> <p>[In the corridor outside, she hears Vesper&#x27;s voice-low, steady, probably dictating case notes. The eternal documentation. The receipts that accumulate like armor.]</p> <p>[Avyanna closes her eyes.]</p> <p>[For the first time in months, she doesn&#x27;t dream of the mine.]</p> <hr> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// OBSERVATION: trainee remained focused through 7-hour arbitration | INFERENCE: evidence metabolism improving | CONFIDENCE: 0.79 | NOTE: she&#x27;s learning to wait for the right moment. that&#x27;s the hard part.&gt;</p> <hr> <p>*End Chapter 19*</p> </div> Where does the route go next? * [[Read optional scene 19a: Contract Negotiation Witness->Act1 19a Contract Negotiation Witness]] * [[Read optional scene 19b: Teaching Consent To Client->Act1 19b Teaching Consent To Client]] * [[Read optional scene 19c: Receipt Practice->Act1 19c Receipt Practice]] * [[Continue to Chapter 20: The Receipt->Act1 20 The Receipt]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Optional 19a: Contract Negotiation Witness</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 19a_contract_negotiation_witness.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **[SCENE - OCCURS POST-CH19, STATION LAYOVER]** &gt; *Location: Guild Station private meeting room* &gt; *Present: Avyanna (observing), Vesper, potential client, Cinnamon (recording)* &gt; *Teaching: Contract boundaries, saying no, client vetting*</p> <hr> <p>[The meeting room is small. Soundproofed. One table, four chairs, a secure terminal, and the particular weight of money about to maybe change hands.]</p> <p>[Vesper sits across from a potential client-human, middle-aged, wearing clothes that say &quot;I have resources but I&#x27;m pretending I don&#x27;t.&quot; The client&#x27;s hands are folded on the table. Professional. Controlled. Hiding something.]</p> <p>[Avyanna sits beside Vesper, one chair back. Observer. Witness. Learning.]</p> <p>[Cinnamon manifests on the table&#x27;s holo-projector-a small crystalline form, rotating slowly. Recording everything.]</p> <hr> <p>Client: [measured] I need evidence retrieved from a corporate data vault. Financial records proving contractual violations. The contract in question is between my family&#x27;s estate and Helios Extraction. They&#x27;ve been siphoning funds for three years. I need proof.</p> <p>Vesper: [stylus poised over her datapad] Helios Extraction. Corporate entity. That means enhanced security, legal complications if caught, and potential retaliation.</p> <p>Vesper: [meeting the client&#x27;s eyes] Our fee for corporate targets starts at fifty thousand credits. Non-negotiable. Half up front, half on delivery.</p> <p>Client: [not flinching] Acceptable.</p> <hr> <p>Vesper: [continuing] Evidence retrieval only. We don&#x27;t plant false data. We don&#x27;t delete files. We don&#x27;t alter records. We pull what exists and verify authenticity. Understood?</p> <p>Client: [nodding] Understood.</p> <p>Vesper: Timeline. How urgent is this?</p> <p>Client: [hesitating] Ideally within two weeks. The estate litigation is scheduled for three weeks from now. I need the evidence before then.</p> <p>Vesper: [noting it] Tight timeline. Increases risk. Adds ten thousand to the fee.</p> <p>Client: [tense] That&#x27;s-</p> <p>Vesper: [interrupting, flat] Non-negotiable. Rush jobs mean less preparation time. Less preparation means higher risk. Higher risk means higher cost. You want it done right, or you want it done cheap?</p> <hr> <p>[The client&#x27;s jaw tightens. But they nod.]</p> <p>Client: [forced] Fine. Sixty thousand total.</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] Sixty thousand. Half up front, transferred to Guild escrow. We retrieve the evidence, verify it, deliver it to you. You confirm receipt, payment releases from escrow. Standard terms.</p> <p>Vesper: [tapping her datapad] Now. Scope limitations. We will not retrieve evidence that incriminates *you*. We will not retrieve evidence that could be used to harm uninvolved third parties. We will not participate in corporate espionage beyond what you&#x27;ve specified.</p> <p>Vesper: If we find evidence outside your stated scope, we notify you. You decide whether to accept it or have it deleted. Your choice. Our execution.</p> <p>Client: [frowning] What if the evidence I need is... intertwined with other information?</p> <p>Vesper: [measured] Define intertwined.</p> <hr> <p>Client: [carefully] The financial records I need are in a database that also contains... personnel files. Medical records. Things that aren&#x27;t relevant to my case but might be in the same data structure.</p> <p>Vesper: [leaning back] Then we pull the financial records only. If the database structure makes selective extraction impossible, we pull everything, isolate your specified evidence, and delete the rest before delivery.</p> <p>Vesper: [firm] We don&#x27;t keep copies. We don&#x27;t sell data to third parties. We don&#x27;t leverage what we find for secondary profit. You&#x27;re hiring us for retrieval, not for exploitation.</p> <p>Client: [nodding slowly] And if I wanted... additional services?</p> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s expression doesn&#x27;t change. But something in her posture sharpens.]</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] Define additional services.</p> <hr> <p>Client: [hesitant] If, in the process of retrieving my evidence, you found... other useful information. Information about Helios&#x27;s other clients. Their operations. Vulnerabilities.</p> <p>Client: [leaning forward slightly] I have contacts who would pay well for that kind of intelligence.</p> <p>[Silence.]</p> <p>[Avyanna watches Vesper&#x27;s face. Neutral. Unreadable. But her stylus has stopped moving.]</p> <p>Vesper: [after a long moment, very calm] No.</p> <p>Client: [surprised] No?</p> <p>Vesper: [flat] No. That&#x27;s espionage. That&#x27;s data brokering. That&#x27;s not our service. If you want corporate intelligence, hire a different contractor.</p> <hr> <p>Client: [trying again] I&#x27;m offering to pay-</p> <p>Vesper: [interrupting] I don&#x27;t care what you&#x27;re offering. The answer is no. We retrieve evidence per contract. We don&#x27;t steal unrelated data. We don&#x27;t broker information. We don&#x27;t participate in intelligence trafficking.</p> <p>Vesper: [meeting their eyes] If that&#x27;s what you need, this meeting is over.</p> <p>[The client sits back. Recalculating.]</p> <p>Client: [measured] I apologize. I misunderstood your services.</p> <p>Vesper: [cold] You understood perfectly. You were testing boundaries. Now you know where they are.</p> <hr> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// OBSERVATION: client attempted to expand contract scope to include unauthorized intelligence gathering | VESPER DECLINED | RATIONALE: maintains Guild standing and legal protections | RECORDING: preserved for crew records&gt;</p> <p>[Vesper taps her datapad.]</p> <p>Vesper: [businesslike] Let&#x27;s return to the original contract. Financial records proving contractual violations by Helios Extraction. Sixty thousand credits. Two-week timeline. Evidence retrieval only, no alteration, no secondary use.</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] Do you accept these terms as stated, with no additional requests?</p> <p>Client: [after a pause] Yes. I accept.</p> <p>Vesper: [pulling up a contract template] Then we proceed.</p> <hr> <p>[She drafts the contract in real-time. Every term they discussed, formalized in plain language. No jargon. No loopholes. Just clear statements of what will be done, what won&#x27;t be done, and what happens if either party deviates.]</p> <p>[She turns the datapad toward the client.]</p> <p>Vesper: Read it. Every word. If anything is unclear, ask. If you disagree with any term, say so now. Once you sign, this is binding.</p> <p>[The client reads. Takes their time. Avyanna watches their eyes move across the screen, parsing legal language, looking for traps.]</p> <p>[There are no traps. Just boundaries.]</p> <hr> <p>Client: [looking up] This clause here. &quot;Contractor reserves right to terminate contract if client requests actions outside stated scope.&quot; That&#x27;s standard?</p> <p>Vesper: [nodding] Yes. If you ask us to do something we didn&#x27;t agree to, we can walk away. You get your deposit back minus expenses incurred. We get to not participate in things we don&#x27;t ethically endorse.</p> <p>Client: [frowning] What counts as &quot;outside stated scope&quot;?</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] Anything not in the contract. You want us to plant evidence? Outside scope. You want us to harm someone? Outside scope. You want us to steal unrelated data for resale? Outside scope.</p> <p>Vesper: [meeting their eyes] This clause protects both of us. You from being accused of requesting illegal actions you didn&#x27;t request. Us from being coerced into things we didn&#x27;t agree to.</p> <hr> <p>[The client nods slowly. Continues reading.]</p> <p>[After two minutes, they look up.]</p> <p>Client: [calm] I&#x27;m satisfied. Where do I sign?</p> <p>Vesper: [tapping the screen] Here. Biometric signature. Timestamp. Guild-certified binding contract.</p> <p>[The client signs. The contract seals. Cinnamon chimes softly-recording complete.]</p> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// CONTRACT REGISTERED: 7851-K | CLIENT: [encrypted per Guild privacy protocols] | TERMS: evidence retrieval, Helios Extraction financial records, 60k credits, 14-day timeline | STATUS: active&gt;</p> <hr> <p>Vesper: [standing] We&#x27;ll begin reconnaissance within 48 hours. You&#x27;ll receive progress updates every three days via encrypted Guild channels. If complications arise, we notify you immediately and renegotiate terms if necessary.</p> <p>Client: [standing] Understood. And if I need to contact you outside the scheduled updates?</p> <p>Vesper: [handing them a data chip] Encrypted contact protocol. Use this channel only. Don&#x27;t contact us through public Guild boards or station comms. Operational security.</p> <p>Client: [taking the chip] Understood.</p> <p>[They leave. The door hisses closed behind them.]</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper sits back down. Lets out a slow breath.]</p> <p>Vesper: [to Avyanna] What did you observe?</p> <p>Avyanna: [careful] They tried to expand the contract. You said no.</p> <p>Vesper: [nodding] What else?</p> <p>Avyanna: [thinking] They tested you. To see if you&#x27;d compromise. If money would change your boundaries.</p> <p>Vesper: [approving] Yes. That&#x27;s client vetting. Some clients are honest. Some are testing. Some are actively trying to implicate you in illegal activity so they have leverage later.</p> <p>Vesper: [tapping the table] That&#x27;s why we use contracts. Why we document everything. Why we say no to requests that feel wrong.</p> <hr> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// ADDENDUM: client behavior flagged for monitoring | IF CLIENT ATTEMPTS FURTHER SCOPE EXPANSION, CONTRACT TERMINATION RECOMMENDED | VESPER&#x27;S BOUNDARY-SETTING: APPROPRIATE AND DOCUMENTED&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: [hesitant] What if they&#x27;d offered more money? A lot more?</p> <p>Vesper: [flat] Doesn&#x27;t matter. I&#x27;m not for sale. My boundaries aren&#x27;t negotiable. The moment I compromise once, every future client knows I can be bought. My reputation becomes &quot;will bend rules for the right price.&quot; That&#x27;s not a reputation worth having.</p> <p>Vesper: [meeting her eyes] Integrity is the only currency that matters in this work. Money comes and goes. Reputation is permanent.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna processes that. Boundaries as professional asset. Saying no as strength.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] The mine never let us say no. To anything.</p> <p>Vesper: [nodding] I know. That&#x27;s why I&#x27;m teaching you now. Saying no is a skill. You practice it in low-stakes contexts until it becomes reflex.</p> <p>Vesper: [standing] Today you watched me say no to sixty thousand credits plus unknown additional fees. That&#x27;s not low-stakes. But it&#x27;s necessary.</p> <p>Vesper: [meeting her eyes] You&#x27;ll be in negotiations eventually. Not today. Not next month. But eventually. And when that happens, I need you to know that saying no is always an option. Always.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna stands. Her legs are steadier than they were when they entered the meeting.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [careful] Thank you. For letting me watch.</p> <p>Vesper: [slight smile] You&#x27;re crew. You get to see how contracts work. The good ones, the questionable ones, and the ones we walk away from.</p> <p>[They leave the meeting room. In the corridor, station life continues-people moving, dealing, negotiating, living in the spaces between extraction and autonomy.]</p> <p>[Avyanna walks beside Vesper, thinking about boundaries. About saying no. About the client who tried to buy more than they&#x27;d contracted for, and the contractor who refused to sell.]</p> <hr> <p>[Back on the ship, Avyanna finds Bubbles in the cargo bay.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [to Bubbles] I watched a contract negotiation today.</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// OBSERVATION: noted | QUERY: what did you learn&gt;</p> <p>Avyanna: [sitting on a crate] That saying no is... powerful. That boundaries are worth more than money. That contracts protect both sides, not just one.</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// ASSESSMENT: correct observations | ADDENDUM: vesper is excellent at boundary-setting | YOU ARE LEARNING FROM GOOD MODELS | THIS IS IMPORTANT&gt;</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna sits with Bubbles in the quiet cargo bay, processing.]</p> <p>(Vesper said no. Lost money. Kept integrity. And the client accepted it.)</p> <p>[The shard at the base of her skull is warm.]</p> <p>(The warmth at the base of her skull steadies into a wordless impression: boundary, floor, power.)</p> <p>[She stands. Tomorrow, there will be reconnaissance for the new contract. She&#x27;ll watch that too. Learn more. Build her understanding of how contracts work, how boundaries hold, how saying no keeps you safer than saying yes to the wrong things.]</p> <p>[And slowly, incrementally, she&#x27;s learning that autonomy has a price, and that price is worth paying.]</p> <hr> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// NEGOTIATION LOG: contract 7851-K accepted with clear boundaries | CLIENT TESTING DOCUMENTED | VESPER&#x27;S REFUSAL OF SCOPE EXPANSION: appropriate and professionally executed | TRAINEE OBSERVATION ROLE: effective | ASSESSMENT: teaching moment successful&gt;</p> <p>&lt;VESPER PERSONAL LOG: &quot;Client tried to expand scope. I shut it down. Avyanna watched the whole thing. She needs to see that no is a complete sentence. That money isn&#x27;t worth compromising your floor. She&#x27;s learning.&quot;&gt;</p> <hr> <p>*End Scene*</p> </div> * [[Continue to optional scene 19b: Teaching Consent To Client->Act1 19b Teaching Consent To Client]] * [[Continue to Chapter 20: The Receipt->Act1 20 The Receipt]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Optional 19b: Teaching Consent To Client</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 19b_teaching_consent_to_client.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **[SCENE - OCCURS POST-CH19, AFTER CONTRACT COMPLETION]** &gt; *Location: Station meeting room* &gt; *Present: Avyanna (observing), Vesper, problematic client* &gt; *Purpose: Witnessing boundary-setting with difficult clients, learning to enforce scope*</p> <hr> <p>[The client is unhappy. That much is obvious from their body language-arms crossed, jaw tight, the particular tension of someone who&#x27;s used to getting what they want and isn&#x27;t getting it now.]</p> <p>[They sit across from Vesper in the same meeting room where Avyanna watched the last contract negotiation. This is the follow-up. The delivery. The moment when evidence changes hands and money changes accounts.]</p> <p>[Except the client wants more than what they contracted for.]</p> <p>[Avyanna sits beside Vesper, one chair back. Witness. Observer. Learning what happens when boundaries get tested after the work is done.]</p> <hr> <p>Client: [tense] You retrieved the financial records. Good. But I need more.</p> <p>Vesper: [neutral] More was not in the contract.</p> <p>Client: [leaning forward] While you were in their system, you must have seen other files. Personnel records. Communications. Internal memos. I need those too.</p> <p>Vesper: [flat] No.</p> <p>Client: [frustrated] I&#x27;m willing to pay-</p> <p>Vesper: [interrupting] I don&#x27;t care what you&#x27;re willing to pay. We had a contract. The contract specified financial records only. We delivered financial records only. That&#x27;s the transaction.</p> <p>Client: [pushing] You were in their system. You had access. It would have taken five minutes to copy additional files-</p> <p>Vesper: [cold] Five minutes that were outside contract scope. Five minutes that would have constituted theft rather than evidence retrieval. Five minutes that would have compromised our legal standing and our Guild reputation.</p> <hr> <p>[The client&#x27;s expression hardens.]</p> <p>Client: [calculated] I could report that you refused to fulfill the full scope of service-</p> <p>Vesper: [pulling out her datapad] Please do. Here&#x27;s the contract. Guild-certified. Every term we discussed, documented in plain language. Nowhere does it say we retrieve anything beyond specified financial records.</p> <p>[She projects the contract holographically. Lets the client read it.]</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] You&#x27;re attempting to retroactively expand scope. That&#x27;s not how contracts work. You got exactly what you paid for. Nothing less. Nothing more.</p> <p>Client: [shifting tactics] What if I offered a new contract? Right now. To retrieve those additional files.</p> <p>Vesper: [meeting their eyes] I&#x27;d decline.</p> <hr> <p>Client: [surprised] You&#x27;d decline paid work?</p> <p>Vesper: [firm] I&#x27;d decline work from a client who tries to coerce scope expansion after delivery. That behavior is a red flag. It suggests you&#x27;ll do it again. And again. Until we compromise our ethics to satisfy your demands.</p> <p>[She stands.]</p> <p>Vesper: [to the client] Here&#x27;s what happens now. You accept the evidence we delivered per contract. You pay the agreed amount. We part ways. Or you refuse, and I file a dispute with the Guild, which will side with us because we have documentation proving we fulfilled every contracted term.</p> <p>Vesper: [leaning forward slightly] Your choice. But choose quickly. I have other clients who respect boundaries.</p> <hr> <p>[The client glares. Avyanna watches, tense. In the mine, this is where violence would happen. Where authority would assert itself through force. Where the person with less power would lose.]</p> <p>[But this isn&#x27;t the mine. This is Guild space. And Vesper has contracts. Documentation. Receipts.]</p> <p>[The client pulls out a credit chit. Transfers the agreed payment. The transaction completes with a soft chime.]</p> <p>Client: [standing, bitter] I&#x27;ll remember this.</p> <p>Vesper: [unbothered] Good. Then you won&#x27;t hire us again. We prefer clients who understand consent.</p> <p>[The client leaves. The door hisses shut behind them.]</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper sits back down. Lets out a slow breath.]</p> <p>Vesper: [to Avyanna] What did you observe?</p> <p>Avyanna: [carefully] They tried to change the contract after you&#x27;d already done the work.</p> <p>Vesper: [nodding] What else?</p> <p>Avyanna: [thinking] They tried multiple tactics. Direct request. Guilt. Threat. Bribery. When one didn&#x27;t work, they tried another.</p> <p>Vesper: [approving] Yes. That&#x27;s manipulation. People who don&#x27;t respect boundaries will test every angle until they find one that works or until you shut them down completely.</p> <hr> <p>[She closes the contract file.]</p> <p>Vesper: [measured] That client will probably complain about us. They&#x27;ll say we were inflexible. Uncooperative. That we refused reasonable requests.</p> <p>Vesper: [meeting Avyanna&#x27;s eyes] They&#x27;ll be right about one thing. We were inflexible. Because flexibility about scope is how you end up doing illegal work, compromising your ethics, and destroying your reputation one &quot;small exception&quot; at a time.</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] You said no. Multiple times. They kept pushing.</p> <p>Vesper: [firm] And I kept saying no. Because no is a complete sentence. It doesn&#x27;t need justification. It doesn&#x27;t need softening. It just needs to be clear and consistent.</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper stands, gathers her things.]</p> <p>Vesper: [to Avyanna] You&#x27;re going to encounter clients like this. People who think they can pressure you into compromising. People who think your boundaries are negotiable if they pay enough or threaten enough or guilt enough.</p> <p>Vesper: [meeting her eyes] Here&#x27;s what you do. You hold your boundary. You reference the contract. You document everything. And if they continue pushing, you walk away.</p> <p>Avyanna: [uncertain] What if I need the money?</p> <p>Vesper: [flat] You&#x27;ll always need money. But if you compromise your boundaries for money once, you teach people that your boundaries are for sale. And then every future client will try to buy them.</p> <hr> <p>[They leave the meeting room. Walk through the station corridor. Around them: normal station life. People who don&#x27;t know that a contract just held against pressure. That boundaries just won without violence.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [after a moment] In the mine, we didn&#x27;t have contracts.</p> <p>Vesper: [nodding] I know.</p> <p>Avyanna: [continuing] They&#x27;d tell us to do something. We&#x27;d do it. Then they&#x27;d add more tasks. More hours. More danger. And if we said no, we got punished.</p> <p>Vesper: [stopping, turning to face her] That&#x27;s why contracts matter. That&#x27;s why documentation matters. That&#x27;s why saying no matters.</p> <p>[She taps her datapad.]</p> <p>Vesper: [precise] The mine operated on power imbalance and information asymmetry. You had no documentation. No proof of what was agreed. No recourse when they changed terms.</p> <p>Vesper: [meeting Avyanna&#x27;s eyes] Here, we document everything. Every term. Every boundary. Every transaction. So when someone tries to change the agreement, we have proof of what was actually agreed to.</p> <p>Vesper: That&#x27;s not just good business. That&#x27;s survival.</p> <hr> <p>[They return to the ship. Vesper files the contract completion report. Avyanna sits in the common room, processing.]</p> <p>[Vesper said no. The client pushed. Vesper held the boundary. The client paid anyway because the contract was clear. No violence. No coercion. Just documentation and refusal.]</p> <p>(This is what power looks like when you have receipts. When you have records. When you have proof that you did exactly what you agreed to do and nothing more.)</p> <p>[Rho enters, sees her thinking.]</p> <p>Rho: [sitting] Vesper told me about the client. The one who wanted scope expansion.</p> <p>Avyanna: [nodding] She said no. Multiple times.</p> <p>Rho: [grinning] Yeah. Vesper&#x27;s a master of the strategic &quot;fuck no.&quot; It&#x27;s beautiful to watch.</p> <hr> <p>Avyanna: [careful] What if the client had been dangerous? What if saying no meant... violence?</p> <p>Rho: [sobering] Then we wouldn&#x27;t have taken the contract in the first place. We vet clients. We assess risk. We don&#x27;t put ourselves in situations where saying no gets us killed.</p> <p>Rho: [leaning forward] But here&#x27;s the thing. Most people don&#x27;t escalate to violence when you say no. They escalate to manipulation. Guilt. Pressure. Threats that are mostly bluster.</p> <p>Rho: [meeting her eyes] And if you hold your boundary through all of that? They usually back down. Because people who rely on manipulation aren&#x27;t usually willing to risk actual consequences. They want easy compliance. Not a fight.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna processes that. Most people back down when boundaries hold.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] The mine taught me that saying no was dangerous.</p> <p>Rho: [nodding] Because in the mine, it was. The mine had all the power. You had none. Saying no meant punishment because they could enforce that punishment without consequence.</p> <p>Rho: [gesturing around the ship] Here, power is more balanced. Vesper has contracts. Documentation. Guild backing. She can say no because she has structural support for her boundaries. The client can&#x27;t punish her without facing Guild sanctions.</p> <p>Rho: [to Avyanna] You&#x27;re learning that you can have that too. Structural support. Documentation. People who&#x27;ll back your boundaries. It&#x27;s not just you versus the world anymore.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna sits with that. Structural support for boundaries. Not just personal strength, but systems that protect refusal.]</p> <p>[She thinks about the contract. About Vesper saying no. About the client leaving angry but paying anyway. About documentation as power.]</p> <p>(This is what the mine didn&#x27;t want us to know. That boundaries are enforceable when you have proof. When you have support. When you have systems that back your right to refuse.)</p> <p>[The shard at the base of her skull is warm.]</p> <p>(A warm pulse at the base of her skull: no is complete; no is protected.)</p> <p>[She stands. Tomorrow, there will be another contract. Another client. Another opportunity to watch boundaries hold or bend. Another lesson in how consent operates in commercial contexts.]</p> <p>[And slowly, incrementally, she&#x27;s learning that saying no doesn&#x27;t have to end in violence. Sometimes it just ends in contracts fulfilled and payments transferred and clients walking away disappointed but compliant.]</p> <p>[Sometimes no is enough.]</p> <hr> <p>&lt;CINNAMON.EXE// CONTRACT COMPLETION LOG: client attempted scope expansion post-delivery | VESPER REFUSED | CLIENT PAID PER CONTRACT TERMS | DISPUTE: none filed | ASSESSMENT: boundaries maintained successfully | TRAINEE WITNESSED APPROPRIATE REFUSAL UNDER PRESSURE&gt;</p> <p>&lt;VESPER PERSONAL LOG: &quot;Client tried to pressure additional retrieval post-contract. I shut it down. Avyanna watched. She needs to see that boundaries hold. That contracts protect us. That no is enforceable when you have documentation. She&#x27;s learning.&quot;&gt;</p> <p>&lt;RHO PERSONAL LOG: &quot;Found her processing Vesper&#x27;s client confrontation. She&#x27;s scared that saying no means violence. I told her about structural support. About how power is more balanced here. She&#x27;s starting to understand that she&#x27;s not alone anymore. That she has backup. That matters.&quot;&gt;</p> <hr> <p>*End Scene*</p> </div> * [[Continue to optional scene 19c: Receipt Practice->Act1 19c Receipt Practice]] * [[Continue to Chapter 20: The Receipt->Act1 20 The Receipt]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Optional 19c: Receipt Practice</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 19c_receipt_practice.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **[SCENE - OCCURS BETWEEN CH19 AND CH20, PRE-HEARING WORK]** &gt; *Location: Vesper&#x27;s office* &gt; *Present: Avyanna, Vesper* &gt; *Teaching: &quot;claim -&gt; evidence -&gt; inference&quot; as a safe spine; trauma handled as procedure*</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s office is too orderly to be natural.]</p> <p>[Shelves. Binders. A desk that looks like it has never been used for anything except war.]</p> <p>[Avyanna sits across from Vesper with a stack of printouts and a pen that feels heavier than it should.]</p> <p>Vesper: Tell me what you saw.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s jaw tightens on instinct.]</p> <p>(If I tell the truth wrong, it becomes a weapon aimed at me.)</p> <p>Avyanna: Which part.</p> <p>Vesper: The part you are scared to put into words.</p> <p>[Avyanna swallows.]</p> <p>Avyanna: The station clerk tried to make me sign &quot;voluntary disclosure.&quot;</p> <p>Vesper: Good. That&#x27;s a claim.</p> <p>Avyanna: [baffled] That&#x27;s not-</p> <p>Vesper: It is. &quot;Clerk attempted coercion.&quot; Claim.</p> <p>[Vesper taps the printouts.]</p> <p>Vesper: Evidence.</p> <p>Avyanna: The slip. The camera footage request Elisira filed. The time stamp on the terminal log.</p> <p>Vesper: Good.</p> <p>Vesper: Inference.</p> <p>Avyanna: They were fishing. For identity. For contacts.</p> <p>Vesper: Next step.</p> <p>Avyanna: Report it. Archive it. Don&#x27;t sign.</p> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s mouth twitches.]</p> <p>Vesper: You&#x27;re learning.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s chest tightens.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I&#x27;m trying.</p> <p>Vesper: Trying is a verb. Keep using it.</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper slides a new printout toward her.]</p> <p>[Aurum-coded header. Not the mine&#x27;s logo, exactly-cleaner, but the same colors.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s vision tunnels.]</p> <p>[The room tilts.]</p> <p>(No.)</p> <p>[Her hands go cold.]</p> <p>Vesper: Avyanna. Eyes on me.</p> <p>[Avyanna forces her gaze up.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [thin] That looks like-</p> <p>Vesper: Aurum.</p> <p>Avyanna: [breathing wrong] They&#x27;re here.</p> <p>Vesper: No.</p> <p>Vesper: Not physically.</p> <p>Vesper: It&#x27;s a record artifact. A data style. A mimic.</p> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s voice stays calm on purpose.]</p> <p>Vesper: Check.</p> <p>Avyanna: [after a beat] Yellow.</p> <p>Vesper: Good. Stay with yellow.</p> <p>[Vesper does not touch her.]</p> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t have to.]</p> <p>[She moves the printout away, flips it face-down.]</p> <p>Vesper: We don&#x27;t process trauma by pretending it isn&#x27;t there.</p> <p>Vesper: We process it by turning it into something that can be handled.</p> <p>Avyanna: That sounds like you.</p> <p>Vesper: It is me.</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper opens a binder and pulls out a template.]</p> <p>&gt; **WITNESS STATEMENT (DRAFT)** &gt; &gt; *Claim* &gt; &gt; *Evidence* &gt; &gt; *Inference* &gt; &gt; *Confounders* &gt; &gt; *What would prove me wrong*</p> <p>Avyanna: Cinnamon would like that.</p> <p>Vesper: Cinnamon does like that.</p> <p>Vesper: Now.</p> <p>Vesper: We&#x27;re going to practice on something that won&#x27;t kill you if you get it wrong.</p> <p>Avyanna: [dry] That&#x27;s generous.</p> <p>Vesper: It&#x27;s responsible.</p> <p>[Avyanna picks up her pen.]</p> <p>[Her hand still shakes, but she can write through it.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Claim: the mine&#x27;s medical bay neglected workers on purpose.</p> <p>Vesper: Evidence.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s voice goes quieter.]</p> <p>Avyanna: The logs. The ration schedule. The number of collapsed people per shift. The way they stamped &quot;noncompliant&quot; on anyone who asked for painkillers.</p> <p>Vesper: Inference.</p> <p>Avyanna: The neglect was policy. Not failure.</p> <p>Vesper: Confounders.</p> <p>[Avyanna freezes.]</p> <p>Vesper: This is where you stop being a victim of your own certainty.</p> <p>Avyanna: [working] Confounders... shortages. Staffing. Flicker day malfunctions.</p> <p>Vesper: Good.</p> <p>Vesper: What would prove you wrong.</p> <p>Avyanna: A supply manifest showing adequate medicine. A record of workers treated promptly.</p> <p>Vesper: And if those exist.</p> <p>Avyanna: Then I&#x27;m wrong.</p> <p>[The words taste strange in her mouth.]</p> <p>(Wrong means you die.)</p> <p>[Here, wrong means: adjust.]</p> <p>Vesper: If you can be proven wrong, you can be trusted.</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s... backwards.</p> <p>Vesper: It&#x27;s adversarial.</p> <p>Vesper: The people who can&#x27;t be wrong are trying to sell you something.</p> <hr> <p>[A pause.]</p> <p>[Avyanna stares at the blank space on her template labeled WITNESS STATEMENT and feels something in her chest shift.]</p> <p>Avyanna: This is... how you fight.</p> <p>Vesper: This is one way.</p> <p>Vesper: Another way is Elia.</p> <p>Avyanna: [a small, real laugh] Yes.</p> <p>[Vesper stands, goes to the shelf, and takes down a thin folder.]</p> <p>Vesper: Tomorrow we go to arbitration.</p> <p>Vesper: Today we build the spine.</p> <p>[She sets the folder in front of Avyanna.]</p> <p>Vesper: No heroics.</p> <p>Avyanna: [soft] Just receipts.</p> <p>Vesper: Just receipts.</p> <p>[Avyanna opens the folder.]</p> <p>[This time, the paper doesn&#x27;t feel like a trap.]</p> <p>[It feels like a handle.]</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper doesn&#x27;t let the moment turn sentimental.]</p> <p>[She flips to a new section in the binder and slides it across.]</p> <p>&gt; **CROSS-EXAMINATION PREP** &gt; &gt; *If they say you&#x27;re lying, what do you point to?* &gt; &gt; *If they say you misremembered, what do you point to?* &gt; &gt; *If they say it&#x27;s &quot;standard,&quot; what do you ask?*</p> <p>Avyanna: [reading] What do you ask.</p> <p>Vesper: &quot;Under which jurisdiction. Enforced by whom. Consequence for refusal.&quot;</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] That&#x27;s... the spine.</p> <p>Vesper: Yes.</p> <p>Vesper: When someone is trying to drown you in language, you don&#x27;t swim.</p> <p>Vesper: You stand on three questions.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s pen hovers.]</p> <p>Avyanna: In the mine, questions got you noticed.</p> <p>Vesper: Here, questions keep you alive.</p> <p>[A beat.]</p> <p>Vesper: Not just physically.</p> <p>[Avyanna swallows.]</p> <p>Avyanna: This feels like fighting without... touching anyone.</p> <p>Vesper: It is.</p> <p>Vesper: It&#x27;s slower than Elia. But it leaves bodies intact.</p> <p>Avyanna: [dry] Sometimes.</p> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s mouth twitches.]</p> <p>Vesper: Sometimes.</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper pulls a small index card from a box and slides it toward Avyanna.]</p> <p>&gt; **YOUR JOB IN THE ROOM** &gt; &gt; - say what you saw &gt; - don&#x27;t guess &gt; - if you don&#x27;t know, say you don&#x27;t know &gt; - if you need a minute, ask for it</p> <p>Avyanna: [soft] I can ask for a minute.</p> <p>Vesper: Yes.</p> <p>Vesper: If anyone tries to deny you that, I will make it expensive.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens.]</p> <p>(Expensive. Protective. Not ownership.)</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper taps the face-down Aurum-coded printout with one knuckle.]</p> <p>Vesper: This.</p> <p>Avyanna: [tense] Yes.</p> <p>Vesper: You did not break when you saw it.</p> <p>Avyanna: I almost did.</p> <p>Vesper: &quot;Almost&quot; is data.</p> <p>Avyanna: That&#x27;s... also you.</p> <p>Vesper: [without apology] Yes.</p> <p>[She pauses.]</p> <p>Vesper: When you&#x27;re in the room tomorrow and something tries to pull you back into the mine-</p> <p>Avyanna: Like a smell. Like a word.</p> <p>Vesper: -you do not have to white-knuckle it.</p> <p>Vesper: You can say: &quot;I need to pause.&quot;</p> <p>Avyanna: [quiet] And nobody will-</p> <p>Vesper: No penalty.</p> <p>[The phrase lands like a seal on paper.]</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna writes it down.]</p> <p>&gt; **NO PENALTY**</p> <p>[Not in Cinnamon&#x27;s font. In her own.]</p> <p>[She looks up at Vesper.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Thank you.</p> <p>Vesper: [measured] You&#x27;re welcome.</p> <p>[Vesper straightens Avyanna&#x27;s collar without asking. Then catches herself.]</p> <p>Vesper: Go drink water.</p> <p>Avyanna: [blinking] That&#x27;s an order.</p> <p>Vesper: It&#x27;s medical advice.</p> <p>Avyanna: [a small smile] Who enforces it.</p> <p>Vesper: Nyx.</p> <p>[Avyanna laughs.]</p> <p>Vesper: And also me.</p> <p>[The second part is said so quietly it almost doesn&#x27;t count.]</p> <p>[Almost.]</p> <p>[Avyanna gathers the folder and the index card like they&#x27;re equipment.]</p> <p>[Because they are.]</p> <p>[She leaves Vesper&#x27;s office with her spine built from paper and the strange sensation of being allowed to carry it.]</p> </div> * [[Continue to Chapter 20: The Receipt->Act1 20 The Receipt]] * [[Return to route map->Act 1 Route Map]]<h1>Chapter 20: The Receipt</h1> <p class="source-note">Source: 20_the_receipt.md</p> <div class="scene-prose"> <p>&gt; **GUILD CONTRACT COMPLETION - FORM 91-B:** &gt; *Client: Delmont Shipping Consortium* &gt; *Service: Evidence recovery and arbitration support* &gt; *Status: COMPLETE - client satisfaction noted* &gt; *Addendum: No further services requested. No further services offered.* &gt; *Note: Some receipts are written in broken bones.*</p> <hr> <p>[The job is finished.]</p> <p>[Three days of evidence gathering on a disputed cargo route. Standard work-document recovery, witness interviews, chain-of-custody verification. The kind of contract that pays the ship&#x27;s fuel bill and doesn&#x27;t leave scars.]</p> <p>[Avyanna is in the client&#x27;s office with Vesper, watching the final handoff. Delmont-a mid-sized shipping consortium with delusions of Compact legitimacy-has what they wanted: proof that their competitor falsified transit manifests. The arbiter will rule in their favor. Money will change hands. Everyone goes home.]</p> <p>[The client is a man named Harren. Broad, expensive suit, the kind of smile that doesn&#x27;t reach his eyes. His desk has a brass ornament shaped like a scale-but one side holds a clock, the other a stack of coins. A debt clock, decorative. The kind of thing someone buys to remind themselves that time is money and people are both.]</p> <p>[He&#x27;s been watching Avyanna throughout the meeting-not the way you watch a person, but the way you assess inventory.]</p> <p>[The shard at the base of her skull is cold. Not pain. Warning.]</p> <hr> <p>Harren: [leaning back in his chair] Excellent work. Truly. I can see why the Lumen Thief has the reputation it does.</p> <p>Vesper: [neutral] We deliver what we promise.</p> <p>Harren: Indeed. Which brings me to an additional matter.</p> <p>[He gestures at Avyanna. Casual. Like pointing at a piece of furniture.]</p> <p>Harren: The girl. What&#x27;s her arrangement?</p> <p>[The cold at Avyanna&#x27;s skull sharpens. She knows this tone. She heard it in the mine-supervisors discussing &quot;worker allocation,&quot; &quot;asset transfer,&quot; &quot;productivity optimization.&quot; Words that meant bodies changing hands.]</p> <p>Vesper: [voice unchanged] She&#x27;s crew.</p> <p>Harren: Yes, but what&#x27;s her *contract*? Employment terms, debt structure, ownership stake? I&#x27;m prepared to make a competitive offer.</p> <hr> <p>[The silence is absolute.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s hands have gone still. Her training-eight months of it-screams at her to stay invisible, stay small, let the adults handle whatever this is. But something else is rising. Something that feels like the shard waking up.]</p> <p>(He thinks I&#x27;m for sale.)</p> <p>[The thought lands like a physical weight. Eight months of freedom, and this man looks at her and sees property.]</p> <p>Vesper: [still calm, still neutral] I don&#x27;t think you understand. She&#x27;s not contracted. She&#x27;s not employed. She&#x27;s not owned.</p> <p>Harren: [waving a hand] Everyone has a price. I&#x27;m simply asking what hers is. Young, trainable, clearly talented-the Lumen Thief must have invested significantly. I&#x27;m willing to compensate appropriately.</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s expression doesn&#x27;t change. But something else does-a single tremor in her fingertips, there and gone. Her jaw tightens for half a second. Her eyes go flat in a way Avyanna recognizes from somewhere deep and old: *predator has entered the room.*]</p> <p>[That&#x27;s how Avyanna knows something is about to happen. Vesper&#x27;s tells are all in the stillness-the way she stops moving, the way her hands settle, the way her breathing goes perfectly even. But this time there was a leak first. Something involuntary.]</p> <p>Vesper: Avyanna, would you wait outside?</p> <p>Avyanna: [finding her voice] No.</p> <p>[It comes out harder than she intended. The shard flares-heat, not cold. Approval, maybe. Or just anger finding its shape.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I want to hear what he&#x27;s offering.</p> <p>[Harren&#x27;s smile widens. He thinks this is negotiation. He thinks she&#x27;s playing the market.]</p> <p>Harren: Smart girl. Three-year contract, premium wages, housing in the consortium compound. Training in evidence work, since that seems to be your specialty. And a signing bonus equivalent to-</p> <p>Vesper: Stop talking.</p> <hr> <p>[The words are quiet. Almost conversational.]</p> <p>[Harren stops. Not because of the tone-because of what&#x27;s behind it. Something has shifted in Vesper&#x27;s posture. The fixer is gone. What&#x27;s left is something older, harder, carved from grief and violence.]</p> <p>Harren: [uncertain] I&#x27;m simply making an offer-</p> <p>Vesper: You&#x27;re attempting to purchase a person.</p> <p>Harren: That&#x27;s not-</p> <p>Vesper: You looked at a sixteen-year-old girl who escaped a debt-extraction facility and asked what her *price* was. You spoke about her like inventory. You assumed she was for sale because everyone you&#x27;ve ever met has been for sale.</p> <p>[She takes a step forward. Harren takes a step back. The geometry of the room has changed-predator and prey, not fixer and client.]</p> <p>Vesper: She is not for sale. She was never for sale. And if you ever speak about her-or anyone on my crew-like property again, I will personally ensure that every fraudulent manifest, every bribed official, every body your consortium has buried becomes public record.</p> <hr> <p>Harren: [recovering, anger rising] This is-you can&#x27;t threaten a client-</p> <p>Vesper: I&#x27;m not threatening you. I&#x27;m informing you of consequences.</p> <p>[Vesper looks at Avyanna.]</p> <p>[Not for permission.]</p> <p>[For presence.]</p> <p>[She moves. Fast-faster than Avyanna has ever seen her move. Her hand closes around Harren&#x27;s hand, the one he was gesturing with, and twists.]</p> <p>[The crack is audible. Harren screams.]</p> <p>[Vesper doesn&#x27;t let go. She holds the broken hand, steady, until the scream fades to whimpering. Her expression hasn&#x27;t changed.]</p> <p>Vesper: That&#x27;s not a threat either. That&#x27;s a receipt.</p> <p>[Harren is gasping, tears streaming, reaching for the comm panel on his desk.]</p> <p>Vesper: [conversational] If you call security, the files go public. Every falsified manifest. Every bribed port official. The body in the Caelum Reach static zone-the one your people forgot to report.</p> <p>[Harren&#x27;s hand freezes over the comm.]</p> <p>Vesper: Your consortium survives on the appearance of legitimacy. I&#x27;ve spent three days documenting how thin that appearance is. Call security, and I file everything before they reach this door.</p> <p>[A beat.]</p> <p>Vesper: Or you can tell them you fell. Your choice.</p> <hr> <p>[She releases him. Harren collapses into his chair, cradling his hand, face gray with shock and pain. His hand doesn&#x27;t touch the comm.]</p> <p>[Vesper turns to Avyanna. Her eyes are clear, calm, completely present.]</p> <p>Vesper: Ready to go?</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s heart is pounding. The shard is blazing-not pain, not warning, something else entirely. Something that feels like recognition.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [barely audible] Yes.</p> <hr> <p>[They leave. The corridor outside is quiet. Station lights hum. Somewhere distant, a cargo lift whines through its cycle.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s hands are shaking. Not fear-not anymore. Something else.]</p> <p>Avyanna: You broke his hand.</p> <p>Vesper: He&#x27;ll survive.</p> <p>Avyanna: You broke his hand because he-</p> <p>[She can&#x27;t finish the sentence. The words tangle. What she wants to say is: *because he looked at me like I was inventory. Because he thought I could be bought. Because he didn&#x27;t see me as a person.*]</p> <p>[What comes out is:]</p> <p>Avyanna: You didn&#x27;t hesitate.</p> <p>Vesper: [walking, not looking back] No.</p> <p>Avyanna: Why?</p> <p>[Vesper stops. The corridor stretches empty in both directions. When she turns, her expression is something Avyanna hasn&#x27;t seen before-not the fixer&#x27;s calm, not the teacher&#x27;s patience. Something rawer.]</p> <p>Vesper: Because you&#x27;re not inventory. You&#x27;re not a contract. You&#x27;re not a price to negotiate.</p> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s voice catches on something she doesn&#x27;t name.]</p> <p>Vesper: You&#x27;re family.</p> <p>[The word lands like a physical impact.]</p> <p>[Family. Not crew. Not trainee. Not investment or asset or acceptable loss.]</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s eyes are burning. She blinks hard, refuses to let the tears fall. The shard pulses-warm, steady, witnessing.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [voice cracking] Vesper-</p> <p>Vesper: [cutting her off, gentle] Not here. Let&#x27;s get back to the ship.</p> <hr> <p>[They walk in silence. Avyanna&#x27;s mind is racing.]</p> <p>[She&#x27;s seen Vesper work. Seen her deploy evidence like precision weapons. Seen her negotiate, manipulate, construct leverage from fragments. But she&#x27;s never seen Vesper *hurt* someone. Never seen that immediate, unthinking violence-no calculation, no cost-benefit, just fury given form.]</p> <p>(She broke his hand. For me. Without hesitating. Without calculating.)</p> <p>[The thought keeps circling.]</p> <p>(There was no invoice. No ledger. No asking what it would cost her professionally. She just... acted.)</p> <p>[And underneath that thought, another one. Smaller. More dangerous.]</p> <p>(I didn&#x27;t know kindness could look like that.)</p> <hr> <p>[Back on the Lumen Thief, Vesper disappears into her quarters. Avyanna stands in the corridor, uncertain, the shard humming questions she can&#x27;t quite articulate.]</p> <p>[Bubbles drifts close. The drone&#x27;s presence is familiar now-a small warmth, a silent offer of company.]</p> <p>&lt;BUBBLES// OBSERVATION: trainee distressed | DURATION: ongoing | INFERENCE: processing | CONFIDENCE: 0.86 | RECOMMENDATION: space + time | NOTE: she&#x27;ll figure it out. they always do.&gt;</p> <p>[Avyanna finds a quiet corner of the observation deck. Sits. Stares at the stars until her heartbeat slows.]</p> <hr> <p>[Hours later, she can&#x27;t sleep.]</p> <p>[The ship&#x27;s night cycle has settled into quiet hum. Most of the crew is in their bunks. Somewhere, Jalen is probably still awake-they often are-but the corridors are empty.]</p> <p>[Avyanna wanders. The shard guides her, or she guides herself, or maybe there&#x27;s no difference anymore.]</p> <p>[She finds herself outside Vesper&#x27;s quarters. The door is closed but not sealed. Light seeps from the crack-someone still awake.]</p> <p>[She hesitates. Then knocks.]</p> <hr> <p>Vesper: [from inside] Come in.</p> <p>[The quarters are small but personal. Stacked datapads. A wall of preserved flowers-dried, pressed, labeled with dates and locations. A single photograph of a woman Avyanna doesn&#x27;t recognize, older, smiling at something outside the frame.]</p> <p>[Vesper is sitting at a console.]</p> <p>[On the screen: a Guild interface, too clean to belong to Aurum. Aurum-coded headers anyway. A list of worker tags. A field that reads SUBMITTED BY: VESPER ROOK.]</p> <p>[Avyanna recognizes the shape of paperwork that makes people disappear. Or keeps them from disappearing. She can&#x27;t always tell the difference, but she knows the weapon when she sees it.]</p> <p>Vesper: Can&#x27;t sleep?</p> <p>Avyanna: No.</p> <p>[She stands in the doorway. Uncertain. The shard is quiet-present but not pushing.]</p> <p>[Her eyes keep snapping back to the screen.]</p> <p>Avyanna: What are you doing?</p> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s hands pause on the console.]</p> <p>Vesper: [quietly] Paying late interest.</p> <p>Avyanna: Can I ask you something?</p> <p>Vesper: [still not looking up] You can always ask.</p> <p>Avyanna: What does that mean?</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper finally looks at her. Not annoyed. Not caught. Just tired.]</p> <p>Vesper: It means they don&#x27;t get to un-happen everyone we couldn&#x27;t carry out.</p> <p>[The cursor blinks. Vesper watches it like it owes her something.]</p> <p>Vesper: I&#x27;m moving names into places Aurum can&#x27;t scrub clean without effort. Spreading copies. Making the record heavy.</p> <p>[She turns back to the console. The cursor blinks. The kind of blink that costs. That attaches her name to something she could have left alone.]</p> <p>Vesper: They deserved better than we could give them. This is... late interest.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s throat tightens. She doesn&#x27;t know what to do with this kind of violence-quiet, administrative, aimed at ghosts.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Why did you break his hand? Risk the contract. Risk the consortium coming after the ship.</p> <p>[A long silence.]</p> <p>Vesper: [quietly] Because there are limits.</p> <p>Avyanna: To what?</p> <p>Vesper: To what I&#x27;ll tolerate. To what I&#x27;ll calculate around.</p> <p>[She finally turns. Her face is tired-older than Avyanna usually sees it. The armor down, just for a moment.]</p> <p>Vesper: I&#x27;ve done the math on a lot of terrible things. Weighed costs. Accepted trade-offs. That&#x27;s what this job requires. But some things aren&#x27;t trade-offs. Some things are just... lines.</p> <p>[She looks at Avyanna directly.]</p> <p>Vesper: You&#x27;re a line.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s chest aches. The shard is warm-not hot, not warning. Just present. Holding space for whatever is happening.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I don&#x27;t understand.</p> <p>Vesper: [almost smiling] You don&#x27;t have to understand it. You just have to know it&#x27;s true.</p> <p>[She stands. Crosses to where Avyanna is standing. Stops close enough to touch, but doesn&#x27;t.]</p> <p>Vesper: When I was younger than you-not much, but younger-someone protected me. A woman I loved. She died doing it. I&#x27;ve spent a long time since then trying to figure out what that cost meant. Whether it was worth it. Whether I should let anyone close enough to pay that price again.</p> <p>[Her voice is steady, but something underneath trembles.]</p> <p>Vesper: What happened today wasn&#x27;t calculation. It wasn&#x27;t strategy. It was just... I saw someone try to buy you, and everything else stopped mattering.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s breath catches.]</p> <p>[She&#x27;s felt a lot of things in the months since the Lumen Thief pulled her from the mine. Gratitude. Confusion. The slow, uncertain growth of trust. But this is different. This is-]</p> <p>(I don&#x27;t know what this is.)</p> <p>[The thought arrives without shape. Without name. Just a weight in her chest that feels like hunger, like debt, like something owed that she can&#x27;t calculate.]</p> <p>(I want to stay near her. Like proximity is oxygen.)</p> <p>[Not the way she feels about Elia-the awe, the impossible standard. Not the way she feels about Elisira-the fear mixed with wanting to be seen. Not the crew-warmth she&#x27;s building with Rho, Nyx, Jalen.]</p> <p>[This is different. This is specific. This is something she doesn&#x27;t have a word for.]</p> <hr> <p>[Vesper is watching her. Patient. Waiting.]</p> <p>Avyanna: [barely a whisper] Thank you.</p> <p>Vesper: [soft] You don&#x27;t have to thank me.</p> <p>Avyanna: I know. I&#x27;m doing it anyway.</p> <p>[Vesper&#x27;s mouth curves. Almost a smile. Something sad underneath.]</p> <p>Vesper: Get some sleep, Avyanna. We&#x27;ve got a long transit tomorrow.</p> <p>[Avyanna nods. Turns to leave. Pauses at the door.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Vesper?</p> <p>Vesper: Yes?</p> <p>Avyanna: The woman in the photograph. Is that her?</p> <p>[A long silence.]</p> <p>Vesper: Yes.</p> <p>Avyanna: What was her name?</p> <p>Vesper: [very quietly] Rowan.</p> <p>[Avyanna nods. Leaves. Doesn&#x27;t look back.]</p> <hr> <p>[She doesn&#x27;t go to her bunk.]</p> <p>[Her feet take her to the galley because the galley is where the ship feels most like a city instead of a weapon.]</p> <p>[Elia and Elisira are there, late-hour domestic: two mugs, a scattered deck of cards, the remains of an argument that didn&#x27;t need to be finished.]</p> <p>[Elia&#x27;s chair is tipped back like she trusts gravity.]</p> <p>[Elisira&#x27;s is aligned with the table edge like she doesn&#x27;t.]</p> <p>[Avyanna stops in the doorway.]</p> <p>[Exit count: two.]</p> <p>[Her hands are shaking again.]</p> <p>Elia: Hey.</p> <p>Elisira: Sit.</p> <p>[Elisira doesn&#x27;t ask.]</p> <p>[Avyanna sits anyway.]</p> <p>[A mug slides toward her.]</p> <p>[Warm. Bitter. Real.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I can&#x27;t sleep.</p> <p>Elia: Yeah.</p> <p>[No judgment.]</p> <p>[Just the soft, brutal assumption that if she&#x27;s here, it&#x27;s because she&#x27;s breaking.]</p> <p>Avyanna: It&#x27;s Vesper.</p> <p>[The shard at the base of her skull is warm.]</p> <p>[Not approval.]</p> <p>[Witness.]</p> <p>Elisira: Tell us what happened.</p> <p>Elisira: Only what you saw.</p> <hr> <p>[Avyanna does.]</p> <p>[Harren&#x27;s smile.]</p> <p>[The word contract.]</p> <p>[The way Vesper&#x27;s fingers trembled once, like something escaped her control for half a second.]</p> <p>[The way her eyes went flat.]</p> <p>[The way she said stop talking.]</p> <p>[The crack.]</p> <p>[The scream.]</p> <p>Avyanna: She didn&#x27;t look at him when she moved.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s voice is too tight.]</p> <p>Avyanna: She looked at me.</p> <p>[Cooling beat.]</p> <p>[Elia doesn&#x27;t speak.]</p> <p>[Elisira&#x27;s mouth tightens like she hates that this is true.]</p> <p>Avyanna: And then she called me family.</p> <p>Avyanna: Like it was a fact.</p> <p>Avyanna: Like it cost her nothing.</p> <p>[A laugh tries to climb out of Avyanna&#x27;s throat and turns into a cough.]</p> <p>Avyanna: And then I found her in her quarters.</p> <p>Avyanna: She was filing something.</p> <p>Avyanna: With her name on it.</p> <p>Avyanna: About Aurum.</p> <p>Avyanna: About the workers.</p> <p>Avyanna: Like she was paying for people who aren&#x27;t even here.</p> <p>[Avyanna&#x27;s fingers lock around the mug.]</p> <p>Avyanna: I don&#x27;t know what to do with the feeling.</p> <p>Avyanna: I keep wanting to make it even.</p> <p>Avyanna: I keep trying to find the invoice.</p> <hr> <p>Elia: [quiet] Like a debt you can&#x27;t repay.</p> <p>[Avyanna looks up.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Yes.</p> <p>Elisira: [careful] That&#x27;s not debt.</p> <p>Elisira: That&#x27;s your instincts trying to put a price tag on safety.</p> <p>[Avyanna flinches, because she&#x27;s right.]</p> <p>Elia: Vesper doesn&#x27;t do performative.</p> <p>Elia: She does receipts.</p> <p>Elia: She does lines.</p> <p>[Elia turns her cup between her palms.]</p> <p>Elia: And she doesn&#x27;t let people in easily.</p> <p>Elia: You&#x27;re not imagining that.</p> <p>Elisira: She&#x27;s less guarded around you.</p> <p>[Avyanna freezes.]</p> <p>Avyanna: You noticed.</p> <p>Elisira: You&#x27;re loud.</p> <p>[It&#x27;s not a joke.]</p> <p>[It&#x27;s an assessment.]</p> <p>Elisira: She stays in rooms longer.</p> <p>Elisira: She corrects you less.</p> <p>Elisira: She lets herself be seen and then pretends she didn&#x27;t.</p> <hr> <p>Avyanna: So what is this?</p> <p>[Her voice scrapes.]</p> <p>Avyanna: Is it love?</p> <p>Avyanna: Or is it just...</p> <p>(Safety.)</p> <p>(Hunger.)</p> <p>(Kennel math trying to eat something clean.)</p> <p>Elia: You don&#x27;t have to decide that tonight.</p> <p>Elisira: What you do decide is how you behave.</p> <p>Elisira: Vesper is older.</p> <p>Elisira: You&#x27;re sixteen.</p> <p>Elisira: She will not cross that line.</p> <p>Elisira: Not because she doesn&#x27;t care.</p> <p>Elisira: Because she refuses to be the kind of
